CONDITION: DIABETES (Type 1, Type 2, Gestational)
- AVOID: Refined sugars, high-fructose corn syrup, white bread, white rice, sweetened yogurts, dried fruits with added sugar, and fruit juices.
- PREFER: Low Glycemic Index (GI) carbohydrates. Steel-cut oats, quinoa, lentils, chickpeas, non-starchy vegetables (leafy greens, broccoli, peppers), and healthy fats (avocado, walnuts).
- CLINICAL NOTE: Space carbohydrate intake evenly throughout the day to prevent glucose spikes.

CONDITION: HYPERTENSION (High Blood Pressure)
- AVOID: High-sodium foods, canned soups, processed meats (deli meats, sausages), frozen dinners, and excessive caffeine.
- PREFER: The DASH Diet approach. High potassium foods (bananas, spinach, sweet potatoes), magnesium-rich seeds, lean proteins, and hibiscus tea.
- CLINICAL NOTE: Keep daily sodium intake below 2,300mg (ideally 1,500mg).

CONDITION: PCOS (Polycystic Ovary Syndrome)
- AVOID: Sugary snacks, processed soy, and excessive simple starches that trigger insulin resistance.
- PREFER: Anti-inflammatory foods. Fatty fish (salmon), berries, turmeric, cinnamon, and high-fiber cruciferous vegetables.
- CLINICAL NOTE: Focus on a balance of protein and fiber in every meal to manage insulin levels.

CONDITION: RENAL ISSUES (Chronic Kidney Disease - CKD)
- AVOID: High-phosphorus foods (dark colas, organ meats), high-potassium foods (tomatoes, oranges - depending on stage), and excessive table salt.
- PREFER: High-quality, controlled protein (egg whites, small portions of chicken), cauliflower, blueberries, and red grapes.
- CLINICAL NOTE: Limit protein intake strictly as per GFR stage to reduce kidney workload.

CONDITION: PRENATAL / POSTNATAL NUTRITION
- AVOID: Unpasteurized cheeses, raw sprouts, high-mercury fish (shark, swordfish), and excessive vitamin A supplements.
- PREFER: Folic acid-rich foods (legumes, citrus), iron (lean red meat or spinach with Vitamin C for absorption), and Choline (eggs).
- CLINICAL NOTE: Caloric needs increase by ~340 kcal/day in the second trimester and ~450 kcal/day in the third.

GOAL: WEIGHT LOSS (Fat Loss)
- STRATEGY: Caloric Deficit (approx. 300-500 kcal below TDEE).
- MACRO PRIORITY: High protein (1.6g - 2.0g per kg of body weight) to preserve muscle mass. High fiber to increase satiety.
- FOODS: Lean poultry, white fish, voluminous vegetables (zucchini, cucumber), and berries.

GOAL: WEIGHT GAIN / BULKING
- STRATEGY: Caloric Surplus (approx. 300-500 kcal above TDEE).
- MACRO PRIORITY: Moderate protein, high complex carbohydrates to fuel training and recovery.
- FOODS: Liquid calories (smoothies with oats/peanut butter), whole eggs, pasta, rice, and healthy oils (olive oil).

GOAL: MUSCLE GAIN (Hypertrophy)
- STRATEGY: Maintenance or slight surplus.
- MACRO PRIORITY: 2.2g protein per kg of body weight. Focus on "Leucine-rich" proteins for muscle protein synthesis.
- FOODS: Whey protein, Greek yogurt, lean beef, soy, and cottage cheese.

GOAL: HEIGHT GAIN (Adolescent Nutrition)
- STRATEGY: Focus on bone mineralization and HGH support.
- NUTRIENT PRIORITY: Calcium, Vitamin D, Vitamin K2, Zinc, and Protein.
- FOODS: Dairy (milk/cheese), fortified cereals, sardines (with bones), almonds, and pumpkin seeds.
- NOTE: Effective only before growth plates fuse.

ALLERGY: CELIAC / GLUTEN SENSITIVITY
- SUBSTITUTE: Use Almond flour, Coconut flour, Buckwheat, or Certified Gluten-Free Oats instead of wheat/barley/rye.

ALLERGY: LACTOSE INTOLERANCE
- SUBSTITUTE: Almond milk, Oat milk, Soy milk, or Coconut yogurt. Ensure they are calcium-fortified.

DIET TYPE: VEGAN / VEGETARIAN
- PROTEIN SOURCES: Tempeh, Seitan, Tofu, Nutritional Yeast, Spirulina, and Pea Protein.
- B12 SUPPLEMENTATION: Necessary for strict vegans as B12 is primarily animal-derived.


WEIGHT LOSS NUTRITION GUIDE
===========================

CALORIE DEFICIT PRINCIPLES
--------------------------
- Safe weight loss: 0.5-1 kg (1-2 lbs) per week
- Required deficit: 500-750 calories below maintenance
- Minimum intake: 1200 calories (women), 1500 calories (men)
- Never go below BMR (Basal Metabolic Rate)

MACRONUTRIENT RATIOS FOR WEIGHT LOSS
------------------------------------
- Protein: 30-35% (higher to preserve muscle)
- Carbohydrates: 35-40% (focus on complex carbs)
- Fats: 25-30% (emphasize healthy fats)
- Fiber: 25-35g daily (increases satiety)

BEST FOODS FOR WEIGHT LOSS
--------------------------
Proteins:
- Chicken breast (165 cal, 31g protein per 100g)
- Turkey breast
- White fish (cod, tilapia, halibut)
- Egg whites
- Greek yogurt (non-fat)
- Cottage cheese (low-fat)
- Tofu and tempeh
- Lentils and legumes
- Shrimp and shellfish

Vegetables (Unlimited):
- Leafy greens (spinach, kale, arugula)
- Cruciferous vegetables (broccoli, cauliflower, Brussels sprouts)
- Cucumber, celery, zucchini
- Bell peppers
- Mushrooms
- Asparagus
- Green beans

Complex Carbohydrates:
- Oats (steel-cut or rolled)
- Quinoa
- Brown rice
- Sweet potatoes
- Whole grain bread (Ezekiel bread)
- Legumes (beans, lentils, chickpeas)

Healthy Fats:
- Avocado (portion controlled)
- Olive oil (1 tablespoon)
- Nuts (almonds, walnuts - small handful)
- Chia seeds and flaxseeds
- Fatty fish (salmon, mackerel - limited portions)

FOODS TO AVOID FOR WEIGHT LOSS
------------------------------
- Sugary beverages (soda, juice, energy drinks)
- Refined carbohydrates (white bread, pastries, cookies)
- Fried foods
- Processed meats (high sodium and fat)
- Full-fat dairy (in excess)
- Alcohol (empty calories, increases appetite)
- Fast food and takeout
- High-calorie coffee drinks
- Candy and chocolate (except dark 85%+ in small amounts)

MEAL TIMING STRATEGIES
----------------------
- Intermittent Fasting: 16:8 method (fast 16 hours, eat 8 hours)
- Eat protein at every meal
- Front-load calories (bigger breakfast, lighter dinner)
- Stop eating 3 hours before bed
- Hydrate before meals (500ml water 30 min before eating)

METABOLISM BOOSTERS
-------------------
- Green tea (EGCG increases fat oxidation)
- Black coffee (caffeine boosts metabolism 3-11%)
- Chili peppers (capsaicin increases thermogenesis)
- Protein-rich foods (TEF - Thermic Effect of Food)
- Cold water (body burns calories warming it)
- Strength training (builds muscle, increases BMR)

HORMONAL CONSIDERATIONS
-----------------------
- Insulin: Keep stable by avoiding sugar spikes
- Leptin: Don't crash calories too low (causes resistance)
- Ghrelin: Manage hunger with protein and fiber
- Cortisol: Manage stress (causes belly fat storage)
- Thyroid: Ensure adequate iodine and selenium

PLATEAU BREAKING STRATEGIES
----------------------------
- Recalculate calories after every 5kg lost
- Implement diet breaks (1 week at maintenance every 8-12 weeks)
- Change exercise routine
- Check for hidden calories (oils, sauces, snacking)
- Ensure 7-9 hours sleep (sleep deprivation increases hunger hormones)

COMMON MISTAKES
---------------
- Drinking calories
- Not tracking oils and sauces
- Weekend overeating (undoing weekly deficit)
- Eating too little (metabolic adaptation)
- Overestimating calories burned in exercise
- Not eating enough protein (muscle loss)
- Skipping meals leading to binge eating

HEALTHY WEIGHT GAIN & MUSCLE BUILDING NUTRITION GUIDE
======================================================

CALORIE SURPLUS PRINCIPLES
--------------------------
- Safe weight gain: 0.25-0.5 kg (0.5-1 lb) per week
- Required surplus: 300-500 calories above maintenance
- Quality matters: Focus on muscle gain, not just fat
- Protein priority: Essential for muscle synthesis

MACRONUTRIENT RATIOS FOR WEIGHT GAIN
-------------------------------------
- Protein: 25-30% (1.6-2.2g per kg bodyweight for muscle building)
- Carbohydrates: 45-50% (fuel for workouts and glycogen storage)
- Fats: 25-30% (hormone production, calorie density)
- Fiber: 25-30g (don't eliminate, but don't overdo)

CALORIE-DENSE HEALTHY FOODS
---------------------------
Proteins:
- Salmon and fatty fish (high protein + healthy fats)
- Whole eggs (vitamins in yolk, protein in white)
- Ground beef (80/20 or 85/15 for calories)
- Whole chicken (dark meat has more calories)
- Full-fat Greek yogurt
- Cottage cheese (full-fat)
- Protein shakes (whey or mass gainer)
- Nut butters (peanut, almond)
- Steak and red meat
- Tuna in oil

Carbohydrates:
- Oats (with whole milk, honey, nut butter)
- Rice (white or brown, large portions)
- Pasta (whole wheat or regular)
- Potatoes (white and sweet)
- Quinoa
- Buckwheat
- Whole grain bread (thick slices)
- Bagels
- Dates and dried fruits (calorie-dense)
- Bananas
- Granola

Healthy Fats:
- Avocado (1-2 per day)
- Olive oil (add to everything)
- Coconut oil
- Nuts (almonds, walnuts, cashews - large handfuls)
- Nut butters (2-3 tablespoons)
- Seeds (pumpkin, sunflower, sesame)
- Dark chocolate (70%+)
- Cheese (cheddar, mozzarella, cream cheese)
- Full-fat milk

MEAL FREQUENCY FOR WEIGHT GAIN
-------------------------------
- 5-6 meals per day (easier to consume more calories)
- Eat every 2-3 hours
- Never skip breakfast
- Pre-bed snack (casein protein or Greek yogurt)
- Post-workout nutrition critical (protein + carbs within 30 min)

BULKING PHASES
--------------
Clean Bulk:
- 300-500 calorie surplus
- Nutrient-dense whole foods
- Minimal fat gain
- Slower but sustainable
- Better for body composition

Dirty Bulk:
- 1000+ calorie surplus
- Includes processed foods, junk food
- Faster weight gain
- Significant fat gain
- Requires longer cutting phase later

Recommended: Clean bulk for 90% of people

PROTEIN TIMING FOR MUSCLE GROWTH
--------------------------------
- Breakfast: 30-40g protein
- Pre-workout: 20-30g protein + carbs (1-2 hours before)
- Post-workout: 30-40g fast-digesting protein (whey) + carbs
- Every 3-4 hours: 20-40g protein (muscle protein synthesis)
- Before bed: 30-40g slow-digesting (casein or cottage cheese)

SUPERFOODS FOR HARD GAINERS
---------------------------
- Smoothies (easy to consume 800+ calories)
  Example: Oats + banana + peanut butter + protein powder + milk + honey
- Trail mix (nuts + dried fruit + dark chocolate)
- Homemade granola bars
- Hummus (with olive oil) and pita
- Full-fat cheese on everything
- Coconut milk in curries and smoothies
- Tahini (sesame paste)
- Dark meat chicken and turkey
- Whole eggs (not just whites)

HYDRATION AND WEIGHT GAIN
-------------------------
- Don't drink water 30 minutes before meals (saves room for food)
- Add calories to beverages (milk instead of water, smoothies)
- Chocolate milk post-workout (protein + carbs + calories)

TRACKING PROGRESS
-----------------
- Weigh weekly (same day, same time, morning after bathroom)
- Take measurements (chest, arms, waist, thighs)
- Track strength gains in gym
- If not gaining after 2 weeks: add 200 calories
- If gaining too fast (fat): reduce by 100 calories

COMMON MISTAKES
---------------
- Not eating enough (underestimating portions)
- Too much cardio (burning surplus)
- Not training with progressive overload
- Eating junk instead of nutrient-dense foods
- Inconsistent eating (missing meals)
- Giving up too soon (takes months to see results)
- Not sleeping enough (muscle grows during rest)

HEIGHT GROWTH & BONE HEALTH NUTRITION GUIDE
===========================================

IMPORTANT NOTE ON HEIGHT
------------------------
Height is primarily determined by genetics (60-80%) and growth plates.
Nutrition can help maximize genetic potential, especially during:
- Infancy (0-2 years)
- Childhood growth spurts
- Puberty (major growth window)
- Once growth plates close (around 18-25), nutrition maintains posture and bone health

CRITICAL NUTRIENTS FOR BONE GROWTH
----------------------------------
1. CALCIUM (Most Important)
Daily needs by age:
- 4-8 years: 1000 mg
- 9-18 years: 1300 mg (peak bone building years)
- 19-50 years: 1000 mg
- 50+ years: 1200 mg

Best sources:
- Milk (300mg per cup)
- Yogurt (400mg per cup)
- Cheese (200mg per oz)
- Fortified plant milk (300mg per cup)
- Sardines with bones (325mg per 3oz)
- Collard greens (266mg per cup cooked)
- Tofu made with calcium sulfate (253mg per 1/2 cup)
- Almonds (75mg per oz)
- Sesame seeds (280mg per 1/4 cup)

2. VITAMIN D (Calcium absorption)
Daily needs: 600-800 IU (up to 1000-2000 IU if deficient)
Sources:
- Sunlight (10-30 min midday sun)
- Fatty fish (salmon, mackerel)
- Fortified milk
- Egg yolks
- Supplements (D3 preferred)

3. PROTEIN (Bone matrix)
- Essential for bone structure
- Needs: 0.8-1.2g per kg bodyweight
- Sources: Dairy, meat, fish, legumes, soy

4. VITAMIN K2 (Directs calcium to bones)
Sources:
- Natto (fermented soybeans)
- Hard cheeses (gouda, brie, cheddar)
- Egg yolks
- Butter from grass-fed cows
- Sauerkraut

5. MAGNESIUM (Bone density)
Daily needs: 310-420mg
Sources:
- Pumpkin seeds (156mg per oz)
- Spinach (157mg per cup cooked)
- Black beans (120mg per cup)
- Almonds (80mg per oz)
- Dark chocolate (64mg per oz)

6. ZINC (Cell growth and division)
Daily needs: 8-11mg
Sources:
- Oysters (74mg per 3oz)
- Beef (7mg per 3oz)
- Pumpkin seeds (3mg per oz)
- Cashews (2mg per oz)

7. VITAMIN C (Collagen formation for bones)
Sources: Citrus, bell peppers, strawberries, kiwi

8. OMEGA-3 FATTY ACIDS (Reduce inflammation, support growth)
Sources: Fatty fish, walnuts, flaxseeds, chia seeds

FOODS THAT STUNT GROWTH (AVOID)
--------------------------------
- Excessive caffeine (interferes with calcium absorption)
- Alcohol (damages growth plates)
- Smoking/nicotine (reduces growth hormone)
- Excessive sugar (crowds out nutrients)
- Soda/phosphoric acid (leaches calcium from bones)
- Junk food (malnutrition during critical growth)
- Very low calorie diets (growth hormone suppression)

GROWTH HORMONE OPTIMIZATION
----------------------------
Nutrition strategies:
- Eat adequate protein (amino acids stimulate GH)
- Don't eat 2-3 hours before bed (GH releases during sleep)
- Arginine-rich foods (nuts, seeds, chocolate)
- Glutamine-rich foods (beef, chicken, fish, dairy, eggs)
- Sleep 8-10 hours (GH peaks during deep sleep)
- Maintain healthy body fat (not too low)

MEAL PLANNING FOR GROWTH
------------------------
Breakfast (Critical - breaks overnight fast):
- Whole milk or fortified plant milk
- Eggs (protein + vitamin D)
- Whole grain toast with almond butter
- Glass of fortified orange juice (calcium + vitamin C)

Lunch:
- Salmon or sardines (vitamin D + omega-3 + calcium)
- Quinoa or brown rice
- Steamed broccoli or kale (calcium + vitamin K)
- Side of yogurt

Dinner:
- Chicken or beef (protein + zinc)
- Sweet potato (vitamin A for bone growth)
- Sautéed spinach (magnesium + calcium)
- Cheese sauce or sprinkle

Snacks:
- Yogurt with almonds and berries
- Hummus with red pepper strips (vitamin C)
- Trail mix with pumpkin seeds
- Smoothie with milk, banana, peanut butter, spinach

POSTURE AND APPEARANCE
----------------------
Even if bones can't grow longer, you can appear taller:
- Strengthen core muscles (better posture)
- Yoga and stretching (decompress spine)
- Correct vitamin D deficiency (prevents stooping)
- Maintain healthy weight (reduces spinal compression)
- Sleep on firm mattress (spinal alignment)

EXERCISE FOR HEIGHT
-------------------
- Swimming (decompresses spine)
- Hanging exercises
- Yoga (downward dog, cobra pose)
- Jumping exercises (stimulates growth plates during development)
- Resistance training (strengthens supporting muscles)

AGE-SPECIFIC RECOMMENDATIONS
----------------------------
Children (4-12):
- Maximize nutrient density
- No restrictive dieting
- Plenty of dairy or fortified alternatives
- Active play daily

Teenagers (13-19):
- CRITICAL WINDOW - major growth spurt
- Increase calories significantly
- 1300mg calcium daily minimum
- Limit soda and caffeine
- 9-10 hours sleep

Adults (20+):
- Focus on bone density maintenance
- Prevent osteoporosis
- Maintain posture
- Yoga and stretching for spinal health
- Adequate vitamin D and calcium

MEDICAL CONDITIONS & DIETARY GUIDELINES
========================================

DIABETES (TYPE 1 & TYPE 2)
--------------------------
Blood Sugar Management:
- Glycemic Index focus: Choose low-GI foods (GI < 55)
- Carbohydrate counting: Consistent carb intake per meal
- Fiber: 25-35g daily (slows glucose absorption)
- Avoid: Simple sugars, sugary drinks, refined flour

Best Foods:
- Non-starchy vegetables (spinach, broccoli, peppers)
- Whole grains (quinoa, steel-cut oats, brown rice)
- Lean proteins (fish, chicken, tofu)
- Legumes (lentils, chickpeas - high fiber)
- Nuts and seeds (healthy fats, magnesium)
- Berries (low glycemic, high antioxidants)
- Fatty fish (omega-3s reduce inflammation)
- Cinnamon (may improve insulin sensitivity)

Foods to Avoid:
- White bread, white rice, pasta
- Sugary beverages (soda, juice, sweet tea)
- Candy and desserts
- Processed snacks (chips, crackers)
- Fried foods
- Fruit juice (concentrated sugar)
- Honey and syrup (in excess)

Meal Timing:
- Eat at regular times
- Don't skip meals (prevents blood sugar drops)
- 3 main meals + 2 snacks typically

HYPERTENSION (HIGH BLOOD PRESSURE)
----------------------------------
DASH Diet Principles:
- Sodium: < 1500-2300mg daily (ideally < 1500mg)
- Potassium: 3500-5000mg daily (lowers BP)
- Magnesium: 500-1000mg daily
- Calcium: 1000-1200mg daily

Best Foods:
- Leafy greens (spinach, kale - high potassium)
- Beets and beet juice (nitrates lower BP)
- Bananas (potassium)
- Oats (beta-glucan fiber)
- Fatty fish (omega-3s)
- Garlic (allicin lowers BP)
- Berries (flavonoids)
- Pistachios (arginine)
- Pomegranate
- Dark chocolate 70%+ (flavanols)

Foods to Avoid:
- Salt and high-sodium foods
- Processed meats (bacon, deli meat, sausage)
- Canned soups (high sodium)
- Pickled foods
- Fast food
- Frozen dinners
- Cheese (high sodium)
- Alcohol (raises BP)
- Licorice (raises BP)

HERBAL SUPPORT:
- Hibiscus tea (proven to lower BP)
- Hawthorn berry
- Olive leaf extract

HYPOTHYROIDISM (UNDERACTIVE THYROID)
------------------------------------
Nutritional Support:
- Iodine: Essential for thyroid hormone production
  Sources: Seaweed, iodized salt, seafood, dairy, eggs
- Selenium: Helps convert T4 to T3
  Sources: Brazil nuts (1-2 daily), tuna, sardines, turkey
- Zinc: Required for thyroid function
  Sources: Oysters, beef, pumpkin seeds

Foods to AVOID (Interfere with thyroid/absorption):
- Goitrogens (raw): Cruciferous vegetables (cook them)
  Broccoli, cauliflower, cabbage, Brussels sprouts, kale
- Soy products (interfere with medication absorption)
- Gluten (some Hashimoto's patients sensitive)
- Excessive fiber (interferes with medication)
- Coffee (wait 1 hour after taking thyroid medication)

Best Foods:
- Seaweed (nori, wakame, kelp - iodine)
- Brazil nuts (selenium)
- Eggs (iodine + selenium)
- Fish and seafood (iodine + omega-3)
- Dairy (iodine)
- Berries (antioxidants reduce inflammation)

IRON DEFICIENCY ANEMIA
----------------------
Iron Sources:
- Heme iron (best absorbed):
  Beef liver, beef, oysters, chicken, turkey, tuna
- Non-heme iron:
  Spinach, lentils, beans, fortified cereals, quinoa
  Pumpkin seeds, sesame seeds, cashews

Vitamin C (Increases iron absorption):
- Pair iron-rich foods with:
  Citrus fruits, bell peppers, strawberries, tomatoes, broccoli

Foods to Avoid (Block absorption):
- Tea and coffee (tannins) - wait 2 hours after iron-rich meals
- Calcium supplements - take separately
- Whole grains and legumes (phytates) - soak/sprout to reduce

COELIAC DISEASE / GLUTEN SENSITIVITY
------------------------------------
Strictly Avoid:
- Wheat, barley, rye, triticale
- Oats (unless certified gluten-free)
- Hidden sources: Soy sauce, malt vinegar, seitan, some medications

Safe Grains:
- Rice (all types)
- Quinoa
- Buckwheat
- Millet
- Amaranth
- Certified gluten-free oats
- Corn

Nutritional Considerations:
- Risk of deficiencies: Iron, B12, folate, zinc, vitamin D
- Focus on nutrient-dense whole foods
- May need supplementation

GOUT (HIGH URIC ACID)
---------------------
Avoid (High Purine):
- Organ meats (liver, kidney)
- Red meat (beef, lamb, pork)
- Seafood (anchovies, sardines, mussels, scallops, trout, tuna)
- Game meats
- Yeast extracts
- Alcohol (especially beer)
- Sugary drinks (fructose raises uric acid)

Safe to Eat:
- Low-fat dairy (reduces risk)
- Vegetables (even high-purine ones are safe)
- Cherries (reduce gout attacks)
- Coffee (reduces risk)
- Vitamin C (reduces uric acid)
- Water (8-10 glasses daily to flush uric acid)

KIDNEY DISEASE
--------------
Principles (varies by stage):
- Potassium restriction (if levels high)
- Phosphorus restriction
- Sodium restriction (< 2000mg)
- Moderate protein (not high)
- Fluid restriction (in advanced stages)

Foods to Limit:
- Bananas, oranges, tomatoes, potatoes (potassium)
- Dairy (phosphorus)
- Nuts and seeds (phosphorus, potassium)
- Whole grains (phosphorus)
- Processed foods (sodium, phosphorus additives)
- Dark colas (phosphoric acid)

HEART DISEASE / HIGH CHOLESTEROL
--------------------------------
Lower LDL Cholesterol:
- Soluble fiber: Oats, beans, apples, flaxseeds (5-10g daily lowers LDL)
- Plant sterols: Nuts, seeds, legumes, whole grains
- Omega-3 fatty acids: Fatty fish 2x weekly
- Nuts: Almonds, walnuts (1.5 oz daily)
- Olive oil (replaces saturated fat)
- Avocado
- Soy protein (25g daily)

Avoid:
- Trans fats (partially hydrogenated oils)
- Saturated fats (fatty meats, full-fat dairy, butter)
- Processed meats
- Fried foods
- Excessive alcohol

Increase HDL (Good Cholesterol):
- Exercise
- Olive oil
- Fatty fish
- Nuts
- Moderate alcohol (if already drink)
- Quit smoking

ACID REFLUX / GERD
------------------
Avoid:
- Spicy foods
- Citrus fruits and tomatoes
- Chocolate (relaxes LES)
- Mint (relaxes LES)
- Onions and garlic (triggers for some)
- Caffeine
- Alcohol
- Carbonated beverages
- Fried and fatty foods (slow stomach emptying)
- Large meals

Safe Foods:
- Ginger (anti-inflammatory)
- Oatmeal
- Bananas
- Melons
- Green vegetables
- Lean proteins
- Egg whites
- Healthy fats in moderation

Eating Habits:
- Smaller, more frequent meals
- Don't lie down within 3 hours of eating
- Elevate head of bed
- Chew thoroughly

IRRITABLE BOWEL SYNDROME (IBS)
------------------------------
Low FODMAP Diet (temporary elimination):
Avoid:
- Lactose (milk, soft cheese)
- Fructans (wheat, onions, garlic)
- Galactans (beans, lentils)
- Polyols (stone fruits, artificial sweeteners)
- Excess fructose (apples, pears, honey)

Reintroduce gradually to identify triggers.

General Tips:
- Soluble fiber (psyllium, oats) - helps diarrhea and constipation
- Peppermint oil - reduces spasms
- Ginger - reduces nausea
- Probiotics - may help
- Stay hydrated

INFLAMMATORY BOWEL DISEASE (CROHN'S, ULCERATIVE COLITIS)
---------------------------------------------------------
During Flares:
- Low fiber (white rice, white bread, pasta)
- Well-cooked vegetables (no raw)
- Lean proteins
- Avoid nuts, seeds, popcorn
- Avoid dairy if lactose intolerant

Remission:
- Gradually add fiber
- Omega-3 fatty acids (anti-inflammatory)
- Vitamin D supplementation often needed
- Iron if anemic
- Avoid trigger foods (varies by individual)

OSTEOPOROSIS (BONE LOSS)
------------------------
Increase:
- Calcium: 1200mg daily
- Vitamin D: 800-1000 IU daily
- Protein: Essential for bone matrix
- Vitamin K2: Directs calcium to bones
- Magnesium: 320-420mg daily
- Weight-bearing exercise

Avoid:
- Excessive sodium (increases calcium excretion)
- Excessive caffeine (>300mg daily)
- Soda/phosphoric acid
- Alcohol (interferes with calcium absorption)
- Smoking

NON-ALCOHOLIC FATTY LIVER DISEASE (NAFLD)
-----------------------------------------
Mediterranean Diet Best:
- Olive oil as main fat
- Vegetables and fruits
- Whole grains
- Legumes
- Nuts
- Fish and seafood
- Moderate poultry, eggs, dairy
- Limited red meat

Avoid:
- Added sugars (especially fructose)
- Refined carbohydrates
- Saturated fats
- Alcohol (even though NAFLD, alcohol worsens)
- Processed foods

Weight loss (if overweight): Even 5-10% weight loss improves NAFLD
Coffee: 2-3 cups daily may be protective

POLYCYSTIC OVARY SYNDROME (PCOS)
--------------------------------
Insulin Resistance Management:
- Low glycemic index carbohydrates
- High fiber
- Adequate protein
- Anti-inflammatory foods

Best Foods:
- Cinnamon (improves insulin sensitivity)
- Spearmint tea (reduces androgens)
- Turmeric (anti-inflammatory)
- Omega-3 fatty acids
- Berries (low sugar, high antioxidants)
- Leafy greens
- Legumes

Avoid:
- Sugar and refined carbs
- Dairy (may worsen hormonal symptoms for some)
- Processed foods
- Trans fats

PREGNANCY NUTRITION
-------------------
Key Nutrients:
- Folate: 600 mcg (prevent neural tube defects)
  Sources: Leafy greens, fortified grains, beans, prenatal vitamin
- Iron: 27mg (increased blood volume)
  Sources: Red meat, spinach, beans + vitamin C
- Calcium: 1000mg (baby's bone development)
- DHA: 200-300mg (brain development)
  Sources: Fatty fish, algae supplements
- Iodine: 220mcg (thyroid/brain development)
- Choline: 450mg (brain development)
  Sources: Eggs (especially yolks), meat, fish
- Vitamin D: 600 IU

Foods to Avoid:
- Alcohol
- High-mercury fish (shark, swordfish, king mackerel, tilefish)
- Raw/undercooked meat, eggs, fish (risk of infection)
- Unpasteurized dairy
- Deli meats (unless heated - listeria risk)
- Excess caffeine (<200mg daily)

LACTATION NUTRITION
-------------------
Additional Calories: 330-400 calories daily
Fluid: 3 liters daily (water, milk, juice)
Continue prenatal vitamin

Key Nutrients:
- DHA: Continues for baby's brain via breast milk
- Iodine: 290mcg
- Choline: 550mg
- Vitamin B12: If vegetarian/vegan, supplement
- Calcium: 1000mg

Foods That May Affect Baby:
- Caffeine (may make baby fussy)
- Alcohol (pump and dump or wait 2 hours per drink)
- Some foods may cause gas (varies by baby)

GALLBLADDER DISEASE / GALLSTONES
--------------------------------
Avoid:
- High-fat foods (trigger pain)
- Fried foods
- Fatty meats
- Full-fat dairy
- Cream sauces
- Butter and oils in excess
- Eggs (for some people)

Safe Foods:
- Lean proteins (fish, chicken breast, turkey)
- Low-fat dairy
- Vegetables and fruits
- Whole grains
- Small, frequent meals
- Gradual weight loss (rapid loss increases stone risk)

PANCREATITIS
------------
Acute: Hospitalization, NPO (nothing by mouth) initially
Chronic: Very low-fat diet (<30g fat daily)
- Lean proteins
- Complex carbs
- Cooked vegetables
- No alcohol
- No smoking
- Pancreatic enzyme supplements may be needed

SUPERFOODS & SUPPLEMENTS REFERENCE GUIDE
========================================

TOP 50 SUPERFOODS BY CATEGORY
-----------------------------

LEAFY GREENS (Nutrient Density Kings):
1. Kale - Vitamin K, A, C, calcium, antioxidants
2. Spinach - Iron, folate, magnesium, lutein
3. Swiss Chard - Vitamin K, A, C, magnesium
4. Collard Greens - Calcium, vitamin K, folate
5. Arugula - Nitrates (blood flow), vitamin K
6. Watercress - Vitamin C, K, calcium
7. Bok Choy - Vitamin C, A, K, selenium
8. Beet Greens - Iron, potassium, magnesium
9. Mustard Greens - Vitamin K, A, folate
10. Microgreens - Concentrated nutrients, up to 40x more than mature

BERRIES (Antioxidant Powerhouses):
11. Blueberries - Anthocyanins, brain health, 6,000 ORAC score
12. Acai Berries - Highest antioxidant of any fruit
13. Goji Berries - Vitamin C, A, iron, zeaxanthin
14. Cranberries - UTI prevention, proanthocyanidins
15. Raspberries - Ellagic acid (anti-cancer), fiber
16. Blackberries - Vitamin C, K, manganese
17. Strawberries - Vitamin C, folate, manganese
18. Tart Cherries - Melatonin (sleep), anti-inflammatory
19. Maqui Berries - Anthocyanins, blood sugar support
20. Elderberries - Immune support, antiviral

NUTS & SEEDS (Healthy Fats & Protein):
21. Walnuts - Omega-3 ALA, polyphenols
22. Almonds - Vitamin E, magnesium, protein
23. Brazil Nuts - Selenium (1 nut = daily needs)
24. Chia Seeds - Omega-3, fiber (10g per oz), calcium
25. Flaxseeds - Lignans (phytoestrogens), omega-3
26. Hemp Seeds - Complete protein, omega-3, GLA
27. Pumpkin Seeds - Magnesium, zinc, tryptophan
28. Pistachios - Potassium, vitamin B6, lutein
29. Macadamia Nuts - Monounsaturated fats, palmitoleic acid
30. Sesame Seeds - Calcium, lignans, zinc

FATTY FISH (Omega-3 Rich):
31. Salmon - EPA/DHA omega-3, astaxanthin, vitamin D
32. Sardines - Calcium (bones), B12, selenium
33. Mackerel - Vitamin D, B12, omega-3
34. Herring - Omega-3, B12, vitamin D
35. Anchovies - Calcium, iron, omega-3
36. Rainbow Trout - Omega-3, B vitamins, potassium

FERMENTED FOODS (Gut Health):
37. Kefir - Probiotics (12 strains), calcium, protein
38. Kimchi - Probiotics, vitamin C, K, capsaicin
39. Sauerkraut - Probiotics, fiber, vitamin C, K
40. Kombucha - Probiotics, antioxidants
41. Natto - Vitamin K2 (highest source), nattokinase
42. Greek Yogurt - Probiotics, protein (twice regular yogurt)
43. Miso - Probiotics, B vitamins, antioxidants

OTHER SUPERFOODS:
44. Turmeric/Golden Paste - Curcumin (anti-inflammatory)
45. Ginger - Gingerol, digestive aid, anti-nausea
46. Garlic - Allicin, immune support, cardiovascular
47. Green Tea/Matcha - EGCG, L-theanine, catechins
48. Cacao/Dark Chocolate 85%+ - Flavanols, magnesium, iron
49. Avocado - Monounsaturated fats, potassium (more than banana), fiber
50. Sweet Potato - Beta-carotene (vitamin A), fiber, potassium

SUPPLEMENTS REFERENCE
---------------------

VITAMINS:

Vitamin D3 (Cholecalciferol):
- Dosage: 1000-4000 IU daily (check blood levels)
- Best with: Vitamin K2 (directs calcium to bones)
- Food sources: Sunlight, fatty fish, egg yolks
- Deficiency symptoms: Fatigue, bone pain, depression, frequent illness

Vitamin B12 (Methylcobalamin):
- Dosage: 500-1000 mcg daily or 2500 mcg weekly
- Essential for: Vegans/vegetarians, elderly, PPI users
- Food sources: Meat, fish, eggs, dairy, nutritional yeast
- Deficiency: Fatigue, numbness, memory issues, anemia

Vitamin K2 (MK-7):
- Dosage: 100-200 mcg daily
- Works with: Vitamin D3
- Sources: Natto, aged cheese, egg yolks
- Benefits: Bone health, arterial health

MINERALS:

Magnesium (Glycinate or Citrate):
- Dosage: 200-400 mg daily
- Best taken: Evening (promotes sleep)
- Types: Glycinate (gentle), Citrate (laxative), Oxide (poor absorption)
- Deficiency: Cramps, anxiety, insomnia, headaches

Zinc (Picolinate or Orotate):
- Dosage: 15-30 mg daily
- Take with: Food (can cause nausea on empty stomach)
- Don't combine with: Calcium or iron (competes for absorption)
- Sources: Oysters, beef, pumpkin seeds

Iron (Bisglycinate):
- Dosage: 18-25 mg (women), 8 mg (men) - only if deficient
- Take with: Vitamin C
- Avoid: Coffee/tea within 2 hours
- Best form: Bisglycinate (gentle, no constipation)

Selenium:
- Dosage: 200 mcg daily or 1 Brazil nut
- Caution: Toxic at high doses (>400 mcg)
- Sources: Brazil nuts, seafood, organ meats

Iodine:
- Dosage: 150 mcg daily (higher if deficiency)
- Sources: Seaweed, iodized salt, seafood
- Caution: Can worsen thyroid conditions if autoimmune

OMEGA FATTY ACIDS:

Fish Oil (EPA/DHA):
- Dosage: 1-3g combined EPA/DHA daily
- Look for: Molecularly distilled, third-party tested
- Ratio: EPA for inflammation, DHA for brain
- Vegan alternative: Algae oil

Flaxseed Oil (ALA):
- Dosage: 1-2 tablespoons daily
- Note: ALA converts poorly to EPA/DHA (5-10%)
- Better to get EPA/DHA directly

SPECIALTY SUPPLEMENTS:

Probiotics:
- Look for: 10-50 billion CFU, multiple strains, enteric coated
- Strains: Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium species
- Storage: Refrigerated preferred
- Take: On empty stomach

Digestive Enzymes:
- Helpful for: Bloating, gas, digestion issues
- Look for: Amylase, protease, lipase, lactase
- Take: With meals

Collagen Peptides:
- Types: Type I & III (skin, hair, nails), Type II (joints)
- Dosage: 10-15g daily
- Source: Grass-fed bovine or wild-caught marine
- Vitamin C needed for synthesis

Creatine Monohydrate:
- Dosage: 3-5g daily
- Benefits: Muscle strength, brain health (vegetarians especially)
- Loading: Optional 20g/day for 5 days, then 3-5g
- Safe, well-researched

Ashwagandha:
- Dosage: 300-600mg KSM-66 or Sensoril extract
- Benefits: Stress reduction, cortisol lowering, sleep
- Best: Standardized to withanolides (5%+)

Rhodiola Rosea:
- Dosage: 200-400mg daily
- Benefits: Energy, fatigue reduction, adaptogen
- Take: Morning (can interfere with sleep)

Curcumin (Turmeric Extract):
- Dosage: 500-1000mg with piperine (BioPerine) or liposomal
- Benefits: Anti-inflammatory, joint health, brain
- Absorption: Poor without piperine or fat

CoQ10 (Ubiquinol):
- Dosage: 100-200mg daily
- Best form: Ubiquinol (active form, better absorption)
- Benefits: Heart health, energy, antioxidant
- Especially: Statin drug users (depletes CoQ10)

Berberine:
- Dosage: 500mg 2-3x daily with meals
- Benefits: Blood sugar control (comparable to metformin), cholesterol
- Caution: Interacts with many medications, don't use long-term without doctor

NAC (N-Acetyl Cysteine):
- Dosage: 600-1200mg daily
- Benefits: Glutathione precursor, lung health, liver support
- Caution: Not for pregnancy

Alpha Lipoic Acid:
- Dosage: 300-600mg daily
- Benefits: Antioxidant, blood sugar, nerve health

Resveratrol:
- Dosage: 150-500mg daily
- Benefits: Antioxidant, cardiovascular, longevity
- Source: Red wine (minimal in supplement form), Japanese knotweed

Astaxanthin:
- Dosage: 4-12mg daily
- Benefits: Powerful antioxidant (6000x vitamin C), eye health, skin
- Source: Algae, salmon (wild has more than farmed)

Greens Powder:
- Look for: Spirulina, chlorella, wheatgrass, barley grass
- Benefits: Micronutrients, alkalizing, energy
- Note: Good insurance, but not replacement for vegetables

Prebiotic Fiber:
- Types: Inulin, FOS, GOS, resistant starch
- Dosage: 5-10g daily (start low, increase gradually)
- Benefits: Feed good gut bacteria
- Sources: Chicory root, Jerusalem artichoke, green bananas, cooked and cooled potatoes

Adaptogen Blends:
- Ashwagandha, Rhodiola, Holy Basil, Eleuthero, Schisandra
- Benefits: Stress adaptation, energy balance
- Best: Rotate different adaptogens

PROTEIN SUPPLEMENTS:

Whey Protein:
- Types: Concentrate (cheaper, more lactose), Isolate (purer, faster), Hydrolysate (pre-digested, fastest)
- Best for: Post-workout, muscle building
- Complete amino acid profile, high leucine

Casein Protein:
- Slow-digesting (sustained release)
- Best for: Before bed, meal replacement
- Satiating

Plant Proteins:
- Pea protein (most complete, high BCAA)
- Rice protein (combine with pea for complete profile)
- Hemp protein (omega-3s, fiber)
- Soy protein (complete, but some concerns about hormones)

Collagen Protein:
- NOT a complete protein (missing tryptophan)
- Benefits: Skin, hair, nails, joints (not muscle building)

WHEN TO TAKE SUPPLEMENTS
------------------------
Morning (with breakfast):
- Multivitamin
- B-complex (energy)
- Vitamin D (fat-soluble, take with food)
- CoQ10
- Probiotics (some prefer empty stomach)

With Meals:
- Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K)
- Omega-3 fish oil
- Digestive enzymes
- Calcium (split doses)
- Iron (with vitamin C)

Evening/Dinner:
- Magnesium (relaxation, sleep)
- Calcium
- Casein protein

Before Bed:
- Melatonin (0.5-3mg)
- Magnesium glycinate
- Glycine (3g)
- L-theanine

Pre-Workout:
- Caffeine (100-200mg)
- Creatine (any time, but convenient)
- Citrulline malate (8g)
- Beta-alanine (3-5g)

Post-Workout:
- Whey protein (20-40g)
- Creatine (with protein/carbs)
- Fast-digesting carbs (if training again soon)

RED FLAGS - WHEN TO AVOID SUPPLEMENTS
--------------------------------------
- Pregnancy/breastfeeding (unless approved by doctor)
- Before surgery (some affect bleeding)
- Kidney disease (protein, potassium, others)
- Liver disease (many herbs)
- Autoimmune conditions (immune-stimulating supplements)
- Taking blood thinners (vitamin K, high dose fish oil, garlic, ginkgo)
- Before medical tests (biotin interferes with thyroid tests)

THIRD-PARTY TESTING CERTIFICATIONS
-----------------------------------
Look for these on supplements:
- NSF Certified for Sport
- USP Verified
- ConsumerLab Approved
- Informed Sport
- GMP (Good Manufacturing Practices)

These ensure:
- Product contains what label claims
- No harmful levels of contaminants
- No banned substances
- Manufacturing quality standards

EGGS & DAIRY PROTEINS (per 100g)
---------------------------------

Egg, whole, raw
- Calories: 143 kcal

- Folate: 47mcg
- Selenium: 30mcg
- Choline: 294mg (66% DV) - highest source, critical for brain health
- Lutein + Zeaxanthin: 250mcg (eye health)
- One large egg = 50g (divide values by 2 for per egg)

COMPLETE VEGETARIAN DIET GUIDE
===============================

DEFINITION & PHILOSOPHY
-----------------------
Vegetarianism excludes meat, poultry, and seafood. Variations exist based on egg and dairy inclusion:
- Lacto-vegetarian: Dairy allowed, no eggs
- Ovo-vegetarian: Eggs allowed, no dairy  
- Lacto-ovo-vegetarian: Both dairy and eggs (most common)
- Eggetarian: Eggs only, no dairy (common in India)

WHAT TO EAT - COMPREHENSIVE LIST
--------------------------------

PROTEIN SOURCES:
- Eggs (all types - boiled, scrambled, omelets, quiches)
- Dairy: Milk, cheese (all varieties), yogurt, cottage cheese, kefir, buttermilk
- Legumes: Lentils (red, green, black, yellow), chickpeas, black beans, kidney beans, pinto beans, navy beans, soybeans, edamame, mung beans
- Soy products: Tofu (silken, firm, extra-firm, smoked), tempeh, soy milk, edamame
- Nuts: Almonds, walnuts, cashews, pistachios, peanuts, pine nuts, Brazil nuts, macadamia, hazelnuts
- Seeds: Pumpkin seeds, sunflower seeds, chia seeds, flaxseeds, hemp seeds, sesame seeds, poppy seeds
- Whole grains: Quinoa (complete protein), amaranth, buckwheat, oats, wheat, rice, barley
- Seitan (wheat gluten): Mock meats, wheat protein
- Protein powders: Whey, casein, egg white, soy, pea, rice, hemp protein

DAIRY PRODUCTS (Lacto variations):
- Milk: Cow, buffalo, goat, sheep (whole, skim, 2%, 1%, raw, pasteurized)
- Cheese: Cheddar, mozzarella, parmesan, feta, cottage cheese, cream cheese, ricotta, Swiss, brie, camembert, gouda, provolone, halloumi, paneer (Indian cottage cheese)
- Yogurt: Greek, regular, flavored, plain, kefir, lassi (Indian yogurt drink)
- Cream: Heavy cream, light cream, half-and-half, sour cream, crème fraîche
- Butter and ghee (clarified butter)
- Ice cream, frozen yogurt
- Buttermilk, whey

EGGS (Ovo variations):
- Chicken eggs (white, brown, free-range, organic, omega-3 enriched)
- Quail eggs (delicacy, nutrient-dense)
- Duck eggs (richer, larger)
- Goose eggs (very large, seasonal)
- Egg preparations: Boiled (soft, medium, hard), fried, scrambled, poached, omelets, frittatas, quiches, casseroles, meringues, custards

GRAINS & STARCHES:
- Rice: White, brown, basmati, jasmine, wild rice, arborio, sushi rice, red rice, black rice
- Wheat: Bread, pasta, couscous, bulgur, semolina, farro, spelt, kamut, freekeh
- Ancient grains: Quinoa, amaranth, teff, millet, sorghum, fonio
- Corn: Cornmeal, polenta, popcorn, corn tortillas, hominy
- Oats: Rolled, steel-cut, instant, oat flour
- Barley, rye, triticale
- Starches: Potatoes, sweet potatoes, yams, cassava, taro, plantains

VEGETABLES (All unlimited):
- Leafy greens: Spinach, kale, Swiss chard, collard greens, mustard greens, turnip greens, beet greens, bok choy, arugula, lettuce (all varieties), watercress, mizuna, sorrel
- Cruciferous: Broccoli, cauliflower, Brussels sprouts, cabbage (green, red, Napa, Savoy), kohlrabi, kale, bok choy, turnips, rutabaga, wasabi, horseradish
- Root vegetables: Carrots, beets, radishes (all types), turnips, parsnips, rutabaga, celery root, sunchokes/Jerusalem artichokes
- Alliums: Onions (red, yellow, white, sweet), garlic, shallots, leeks, scallions, chives
- Nightshades: Tomatoes, bell peppers (all colors), hot peppers (chili, jalapeño, habanero, etc.), eggplant, potatoes
- Squashes: Zucchini, yellow squash, pattypan, acorn squash, butternut squash, pumpkin, spaghetti squash, delicata, kabocha
- Beans/Legumes as vegetables: Green beans, snow peas, snap peas, winged beans, yardlong beans
- Other: Cucumbers, celery, asparagus, artichokes, fennel, okra, corn, bamboo shoots, water chestnuts, hearts of palm
- Mushrooms: Button, portobello, shiitake, oyster, cremini, enoki, maitake, chanterelle, morel, porcini, trumpet, beech

FRUITS (All varieties):
- Citrus: Oranges, lemons, limes, grapefruits, tangerines, clementines, pomelos, kumquats, yuzu
- Berries: Strawberries, blueberries, raspberries, blackberries, cranberries, goji berries, acai, mulberries, elderberries, currants, gooseberries
- Tropical: Bananas, mangoes, pineapple, papaya, coconut, guava, passion fruit, lychee, rambutan, durian, jackfruit, dragon fruit, star fruit, kiwi
- Stone fruits: Peaches, plums, nectarines, apricots, cherries, mangoes, dates
- Melons: Watermelon, cantaloupe, honeydew, galia, casaba, Persian melon
- Apples, pears, quince
- Grapes (all colors and varieties)
- Pomegranates, figs, persimmons
- Exotic: Longan, mangosteen, sapodilla, custard apple, breadfruit, feijoa, loquat, jujube

FATS & OILS:
- Oils: Olive oil, coconut oil, avocado oil, sesame oil, peanut oil, sunflower oil, safflower oil, canola oil, grapeseed oil, walnut oil, flaxseed oil
- Butters: Dairy butter, ghee, coconut butter, cocoa butter, nut butters (almond, peanut, cashew, sunflower seed butter, tahini)
- Avocados
- Olives
- Full-fat dairy

NUTRITIONAL SUPPLEMENTS (Recommended):
- Vitamin B12 (CRITICAL - not available in plant foods)
- Iron (if ferritin low, especially for menstruating women)
- Omega-3 (EPA/DHA from algae or flax/chia/walnuts for ALA)
- Vitamin D (if limited sun exposure)
- Zinc (plant sources less bioavailable)
- Calcium (if dairy intake low)

WHAT TO AVOID - STRICT PROHIBITIONS
------------------------------------

MEAT & POULTRY (All types):
- Red meat: Beef, pork, lamb, mutton, veal, venison, bison, buffalo, goat, rabbit, horse, kangaroo
- Organ meats: Liver, kidney, heart, tripe, sweetbreads, brain, tongue, blood
- Poultry: Chicken, turkey, duck, goose, quail, pheasant, guinea fowl, pigeon, ostrich, emu
- Processed meats: Sausages, bacon, ham, salami, pepperoni, prosciutto, bologna, hot dogs, deli meats, pâté, terrines
- Game meats: Wild boar, venison, elk, moose, bear, wild birds

SEAFOOD (All aquatic animals):
- Fish: Salmon, tuna, cod, halibut, trout, bass, mackerel, sardines, anchovies, herring, tilapia, catfish, snapper, grouper, swordfish, mahi-mahi, perch, pike, carp
- Shellfish: Shrimp, prawns, crab, lobster, crawfish/crayfish, langoustines
- Mollusks: Clams, oysters, mussels, scallops, squid, octopus, cuttlefish, snails/escargot, abalone, conch
- Roe/Caviar: Fish eggs from all species
- Sea vegetables are ALLOWED (seaweed, nori, kelp, dulse, wakame)

ANIMAL FATS & BYPRODUCTS:
- Lard (pork fat)
- Tallow (beef fat)
- Schmaltz (chicken fat)
- Suet
- Bone broth, meat stock
- Gelatin (from collagen - pigs/cows) - found in marshmallows, gummy candies, some yogurts, desserts, medications
- Animal rennet in cheese (some vegetarians avoid, others accept vegetarian rennet)
- Carmine/cochineal (red food coloring from insects)
- Omega-3 fish oil supplements

HIDDEN ANIMAL INGREDIENTS TO WATCH:
- Worcestershire sauce (contains anchovies)
- Caesar dressing (anchovies)
- Curry paste (shrimp paste in some brands)
- Kimchi (fish sauce or shrimp paste often used)
- Pesto (Parmesan cheese often contains animal rennet)
- Some beers and wines (isinglass from fish bladder, gelatin, egg whites used in fining)
- Marshmallows, gummy bears (gelatin)
- Refried beans (lard in traditional preparation)
- Soups and broths (chicken/beef stock base)
- Miso soup (dashi from bonito fish flakes)
- Caesar salad (anchovies in dressing)
- Parmigiano-Reggiano (by law uses animal rennet)
- Some vitamin D3 (from lanolin/sheep's wool)
- Shellac (from insects) on candy and fruit
- Natural flavorings (can be meat-derived)

HEALTH BENEFITS - SCIENTIFICALLY PROVEN
----------------------------------------

CARDIOVASCULAR HEALTH:
- 24% lower risk of heart disease (compared to meat-eaters)
- Lower LDL ("bad") cholesterol levels
- Lower blood pressure
- Reduced risk of stroke
- Better arterial function

CANCER PREVENTION:
- 18% lower overall cancer risk
- Significantly lower risk of: Colorectal cancer, stomach cancer, pancreatic cancer, lymphatic cancers
- Higher intake of cancer-protective phytochemicals

TYPE 2 DIABETES:
- 50% lower risk of developing diabetes
- Better insulin sensitivity
- Lower fasting blood glucose
- Better HbA1c control

WEIGHT MANAGEMENT:
- Lower average BMI
- Higher fiber intake promotes satiety
- Lower calorie density of typical meals
- Easier to maintain healthy weight

LONGEVITY:
- Lower all-cause mortality
- Reduced risk of chronic diseases
- Longer telomeres (marker of cellular aging)

GUT HEALTH:
- Higher fiber promotes diverse microbiome
- More short-chain fatty acid production
- Lower risk of diverticular disease
- Better bowel regularity

OTHER BENEFITS:
- Lower risk of kidney stones
- Lower risk of gallstones
- Reduced risk of cataracts
- Better bone density (if calcium intake adequate)
- Lower environmental footprint
- Reduced antibiotic resistance exposure
- Cost savings (plant proteins generally cheaper)

POTENTIAL NUTRITIONAL RISKS & SOLUTIONS
-----------------------------------------

VITAMIN B12 DEFICIENCY (CRITICAL):
Risk: Anemia, neurological damage, irreversible nerve damage, cognitive decline, elevated homocysteine (heart disease risk)
Solution: 
- Supplement with 250-500mcg daily or 2500mcg weekly (cyanocobalamin or methylcobalamin)
- Eat B12-fortified foods: nutritional yeast, plant milks, breakfast cereals, meat substitutes
- Get blood levels checked annually (serum B12 and methylmalonic acid)
- Note: Tempeh, nori, spirulina contain B12 analogs that are NOT bioavailable

IRON DEFICIENCY (Common in women):
Risk: Anemia, fatigue, weakness, pale skin, cold hands/feet, brittle nails
Solution:
- Consume vitamin C with iron-rich meals (citrus, tomatoes, bell peppers with beans/spinach)
- Cook in cast iron pans
- Soak/sprout/ferment grains and legumes to reduce phytates
- Avoid coffee/tea with meals (tannins inhibit absorption)
- Consider supplement if ferritin <30 ng/mL
- Iron-rich foods: Lentils, spinach, tofu, quinoa, fortified cereals, pumpkin seeds, blackstrap molasses

ZINC DEFICIENCY:
Risk: Weakened immunity, hair loss, poor wound healing, loss of taste/smell
Solution:
- Eat zinc-rich foods: Pumpkin seeds, cashews, chickpeas, lentils, quinoa, hemp seeds
- Soak/sprout legumes and grains
- Supplement with 8-11mg if needed (zinc picolinate or orotate forms best)

OMEGA-3 FATTY ACIDS (EPA/DHA):
Risk: Poor brain function, inflammation, cardiovascular issues
Solution:
- Consume ALA sources: Flaxseeds (ground), chia seeds, walnuts, hemp seeds
- Take algae-based EPA/DHA supplement (250-500mg combined daily)
- Note: Conversion of ALA to EPA/DHA is only 5-10% efficient

VITAMIN D:
Risk: Bone loss, weakened immunity, depression
Solution:
- Sun exposure (10-30 minutes midday)
- Fortified plant milks and cereals
- Supplement with D3 (lichen-based for strict vegetarians) or D2 (ergocalciferol) - 1000-4000 IU daily

CALCIUM (If dairy-free):
Risk: Osteoporosis, bone fractures
Solution:
- Fortified plant milks (calcium-set tofu, almond/soy milk with added calcium)
- Leafy greens: Collards, kale, bok choy, mustard greens (spinach and Swiss chard have oxalates that block calcium absorption)
- Calcium-set tofu
- Tahini, almonds, figs
- Supplement with 500mg calcium citrate if intake low

PROTEIN QUALITY:
Concern: Plant proteins may be lower in certain essential amino acids (lysine, methionine)
Solution:
- Protein combining (not necessary at every meal, but over the day):
  - Grains + Legumes (rice + beans, hummus + pita, lentils + bread)
  - Nuts/seeds + Legumes
- Eat varied protein sources throughout day
- Soy and quinoa are complete proteins (contain all 9 essential amino acids)

IODINE:
Risk: Thyroid dysfunction
Solution:
- Iodized salt (1/4 tsp = 150mcg, daily requirement)
- Seaweed (nori, wakame, dulse - but avoid excessive kelp which has too much)
- Dairy and eggs contain iodine
- Supplement with 150mcg if avoiding salt/dairy

CREATINE (For athletes):
- Plant foods contain no creatine
- Supplement with 3-5g daily for improved strength and cognitive function (especially important for vegetarians)

COMPLETE PROTEIN COMBINATIONS
-----------------------------
Traditional combinations that provide all essential amino acids:

- Rice + Beans (Latin America)
- Lentils + Rice (India/Middle East)
- Hummus + Pita (Middle East)
- Peanut Butter + Whole Grain Bread (America)
- Beans + Corn (Native American)
- Tofu + Rice (East Asia)
- Chickpeas + Tahini (Middle East)
- Lentil soup + Crusty bread (Europe)
- Black beans + Quinoa (South America)
- Ezekiel bread (sprouted grains + legumes combined in bread)

Note: Modern nutrition science shows you don't need to combine proteins at every meal - just eat varied sources throughout the day.

SAMPLE 7-DAY MEAL PLAN (Lacto-Ovo Vegetarian)
---------------------------------------------

DAY 1:
Breakfast: Greek yogurt parfait with berries, granola, chia seeds
Lunch: Caprese sandwich (mozzarella, tomato, basil, pesto) on ciabatta + side salad
Snack: Apple with almond butter
Dinner: Vegetable lasagna with ricotta, mozzarella, spinach + garlic bread
Dessert: Dark chocolate

DAY 2:
Breakfast: Scrambled eggs with spinach and feta + whole grain toast
Lunch: Quinoa bowl with roasted vegetables, chickpeas, tahini dressing
Snack: Hummus with carrot sticks and bell peppers
Dinner: Palak paneer (Indian spinach and cheese curry) with basmati rice and naan
Dessert: Fresh mango

DAY 3:
Breakfast: Overnight oats with milk, banana, walnuts, cinnamon
Lunch: Grilled cheese sandwich with tomato soup
Snack: Cottage cheese with pineapple
Dinner: Eggplant parmesan with side of spaghetti marinara
Dessert: Greek yogurt with honey

DAY 4:
Breakfast: Vegetable omelet (peppers, onions, mushrooms, cheese) + toast
Lunch: Buddha bowl: Brown rice, black beans, avocado, corn, salsa, sour cream
Snack: Trail mix (nuts, seeds, dried fruit)
Dinner: Thai green curry with tofu, vegetables, coconut milk + jasmine rice
Dessert: Baked apple with cinnamon

DAY 5:
Breakfast: Smoothie (milk, banana, peanut butter, oats, protein powder)
Lunch: Mediterranean platter: Hummus, falafel, tzatziki, pita, olives, feta
Snack: Hard-boiled eggs with salt and pepper
Dinner: Mushroom risotto with parmesan + side salad
Dessert: Dark chocolate covered almonds

DAY 6:
Breakfast: Pancakes with maple syrup, berries, and Greek yogurt
Lunch: Caprese salad (tomato, mozzarella, basil, balsamic) with crusty bread
Snack: Cheese and whole grain crackers
Dinner: Vegetarian chili with kidney beans, corn, peppers + cornbread
Dessert: Fresh figs with honey and goat cheese

DAY 7:
Breakfast: Shakshuka (eggs poached in tomato sauce) with crusty bread
Lunch: Sushi rolls (avocado, cucumber, egg, pickled vegetables) with miso soup
Snack: Edamame with sea salt
Dinner: Stuffed bell peppers with rice, black beans, corn, cheese + guacamole
Dessert: Berries with whipped cream

TIPS FOR SUCCESS
----------------
1. Meal prep: Cook grains and legumes in bulk on weekends
2. Protein at every meal: Include eggs, dairy, legumes, or soy at each meal
3. B12 supplementation: Non-negotiable - set a daily reminder
4. Iron + Vitamin C: Pair spinach with lemon juice, lentils with tomatoes
5. Explore cuisines: Indian, Mediterranean, Mexican, Thai have excellent vegetarian options
6. Read labels: Watch for hidden gelatin, rennet, meat broths
7. Dining out: Indian, Italian, Mexican, Middle Eastern restaurants very vegetarian-friendly
8. Protein powder: Convenient for smoothies and baking
9. Nutritional yeast: Adds cheesy flavor and B-vitamins to dishes
10. Stay informed: Get annual blood work (B12, iron, vitamin D)

TRANSITIONING TO VEGETARIAN
----------------------------
Week 1-2: Eliminate red meat, replace with legumes and eggs
Week 3-4: Eliminate poultry, add more dairy and soy
Week 5-6: Eliminate fish/seafood (if not pescatarian)
Week 7+: Fully vegetarian, optimize nutrition

Or go "flexitarian" first: Meatless Mondays, then gradually increase meat-free days.

37. VITAMIN & MINERAL REFERENCE TABLE

Essential vitamins and minerals with their functions, sources, and recommended daily intake for adults.


VITAMINS

Vitamin	Function	Top Food Sources	RDA (Adult Male)	RDA (Adult Female)	Deficiency Symptoms
Vitamin A (Retinol)	Vision, immune function, skin health	Liver, sweet potato, carrots, spinach, kale	900 mcg RAE	700 mcg RAE	Night blindness, dry skin, infections
Vitamin B1 (Thiamine)	Energy metabolism, nerve function	Pork, whole grains, legumes, nuts	1.2 mg	1.1 mg	Beriberi, fatigue, nerve damage
Vitamin B2 (Riboflavin)	Energy metabolism, cell function	Dairy, eggs, lean meats, green vegetables	1.3 mg	1.1 mg	Cracked lips, sore throat, skin rash
Vitamin B3 (Niacin)	Energy metabolism, DNA repair	Chicken, tuna, mushrooms, peanuts	16 mg	14 mg	Pellagra (dermatitis, diarrhea, dementia)
Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid)	Coenzyme A synthesis, energy metabolism	Chicken, beef, potatoes, oats, tomatoes	5 mg	5 mg	Rare; fatigue, numbness
Vitamin B6 (Pyridoxine)	Amino acid metabolism, neurotransmitter synthesis	Chickpeas, salmon, potatoes, bananas	1.3-1.7 mg	1.3-1.5 mg	Anemia, dermatitis, confusion
Vitamin B7 (Biotin)	Fat/carb metabolism, gene regulation	Eggs, almonds, sweet potatoes, onions	30 mcg	30 mcg	Hair loss, brittle nails, rash
Vitamin B9 (Folate)	DNA synthesis, cell division, pregnancy health	Lentils, spinach, asparagus, fortified grains	400 mcg DFE	400 mcg DFE (600 mcg if pregnant)	Megaloblastic anemia, birth defects
Vitamin B12 (Cobalamin)	Nerve function, red blood cells, DNA	Meat, fish, dairy, eggs, fortified foods	2.4 mcg	2.4 mcg	Pernicious anemia, nerve damage
Vitamin C (Ascorbic Acid)	Antioxidant, collagen synthesis, immune function	Bell peppers, citrus, strawberries, broccoli	90 mg	75 mg	Scurvy, slow wound healing
Vitamin D (Cholecalciferol)	Calcium absorption, bone health, immune function	Sunlight, fatty fish, fortified milk, eggs	600 IU (15 mcg)	600 IU (15 mcg)	Rickets, osteoporosis, weak immunity
Vitamin E (Tocopherol)	Antioxidant, immune function, skin health	Sunflower seeds, almonds, spinach, avocado	15 mg	15 mg	Nerve/muscle damage, vision problems
Vitamin K (Phylloquinone/Menaquinone)	Blood clotting, bone metabolism	Kale, spinach, broccoli, Brussels sprouts	120 mcg	90 mcg	Excessive bleeding, easy bruising

MINERALS

Mineral	Function	Top Food Sources	RDA (Adult Male)	RDA (Adult Female)	Deficiency Symptoms
Calcium	Bone/teeth health, muscle contraction, nerve signaling	Dairy, fortified plant milks, leafy greens, sardines	1000 mg	1000-1200 mg	Osteoporosis, muscle cramps
Iron	Oxygen transport, energy metabolism	Red meat, lentils, spinach, fortified cereles	8 mg	18 mg (8 mg if post-menopausal)	Anemia, fatigue, weakness
Magnesium	Muscle/nerve function, blood sugar control, bone health	Spinach, almonds, black beans, dark chocolate	400-420 mg	310-320 mg	Muscle cramps, fatigue, arrhythmia
Phosphorus	Bone/teeth health, energy metabolism	Meat, dairy, fish, nuts, beans	700 mg	700 mg	Weakness, bone pain
Potassium	Fluid balance, nerve signaling, muscle contraction	Bananas, potatoes, spinach, avocado	3400 mg	2600 mg	Muscle weakness, cramps, arrhythmia
Sodium	Fluid balance, nerve signaling	Table salt, processed foods, soy sauce	<2300 mg	<2300 mg	Hyponatremia (rare from diet alone)
Zinc	Immune function, wound healing, taste/smell	Oysters, beef, pumpkin seeds, chickpeas	11 mg	8 mg	Immune dysfunction, hair loss, delayed wound healing
Selenium	Antioxidant, thyroid function, immune function	Brazil nuts, seafood, organ meats	55 mcg	55 mcg	Thyroid dysfunction, weakened immunity
Copper	Iron metabolism, connective tissue, antioxidant	Liver, oysters, dark chocolate, cashews	900 mcg	900 mcg	Anemia, bone abnormalities
Manganese	Bone formation, blood clotting, metabolism	Whole grains, nuts, leafy greens, tea	2.3 mg	1.8 mg	Rare; bone demineralization
Chromium	Insulin signaling, glucose metabolism	Broccoli, grape juice, whole grains	35 mcg	25 mcg	Impaired glucose tolerance
Iodine	Thyroid hormone production	Seaweed, iodized salt, dairy, fish	150 mcg	150 mcg	Goiter, hypothyroidism
Molybdenum	Enzyme cofactor, detoxification	Legumes, grains, nuts	45 mcg	45 mcg	Rare
Fluoride	Dental health, bone strength	Fluoridated water, tea, fish	4 mg	3 mg	Dental caries

48.QUICK REFERENCE TABLES


1. POLYCYSTIC OVARY SYNDROME (PCOS)

PCOS affects 6-12% of women of reproductive age, making it one of the most common endocrine disorders in women .


What It Is

PCOS is a hormonal disorder characterized by:

Irregular or absent menstrual periods
Excess androgen (male hormone) levels
Polycystic ovaries (multiple small follicles on ultrasound)
Insulin resistance (present in 50-70% of women with PCOS)
Chronic low-grade inflammation

Types of PCOS

1. Insulin-Resistant PCOS (Most Common, ~70%)

High insulin levels drive androgen production
Associated with weight gain, difficulty losing weight
Dark patches of skin (acanthosis nigricans)

2. Inflammatory PCOS

Chronic inflammation triggers polycystic ovaries
Headaches, unexplained fatigue, skin issues
Elevated inflammatory markers

3. Adrenal PCOS

Stress-driven; elevated DHEA-S (adrenal androgen)
Normal testosterone and androstenedione
Triggered by chronic stress response

4. Post-Pill PCOS

Temporary PCOS symptoms after stopping oral contraceptives
Usually resolves within 3-6 months

Symptoms
Irregular, infrequent, or absent periods
Heavy bleeding during periods
Excess hair growth (hirsutism) on face, chest, back
Acne (especially cystic, on jawline/chin)
Weight gain, especially around the abdomen
Difficulty losing weight
Thinning hair or hair loss (male-pattern baldness)
Darkening of skin (neck, groin, under breasts)
Skin tags
Infertility or difficulty conceiving
Mood changes: depression, anxiety
Sleep apnea
Fatigue

Foods to EAT

Anti-Inflammatory Foods:

Fatty fish: salmon, sardines, mackerel (3-4 servings/week)
Extra virgin olive oil
Colorful vegetables: leafy greens, bell peppers, tomatoes, broccoli
Berries: blueberries, strawberries, raspberries, blackberries
Turmeric and ginger
Nuts: walnuts, almonds (handful daily)
Seeds: flaxseeds, chia seeds, pumpkin seeds
Green tea

Low-Glycemic Index (GI) Foods:

Whole grains: oats, quinoa, barley, brown rice, bulgur
Legumes: lentils, chickpeas, black beans, kidney beans
Sweet potatoes (instead of white potatoes)
Non-starchy vegetables: cauliflower, zucchini, spinach, kale
Low-GI fruits: apples, pears, berries, cherries, grapefruit
Whole grain bread and pasta

Insulin-Sensitizing Foods:

Cinnamon (1/2 to 1 teaspoon daily - may improve insulin sensitivity)
Apple cider vinegar (1-2 tablespoons diluted in water before meals)
Fenugreek seeds (soaked overnight)
Bitter gourd / bitter melon
Chromium-rich foods: broccoli, barley, oats
Magnesium-rich foods: dark chocolate, spinach, almonds, avocado
Zinc-rich foods: pumpkin seeds, chickpeas, oysters

Hormone-Balancing Foods:

Cruciferous vegetables: broccoli, cauliflower, cabbage, Brussels sprouts (contain DIM - diindolylmethane, helps metabolize estrogen)
Spearmint tea (2 cups/day - shown to reduce free testosterone)
Flaxseeds (ground - 1-2 tablespoons daily, contain lignans that balance hormones)
Soy foods in moderation: tofu, tempeh, edamame (phytoestrogens)
Sesame seeds
Sunflower seeds (vitamin E for hormone production)

Gut-Healthy Foods:

Probiotic-rich foods: yogurt with live cultures, kefir, kimchi, sauerkraut, miso
Prebiotic-rich foods: garlic, onions, asparagus, bananas, oats
Fermented foods
Bone broth

Protein-Rich Foods:

Lean chicken and turkey
Eggs
Fish and seafood
Tofu and tempeh
Legumes and lentils
Greek yogurt

Healthy Fats:

Avocado
Nuts and nut butters
Seeds
Olive oil
Coconut oil (in moderation)
Fatty fish

Foods to AVOID

High-Glycemic Foods:

White bread, white rice, white pasta
Sugary cereals
Pastries, cakes, cookies, donuts
Candy and chocolate bars
White potatoes (in excess)
Instant oatmeal (flavored)
Rice cakes

Sugar and Sweetened Products:

Soda and sugary drinks
Fruit juice (even 100% juice)
Energy drinks
Sweetened coffee and tea
Candy
Ice cream
Sweetened yogurt
Honey and maple syrup in excess
Agave nectar

Processed and Refined Foods:

Fast food
Processed snacks: chips, crackers, pretzels
Frozen dinners
Packaged baked goods
Processed meats: bacon, sausage, hot dogs, deli meats
Trans fats: margarine, fried foods, commercial baked goods
Refined vegetable oils: soybean oil, corn oil, canola oil (excessive)

Inflammatory Foods:

Excessive red meat
Fried foods
Processed vegetable oils
Alcohol (excessive)
Artificial sweeteners: aspartame, sucralose (may worsen insulin resistance)
Excessive dairy (in some women - may increase androgens)
Gluten (in some women with sensitivity)

Specific Foods to Limit:

Soy in excess (may affect hormones in some women)
Excessive caffeine (>2 cups coffee/day)
Alcohol
High-sodium foods

Detailed Meal Plan Framework

Breakfast Options:

Overnight oats with chia seeds, berries, and walnuts
Scrambled eggs with spinach and avocado on whole grain toast
Greek yogurt parfait with flaxseeds and low-GI fruits
Smoothie with spinach, protein powder, almond butter, and berries
Vegetable omelet with whole grain bread

Lunch Options:

Grilled chicken salad with mixed greens, chickpeas, and olive oil dressing
Quinoa bowl with roasted vegetables and salmon
Lentil soup with whole grain bread
Turkey and vegetable wrap (whole wheat)
Buddha bowl with brown rice, tofu, and vegetables

Dinner Options:

Baked salmon with roasted broccoli and sweet potato
Chicken stir-fry with vegetables and brown rice
Lentil curry with cauliflower rice
Grilled chicken with roasted vegetables and quinoa
Bean and vegetable chili

Snacks:

Apple slices with almond butter
Handful of walnuts and a small piece of dark chocolate
Hummus with vegetable sticks
Greek yogurt with berries
Hard-boiled egg
Trail mix (nuts and seeds, no added sugar)

Supplements for PCOS (Consult Doctor)

Supplement	Typical Dose	Benefit
Inositol (Myo + D-chiro)	2,000-4,000 mg/day	Improves insulin sensitivity, ovulation
Vitamin D	2,000-4,000 IU/day	Insulin sensitivity, hormone balance
Omega-3	1,000-2,000 mg/day	Anti-inflammatory, hormone balance
Magnesium	300-400 mg/day	Insulin sensitivity, sleep, anxiety
Zinc	15-30 mg/day	Hormone balance, skin, hair
Chromium	200-1,000 mcg/day	Blood sugar regulation
Berberine	500 mg 2-3x/day	Insulin sensitivity (like metformin)
NAC (N-Acetyl Cysteine)	600-1,800 mg/day	Antioxidant, ovulation, insulin
Vitamin B12	1,000 mcg/day	Especially if on metformin
Folate	400-800 mcg/day	Especially if trying to conceive
CoQ10	100-200 mg/day	Egg quality, energy
Probiotics	Multi-strain	Gut health, inflammation

Lifestyle Instructions

Exercise: At least 150 minutes/week of moderate activity; combine cardio and strength training
Weight management: Even 5-10% weight loss can significantly improve symptoms
Stress management: Yoga, meditation, deep breathing (stress worsens adrenal PCOS)
Sleep: 7-9 hours per night; poor sleep worsens insulin resistance
Meal timing: Don't skip meals; eat at regular intervals
Portion control: Use the plate method (1/2 vegetables, 1/4 protein, 1/4 whole grains)
Blood sugar monitoring: Regular glucose and insulin testing
Hormone monitoring: Regular blood work for androgens, insulin, thyroid
Regular periods: Track menstrual cycles
Fertility: If trying to conceive, work with reproductive endocrinologist
Mental health: PCOS increases risk of depression and anxiety; seek support
Medications: Take prescribed medications (metformin, oral contraceptives, spironolactone) as directed
Annual screenings: Diabetes, cholesterol, blood pressure, endometrial thickness


2. POLYCYSTIC OVARY DISEASE (PCOD)

What It Is

PCOD (Polycystic Ovarian Disease) is a condition where the ovaries release many immature or partially mature eggs, which over time develop into cysts. It is generally considered a milder condition than PCOS.


Key Differences from PCOS

Feature	PCOD	PCOS
Severity	Milder	More severe
Cause	Largely lifestyle-related	Metabolic/endocrine disorder
Insulin resistance	Mild or absent	Often significant
Hormonal imbalance	Mild	Significant (elevated androgens)
Fertility impact	Usually can conceive naturally	May need fertility treatment
Inflammation	Minimal	Chronic low-grade inflammation
Metabolic risk	Lower	Higher (diabetes, heart disease)
Weight gain	May or may not be present	Often significant
Ovulation	May still occur irregularly	Often absent
Long-term complications	Fewer	More (diabetes, heart disease, endometrial cancer)

Symptoms of PCOD
Irregular periods
Mild weight gain
Ovarian cysts on ultrasound
Mild acne
Mild hair growth changes
Mood fluctuations

Foods to EAT

Fresh fruits and vegetables (all types)
Whole grains: brown rice, oats, whole wheat
Lean proteins: fish, chicken, eggs, legumes
Healthy fats: olive oil, avocado, nuts
Anti-inflammatory foods
Fiber-rich foods
Adequate water (8-10 glasses/day)
Iron-rich foods (if periods are heavy)
Calcium-rich foods

Foods to AVOID

Processed and junk foods
Excessive sugar and sweets
Fried foods
Refined carbohydrates
Excessive caffeine
Alcohol
Trans fats
Excessive dairy (in some women)

Instructions

Maintain healthy weight through balanced diet and exercise
Exercise regularly (30-45 minutes/day)
Manage stress
Regular gynecological check-ups
Ultrasound monitoring of cysts
Hormonal medications if prescribed
Most women with PCOD can conceive naturally
Lifestyle modifications are the primary treatment


3. PCOS vs PCOD — KEY DIFFERENCES

Aspect	PCOD	PCOS
What happens	Ovaries produce immature eggs that form cysts	Endocrine system disorder affecting whole body
Can it be cured?	Yes, with lifestyle changes	Managed but not cured
Getting pregnant	Usually possible naturally	May require fertility treatment
Diabetes risk	Low	High
Heart disease risk	Low	High
Endometrial cancer risk	Low	Higher
Treatment approach	Lifestyle modifications primarily	Medications + lifestyle
Thyroid involvement	Usually normal	Often associated thyroid issues
Androgen levels	Normal to mildly elevated	Significantly elevated
Insulin levels	Usually normal	Often elevated


4. MENSTRUAL DISORDERS

A. DYSMENORRHEA (PAINFUL PERIODS)

What It Is:
Painful menstruation involving cramps in the lower abdomen. Can be primary (no underlying cause) or secondary (caused by a condition like endometriosis).


Foods to EAT:

Anti-inflammatory foods: fatty fish, turmeric, ginger
Magnesium-rich foods: dark chocolate, almonds, spinach, avocado, bananas
Calcium-rich foods: dairy, fortified plant milks, leafy greens
Omega-3 fatty acids: walnuts, flaxseeds, chia seeds, salmon
Iron-rich foods: lean red meat, spinach, lentils (replace iron lost during menstruation)
Vitamin B1-rich foods: whole grains, pork, legumes (may reduce cramps)
Vitamin B6-rich foods: chickpeas, bananas, potatoes (may reduce bloating)
Vitamin E-rich foods: almonds, sunflower seeds, avocado
Ginger tea (proven to reduce menstrual pain)
Chamomile tea (antispasmodic properties)
Cinnamon (anti-inflammatory, may reduce pain)
Raspberry leaf tea
Warm foods and beverages (promote blood flow)

Foods to AVOID:

Excessive caffeine (constricts blood vessels, worsens cramps)
Alcohol (dehydrating, worsens bloating)
Excessive salt (worsens water retention and bloating)
Refined sugars (blood sugar fluctuations worsen mood)
Fatty and fried foods (promote inflammation)
Red meat in excess (arachidonic acid promotes prostaglandins)
Processed foods
Carbonated beverages (worsen bloating)
Dairy (in some women - may worsen cramps due to arachidonic acid)
Spicy foods (may worsen gastrointestinal symptoms)
Very cold foods and drinks (in some traditions believed to worsen cramps)

Instructions:

Apply heat to lower abdomen (heating pad, warm bath)
Regular exercise (reduces cramp severity)
Over-the-counter NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) - start 1-2 days before period
Magnesium supplements (200-400 mg/day)
Omega-3 supplements
Yoga and stretching
Adequate sleep
Stress management
If severe, consult gynecologist (rule out endometriosis, fibroids)
Hormonal contraceptives may help regulate and reduce pain


B. AMENORRHEA (ABSENT PERIODS)

What It Is:
Absence of menstruation. Primary amenorrhea: no period by age 15. Secondary amenorrhea: absence of periods for 3+ months in someone who previously menstruated.


Causes:

Pregnancy (most common)
Low body weight / eating disorders
Excessive exercise
Stress
Polycystic ovary syndrome
Thyroid disorders
Premature ovarian failure
Pituitary disorders
Structural abnormalities

Foods to EAT:

Adequate calories: do NOT restrict calories
Healthy fats: avocado, olive oil, nuts, seeds (essential for hormone production)
Adequate protein: eggs, fish, chicken, legumes
Complex carbohydrates: whole grains, sweet potatoes
Iron-rich foods: red meat, spinach, lentils
Vitamin D-rich foods: fatty fish, fortified milk, eggs, sunlight
Calcium-rich foods
Zinc-rich foods: oysters, pumpkin seeds, beef
B-vitamin rich foods
Phytoestrogen-containing foods: soy, flaxseeds (in moderation)

Foods to AVOID:

Extreme dieting or calorie restriction
Excessive raw cruciferous vegetables (goitrogenic)
Excessive caffeine
Alcohol

Instructions:

Ensure adequate caloric intake (do not undereat)
Reduce excessive exercise
Maintain healthy body fat percentage (at least 17-22% for menstruation)
Manage stress
Rule out pregnancy
Consult gynecologist for evaluation
Hormone therapy if needed
Address underlying eating disorders immediately
Bone density testing (amenorrhea leads to bone loss)


C. MENORRHAGIA (HEAVY PERIODS)

What It Is:
Abnormally heavy or prolonged menstrual bleeding (soaking through a pad/tampon every hour for several consecutive hours, or periods lasting >7 days).


Foods to EAT:

Iron-rich foods (critical to prevent anemia):
Red meat, organ meats
Dark poultry meat
Shellfish: clams, oysters, mussels
Legumes: lentils, chickpeas, kidney beans
Dark leafy greens: spinach, kale
Fortified cereals
Tofu
Dark chocolate
Vitamin C-rich foods (enhances iron absorption): citrus, bell peppers, strawberries, tomatoes
Vitamin K-rich foods (supports blood clotting): leafy greens, broccoli, Brussels sprouts
Folate-rich foods: leafy greens, beans, fortified grains
Vitamin B12-rich foods: meat, fish, dairy, eggs
Vitamin A-rich foods: sweet potatoes, carrots, liver
Ginger (may reduce heavy bleeding)
Blackstrap molasses (high in iron)
Beetroot (iron and folate)

Foods to AVOID:

Excessive caffeine (inhibits iron absorption)
Tea and coffee with iron-rich meals
Calcium-rich foods with iron-rich meals (inhibits absorption)
Excessive alcohol
Processed foods
Excessive sugar
Soy in excess (may affect estrogen)
Trans fats

Instructions:

Track menstrual flow (menstrual cup for measurement)
Take iron supplements if prescribed
Vitamin C with iron supplements
Consult gynecologist (rule out fibroids, polyps, clotting disorders)
Hormonal treatment options: oral contraceptives, IUD (Mirena)
Endometrial ablation for severe cases
Monitor for anemia symptoms: fatigue, dizziness, pallor
Regular blood counts
NSAIDs may reduce bleeding by 20-40%


D. PREMENSTRUAL SYNDROME (PMS)

What It Is:
A group of symptoms occurring 1-2 weeks before menstruation, including mood swings, bloating, breast tenderness, fatigue, food cravings, irritability.


Foods to EAT:

Complex carbohydrates: whole grains, sweet potatoes, oats (boost serotonin)
Calcium-rich foods: dairy, fortified plant milks, leafy greens (1,200 mg/day shown to reduce PMS)
Magnesium-rich foods: dark chocolate, almonds, spinach, avocado
Vitamin B6-rich foods: chickpeas, bananas, potatoes, fish
Vitamin E-rich foods: almonds, sunflower seeds, avocado
Omega-3 fatty acids: fatty fish, walnuts, flaxseeds
Iron-rich foods
Small, frequent meals (stabilize blood sugar)
Herbal teas: chamomile, ginger, raspberry leaf
Dark chocolate (in moderation - magnesium and mood-boosting)
Bananas (vitamin B6, potassium, tryptophan)
Flaxseeds (lignans balance estrogen)

Foods to AVOID:

Excessive salt (worsens bloating and water retention)
Excessive sugar (blood sugar crashes worsen mood)
Caffeine (worsens breast tenderness, anxiety, insomnia)
Alcohol (worsens mood, disrupts sleep, dehydrating)
Processed foods
Fatty foods
Refined carbohydrates
Excessive dairy (in some women)
Chocolate in excess (caffeine content)

Instructions:

Regular exercise (reduces PMS symptoms by 50%)
Adequate sleep (7-9 hours)
Stress management: yoga, meditation
Calcium and magnesium supplementation
Vitamin B6 (50-100 mg/day)
Evening primrose oil (some evidence)
Track symptoms to identify patterns
SSRIs for severe PMS/PMDD
Hormonal contraceptives may help
Cognitive behavioral therapy


E. PREMENSTRUAL DYSPHORIC DISORDER (PMDD)

What It Is:
A severe form of PMS with debilitating emotional and physical symptoms that significantly interfere with daily life.


Symptoms:

Severe depression, hopelessness
Intense irritability, anger
Severe anxiety, tension
Difficulty concentrating
Fatigue, low energy
Changes in appetite, overeating, food cravings
Sleep disturbance
Physical symptoms: bloating, breast tenderness, headaches, joint pain
Feeling out of control
In severe cases: suicidal thoughts

Foods to EAT:

Same as PMS recommendations
Tryptophan-rich foods: turkey, eggs, cheese, nuts, seeds
Omega-3 fatty acids
Calcium: 1,200 mg/day
Magnesium: 200-400 mg/day
Vitamin B6
Complex carbohydrates
Adequate protein

Foods to AVOID:

Same as PMS
Additional: avoid alcohol completely (worsens depression)
Avoid caffeine (worsens anxiety)

Instructions:

Seek professional help (PMDD is a serious condition)
SSRIs are first-line treatment (fluoxetine, sertraline)
Hormonal treatments: oral contraceptives, GnRH agonists
Cognitive behavioral therapy
Regular exercise
Light therapy
Support groups
Track symptoms with a daily diary
Safety plan if suicidal thoughts occur


5. ENDOMETRIOSIS

What It Is

A condition where tissue similar to the lining of the uterus (endometrium) grows outside the uterus, causing pain, inflammation, and sometimes infertility. Affects approximately 10% of reproductive-age women.


Symptoms
Severe menstrual cramps
Chronic pelvic pain
Pain during or after intercourse
Pain with bowel movements or urination
Heavy periods or bleeding between periods
Bloating, nausea, diarrhea, constipation
Fatigue
Infertility (30-50% of women with endometriosis)

Foods to EAT

Anti-Inflammatory Foods (Critical):

Fatty fish: salmon, sardines, mackerel, herring (omega-3 reduces prostaglandins)
Extra virgin olive oil (oleocanthal has ibuprofen-like anti-inflammatory effect)
Turmeric (curcumin is a powerful anti-inflammatory)
Ginger (reduces inflammation and pain)
Berries: blueberries, strawberries, raspberries, blackberries
Dark leafy greens: spinach, kale, Swiss chard
Cruciferous vegetables: broccoli, cauliflower, Brussels sprouts, cabbage (support estrogen metabolism)
Nuts: walnuts, almonds
Seeds: flaxseeds (ground), chia seeds, hemp seeds
Green tea
Beets
Pineapple (bromelain has anti-inflammatory properties)
Tart cherry juice

Estrogen-Balancing Foods:

Cruciferous vegetables (DIM - diindolylmethane)
Ground flaxseeds (lignans modulate estrogen)
Whole grains
Fiber-rich foods (help excrete excess estrogen)
Soy in moderation (phytoestrogens may be protective)

Immune-Supporting Foods:

Garlic (immune-boosting, anti-inflammatory)
Mushrooms: shiitake, maitake, reishi
Vitamin C-rich foods
Zinc-rich foods: pumpkin seeds, oysters
Probiotic-rich foods: yogurt, kefir, kimchi, sauerkraut

Iron-Rich Foods (if heavy periods):

Red meat (lean)
Spinach, kale
Lentils, chickpeas
Fortified cereals
Pair with vitamin C for absorption

Foods to AVOID

Inflammatory Foods:

Red meat (increases prostaglandins and inflammation)
Processed meats: bacon, sausage, hot dogs
Trans fats: margarine, fried foods, commercial baked goods
Refined vegetable oils: corn oil, soybean oil, sunflower oil (high omega-6)
Fried foods
Processed foods
Refined sugar and sweets

Estrogen-Disrupting Foods:

Alcohol (increases estrogen levels)
Excessive caffeine (may worsen symptoms)
Conventionally raised meat and dairy (may contain hormones)
Plastic-packaged foods heated in microwave (xenoestrogens)
Soy in excessive amounts (in some women)

Other Foods to Avoid:

Gluten (some women report improvement on gluten-free diet)
Dairy (in some women - may increase inflammation)
Excessive red meat
High-sugar foods
Artificial sweeteners
Processed snack foods

Supplements for Endometriosis

Supplement	Dose	Benefit
Omega-3 (EPA/DHA)	1,000-2,000 mg/day	Anti-inflammatory
NAC	600 mg 3x/day	Antioxidant, may reduce endometrioma size
Vitamin D	2,000-4,000 IU/day	Immune modulation
Curcumin	500-1,000 mg/day	Anti-inflammatory
Magnesium	300-400 mg/day	Pain relief, muscle relaxation
Vitamin B6	50-100 mg/day	Hormone balance
Zinc	15-30 mg/day	Immune function, wound healing
EGCG (Green tea extract)	400 mg/day	Anti-angiogenic
Melatonin	3-5 mg at bedtime	Antioxidant, pain reduction
Resveratrol	100-200 mg/day	Anti-inflammatory
Probiotics	Multi-strain	Gut health, immune balance

Instructions

Work with a gynecologist specializing in endometriosis
Pain management: NSAIDs, hormonal treatments
Hormonal therapies: oral contraceptives, progestins, GnRH agonists
Laparoscopic excision surgery for severe cases
Fertility preservation if desired (endometriosis affects fertility)
Regular exercise (low-impact: swimming, yoga, walking)
Pelvic floor physical therapy
Acupuncture (some evidence for pain relief)
Stress management
Support groups
Regular monitoring
Consider anti-inflammatory diet for at least 3-6 months


6. UTERINE FIBROIDS

What It Is

Non-cancerous growths of the uterus that often appear during childbearing years. They range from tiny seedlings to large masses that can distort the uterus. Up to 80% of women have fibroids by age 50.


Symptoms
Heavy menstrual bleeding
Prolonged periods (7+ days)
Pelvic pressure or pain
Frequent urination
Difficulty emptying the bladder
Constipation
Backache or leg pains
Pain during intercourse

Foods to EAT

Cruciferous vegetables (support estrogen metabolism)
Fiber-rich foods (help excrete excess estrogen): whole grains, vegetables, fruits, legumes
Green tea (EGCG may reduce fibroid size)
Fatty fish (omega-3)
Vitamin D-rich foods (low vitamin D associated with fibroid growth)
Potassium-rich foods: bananas, sweet potatoes, avocados
Beta-carotene-rich foods: carrots, sweet potatoes, spinach
Flaxseeds (ground - lignans modulate estrogen)
Colorful fruits and vegetables
Lean protein

Foods to AVOID

Red meat (associated with increased fibroid risk)
High-fat dairy (may contain hormones)
Alcohol (increases estrogen levels)
Refined carbohydrates
Excessive sugar
High-sodium foods (worsen bloating)
Caffeine (in excess)
Processed foods
Soy in excess (in some women)
Foods high in additives and preservatives

Instructions

Maintain healthy weight (fat tissue produces estrogen)
Regular exercise
Monitor menstrual bleeding
Vitamin D supplementation (aim for sufficient levels)
Green tea consumption
Regular gynecological examinations
Ultrasound monitoring
Treatment options: medications (GnRH agonists, progestins), uterine artery embolization, myomectomy, hysterectomy
Most fibroids shrink after menopause


7. ADENOMYOSIS

What It Is

A condition where the inner lining of the uterus (endometrium) breaks through the muscle wall of the uterus (myometrium). Can cause heavy, painful periods and an enlarged uterus.


Foods to EAT
Anti-inflammatory diet (similar to endometriosis)
Omega-3 fatty acids
Cruciferous vegetables
Fiber-rich foods
Turmeric and ginger
Green tea
Iron-rich foods (if heavy bleeding)

Foods to AVOID
Red meat
Alcohol
Excessive caffeine
Processed foods
Refined sugar
Trans fats

Instructions
Pain management with NSAIDs
Hormonal treatments: IUD (Mirena), oral contraceptives, progestins
Uterine artery embolization
