Endometrial ablation
Hysterectomy (definitive treatment for severe cases)
Regular monitoring


8. PREMATURE OVARIAN FAILURE / PRIMARY OVARIAN INSUFFICIENCY (POI)

What It Is

Loss of normal ovarian function before age 40. The ovaries don't produce normal amounts of estrogen or release eggs regularly.


Symptoms
Irregular or absent periods
Hot flashes
Night sweats
Vaginal dryness
Difficulty conceiving
Decreased libido
Mood changes
Dry eyes

Foods to EAT
Calcium and vitamin D-rich foods (bone protection)
Phytoestrogen-containing foods: soy, flaxseeds, sesame seeds
Omega-3 fatty acids
Antioxidant-rich foods
Whole grains
Lean proteins
Fruits and vegetables
DHEA-supporting foods (if DHEA supplement prescribed)

Foods to AVOID
Excessive alcohol
Excessive caffeine
Processed foods
Smoking (major risk factor for POI)
Excessive sugar

Instructions
Hormone replacement therapy (HRT) until at least average age of menopause (51)
Calcium and vitamin D supplementation
Regular bone density testing
Cardiovascular monitoring
Psychological support
Fertility counseling (may conceive with donor eggs or IVF)
Regular follow-up with endocrinologist


9. OVARIAN CYSTS

What It Is

Fluid-filled sacs on or within an ovary. Most are functional cysts that resolve on their own. Types include functional cysts, dermoid cysts, cystadenomas, and endometriomas.


Foods to EAT
Fiber-rich foods: whole grains, vegetables, fruits, legumes
Lean protein
Anti-inflammatory foods
Cruciferous vegetables
Vitamin D-rich foods
Magnesium-rich foods
Green tea
Turmeric

Foods to AVOID
Excessive red meat
Processed foods
Excessive sugar
Alcohol
Refined carbohydrates
High-fat foods
Excessive caffeine

Instructions
Most functional cysts resolve within 1-3 menstrual cycles
Regular ultrasound monitoring
Hormonal contraceptives may prevent new cysts
Surgery if cyst is large (>5 cm), persistent, or causing symptoms
Seek emergency care for sudden severe pain (ruptured cyst or ovarian torsion)


10. PELVIC INFLAMMATORY DISEASE (PID)

What It Is

An infection of the female reproductive organs, usually caused by sexually transmitted bacteria (chlamydia, gonorrhea). Can cause infertility if untreated.


Symptoms
Lower abdominal pain
Abnormal vaginal discharge
Fever
Pain during intercourse
Painful urination
Irregular menstrual bleeding

Foods to EAT
Anti-inflammatory foods
Probiotic-rich foods: yogurt, kefir, kimchi
Vitamin C-rich foods: citrus, bell peppers, berries
Zinc-rich foods: oysters, pumpkin seeds, lean meat
Garlic (natural antibacterial)
Adequate hydration
Lean proteins
Fruits and vegetables

Foods to AVOID
Alcohol
Excessive sugar (feeds harmful bacteria)
Processed foods
Spicy foods (may irritate)
Caffeine in excess
Refined foods

Instructions
Complete full course of antibiotics as prescribed
Sexual partners must be tested and treated
Avoid intercourse until treatment complete
Probiotics during and after antibiotics
Follow-up with gynecologist
Regular STI screening
Practice safe sex
If untreated, can lead to infertility, ectopic pregnancy, chronic pelvic pain


11. VAGINAL INFECTIONS

A. BACTERIAL VAGINOSIS (BV)

Foods to EAT:

Probiotic-rich foods: yogurt with Lactobacillus, kefir, sauerkraut, kimchi
Prebiotic foods: garlic, onions, asparagus, bananas, oats
Vitamin C-rich foods (boost immunity)
Garlic (natural antibacterial)
Cranberries (prevent bacterial adhesion)
Adequate water

Foods to AVOID:

Excessive sugar
Alcohol
Processed foods
Artificial sweeteners
Excessive dairy (in some)
Douching (disrupts vaginal flora)

Instructions:

Antibiotics as prescribed (metronidazole, clindamycin)
Probiotics (Lactobacillus rhamnosus, L. reuteri)
Avoid douching
Wear cotton underwear
Avoid scented products in the vaginal area
Complete full course of treatment


B. YEAST INFECTION (CANDIDIASIS)

Foods to EAT:

Probiotic-rich foods: yogurt with live cultures, kefir
Garlic (antifungal properties)
Coconut oil (caprylic acid has antifungal properties)
Turmeric
Ginger
Low-sugar diet
Non-starchy vegetables
Lean proteins
Healthy fats

Foods to AVOID:

Sugar (feeds Candida): candy, pastries, soda, fruit juice
White bread, white rice, white pasta
Alcohol
Yeast-containing foods: bread, beer, wine, cheese (in excess)
Mushrooms (in some guidelines)
Processed foods
Excessive dairy
Vinegar (in excess)
Fermented foods during acute infection

Instructions:

Antifungal treatment (fluconazole, topical clotrimazole)
Reduce sugar intake significantly
Wear cotton underwear
Avoid tight-fitting clothing
Don't douche
Probiotics during and after treatment
Manage blood sugar if diabetic
Avoid unnecessary antibiotics
Change out of wet clothing promptly


12. URINARY TRACT INFECTIONS (UTIs)

What It Is

Infection in any part of the urinary system: kidneys, ureters, bladder, and urethra. Women are at higher risk due to shorter urethra.


Foods to EAT
Water: at least 8-10 glasses/day (most important)
Cranberry juice (unsweetened) or cranberry supplements (prevent bacterial adhesion)
Blueberries (similar compounds to cranberries)
Probiotic-rich foods: yogurt, kefir (Lactobacillus)
Vitamin C-rich foods: citrus, bell peppers, kiwi (acidifies urine)
Garlic (antibacterial)
Pineapple (bromelain)
Parsley tea (natural diuretic)
Celery, cucumber (high water content)
Bone broth

Foods to AVOID
Excessive sugar (feeds bacteria)
Alcohol (irritates bladder)
Caffeine (irritates bladder)
Carbonated beverages
Spicy foods (irritate bladder)
Artificial sweeteners (may irritate bladder)
Acidic foods if bladder irritation present: tomatoes, citrus
Processed foods

Instructions
Drink plenty of water
Urinate frequently; don't hold urine
Wipe front to back
Urinate after intercourse
Avoid irritating feminine products
Wear cotton underwear
Take full course of antibiotics
Probiotics during and after antibiotics
Cranberry supplements for prevention
See doctor for recurrent UTIs (3+/year)
Postmenopausal women: vaginal estrogen may help prevent recurrent UTIs


13. INTERSTITIAL CYSTITIS (PAINFUL BLADDER SYNDROME)

What It Is

A chronic condition causing bladder pressure, bladder pain, and sometimes pelvic pain.


Foods to EAT (IC Diet)
Most vegetables (except tomatoes)
Most fruits (except citrus, cranberries)
Whole grains
Lean proteins
Water
Low-acid foods
Alkaline water
Pears, blueberries, melons (bladder-friendly)
Rice, oats

Foods to AVOID (Bladder Irritants)
Coffee and tea (caffeine)
Alcohol
Carbonated beverages
Citrus fruits and juices
Tomatoes and tomato products
Spicy foods
Chocolate
Artificial sweeteners
Onions
Vinegar
Aged cheeses

Instructions
Follow the elimination diet to identify personal triggers
Bladder training
Pelvic floor physical therapy
Medications: pentosan polysulfate (Elmiron), antihistamines
Stress management
Support groups


14. VULVODYNIA & VULVAR CONDITIONS

What It Is

Chronic vulvar pain or discomfort without an identifiable cause.


Foods to EAT
Anti-inflammatory foods
Low-oxalate diet (some evidence)
Calcium citrate supplements (bind oxalates)
Omega-3 fatty acids
Probiotic-rich foods
Alkaline foods

Foods to AVOID
High-oxalate foods: spinach, rhubarb, beets, nuts, chocolate, tea
Acidic foods
Spicy foods
Caffeine
Alcohol

Instructions
Vulvar care: gentle cleansing, no scented products
Cotton underwear
Loose-fitting clothing
Topical treatments
Pelvic floor physical therapy
Pain management
Psychological support


15. FIBROCYSTIC BREAST DISEASE

What It Is

Lumpy, sometimes painful breast tissue that is benign (non-cancerous). Very common, affecting up to 50% of women.


Foods to EAT
Low-fat diet
High-fiber foods
Vitamin E-rich foods: almonds, sunflower seeds
Evening primrose oil (GLA)
Iodine-rich foods (if deficient): seaweed, fish
Flaxseeds
Cruciferous vegetables

Foods to AVOID
Caffeine: coffee, tea, chocolate, cola (reduces symptoms in many women)
Excessive fat
Alcohol
Excessive salt (worsens breast swelling)
Methylxanthines: found in coffee, tea, chocolate, cola

Instructions
Reduce caffeine intake (often significant improvement)
Wear a supportive bra
Evening primrose oil supplements
Vitamin E supplements
Regular breast self-examinations
Regular mammograms as recommended
Most cases do not increase cancer risk


16. CERVICAL DYSPLASIA & CERVICAL CANCER

What It Is

Cervical dysplasia: abnormal cell changes on the cervix that may progress to cervical cancer. Cervical cancer is primarily caused by HPV (human papillomavirus).


Foods to EAT
Folate-rich foods: leafy greens, beans, fortified cereals (low folate associated with cervical dysplasia)
Vitamin C-rich foods: citrus, bell peppers, kiwi, strawberries
Vitamin A-rich foods: sweet potatoes, carrots, liver
Cruciferous vegetables (support detoxification)
Lycopene-rich foods: tomatoes, watermelon
Green tea (EGCG)
Turmeric
Antioxidant-rich foods
Beta-carotene-rich foods
Zinc-rich foods
Selenium-rich foods: Brazil nuts, fish

Foods to AVOID
Smoking (major risk factor - doubles cervical cancer risk)
Excessive alcohol
Processed foods
Excessive sugar
Folic acid deficiency
Immunosuppressive behaviors

Instructions
Regular Pap smears (starting at age 21)
HPV vaccination (ages 9-26, ideally before sexual activity)
HPV testing
Colposcopy if abnormal Pap
LEEP or cone biopsy for high-grade dysplasia
Don't smoke
Safe sex practices
Folate supplementation
Regular follow-up


17. BREAST CANCER (DETAILED)

What It Is

Cancer that forms in the cells of the breast. Most common cancer in women worldwide.


Risk Factors
Being female
Increasing age
Family history
BRCA1/BRCA2 gene mutations
Obesity (especially postmenopausal)
Alcohol use
Hormone replacement therapy
Early menstruation or late menopause
Never having been pregnant
Dense breast tissue
Radiation exposure

Foods to EAT (Prevention & During/After Treatment)

Prevention:

Cruciferous vegetables: broccoli, cauliflower, Brussels sprouts, kale (sulforaphane)
Berries: blueberries, raspberries, strawberries
Fatty fish (omega-3)
Olive oil
Fiber-rich foods (reduce estrogen reabsorption)
Soy foods in moderation (whole soy: tofu, edamame - evidence suggests safe and possibly protective)
Green tea
Turmeric
Flaxseeds (ground)
Mushrooms: shiitake, maitake
Walnuts
Pomegranate
Whole grains
Allium vegetables: garlic, onions
Tomatoes (lycopene)
Carrots and sweet potatoes (beta-carotene)

During Treatment:

High-protein foods (healing, immune function)
Small, frequent meals
Soft, bland foods if mouth sores
Adequate calories
Ginger (for nausea)
Well-cooked foods (infection prevention)
Adequate hydration

After Treatment:

Plant-predominant diet
Maintain healthy weight
Limit alcohol (even small amounts increase recurrence risk)
Regular exercise
Anti-inflammatory foods
Cruciferous vegetables
Fiber-rich foods

Foods to AVOID
Alcohol (even 1 drink/day increases risk by 7-10%)
Processed meats
Excessive red meat
Sugar-sweetened beverages
Highly processed foods
Trans fats
Excessive saturated fat
Excessive calories (obesity increases risk)
Excessive soy supplements (whole soy is safe; concentrated supplements less clear)
Grapefruit (interacts with some breast cancer medications: tamoxifen)
Charred meats

Instructions
Monthly breast self-examinations
Annual mammograms starting at age 40-50 (discuss with doctor)
Genetic testing if family history (BRCA1/BRCA2)
Maintain healthy weight
Exercise regularly (30+ min/day reduces risk by 20-30%)
Limit alcohol
Don't smoke
Breastfeed if possible (reduces risk)
Hormone therapy discussions with doctor
Follow treatment plan: surgery, chemotherapy, radiation, hormonal therapy, targeted therapy
Survivorship care plan
Psychological support
Support groups
Regular follow-up


18. OVARIAN CANCER

What It Is

Cancer that begins in the ovaries. Often called the "silent killer" because symptoms are subtle until advanced.


Foods to EAT
Cruciferous vegetables
Fiber-rich foods
Antioxidant-rich fruits and vegetables
Green tea
Turmeric
Omega-3 fatty acids
Lean proteins
Whole grains

Foods to AVOID
Excessive red meat
Processed foods
Excessive dairy (some studies suggest association)
Excessive sugar
Alcohol
High-fat diet

Instructions
Know symptoms: bloating, pelvic pain, difficulty eating, urinary symptoms
No reliable screening test (unlike breast/cervical cancer)
CA-125 blood test and transvaginal ultrasound for high-risk women
Genetic testing for BRCA1/BRCA2
Discuss risk-reducing surgery if BRCA positive
Regular gynecological check-ups
Treatment: surgery + chemotherapy


19. UTERINE / ENDOMETRIAL CANCER

What It Is

Cancer of the lining of the uterus (endometrium). Most common in postmenopausal women.


Foods to EAT
High-fiber foods (reduce estrogen)
Cruciferous vegetables
Lean proteins
Whole grains
Fruits and vegetables
Maintain healthy weight (obesity is major risk factor)

Foods to AVOID
Excess body fat (produces estrogen)
High-fat diet
Excessive sugar
Processed foods
Alcohol
Excessive red meat

Instructions
Report any postmenopausal bleeding immediately
Maintain healthy weight
Regular gynecological check-ups
Manage diabetes
Hormonal balance
Treatment: surgery (hysterectomy), radiation, hormonal therapy, chemotherapy


20. PRENATAL NUTRITION (COMPREHENSIVE)

Proper nutrition before and during pregnancy is critical for both maternal and fetal health .


PRECONCEPTION NUTRITION (3-6 Months Before Conception)

Foods to EAT:

Folate-rich foods: leafy greens, fortified cereals, beans, oranges, asparagus
Iron-rich foods: lean red meat, spinach, lentils, fortified cereals
Calcium-rich foods: dairy, fortified plant milks, leafy greens
Vitamin D-rich foods: fatty fish, eggs, fortified milk, sunlight
Omega-3 fatty acids: fatty fish (low mercury), walnuts, chia seeds
Iodine-rich foods: iodized salt, dairy, fish
Choline-rich foods: eggs, liver, soybeans, chicken
Zinc-rich foods: oysters, beef, pumpkin seeds, chickpeas
Vitamin B12-rich foods: meat, fish, dairy, fortified foods
Lean proteins
Whole grains
Colorful fruits and vegetables
Healthy fats

Foods to AVOID:

Alcohol (stop completely)
Excessive caffeine (<200 mg/day)
Smoking (stop completely)
Processed foods
Excessive sugar
Trans fats
High-mercury fish
Unpasteurized foods
Raw/undercooked foods

Supplements to START:

Folic acid: 400-800 mcg/day (critical for preventing neural tube defects)
Prenatal multivitamin
Iron if deficient
Vitamin D if deficient
DHA/Omega-3
Iodine if not getting enough from diet


FIRST TRIMESTER (Weeks 1-12)

Foods to EAT:

Folate-rich foods (critical for neural tube development): 600 mcg/day
Small, frequent meals (combat nausea)
Ginger (for morning sickness): ginger tea, ginger candies, ginger ale
Plain, bland foods: crackers, toast, rice, bananas (BRAT diet for nausea)
Cold foods (less aroma, less nausea)
Protein-rich snacks: nuts, cheese, yogurt
Vitamin B6-rich foods (reduce nausea): chicken, potatoes, bananas
Iron-rich foods
Adequate hydration: 8-10 glasses water/day
Complex carbohydrates (steady energy)
Citrus fruits (may help nausea)

Foods to AVOID:

High-mercury fish: shark, swordfish, king mackerel, tilefish, bigeye tuna
Raw or undercooked fish, meat, eggs
Unpasteurized dairy and juices
Deli meats (unless heated to steaming)
Raw sprouts
Alcohol (completely)
Caffeine (<200 mg/day)
Soft cheeses: brie, camembert, blue cheese, queso fresco (unless pasteurized)
Liver (excessive vitamin A)
Pâté
Unwashed produce

Nutrient Focus:

Nutrient	Daily Need	Why
Folate	600 mcg	Neural tube development
Iron	27 mg	Blood volume expansion
Calcium	1,000 mg	Bone development
Vitamin D	600 IU	Calcium absorption
Choline	450 mg	Brain development
DHA	200-300 mg	Brain and eye development
Iodine	220 mcg	Thyroid function, brain development
Protein	71 g	Tissue growth

Instructions:

Take prenatal vitamin daily
Eat small, frequent meals to manage nausea
Stay hydrated
Rest when needed
Avoid raw and unpasteurized foods
First prenatal visit
Genetic screening options
Manage morning sickness (eat before getting out of bed, avoid empty stomach)


SECOND TRIMESTER (Weeks 13-27)

Foods to EAT:

Increased calories: ~340 extra calories/day
Calcium-rich foods: 1,000 mg/day (baby's bones developing)
Iron-rich foods: 27 mg/day (increased blood volume)
Vitamin D-rich foods
Omega-3 fatty acids: DHA for brain development
Protein: 71 g/day
Fiber-rich foods (prevent constipation)
Fruits and vegetables: 7-10 servings/day
Whole grains
Lean proteins
Healthy fats
Magnesium-rich foods (prevent leg cramps)
Potassium-rich foods

Foods to AVOID:

Same as first trimester
Excessive empty calories
Excessive sodium (prevent swelling)

Common Issues and Dietary Solutions:

Constipation: Increase fiber, water, prunes, kiwi, physical activity
Heartburn: Small meals, avoid spicy/fatty foods, don't lie down after eating
Leg cramps: Magnesium-rich foods, calcium, potassium, stretching
Gestational diabetes risk: Monitor sugar intake, choose low-GI foods

Instructions:

Continue prenatal vitamins
Glucose screening test at 24-28 weeks
Anatomy scan at 18-22 weeks
Monitor weight gain
Regular prenatal check-ups
Begin planning for birth


THIRD TRIMESTER (Weeks 28-40)

Foods to EAT:

Increased calories: ~450 extra calories/day
Higher protein needs
Iron-rich foods (prevent anemia)
Calcium-rich foods
DHA-rich foods (brain development peaks)
Vitamin K-rich foods (blood clotting for delivery)
Fiber-rich foods (prevent constipation and hemorrhoids)
Vitamin C-rich foods (tissue repair, immune function)
Adequate hydration (prevent preterm labor, swelling)
Small, frequent meals (stomach compressed by growing baby)
Dates (some evidence for cervical ripening in late pregnancy)
Red raspberry leaf tea (traditionally used in late pregnancy)

Foods to AVOID:

Same as first trimester
Large meals (discomfort)
Excessive sodium (swelling)
Excessive sugar

Common Issues and Dietary Solutions:

Heartburn: Small meals, elevate head, avoid trigger foods
Constipation: Fiber, water, prunes, movement
Swelling/Edema: Reduce sodium, elevate feet, adequate water
Fatigue: Iron-rich foods, adequate calories, rest
Insomnia: Magnesium-rich foods, chamomile tea, avoid caffeine

Instructions:

Continue prenatal vitamins
Weekly prenatal visits in final month
Monitor fetal movement (kick counts)
Pack hospital bag
Birth plan preparation
Signs of labor education
Group B strep test at 36 weeks
Final blood work


WEIGHT GAIN GUIDELINES DURING PREGNANCY

Pre-Pregnancy BMI	Recommended Weight Gain
Underweight (<18.5)	28-40 pounds
Normal weight (18.5-24.9)	25-35 pounds
Overweight (25-29.9)	15-25 pounds
Obese (≥30)	11-20 pounds
Twins	37-54 pounds (normal weight)
Triplets	No established guidelines


21. HYPEREMESIS GRAVIDARUM (SEVERE MORNING SICKNESS)

What It Is

Severe nausea and vomiting during pregnancy that goes beyond typical morning sickness. Can cause dehydration, weight loss, and electrolyte imbalances. Affects 0.3-2% of pregnancies.


Foods to EAT
Small, frequent meals (every 1-2 hours)
Plain, bland foods: crackers, toast, rice, plain pasta
Cold foods (less aroma)
Ginger: ginger tea, ginger candies, ginger ale (real ginger)
Protein-rich snacks: nuts, cheese, hard-boiled eggs
Watermelon, cucumber (hydrating)
Popsicles (hydration)
Electrolyte drinks (Pedialyte)
Lemon water
Peppermint tea
Potatoes, bananas (easy on stomach)
BRAT diet: bananas, rice, applesauce, toast
Vitamin B6-rich foods

Foods to AVOID
Spicy foods
Greasy, fried foods
Strong-smelling foods
Hot foods (aroma triggers nausea)
Large meals
Foods with strong flavors
Caffeine
Carbonated beverages (in some)
Citrus (in some)

Instructions
Eat before getting out of bed (crackers on nightstand)
Sip fluids throughout the day (don't drink with meals)
Vitamin B6 (10-25 mg, 3x/day) - first-line treatment
Doxylamine (Unisom) + B6 combination
Acupressure wristbands (P6 point)
IV fluids if dehydrated
Hospitalization if severe
Antiemetic medications if prescribed
Monitor weight
Monitor for ketones in urine
Most cases resolve by 20 weeks
Nutritional support if unable to eat


22. GESTATIONAL DIABETES (EXPANDED)

What It Is

Diabetes diagnosed during pregnancy (usually 24-28 weeks) that usually resolves after delivery.


Screening
Glucose challenge test (1-hour): drink 50g glucose solution
If abnormal (>140 mg/dL), glucose tolerance test (3-hour)
Diagnosed if 2+ values abnormal

Foods to EAT
Non-starchy vegetables: fill half your plate
Lean protein at every meal: chicken, fish, eggs, tofu, legumes
Complex carbohydrates in controlled portions: quinoa, brown rice, sweet potatoes
High-fiber foods: beans, lentils, vegetables, whole grains
Healthy fats: avocado, nuts, olive oil
Low-glycemic fruits: berries, apples, pears (controlled portions)
Small, frequent meals: 3 meals + 2-3 snacks
Protein with every carbohydrate
Cinnamon

Foods to AVOID
Sugar-sweetened beverages
Fruit juice
White bread, white rice, white pasta
Sweets, candy, pastries
Fried foods
Large carbohydrate portions at one meal
High-sugar cereals
Sweetened yogurt
Dried fruits in large amounts

Blood Sugar Targets
Test	Target
Fasting	<95 mg/dL
1-hour post-meal	<140 mg/dL
2-hour post-meal	<120 mg/dL

Instructions
Monitor blood glucose 4x daily
Follow meal plan from dietitian
Regular exercise (walking after meals)
Insulin if diet and exercise insufficient
Regular prenatal visits
Monitor fetal growth
Delivery planning (may need induction at 39-40 weeks)
Screen for type 2 diabetes 6-12 weeks postpartum
Lifetime diabetes screening every 1-3 years


23. PREECLAMPSIA & GESTATIONAL HYPERTENSION

What It Is

Gestational Hypertension: High blood pressure developing after 20 weeks of pregnancy.


Preeclampsia: High blood pressure + protein in urine + possible organ damage after 20 weeks. Can be life-threatening if untreated.


Symptoms
High blood pressure
Protein in urine
Severe headaches
Vision changes: blurriness, light sensitivity
Upper abdominal pain
Nausea or vomiting
Decreased urine output
Swelling of face and hands
Sudden weight gain

Foods to EAT
Calcium-rich foods: 1,000-1,200 mg/day (calcium supplementation may reduce risk)
Potassium-rich foods: bananas, sweet potatoes, avocados
Magnesium-rich foods: nuts, seeds, dark leafy greens
Lean protein
Fruits and vegetables
Whole grains
Low-sodium foods
Adequate hydration
Beet juice (natural blood pressure reduction)
Garlic

Foods to AVOID
Excessive sodium: <1,500-2,000 mg/day
Processed foods
Fast food
Canned soups and vegetables (high sodium)
Deli meats
Pickled foods
Excessive sugar
Fried foods
Excessive caffeine
Alcohol

Instructions
Regular blood pressure monitoring
Complete bed rest if prescribed
Hospitalization if severe
Magnesium sulfate during delivery (prevent seizures)
Delivery is the only cure
Antihypertensive medications
Monitor for HELLP syndrome (hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, low platelets)
Regular blood work
Fetal monitoring
Postpartum monitoring (can develop up to 6 weeks after delivery)
Future pregnancy risk is increased
Low-dose aspirin prophylaxis for future pregnancies if high risk


24. PLACENTA PREVIA

What It Is

A condition where the placenta partially or completely covers the cervix. Can cause severe bleeding during pregnancy or delivery.


Foods to EAT
Iron-rich foods (critical - bleeding risk)
Vitamin C-rich foods (enhance iron absorption)
Protein-rich foods
Calcium-rich foods
Folate-rich foods
Adequate hydration
Nutrient-dense foods

Foods to AVOID
Excessive strenuous activity
Sexual intercourse (if advised)
Heavy lifting

Instructions
Pelvic rest (no intercourse, no vaginal exams)
No heavy lifting
Regular ultrasound monitoring
Hospitalization if bleeding occurs
Cesarean delivery almost always required
Blood type and crossmatch ready
Iron supplementation
Monitor for bleeding


25. ECTOPIC PREGNANCY

What It Is

A pregnancy where the fertilized egg implants outside the uterus, most commonly in the fallopian tube. A medical emergency.


Foods to EAT (After Treatment/Recovery)
Iron-rich foods (if bleeding occurred)
Nutrient-dense foods for healing
Protein-rich foods
Anti-inflammatory foods
Adequate hydration
Folate-rich foods (for future pregnancy)

Instructions
Immediate medical attention if suspected (sharp abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding, dizziness)
Treatment: methotrexate injection or surgery (laparoscopy)
Wait 3-6 months before trying to conceive again
Monitor hCG levels until zero
Emotional support (grief counseling)
Future pregnancy monitoring early (higher risk of recurrence)
Rhogam injection if Rh-negative


26. MISCARRIAGE MANAGEMENT & RECOVERY

What It Is

Loss of pregnancy before 20 weeks. Affects approximately 10-20% of known pregnancies.


Foods to EAT (Recovery)
Iron-rich foods (replace blood loss)
Vitamin C-rich foods
Folate-rich foods
Protein-rich foods (tissue healing)
Calcium-rich foods
Vitamin D-rich foods
Omega-3 fatty acids (emotional well-being)
Warm, comforting foods
Adequate hydration
Nutrient-dense whole foods

Foods to AVOID
Alcohol (impairs healing, worsens depression)
Excessive caffeine
Processed foods
Excessive sugar

Instructions
Physical recovery: 1-2 weeks for early miscarriage
Wait for doctor's approval before trying again (usually 1-3 months)
Monitor for signs of infection: fever, foul-smelling discharge, heavy bleeding
Rhogam injection if Rh-negative
Emotional support: grief is normal
Counseling or support groups
Chromosomal testing of products of conception if recurrent miscarriage
Evaluation if 3+ consecutive miscarriages (recurrent pregnancy loss)
Future pregnancy: take folic acid, early prenatal care


27. MULTIPLE PREGNANCY (TWINS/TRIPLETS)

Foods to EAT
Increased calories: twins ~600 extra/day, triplets ~900 extra/day
Higher protein: 100-130 g/day
Increased calcium: 1,500-2,000 mg/day
Increased iron: 30-60 mg/day
Higher folate: 1,000 mcg/day
Increased omega-3
More frequent meals
Adequate hydration: 10-12 glasses/day
Nutrient-dense foods

Weight Gain Guidelines
Pre-Pregnancy BMI	Twins	Triplets
Normal weight	37-54 lbs	Consult specialist
Overweight	31-50 lbs	Consult specialist
Obese	25-42 lbs	Consult specialist

Instructions
More frequent prenatal visits
Higher risk of complications: preterm birth, preeclampsia, gestational diabetes
Specialized prenatal care
Possible bed rest in third trimester
Delivery planning (often early)
NICU awareness


28. POSTNATAL / POSTPARTUM NUTRITION

What It Is

The period after childbirth (typically the first 6-8 weeks, though recovery continues for months). The body needs to heal, establish milk production (if breastfeeding), and recover from the physical demands of pregnancy and delivery.


Foods to EAT

For Healing:

Protein-rich foods: lean meat, fish, eggs, legumes, dairy (tissue repair)
Vitamin C-rich foods: citrus, bell peppers, kiwi, strawberries (wound healing)
Iron-rich foods: red meat, spinach, lentils (blood loss replacement)
Zinc-rich foods: oysters, beef, pumpkin seeds (wound healing)
Vitamin A-rich foods: sweet potatoes, carrots, liver
Collagen-supporting foods: bone broth, fish, citrus

For Energy:

Complex carbohydrates: whole grains, oats, sweet potatoes
Healthy fats: avocado, nuts, olive oil
Adequate calories: 1,800-2,200 (non-breastfeeding) or 2,300-2,500 (breastfeeding)
Small, frequent meals
Nutrient-dense snacks

For Mood:

Omega-3 fatty acids: fatty fish, walnuts, flaxseeds (postpartum depression prevention)
Tryptophan-rich foods: turkey, eggs, cheese, nuts
Vitamin D-rich foods
Probiotic-rich foods (gut-brain axis)
B-vitamin rich foods: whole grains, eggs, leafy greens
Complex carbohydrates (serotonin production)

For Bone Health:

Calcium-rich foods: dairy, fortified plant milks, leafy greens
Vitamin D-rich foods

Traditional Postpartum Foods (Culturally Recognized):

Bone broth (nutrients, collagen, easy to digest)
Warm soups and stews
Oatmeal (supports milk production)
Fenugreek seeds (galactagogue - milk production)
Turmeric milk (golden milk - anti-inflammatory)
Dates (energy, iron)
Ghee (in moderation - traditional in many cultures)
Warm, cooked foods (easier to digest)

Foods to AVOID
Alcohol (passes into breast milk, impairs healing)
Excessive caffeine (passes into breast milk, affects baby's sleep)
Highly processed foods
Excessive sugar
Raw or undercooked foods (infection risk if healing from surgery)
High-mercury fish
Excessive gas-producing foods if baby is sensitive: cabbage, broccoli, beans (in some)
Foods that cause baby discomfort (if breastfeeding): varies by individual

Supplements for Postpartum
Supplement	Dose	Purpose
Postnatal multivitamin	As directed	Overall nutrition
Iron	27-45 mg (if anemic)	Blood loss recovery
Vitamin D	2,000-4,000 IU	Bone health, mood
DHA/Omega-3	200-300 mg	Mood, brain health, breast milk
Calcium	1,000 mg	Bone health
Vitamin B12	2.8 mcg (breastfeeding)	Energy, nerve function
Probiotics	Multi-strain	Gut health

Instructions
Rest when baby sleeps
Accept help with meals from family/friends
Stay hydrated (especially if breastfeeding)
Don't rush weight loss (wait at least 6 weeks)
Gentle walking when cleared by doctor
Pelvic floor exercises (Kegels)
Monitor for postpartum complications
Postpartum check-up at 6 weeks
Mental health screening (postpartum depression)
Contraception planning
Gradual return to exercise


29. BREASTFEEDING / LACTATION NUTRITION

What It Is

Breast milk provides optimal nutrition for infants. The mother's diet directly affects breast milk quality and quantity. Breastfeeding requires approximately 500 additional calories per day.


Foods to EAT

For Milk Production (Galactagogues):

Oatmeal (one of the best galactagogues)
Barley and barley malt
Fenugreek seeds (most studied galactagogue)
Fennel seeds
Brewer's yeast
Garlic (in moderation)
Sesame seeds
Almonds and cashews
Papaya (green, unripe)
Carrots
Spinach
Brown rice
Chickpeas
Blessed thistle herb
Milk thistle
Shatavari (Ayurvedic)
Torbangun leaves (Indonesian traditional)

For Nutrient-Dense Breast Milk:

High protein: eggs, fish, chicken, legumes, dairy
Omega-3 fatty acids: salmon, sardines, walnuts, chia seeds (DHA for baby's brain)
Calcium-rich foods: dairy, fortified plant milks, leafy greens (1,000 mg/day)
Iron-rich foods: red meat, lentils, spinach
Vitamin D-rich foods: fatty fish, eggs, fortified milk, sunlight
Iodine-rich foods: iodized salt, dairy, fish (290 mcg/day while breastfeeding)
Choline-rich foods: eggs, liver, soybeans (550 mcg/day)
Fruits and vegetables (variety of vitamins and antioxidants)
Whole grains
Healthy fats: avocado, olive oil, nuts, seeds
Adequate water: at least 16 cups (128 oz) per day

Hydration Strategy:

Drink a glass of water each time you breastfeed
Keep a water bottle nearby at all times
Coconut water for electrolytes
Herbal teas: fennel, fenugreek, raspberry leaf
Soups and broths

Foods to AVOID or LIMIT

Definitely Avoid:

Alcohol (passes into breast milk; if occasional drink, wait 2-3 hours per drink before nursing)
High-mercury fish: shark, swordfish, king mackerel, tilefish
Excessive caffeine (>2-3 cups coffee/day; can make baby fussy and disrupt sleep)
Illegal drugs
Certain herbs: sage, peppermint (in large amounts may reduce supply)
Parsley (large amounts may reduce supply)

Monitor Baby's Reaction:

Dairy (cow's milk protein allergy is common in infants)
Soy
Wheat/gluten
Eggs
Peanuts and tree nuts
Fish and shellfish
Citrus fruits
Cruciferous vegetables: broccoli, cabbage, cauliflower (gas)
Spicy foods (in some babies)
Garlic and onions (in some babies)

Limit:

Processed foods
Excessive sugar
Artificial sweeteners
Highly caffeinated beverages
Tuna (limit to 6 oz/week - moderate mercury)

Supplements for Breastfeeding

Supplement	Dose	Purpose
Postnatal multivitamin	As directed	Overall nutrition
DHA/Omega-3	200-300 mg/day	Baby's brain development
Vitamin D	2,000-4,000 IU/day	Or give baby 400 IU vitamin D drops
Calcium	1,000 mg/day	Bone health (breastfeeding depletes calcium)
Iodine	290 mcg/day	Baby's thyroid and brain development
Choline	550 mg/day	Baby's brain development
Iron	If anemic	Blood loss recovery
Vitamin B12	2.8 mcg/day	Especially if vegan

Instructions
Eat when hungry (breastfeeding increases appetite)
Don't diet while breastfeeding (needs adequate calories)
Aim for gradual weight loss (1 pound/week max after 6 weeks postpartum)
Eat a varied, balanced diet
Stay hydrated
Feed on demand (8-12 times/day for newborns)
Proper latch technique
Pump and store milk safely if needed
Monitor for mastitis (see Section 30)
Avoid pacifiers and bottles in first 3-4 weeks (establish breastfeeding first)
Seek lactation consultant help if needed
Exclusive breastfeeding recommended for first 6 months
Continue breastfeeding with complementary foods until 12-24 months
Take care of yourself - your nutrition matters


30. MASTITIS

What It Is

Inflammation of breast tissue, sometimes involving infection. Common in breastfeeding women.


Symptoms
Breast swelling, warmth, redness
Pain or burning sensation
Flu-like symptoms: fever, chills, fatigue
Hard lump in breast
Wedge-shaped area of redness

Foods to EAT
Anti-inflammatory foods
Vitamin C-rich foods (immune function)
Probiotic-rich foods (restore healthy bacteria)
Garlic (natural antibacterial)
Turmeric (anti-inflammatory)
Adequate hydration
Vitamin D-rich foods
Echinacea tea (immune support)
Warm compresses on affected area

Foods to AVOID
Excessive saturated fat
Excessive sugar
Alcohol
Caffeine in excess
Processed foods

Instructions
Continue breastfeeding or pumping (do NOT stop - this worsens mastitis)
Apply warm compresses before feeding
Massage the affected area gently
Feed from the affected breast first
Rest as much as possible
Antibiotics if prescribed (usually dicloxacillin or cephalexin)
Complete full course of antibiotics
Probiotics during and after antibiotics
Wear loose, comfortable bra
If abscess forms, may need drainage
See doctor if no improvement within 24-48 hours of antibiotics


31. POSTPARTUM DEPRESSION

What It Is

Depression that occurs after childbirth, affecting mood, energy, and ability to care for oneself and the baby. Affects approximately 10-20% of new mothers.


Symptoms
Persistent sadness, hopelessness
Excessive crying
Difficulty bonding with baby
Withdrawal from partner, family, friends
Loss of appetite or overeating
Insomnia or sleeping too much
Overwhelming fatigue
Intense irritability or anger
Fear of not being a good mother
Hopelessness
Reduced interest in activities
Difficulty concentrating
Thoughts of harming self or baby
Thoughts of death or suicide

Foods to EAT
Omega-3 fatty acids: fatty fish (salmon, sardines), walnuts, flaxseeds, chia seeds
Tryptophan-rich foods: turkey, eggs, cheese, nuts, seeds
Complex carbohydrates: whole grains, oats, sweet potatoes (serotonin production)
Vitamin D-rich foods: fatty fish, eggs, fortified milk, sunlight
B-vitamin rich foods: whole grains, leafy greens, eggs, legumes
Probiotic-rich foods: yogurt, kefir, kimchi, sauerkraut (gut-brain axis)
Iron-rich foods (anemia worsens depression)
Selenium-rich foods: Brazil nuts (1-2/day), fish, eggs
Zinc-rich foods: oysters, pumpkin seeds, beef
Magnesium-rich foods: dark chocolate, spinach, almonds
Folate-rich foods: leafy greens, beans, fortified cereals
Dark chocolate (in moderation - serotonin, endorphins)
Fermented foods
Colorful fruits and vegetables

Foods to AVOID
Alcohol (depressant, passes into breast milk)
Excessive caffeine (worsens anxiety, disrupts sleep)
Refined sugar (blood sugar crashes worsen mood)
Processed foods
Trans fats
Excessive processed carbohydrates
Artificial sweeteners

Instructions
SEEK HELP IMMEDIATELY - postpartum depression is treatable
Therapy: CBT, interpersonal therapy
Medications: SSRIs (sertraline is often preferred for breastfeeding)
Support groups
Rest when possible
Accept help from family and friends
Regular exercise (even short walks)
Adequate sleep (sleep when baby sleeps)
Omega-3 supplementation
Vitamin D supplementation
Light therapy
Screen for thyroid dysfunction (postpartum thyroiditis mimics depression)
If thoughts of self-harm or harming baby: call emergency services immediately
Postpartum Support International Helpline: 1-800-944-4773


32. POSTPARTUM THYROIDITIS

What It Is

Inflammation of the thyroid gland occurring within the first year after delivery. Affects 5-10% of women.


Phases
Phase 1 (Hyperthyroid): Weeks 1-4 postpartum; anxiety, rapid heartbeat, weight loss, irritability
Phase 2 (Hypothyroid): Months 4-8 postpartum; fatigue, weight gain, depression, brain fog
Phase 3 (Recovery): Most women return to normal within 12-18 months

Foods to EAT
During hyperthyroid phase: calorie-dense foods, calcium-rich foods
During hypothyroid phase: selenium-rich foods, iodine-rich foods, zinc-rich foods
Anti-inflammatory foods
Balanced nutrition throughout

Foods to AVOID
During hyperthyroid: excessive iodine, caffeine
During hypothyroid: excessive raw cruciferous vegetables, soy in excess

Instructions
Monitor thyroid function postpartum
May be mistaken for postpartum depression
Thyroid medication if needed
Regular follow-up
20-30% develop permanent hypothyroidism
Annual thyroid screening thereafter


33. DIASTASIS RECTI

What It Is

Separation of the rectus abdominis muscles (six-pack muscles) during pregnancy. The connective tissue (linea alba) stretches and thins, causing a gap.


Foods to EAT
Collagen-supporting foods: bone broth, fish, citrus, berries, garlic
Vitamin C-rich foods (collagen synthesis)
Protein-rich foods (tissue repair)
Zinc-rich foods (wound healing)
Anti-inflammatory foods
Adequate calories for healing
Gelatin

Foods to AVOID
Excessive weight gain
Processed foods
Excessive sugar
Alcohol
Foods that cause bloating (increase abdominal pressure)

Instructions
Avoid crunches, sit-ups, and heavy lifting initially
Pelvic floor physical therapy
Specialized exercises (Tupler technique, MUTU system)
Belly binding/support
Proper posture
Log-roll technique to get out of bed
Surgery ( abdominoplasty) for severe cases that don't respond to exercise
Most cases improve with proper exercise within 6-12 months


34. POSTPARTUM ANEMIA

What It Is

Low red blood cell count after delivery due to blood loss during childbirth. Very common, affecting up to 50% of postpartum women.


Foods to EAT
Heme iron sources (best absorbed): red meat, organ meats, shellfish, dark poultry
Non-heme iron sources: spinach, lentils, fortified cereals, tofu, beans
Vitamin C-rich foods WITH iron-rich meals: citrus, bell peppers, tomatoes, strawberries
Blackstrap molasses (1 tablespoon = 3.6 mg iron)
Dark chocolate
Dried apricots and raisins
Cook in cast iron cookware
Folate-rich foods
Vitamin B12-rich foods

Foods to AVOID (With Iron-Rich Meals)
Coffee and tea (tannins inhibit iron absorption)
Calcium supplements or dairy (inhibit absorption)
Whole grains and bran (phytates inhibit absorption)
Separate iron and calcium by 2+ hours

Instructions
Iron supplements as prescribed (take on empty stomach with vitamin C)
IV iron if severe or oral not tolerated
Blood transfusion if hemoglobin very low (<7 g/dL)
Monitor for symptoms: fatigue, dizziness, pallor, shortness of breath, rapid heartbeat
Regular blood counts
Identify and treat source of bleeding


35. POSTPARTUM HEMORRHAGE RECOVERY

What It Is

Excessive bleeding after childbirth (>500 mL for vaginal delivery, >1,000 mL for cesarean). A leading cause of maternal mortality worldwide.


Foods to EAT
Iron-rich foods (critical)
Vitamin C-rich foods
Protein-rich foods (tissue repair)
Folate-rich foods
Vitamin K-rich foods (blood clotting): leafy greens, broccoli
Hydrating foods and beverages
Nutrient-dense, easy-to-digest foods
Bone broth
Beetroot

Instructions
Monitor for continued bleeding
Iron supplementation (often IV iron)
Blood transfusion if needed
Treat underlying cause
Monitor vital signs
Rest and recovery time
Follow-up with healthcare provider
Watch for signs of infection


36. LACTATION PROBLEMS

A. LOW MILK SUPPLY

Foods to EAT (Galactagogues):

Oatmeal (daily)
Fenugreek (capsules or tea)
Barley and barley malt
Fennel seeds
Brewer's yeast
Sesame seeds
Almonds and cashews
Garlic
Green papaya
Carrots and beets
Spinach
Brown rice
Chickpeas
Warm soups and broths
Plenty of water (16+ cups/day)

Instructions:

Nurse frequently (8-12 times/day)
Ensure proper latch
Pump after nursing to stimulate supply
Power pumping sessions
Skin-to-skin contact
Rest and reduce stress
Avoid pacifiers and bottles initially
Consult lactation consultant
Prescription medications if needed (domperidone - off-label)


B. OVERSUPPLY / ENGORGEMENT

Foods to EAT:

Sage tea (may reduce supply)
Peppermint tea (may reduce supply)
Parsley (large amounts)
Jasmine flowers

Instructions:

Cold compresses after feeding
Express small amounts for comfort (don't empty completely)
Cabbage leaf compresses (cool, in bra)
Wear supportive bra
Reverse pressure softening
Consult lactation consultant


C. CLOGGED MILK DUCT

Foods to EAT:

Lecithin supplements (1,200 mg 3-4x/day - reduces milk viscosity)
Anti-inflammatory foods
Adequate hydration
Sunflower lecithin

Instructions:

Apply warm compress before nursing
Massage the affected area
Nurse frequently from affected side
Dangle feeding (gravity-assisted)
Vary nursing positions
Therapeutic breast massage
If not resolved within 24-48 hours, may progress to mastitis


37. INFERTILITY

What It Is

Inability to conceive after 12 months of regular, unprotected intercourse (6 months if over age 35).


Female Factors
Ovulation disorders (PCOS, thyroid, POI)
Tubal factors (blocked tubes)
Endometriosis
Uterine factors (fibroids, polyps)
Age-related decline
Unexplained

Foods to EAT

For Ovulation and Egg Quality:

Folate-rich foods: leafy greens, beans, fortified cereals
Iron-rich foods: lean red meat, spinach, lentils
Omega-3 fatty acids: fatty fish, walnuts, chia seeds
Antioxidant-rich foods: berries, dark leafy greens, colorful vegetables
Vitamin D-rich foods
Zinc-rich foods: oysters, pumpkin seeds, beef
Selenium-rich foods: Brazil nuts, fish
Vitamin E-rich foods: almonds, sunflower seeds
CoQ10-rich foods (or supplement)
Full-fat dairy (some evidence for fertility vs. low-fat)
Complex carbohydrates (whole grains)
Plant-based proteins (some evidence: beans, lentils, tofu)
Avocado
Cinnamon (may help with ovulation in PCOS)

For Uterine Health:

Iron-rich foods
Vitamin E-rich foods
Vitamin C-rich foods
Warm, nourishing foods (traditional Chinese medicine perspective)
Bone broth
Beetroot (increases blood flow to uterus)
Royal jelly (traditional fertility food)

Foods to AVOID
Trans fats (associated with ovulatory infertility)
Excessive alcohol (impairs fertility)
Excessive caffeine (>200 mg/day)
Refined carbohydrates
Excessive sugar
Processed foods
Excessive soy supplements (whole soy in moderation is fine)
Smoking (major fertility killer)
Excessive exercise (can disrupt ovulation)
Very low body fat
High-mercury fish
Artificial sweeteners

Supplements for Fertility

Supplement	Dose	Purpose
Folic acid	400-800 mcg/day	Neural tube prevention, egg quality
CoQ10	200-600 mg/day	Egg quality, energy
Vitamin D	2,000-4,000 IU/day	Ovulation, implantation
Omega-3 (DHA)	200-300 mg/day	Hormone balance, inflammation
Inositol	2,000-4,000 mg/day (PCOS)	Ovulation, insulin
Vitamin E	200-400 IU/day	Endometrial lining
Iron	If deficient	Ovulation
Selenium	55-200 mcg/day	Thyroid, egg quality
Zinc	15-30 mg/day	Egg quality
NAC	600-1,200 mg/day	Antioxidant, ovulation
Melatonin	3 mg at bedtime	Egg quality (especially IVF)
Prenatal vitamin	Daily	Overall preparation

Instructions
Track ovulation (basal body temperature, OPK, cervical mucus)
Timed intercourse (every 1-2 days during fertile window)
Maintain healthy weight
Manage stress (yoga, meditation, acupuncture)
Limit alcohol and caffeine
Don't smoke
Both partners should optimize health
See reproductive endocrinologist after 12 months (6 months if >35)
Fertility treatments: Clomid, IUI, IVF
Consider acupuncture (some evidence)
Emotional support and counseling
Join infertility support groups


38. PERIMENOPAUSE

What It Is

The transitional period before menopause when the body gradually produces less estrogen. Can last 4-8 years, typically beginning in the 40s.


Symptoms
Irregular periods
Hot flashes and night sweats
Sleep problems
Mood changes: irritability, anxiety, depression
Vaginal dryness
Decreased libido
Weight gain (especially around the abdomen)
Thinning hair
Dry skin
Memory problems ("brain fog")
Breast tenderness
Worsening PMS
Urinary urgency

Foods to EAT

For Hot Flashes:

Soy foods: tofu, tempeh, edamame, soy milk (isoflavones may reduce hot flashes)
Flaxseeds (ground - phytoestrogens)
Sesame seeds
Cooling foods: cucumber, watermelon, mint
Adequate water
Cold water throughout the day

For Mood and Sleep:

Omega-3 fatty acids
Tryptophan-rich foods: turkey, eggs, nuts
Magnesium-rich foods: dark chocolate, spinach, almonds (sleep and mood)
Complex carbohydrates (serotonin production)
Tart cherry juice (natural melatonin)
Chamomile tea
Valerian root tea

For Bone Protection:

Calcium-rich foods: 1,000-1,200 mg/day
Vitamin D-rich foods: 600-800 IU/day
Vitamin K-rich foods: leafy greens
Magnesium

For Weight Management:

High-protein foods (boost metabolism, maintain muscle)
Fiber-rich foods
Low-glycemic foods
Adequate calories (metabolism slowing)

For Heart Health:

Omega-3 fatty acids
Olive oil
Nuts
Fiber-rich foods
Colorful fruits and vegetables

Foods to AVOID
Spicy foods (trigger hot flashes)
Caffeine (triggers hot flashes, disrupts sleep)
Alcohol (triggers hot flashes, disrupts sleep)
Refined sugar (worsens mood, weight gain)
Processed foods
Excessive sodium (blood pressure)
Trans fats
Excessive saturated fat

Supplements for Perimenopause

Supplement	Dose	Purpose
Calcium	1,000-1,200 mg/day	Bone health
Vitamin D	2,000-4,000 IU/day	Bone health, mood
Magnesium	300-400 mg/day	Sleep, mood, bone
Omega-3	1,000-2,000 mg/day	Heart, mood, inflammation
Black cohosh	20-40 mg 2x/day	Hot flashes (some evidence)
Evening primrose oil	500-1,000 mg/day	Breast tenderness, mood
Vitamin B6	50-100 mg/day	Mood, hormone balance
Vitamin E	200-400 IU/day	Hot flashes (mild evidence)
Ashwagandha	300-600 mg/day	Stress, sleep
Probiotics	Multi-strain	Gut health

Instructions
Track menstrual cycle changes
Discuss hormone therapy options with doctor
Regular bone density screening
Cardiovascular screening
Exercise regularly (weight-bearing + cardio)
Stress management
Sleep hygiene
Vaginal moisturizers/lubricants for dryness
Mental health support
Annual well-woman exam
Mammogram as recommended
Pap smear as recommended


39. MENOPAUSE (EXPANDED)

What It Is

The permanent cessation of menstruation, diagnosed after 12 consecutive months without a period. Average age: 51 years (range: 45-55).


Symptoms (Same as Perimenopause, Plus)
Complete cessation of periods
Hot flashes (affect 75% of women)
Vaginal atrophy (dryness, thinning)
Urinary symptoms (urgency, frequency, UTIs)
Bone loss acceleration
Cardiovascular risk increase
Metabolic changes
Skin changes (thinner, drier)
Hair changes
Sexual changes

Foods to EAT

Comprehensive Menopause Diet:


Heart Health (Estrogen decline increases cardiovascular risk):

Fatty fish: salmon, sardines, mackerel (2-3x/week)
Olive oil (extra virgin)
Nuts: walnuts, almonds
Avocado
Fiber-rich foods: oats, beans, vegetables
Colorful fruits and vegetables
Whole grains
Legumes

Bone Health (Estrogen decline accelerates bone loss):

Calcium: 1,200 mg/day from food first
Dairy: yogurt, milk, cheese
Fortified plant milks
Sardines with bones
Leafy greens: kale, bok choy
Tofu (calcium-set)
Almonds
Vitamin D: 800-2,000 IU/day
Fatty fish, eggs, fortified foods, sunlight
Vitamin K: leafy greens, broccoli
Magnesium: nuts, seeds, dark chocolate
Phosphorus: meat, dairy, legumes

Weight Management (Metabolism slows 1-2% per decade after 30):

High-protein foods: lean meat, fish, eggs, legumes, dairy
Fiber-rich foods (satiety)
Low-glycemic foods
Adequate calories (not excessive)
Green tea (metabolism boost)

Mood and Brain Health:

Omega-3 fatty acids
B-vitamin rich foods
Antioxidant-rich foods
Fermented foods (gut-brain axis)
Dark chocolate (in moderation)

Skin Health:

Vitamin C-rich foods (collagen)
Vitamin E-rich foods
Omega-3 fatty acids
Water: adequate hydration
Antioxidant-rich foods

Phytoestrogen-Rich Foods (May help with hot flashes):

Soy: tofu, tempeh, edamame, soy milk
Flaxseeds (ground)
Sesame seeds
Chickpeas
Lentils

Foods to AVOID
Spicy foods (trigger hot flashes)
Caffeine (hot flashes, sleep disruption)
Alcohol (hot flashes, sleep, bone loss, cancer risk)
Refined sugar (weight gain, mood)
Processed foods
Excessive sodium (blood pressure)
Trans fats
Excessive saturated fat
High-calorie, low-nutrient foods
Very hot beverages (trigger hot flashes)

Supplements for Menopause

Supplement	Dose	Purpose
Calcium	1,200 mg/day	Bone health
Vitamin D3	2,000-4,000 IU/day	Bone, mood, immunity
Magnesium	300-400 mg/day	Bone, sleep, mood
Omega-3	1,000-2,000 mg/day	Heart, brain, inflammation
Vitamin K2 (MK-7)	100-200 mcg/day	Bone, heart
Collagen peptides	10-15 g/day	Skin, joints, bone
Vitamin B-complex	As directed	Energy, mood
Vitamin E	200 IU/day	Hot flashes (mild)
Probiotics	Multi-strain	Gut, immunity
Boron	3 mg/day	Bone health

Hormone Replacement Therapy (HRT)

Types:

Estrogen-only (if uterus removed)
Estrogen + Progestogen (if uterus intact)
Bioidentical hormones
Vaginal estrogen (local)

Benefits:

Most effective treatment for hot flashes and night sweats
Prevents bone loss
Improves vaginal dryness
May improve mood and sleep

Risks (discuss with doctor):

Slightly increased breast cancer risk (with combined HRT >5 years)
Blood clots
Stroke (small increase)
Heart disease (if started >10 years after menopause)

Instructions:

Discuss HRT with doctor within 10 years of menopause or before age 60
Use lowest effective dose for shortest duration
Regular mammograms and screenings
Annual review of HRT
Non-hormonal alternatives: SSRIs, gabapentin, clonidine for hot flashes

Lifestyle Instructions
Weight-bearing exercise: walking, dancing, stair climbing (bone health)
Resistance training 2-3x/week (muscle mass, metabolism)
Cardiovascular exercise: 150+ min/week (heart health)
Pelvic floor exercises (Kegels - prevent incontinence)
Yoga and stretching (flexibility, stress)
Maintain healthy weight
Don't smoke (accelerates bone loss, worsens hot flashes)
Limit alcohol
Sleep hygiene (7-8 hours)
Stress management
Social connections
Mental health support
Regular health screenings


40. POSTMENOPAUSAL HEALTH

Increased Risk After Menopause
Heart disease (#1 killer of women)
Osteoporosis
Urinary incontinence
Weight gain
Type 2 diabetes
Breast cancer
Colorectal cancer
Vaginal atrophy
Depression
Cognitive decline

Foods to EAT
Heart-healthy Mediterranean diet
Calcium and vitamin D-rich foods
High-fiber foods
Lean proteins
Omega-3 fatty acids
Colorful fruits and vegetables
Whole grains
Soy foods (phytoestrogens)
Berries (brain health)
Leafy greens

Foods to AVOID
Excessive saturated fat
Trans fats
Excessive sodium
Excessive sugar
Processed foods
Excessive alcohol
Excessive red meat

Instructions
Annual mammogram
Bone density testing (DEXA) at age 65 or earlier if risk factors
Colonoscopy screening
Cardiovascular screening
Blood pressure monitoring
Cholesterol monitoring
Blood sugar screening
Pelvic floor therapy
Vaginal estrogen if needed
Stay physically active
Maintain social connections
Cognitive stimulation
Mental health screening


41. OSTEOPOROSIS IN WOMEN (EXPANDED)

Why Women Are More Affected
Women have smaller, thinner bones
Estrogen decline after menopause accelerates bone loss
Women can lose up to 20% of bone density in the 5-7 years after menopause

Risk Factors Specific to Women
Early menopause (before 45)
Surgical removal of ovaries
Amenorrhea (absent periods)
Eating disorders
Low body weight
Caucasian or Asian ethnicity
Family history
Sedentary lifestyle
Smoking
Excessive alcohol
Low calcium/vitamin D intake
Certain medications: corticosteroids, thyroid hormones

Foods to EAT
Calcium-rich foods: 1,200 mg/day
Dairy: yogurt (best absorbed), milk, cheese
Sardines and canned salmon with bones
Fortified plant milks
Tofu (calcium-set)
Kale, bok choy, broccoli
Almonds
Fortified orange juice
Vitamin D: 800-2,000 IU/day
Fatty fish, eggs, fortified foods, sunlight exposure
Vitamin K: leafy greens (activates osteocalcin)
Magnesium: nuts, seeds, whole grains, dark chocolate
Phosphorus: meat, dairy, fish
Vitamin C: citrus, bell peppers (collagen for bone matrix)
Protein: adequate protein supports bone health
Prunes/dried plums: 5-6 daily (shown to preserve bone density)
Soy foods (isoflavones)
Alkaline diet (fruits and vegetables)

Foods to AVOID
Excessive sodium (>2,300 mg/day increases calcium excretion)
Excessive caffeine (>3 cups/day)
Excessive alcohol
Carbonated cola drinks (phosphoric acid)
Excessive vitamin A (retinol form >10,000 IU/day)
Excessive protein (very high protein may increase calcium excretion)
Oxalate-rich foods WITH calcium sources (reduce absorption)

Instructions
DEXA scan: women at 65, earlier if risk factors
Weight-bearing exercise: walking, dancing, hiking
Resistance training: 2-3x/week
Balance exercises: tai chi, yoga (prevent falls)
Fall prevention at home: remove rugs, improve lighting, handrails
Take medications as prescribed: bisphosphonates, denosumab, teriparatide
Calcium supplements if dietary intake insufficient (take in divided doses)
Vitamin D supplements
Don't smoke
Limit alcohol
Maintain healthy weight
Regular follow-up


42. ANEMIA IN WOMEN (EXPANDED)

Why Women Are More Susceptible
Menstrual blood loss (monthly)
Pregnancy demands
Breastfeeding demands
Lower iron stores than men
Dieting/restrictive eating

Types Common in Women

Iron-Deficiency Anemia (Most Common)

Caused by: blood loss (periods, pregnancy), inadequate intake, poor absorption
Symptoms: fatigue, weakness, pale skin, shortness of breath, dizziness, cold hands/feet, brittle nails, unusual cravings (pica), restless legs

Folate-Deficiency Anemia

Caused by: inadequate intake, malabsorption, pregnancy demands
Especially important: neural tube defect prevention

Vitamin B12-Deficiency Anemia

Caused by: inadequate intake (vegans), pernicious anemia, malabsorption
Symptoms: fatigue, numbness/tingling, cognitive changes

Foods to EAT

Iron-Rich Foods:

Heme iron (best absorbed, 15-35% absorption):
Red meat: beef, lamb (3-4 servings/week)
Organ meats: liver (richest source)
Shellfish: clams, oysters, mussels
Dark poultry meat
Fish: sardines, tuna
Non-heme iron (5-12% absorption):
Legumes: lentils, chickpeas, kidney beans
Tofu and tempeh
Spinach and Swiss chard
Fortified cereals and bread
Quinoa
Dark chocolate
Dried fruits: apricots, raisins, prunes
Pumpkin seeds
Blackstrap molasses

Vitamin C (Enhances Non-Heme Iron Absorption by 2-3x):

Pair with every iron-rich meal
Citrus fruits, bell peppers, strawberries, kiwi, tomatoes, broccoli

Folate-Rich Foods:

Leafy greens: spinach, romaine, kale
Asparagus
Legumes: lentils, chickpeas, black beans
Avocado
Broccoli, Brussels sprouts
Citrus fruits
Fortified cereals
Beets

Vitamin B12-Rich Foods:

Meat, fish, poultry
Eggs
Dairy
Fortified nutritional yeast
Fortified plant milks (for vegans)
Shellfish

Foods to AVOID (At Iron-Rich Meals)
Coffee and tea (tannins reduce absorption by 50-60%)
Calcium supplements and dairy (reduce absorption)
Whole grains and bran (phytates)
Soy protein (inhibits absorption)
Wait 1-2 hours between iron-rich meals and these foods

Instructions
Get regular blood work: CBC, ferritin, serum iron, TIBC
Iron supplements as prescribed (ferrous sulfate, ferrous gluconate)
Take iron on empty stomach with vitamin C
Expect side effects: constipation, dark stools, nausea
IV iron if oral not tolerated
Treat underlying cause of blood loss
Women with heavy periods: discuss with gynecologist
Pregnant women: prenatal iron supplement
Vegan women: MUST supplement B12
Cook in cast iron (leaches iron into food)
Track symptoms and report to doctor


43. THYROID DISORDERS IN WOMEN (EXPANDED)

Why Women Are More Affected
Women are 5-8x more likely to have thyroid disorders than men
Estrogen affects thyroid function
Autoimmune conditions more common in women
Pregnancy affects thyroid function
Postpartum thyroiditis affects 5-10% of women

Hypothyroidism in Women (Expanded)
Additional Symptoms in Women:

Heavy periods
Difficulty conceiving
Miscarriage risk
Postpartum depression
Weight gain
Hair loss (especially outer third of eyebrows)
Dry skin and brittle nails
Fatigue
Cold intolerance
Constipation
Depression

Foods to EAT:

Iodine-rich foods: iodized salt, seaweed (moderate), fish, dairy
Selenium-rich foods: Brazil nuts (1-2/day), fish, eggs, turkey
Zinc-rich foods: oysters, beef, pumpkin seeds, chickpeas
Iron-rich foods (iron deficiency worsens hypothyroidism)
Vitamin D-rich foods
Tyrosine-rich foods: chicken, turkey, fish, dairy, almonds (tyrosine is needed for thyroid hormone production)
Anti-inflammatory foods
Adequate protein
Fiber (combat constipation)

Foods to AVOID:

Excessive raw goitrogenic foods: raw broccoli, cauliflower, cabbage, kale, Brussels sprouts (cooking reduces goitrogenic effect by 50-80%)
Excessive soy (may interfere with thyroid medication)
Gluten (if celiac or sensitive - Hashimoto's associated with celiac)
Highly processed foods
Excessive sugar

Special Instructions for Women:

Thyroid medication: take on empty stomach, 30-60 minutes before eating
Don't take with calcium, iron, or fiber supplements (wait 4 hours)
TSH monitoring before and during pregnancy
Increase levothyroxine dose by 25-50% during pregnancy
Screen for postpartum thyroiditis
Annual thyroid function testing


Hyperthyroidism in Women (Expanded)
Additional Symptoms in Women:

Light or absent periods
Difficulty conceiving
Increased miscarriage risk
Rapid heartbeat
Weight loss despite increased appetite
Anxiety, irritability
Tremors
Sweating
Heat intolerance

Foods to EAT:

Cruciferous vegetables (may help reduce thyroid hormone production)
Calcium and vitamin D-rich foods (protect bones)
Adequate calories (hyperthyroidism increases metabolic rate)
High-calorie foods if losing weight
Anti-inflammatory foods
Adequate protein

Foods to AVOID:

Excessive iodine: seaweed, kelp supplements, iodized salt in excess
Caffeine (worsens heart palpitations, anxiety)
Excessive sugar


Hashimoto's Thyroiditis in Women (Expanded)
What It Is:
The most common cause of hypothyroidism in developed countries. Autoimmune condition where antibodies (TPO, thyroglobulin) attack the thyroid.


Foods to EAT:

Selenium-rich foods (shown to reduce TPO antibodies): Brazil nuts (1-2/day)
Anti-inflammatory diet
Omega-3 fatty acids
Vitamin D-rich foods
Zinc-rich foods
Probiotic-rich foods
Turmeric and ginger
Gluten-free diet (may reduce antibodies in some women)

Foods to AVOID:

Gluten (strong association with Hashimoto's; trial of 3-6 months gluten-free)
Excessive iodine
Highly processed foods
Sugar and refined carbohydrates
Excessive soy
Raw cruciferous in excess

Supplements:

Supplement	Dose	Purpose
Selenium	200 mcg/day	Reduce TPO antibodies
Vitamin D	2,000-4,000 IU/day	Immune modulation
Zinc	15-30 mg/day	Thyroid function
Omega-3	1,000-2,000 mg/day	Anti-inflammatory
Myo-inositol	600 mg/day (with selenium)	May reduce TSH
Iron	If deficient	Thyroid function
B12	If deficient	Common deficiency with Hashimoto's


44. HORMONAL IMBALANCES IN WOMEN

Estrogen Dominance
Symptoms: Weight gain (hips, thighs), bloating, breast tenderness, heavy periods, mood swings, fibroids, endometriosis


Foods to EAT:

Cruciferous vegetables (DIM - diindolylmethane, helps metabolize estrogen)
Ground flaxseeds (lignans modulate estrogen)
Fiber-rich foods (help excrete excess estrogen)
Liver-supporting foods: beets, artichokes, lemon, dandelion greens
Probiotic-rich foods
Rosemary

Foods to AVOID:

Alcohol (increases estrogen)
Conventionally raised meat and dairy (hormones)
Plastic-packaged foods heated in microwave
Soy supplements (concentrated)
Excessive body fat (produces estrogen)
Pesticide-laden produce (xenoestrogens)


Low Progesterone
Symptoms: Short cycles, spotting before period, difficulty conceiving, miscarriage, anxiety, insomnia, breast tenderness


Foods to EAT:

Vitamin B6-rich foods: chickpeas, bananas, potatoes, fish
Zinc-rich foods: oysters, pumpkin seeds, beef
Vitamin C-rich foods
Magnesium-rich foods
Healthy fats (cholesterol is precursor to progesterone)
Adequate calories


High Testosterone (in Women)
Symptoms: Acne, excess hair growth (hirsutism), hair loss, irregular periods, deepened voice


Foods to EAT:

Spearmint tea (2 cups/day - shown to reduce free testosterone)
Flaxseeds (ground)
Soy foods (phytoestrogens may counteract androgens)
Reishi mushroom (anti-androgenic)
Green tea
Licorice root tea (in moderation)


Adrenal Fatigue / HPA Axis Dysfunction
Symptoms: Chronic fatigue, difficulty waking, cravings for salt/sugar, difficulty handling stress, brain fog, weakened immunity


Foods to EAT:

Balanced meals at regular intervals
Protein with every meal
Complex carbohydrates
Healthy fats
Vitamin C-rich foods (adrenals use vitamin C)
B-vitamin rich foods
Magnesium-rich foods
Avoid skipping meals
Salt (if craving salt - adrenals need sodium)

Foods to AVOID:

Excessive caffeine (stresses adrenals)
Excessive sugar (blood sugar roller coaster)
Alcohol
Processed foods
Skipping meals


45. FEMALE SEXUAL DYSFUNCTION

Types
Low libido (hypoactive sexual desire)
Arousal disorder
Orgasmic disorder
Sexual pain disorder (dyspareunia)

Foods to EAT
Dark chocolate (phenylethylamine, serotonin)
Watermelon (citrulline - improves blood flow)
Oysters (zinc - testosterone production)
Avocado (healthy fats, B vitamins)
Fatty fish (omega-3 - blood flow)
Nuts: almonds, walnuts (zinc, omega-3)
Seeds: pumpkin seeds, flaxseeds
Berries (antioxidants)
Beets (nitrates - blood flow)
Saffron (some evidence for desire)
Maca root (traditional aphrodisiac)
Fenugreek (may increase libido)
Ginseng

Foods to AVOID
Excessive alcohol (decreases arousal)
Excessive processed foods
Trans fats (impair blood flow)
Excessive sugar

Instructions
Address underlying medical conditions
Hormone evaluation
Psychological counseling
Couples therapy
Pelvic floor physical therapy
Vaginal moisturizers/lubricants
Medications if appropriate (flibanserin, bremelanotide)
Stress management
Adequate sleep
Regular exercise (improves body image, blood flow, energy)


46. WEIGHT MANAGEMENT ACROSS WOMEN'S LIFE STAGES

Adolescence
Focus on balanced nutrition for growth
Adequate calcium and iron
Avoid restrictive dieting
Healthy body image education
Regular physical activity

Reproductive Years
Maintain healthy weight for fertility
Folate-rich foods
Iron-rich foods
Balanced macronutrients
Regular exercise

Pregnancy
Appropriate weight gain (see Section 20)
Nutrient-dense foods
Don't diet during pregnancy

Postpartum
Gradual weight loss (1 lb/week after 6 weeks)
Don't restrict calories if breastfeeding
Focus on nutrient density
Regular exercise when cleared

Perimenopause/Menopause
Adjust calorie intake (metabolism slowing)
Increase protein (preserve muscle mass)
Weight-bearing exercise
Reduce refined carbohydrates
Monitor portion sizes

Postmenopause
Maintain muscle mass through resistance training
Heart-healthy diet
Bone-protective nutrients
Regular physical activity
Social eating and cooking


47. NUTRITIONAL NEEDS BY LIFE STAGE

Girls Ages 9-13 (Pre-Adolescent)
Nutrient	Daily Need	Key Foods
Calories	1,400-2,200	Balanced meals
Calcium	1,300 mg	Dairy, fortified foods
Iron	8 mg	Lean meats, beans
Vitamin D	600 IU	Fortified milk, sunlight
Folate	400 mcg	Leafy greens, beans

Girls Ages 14-18 (Adolescent)
Nutrient	Daily Need	Key Foods
Calories	1,800-2,400	Balanced meals
Calcium	1,300 mg	Dairy, fortified foods
Iron	15 mg	Red meat, beans, spinach
Vitamin D	600 IU	Fatty fish, fortified foods
Folate	400 mcg	Leafy greens
Menstruation increases iron needs		

Women Ages 19-30
Nutrient	Daily Need	Key Foods
Calories	1,800-2,400	Balanced meals
Calcium	1,000 mg	Dairy, leafy greens
Iron	18 mg	Red meat, beans, spinach
Folate	400 mcg	Leafy greens, fortified foods
Vitamin D	600 IU	Fatty fish, sunlight

Women Ages 31-50
Nutrient	Daily Need	Key Foods
Calories	1,800-2,200	Balanced meals
Calcium	1,000 mg	Dairy, fortified foods
Iron	18 mg (8 mg if postmenopausal)	Red meat, beans
Folate	400 mcg	Leafy greens
Vitamin D	600 IU	Fatty fish, sunlight

Pregnant Women
Nutrient	Daily Need	Key Foods
Calories	+345 extra	Nutrient-dense foods
Calcium	1,000 mg	Dairy, fortified foods
Iron	27 mg	Red meat, beans, spinach
Folate	600 mcg	Leafy greens, fortified foods
Vitamin D	600 IU	Fatty fish, sunlight
DHA	200-300 mg	Fatty fish, supplements
Iodine	220 mcg	Iodized salt, dairy
Choline	450 mg	Eggs, liver

Breastfeeding Women
Nutrient	Daily Need	Key Foods
Calories	+330-400 extra	Nutrient-dense foods
Calcium	1,000 mg	Dairy, fortified foods
Iron	9 mg	Red meat, beans
Folate	500 mcg	Leafy greens
Vitamin D	600 IU	Fatty fish, sunlight
DHA	200-300 mg	Fatty fish
Iodine	290 mcg	Iodized salt, dairy
Choline	550 mg	Eggs, liver
Vitamin C	120 mg	Citrus, bell peppers

Women Ages 51+ (Postmenopausal)
Nutrient	Daily Need	Key Foods
Calories	1,600-2,200	Nutrient-dense foods
Calcium	1,200 mg	Dairy, fortified foods
Iron	8 mg	Lean meats, beans
Vitamin D	800-2,000 IU	Fatty fish, supplements
Vitamin B12	2.4 mcg	Meat, fish, fortified foods
Protein	1.0-1.2 g/kg	Lean meats, fish, legumes
Fiber	21 g	Whole grains, vegetables


48. QUICK REFERENCE TABLES

Women's Health Condition Diet Summary

Condition	Key Foods to Eat	Key Foods to Avoid	Key Nutrients
PCOS	Low-GI foods, omega-3, fiber, cruciferous vegetables	Sugar, refined carbs, processed foods	Inositol, Vitamin D, Omega-3
PCOD	Fresh fruits/veggies, whole grains, lean protein	Junk food, excessive sugar	Balanced nutrition
Endometriosis	Anti-inflammatory foods, omega-3, cruciferous vegetables	Red meat, alcohol, trans fats	Omega-3, NAC, Vitamin D
Uterine Fibroids	Cruciferous vegetables, fiber, green tea	Red meat, alcohol, high-fat dairy	Vitamin D, Fiber
Dysmenorrhea	Magnesium-rich foods, omega-3, ginger	Caffeine, excessive salt, fried foods	Magnesium, Omega-3, B1
Heavy Periods	Iron-rich foods, vitamin C, vitamin K	Caffeine with iron meals, alcohol	Iron, Folate, B12
PMS	Calcium, complex carbs, B6, magnesium	Salt, caffeine, sugar, alcohol	Calcium, Magnesium, B6
PMDD	Same as PMS + omega-3, tryptophan	Alcohol, caffeine	Calcium, SSRIs
Pregnancy	Folate, iron, calcium, DHA, protein	Mercury fish, alcohol, raw foods	Folate, Iron, DHA, Calcium
Postpartum	Protein, iron, omega-3, calcium	Alcohol, excessive caffeine	Iron, DHA, Vitamin D
Breastfeeding	Galactagogues, protein, omega-3, water	Alcohol, excessive caffeine	DHA, Calcium, Iodine
Postpartum Depression	Omega-3, tryptophan, vitamin D, probiotics	Alcohol, excessive caffeine, sugar	Omega-3, Vitamin D, B12
Menopause	Phytoestrogens, calcium, vitamin D, omega-3	Spicy foods, caffeine, alcohol	Calcium, Vitamin D, Magnesium
Osteoporosis	Calcium, vitamin D, vitamin K, prunes	Excessive sodium, caffeine, alcohol	Calcium, Vitamin D, K2
Anemia	Iron-rich + vitamin C, folate, B12	Tea/coffee with iron meals	Iron, Folate, B12
Hypothyroidism	Iodine, selenium, zinc	Excessive raw goitrogens, soy	Iodine, Selenium
Infertility	Antioxidants, omega-3, folate, full-fat dairy	Trans fats, alcohol, excessive caffeine	Folate, CoQ10, Vitamin D
UTIs	Water, cranberry, probiotics, vitamin C	Sugar, alcohol, caffeine	Water, Probiotics
Yeast Infection	Probiotics, garlic, low-sugar diet	Sugar, alcohol, yeast-containing foods	Probiotics
Breast Cancer	Cruciferous vegetables, berries, omega-3, fiber	Alcohol, processed meats, excessive fat	Fiber, Omega-3, Antioxidants
Cervical Health	Folate, vitamin C, cruciferous vegetables	Smoking, alcohol	Folate, Vitamin C, A
Vaginal Dryness	Phytoestrogens, omega-3, vitamin E	Excessive caffeine, alcohol	Vitamin E, Omega-3
Low Libido	Dark chocolate, oysters, watermelon, avocado	Excessive alcohol, processed foods	Zinc, Omega-3
Mastitis	Anti-inflammatory foods, probiotics, vitamin C	Excessive sugar, alcohol	Probiotics, Vitamin C


COMPREHENSIVE HEALTH CONDITIONS & DIETARY MANAGEMENT GUIDE — VOLUME 2
Complete Dietary Instructions, Foods to Eat, Foods to Avoid, and Clinical Management for Every Major Disease Category Not Covered Previously

Compiled from WHO Global Health Data , CDC Chronic Disease Reports , Medical Nutrition Therapy Guidelines , Dietary Guidelines for Americans 2025–2030 , National Institutes of Health , and Clinical Nutrition Research

1. INFECTIOUS DISEASES

A. COMMON COLD & INFLUENZA

What It Is:
Viral infections of the upper respiratory tract. The common cold is caused by rhinoviruses; influenza is caused by influenza viruses.


Foods to EAT:

Water and clear fluids: at least 8-10 glasses/day (prevents dehydration)
Chicken soup (proven to have anti-inflammatory properties, clears nasal congestion)
Bone broth (electrolytes, minerals, easy to digest)
Citrus fruits: oranges, lemons, grapefruit (vitamin C)
Bell peppers (more vitamin C than oranges)
Strawberries, kiwi (vitamin C)
Garlic (allicin has antiviral and antibacterial properties)
Ginger (anti-inflammatory, soothes sore throat)
Honey (1-2 tablespoons - soothes cough, antimicrobial; NOT for children under 1)
Turmeric (anti-inflammatory)
Green tea (catechins, L-theanine)
Probiotic-rich foods: yogurt, kefir (support immune system)
Zinc-rich foods: oysters, pumpkin seeds, beef, chickpeas (zinc shortens cold duration)
Elderberry (shown to reduce cold duration and severity)
Warm liquids: herbal teas, warm water with lemon and honey
Spicy foods: chili peppers, horseradish (clear congestion)
BRAT foods if nauseous: bananas, rice, applesauce, toast
Popsicles (hydration, soothing sore throat)

Foods to AVOID:

Sugar (suppresses immune function for several hours after consumption)
Alcohol (dehydrating, suppresses immune system)
Caffeine (dehydrating)
Dairy (may thicken mucus in some people - though evidence is debated)
Fried and greasy foods (hard to digest, inflammatory)
Processed foods
Refined carbohydrates
Very cold foods (may irritate throat)
Excessive salt

Supplements:

Supplement	Dose	Evidence
Vitamin C	1,000-2,000 mg/day	May reduce cold duration by 8% in adults
Zinc (lozenges)	75 mg/day within 24 hours of onset	Reduces cold duration by 1-2 days
Elderberry	600-900 mg/day	May reduce cold duration and severity
Vitamin D	2,000-4,000 IU/day (prevention)	Supports immune function
Echinacea	300-500 mg 3x/day	Mixed evidence; may help if taken early
Probiotics	Multi-strain	May reduce frequency of colds

Instructions:

Rest as much as possible
Stay hydrated
Gargle with warm salt water for sore throat
Use a humidifier
Wash hands frequently
Cover coughs and sneezes
Stay home to prevent spreading
See doctor if symptoms last >10 days, high fever >103°F, difficulty breathing
Annual flu vaccine recommended
Antiviral medications (Tamiflu) if started within 48 hours of flu symptoms


B. COVID-19

What It Is:
Respiratory illness caused by SARS-CoV-2 virus. Can range from mild to severe.


Foods to EAT (During Infection):

Hydrating fluids: water, herbal tea, broth, electrolyte drinks
Anti-inflammatory foods: turmeric, ginger, garlic, berries
Vitamin C-rich foods: citrus, bell peppers, kiwi, strawberries
Vitamin D-rich foods: fatty fish, eggs, fortified milk
Zinc-rich foods: oysters, pumpkin seeds, beef, lentils
Protein-rich foods (tissue repair, immune function): eggs, chicken, fish, legumes
Probiotic-rich foods: yogurt, kefir, sauerkraut
Omega-3 fatty acids: fatty fish, walnuts
Small, frequent meals if appetite is poor
Soft, easy-to-digest foods if sore throat
Warm soups and broths

Foods to AVOID:

Alcohol
Excessive sugar
Processed foods
Dehydrating beverages
Inflammatory foods

Supplements:

Supplement	Dose	Evidence
Vitamin D	2,000-4,000 IU/day	Low vitamin D associated with worse outcomes
Vitamin C	500-1,000 mg 2x/day	Antioxidant, immune support
Zinc	30-50 mg/day	Antiviral properties
Quercetin	500 mg 2x/day	Anti-inflammatory, may enhance zinc absorption
Melatonin	3-10 mg at bedtime	Anti-inflammatory, immune modulation
Probiotics	Multi-strain	Gut-lung axis, immune support

Instructions:

Isolate per public health guidelines
Monitor oxygen levels (pulse oximeter)
Seek emergency care if: difficulty breathing, chest pain, confusion, inability to stay awake, blue lips/face
Rest and hydration are critical
Antiviral medications if prescribed (Paxlovid within 5 days of symptom onset)
Post-COVID recovery: gradual return to activity, monitor for long COVID symptoms
Long COVID nutrition: anti-inflammatory diet, adequate protein, hydration, rest


C. TUBERCULOSIS (TB)

What It Is:
A bacterial infection caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, primarily affecting the lungs.


Foods to EAT:

HIGH protein diet: eggs, milk, meat, fish, legumes, paneer (tissue repair, immune function)
HIGH calorie diet (TB increases metabolic demands)
Vitamin A-rich foods: sweet potatoes, carrots, liver, leafy greens (supports lung immunity)
Vitamin C-rich foods: citrus, guava, amla, bell peppers
Vitamin D-rich foods: fatty fish, eggs, sunlight exposure (vitamin D deficiency increases TB risk)
Vitamin B6-rich foods: chicken, potatoes, bananas (isoniazid depletes B6)
Zinc-rich foods: oysters, pumpkin seeds, meat
Selenium-rich foods: Brazil nuts, fish
Iron-rich foods (if anemic)
Calcium-rich foods
Omega-3 fatty acids
Garlic and onion (antimicrobial)
Small, frequent meals (if appetite poor)
Plenty of water

Foods to AVOID:

Alcohol (interacts with TB medications, damages liver)
Tobacco (worsens lung damage, reduces treatment effectiveness)
Excessive caffeine (may interfere with medications)
High-sodium foods (if on certain medications)
Grapefruit (interacts with some TB medications)
Processed foods
Excessive sugar
Pickled and smoked foods (contain tyramine if on linezolid)
Aged cheeses (if on linezolid - MAO inhibitor interaction)

Instructions:

Complete FULL course of antibiotics (6-9 months minimum; drug-resistant TB: 18-24 months)
NEVER stop medications early (causes drug resistance)
Take medications on empty stomach (unless otherwise directed)
Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) supplement if on isoniazid (prevent neuropathy)
Regular liver function monitoring (TB medications can damage liver)
Isolation precautions during infectious phase
Contact tracing
Regular sputum tests to confirm clearance
Adequate nutrition is CRITICAL for recovery
Weight monitoring
Mental health support (long treatment course)
DOT (Directly Observed Therapy) if available


D. HIV/AIDS (EXPANDED)

What It Is:
Human Immunodeficiency Virus attacks the immune system, specifically CD4 cells. Without treatment, can progress to Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome (AIDS).


Foods to EAT:

HIGH protein: 1.0-1.5 g/kg/day (prevent muscle wasting)
HIGH calorie: 10-20% above normal needs (increased metabolic demands)
Safe, well-cooked foods (weakened immune system means higher infection risk)
Pasteurized dairy products
Thoroughly washed fruits and vegetables
Well-cooked meat, fish, eggs (no raw/rare)
Whole grains
Healthy fats: olive oil, avocado, nuts
Colorful fruits and vegetables (antioxidants)
Probiotic-rich foods
Vitamin A-rich foods (immune function)
Vitamin C-rich foods
Zinc-rich foods
Selenium-rich foods
Iron-rich foods (if anemic)
Small, frequent meals (if appetite poor)
Adequate hydration

Foods to AVOID:

Raw or undercooked meat, fish, eggs
Unpasteurized dairy and juices
Raw sprouts (alfalfa, bean, clover)
Soft cheeses from unpasteurized milk
Contaminated water
Excessive alcohol (interacts with ARV medications, liver damage)
Grapefruit (interacts with some ARV medications)
Excessive vitamin A supplements (can be toxic)
Street food or foods from questionable sources
Deli meats unless heated to steaming
Leftovers stored too long
High-mercury fish

Food Safety Rules (CRITICAL for Immunocompromised):

Wash hands before preparing food
Cook all meats to proper internal temperatures
Wash all produce thoroughly
Use separate cutting boards for raw meat and produce
Refrigerate leftovers within 2 hours
Consume leftovers within 24-48 hours
Avoid cross-contamination
Drink only purified/filtered water
Avoid ice from questionable sources

Supplements:

Supplement	Dose	Purpose
Multivitamin	Daily	Overall nutrition
Vitamin D	2,000-4,000 IU	Immune function
Vitamin B12	1,000 mcg	Common deficiency in HIV
Zinc	12-15 mg	Immune function (don't exceed - excess suppresses immunity)
Selenium	200 mcg	May slow HIV progression
Vitamin C	500-1,000 mg	Antioxidant
Omega-3	1,000-2,000 mg	Anti-inflammatory
Probiotics	Multi-strain	Gut health

Instructions:

Take antiretroviral therapy (ART) consistently - NEVER miss doses
Maintain undetectable viral load (Undetectable = Untransmittable, U=U)
Regular CD4 count and viral load monitoring
Practice STRICT food safety
Regular screenings: TB, hepatitis, STIs, cancer
Vaccinations: flu, pneumococcal, hepatitis A & B, HPV (avoid live vaccines if CD4 <200)
Dental care every 6 months
Bone density monitoring
Cardiovascular monitoring
Mental health support
Nutritional support if wasting syndrome develops
Manage medication side effects (lipodystrophy, nausea, diarrhea)
Disclosure and support groups


E. DENGUE FEVER

What It Is:
A mosquito-borne viral infection causing high fever, severe headache, pain behind the eyes, joint and muscle pain, rash, and in severe cases, hemorrhagic fever.


Foods to EAT:

PLENTY of fluids: water, coconut water, ORS (Oral Rehydration Solution), fruit juices
Papaya leaf juice (shown to increase platelet count)
Pomegranate juice (iron, increases platelets)
Kiwi fruit (may help increase platelet count)
Beetroot juice (increases hemoglobin and platelets)
Carrot juice (vitamin A, immune support)
Neem leaf juice (traditional remedy)
Protein-rich foods: eggs, chicken soup, dal/lentil soup
Vitamin C-rich foods: citrus, guava, amla
Vitamin K-rich foods: leafy greens (blood clotting)
Iron-rich foods
Easy-to-digest foods: khichdi, porridge, clear soups
Small, frequent meals
Oral rehydration is CRITICAL

Foods to AVOID:

Oily and spicy foods (hard to digest)
Caffeinated beverages (dehydrating)
Alcohol (dehydrating, liver stress)
Red-colored foods/drinks (confuse assessment of bleeding)
Raw foods (infection risk)
Processed foods
High-fat foods
Aspirin and NSAIDs (increase bleeding risk; use paracetamol/acetaminophen only)

Instructions:

Monitor platelet count regularly
Hospitalization if platelet count drops below 50,000 or signs of dengue hemorrhagic fever
Paracetamol/acetaminophen ONLY for fever (NO aspirin, NO ibuprofen)
Watch for warning signs: severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, bleeding gums/nose, blood in vomit/stool, restlessness, rapid breathing
Rest is essential
Mosquito prevention: nets, repellent, eliminate standing water
Most cases resolve in 1-2 weeks
Full recovery may take several weeks


F. TYPHOID FEVER

What It Is:
A bacterial infection caused by Salmonella typhi, spread through contaminated food and water.


Foods to EAT:

High-calorie, high-protein diet (body needs energy to fight infection)
Soft, bland, easily digestible foods
Porridge, khichdi, well-cooked rice
Boiled vegetables
Clear soups and broths
Bananas, applesauce (easy to digest)
Yogurt with live cultures (probiotics)
Eggs (soft-boiled or scrambled)
Well-cooked lean meat
Coconut water
Oral rehydration solution
Small, frequent meals (5-6 per day)
Adequate fluids (3-4 liters/day)

Foods to AVOID:

HIGH-FIBER foods during acute phase (raw vegetables, whole grains, bran) - can cause intestinal perforation
Oily, greasy, fried foods
Spicy foods
Raw vegetables and salads
Unpeeled fruits
Street food
Unpasteurized dairy
Alcohol
Caffeine
Gas-producing foods: cabbage, cauliflower, beans (during acute phase)
Seeds and nuts (during acute phase)
Popcorn
Tough, fibrous meats

Instructions:

Complete full course of antibiotics (typically 10-14 days)
STRICT hygiene: wash hands frequently
Drink only boiled or purified water
Eat only freshly cooked, hot food
Isolation precautions
Monitor for complications: intestinal perforation, bleeding
Blood culture confirmation
Widal test for diagnosis
