Vaccination for prevention (oral or injectable)
Gradual reintroduction of normal diet after recovery


G. MALARIA

What It Is:
A life-threatening parasitic disease transmitted through infected Anopheles mosquito bites.


Foods to EAT:

High-protein, high-calorie diet
Vitamin A-rich foods: sweet potatoes, carrots, liver (supports immunity)
Vitamin C-rich foods: citrus, guava, amla
Iron-rich foods (malaria causes anemia)
Folate-rich foods (malaria destroys red blood cells)
Antioxidant-rich foods: berries, colorful vegetables
Ginger (anti-nausea, anti-inflammatory)
Turmeric (anti-inflammatory)
Coconut water (hydration, electrolytes)
Oral rehydration solution
Light, easily digestible foods
Small, frequent meals
Plenty of fluids

Foods to AVOID:

Oily and heavy foods
Alcohol (interacts with antimalarials, liver stress)
Spicy foods
Processed foods
Excessive sugar
Grapefruit (interacts with some antimalarials)

Instructions:

Complete full course of antimalarial medication
Bed nets and insect repellent for prevention
Chemoprophylaxis if traveling to endemic areas
Monitor for severe malaria: confusion, seizures, difficulty breathing
Regular blood tests to confirm parasite clearance
Iron supplementation if anemic
Rest and recovery time
Report to public health authorities


2. TROPICAL & PARASITIC DISEASES

A. CHOLERA

What It Is:
An acute diarrheal infection caused by Vibrio cholerae, spread through contaminated water and food.


Foods to EAT:

ORAL REHYDRATION SOLUTION (ORS) — THE MOST IMPORTANT TREATMENT
Recipe: 1 liter boiled water + 6 teaspoons sugar + 1/2 teaspoon salt
Rice-based ORS
Coconut water
Clear soups and broths
Mashed bananas
Rice porridge
Boiled potatoes
Well-cooked vegetables
Yogurt (probiotics)
Zinc supplements (reduce diarrhea duration in children)

Foods to AVOID:

Raw foods
Uncooked vegetables and salads
Street food
Unpurified water
Dairy (during acute phase, except yogurt)
High-fiber foods (during acute phase)
Spicy foods
Alcohol

Instructions:

REHYDRATION IS LIFE-SAVING
Hospitalization for severe cases (IV fluids)
Antibiotics for severe cases (doxycycline, azithromycin)
Zinc supplements for children
Sanitation and clean water are critical for prevention
Oral cholera vaccine for prevention in endemic areas
Report to public health authorities


B. AMEBIASIS (ENTAMOEBA HISTOLYTICA)

What It Is:
A parasitic infection of the intestines causing amebic dysentery.


Foods to EAT:

Well-cooked foods
Boiled/purified water
Probiotic-rich foods: yogurt, kefir
Coconut water
BRAT diet during acute phase: bananas, rice, applesauce, toast
Oral rehydration solution
Small, frequent meals
Lean protein
Well-cooked vegetables

Foods to AVOID:

Raw foods
Uncooked vegetables
Street food
Unpurified water
Spicy foods
Alcohol
High-fiber foods during acute phase

Instructions:

Antiparasitic medication as prescribed (metronidazole, tinidazole)
Complete full course of treatment
Stool testing to confirm clearance
Strict hygiene and sanitation
Avoid reinfection from contaminated water


C. WORM INFECTIONS (HELMINTHIASIS)

Types:

Roundworms (Ascaris)
Hookworms
Tapeworms
Pinworms
Whipworms
Threadworms

Foods to EAT:

High-protein diet (worms cause malnutrition and anemia)
Iron-rich foods (hookworms cause iron-deficiency anemia)
Vitamin A-rich foods
Vitamin C-rich foods
Zinc-rich foods
Garlic (antiparasitic properties)
Pumpkin seeds (contain cucurbitin - antiparasitic)
Papaya seeds (contain carpaine - antiparasitic)
Pineapple (bromelain)
Carrots
Coconut (antiparasitic)
Probiotic-rich foods
Turmeric
Neem leaves (traditional)
Well-cooked foods
Clean water

Foods to AVOID:

Raw or undercooked meat (tapeworms)
Raw fish (tapeworms)
Unwashed produce
Contaminated water
Walking barefoot in endemic areas (hookworms)

Instructions:

Deworming medication: albendazole or mebendazole (as prescribed)
Regular deworming in endemic areas (every 6-12 months)
Proper hygiene: handwashing, nail trimming
Cook meat thoroughly
Wash all produce
Wear shoes in endemic areas
Clean water supply
Stool testing to identify worm type
Iron supplementation if anemic


D. LEPTOSPIROSIS

What It Is:
A bacterial infection spread through contact with water contaminated by infected animal urine.


Foods to EAT:

High-protein, nutritious diet
Vitamin C-rich foods
Vitamin K-rich foods (liver involvement)
Adequate hydration
Liver-supporting foods: beets, leafy greens, garlic
Small, frequent meals

Foods to AVOID:

Alcohol (liver and kidney stress)
Contaminated water
Excessive protein (if kidney involvement)
Processed foods

Instructions:

Antibiotics: doxycycline or penicillin
Avoid contact with flood water
Wear protective footwear
Rodent control
Monitor liver and kidney function


3. CHRONIC FATIGUE SYNDROME & FIBROMYALGIA

A. CHRONIC FATIGUE SYNDROME (CFS/ME)

What It Is:
A complex, chronic condition characterized by extreme fatigue that doesn't improve with rest and worsens with physical or mental activity. Also called Myalgic Encephalomyelitis (ME).


Symptoms:

Extreme fatigue lasting 6+ months
Post-exertional malaise (PEM) - worsening after activity
Unrefreshing sleep
Cognitive dysfunction ("brain fog")
Muscle pain
Joint pain
Headaches
Sore throat
Tender lymph nodes

Foods to EAT:

Anti-inflammatory foods: fatty fish, olive oil, berries, leafy greens
Complex carbohydrates: whole grains, sweet potatoes (steady energy)
Lean protein at every meal (sustained energy)
Magnesium-rich foods: dark chocolate, spinach, almonds, avocado (muscle relaxation, energy)
B-vitamin rich foods: whole grains, eggs, leafy greens (energy metabolism)
CoQ10-rich foods: organ meats, sardines, broccoli (mitochondrial function)
Iron-rich foods (fatigue often worsened by iron deficiency)
Vitamin D-rich foods (low vitamin D common in CFS)
Probiotic-rich foods (gut-brain axis)
Adequate hydration
Small, frequent meals (prevent energy crashes)
Anti-oxidant rich foods
Ginger and turmeric (anti-inflammatory)

Foods to AVOID:

Sugar and refined carbohydrates (energy crashes)
Processed foods
Excessive caffeine (disrupts sleep, causes crashes)
Alcohol
Trans fats
Artificial additives
Excessive saturated fat
Gluten (in some individuals)
Dairy (in some individuals)
MSG

Supplements:

Supplement	Dose	Purpose
CoQ10	200-400 mg/day	Mitochondrial function
Magnesium	300-400 mg/day	Muscle relaxation, energy
Vitamin D	2,000-4,000 IU/day	Immune function, energy
B-complex	Daily	Energy metabolism
Omega-3	1,000-2,000 mg/day	Anti-inflammatory
D-Ribose	5 g 3x/day	Cellular energy
Acetyl-L-Carnitine	500-1,500 mg/day	Mitochondrial function
Iron	If deficient	Oxygen transport
Probiotics	Multi-strain	Gut health
NADH	10-20 mg/day	Energy production

Instructions:

Pacing: balance activity and rest to avoid PEM
Graded exercise therapy (carefully - can worsen symptoms if too aggressive)
Sleep hygiene: consistent schedule, dark room, cool temperature
Cognitive behavioral therapy
Stress management
Pain management
No known cure; focus on symptom management
Regular medical follow-up
Support groups
Disability accommodations if needed


B. FIBROMYALGIA

What It Is:
A chronic condition causing widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, sleep problems, and cognitive difficulties.


Symptoms:

Widespread pain (all four quadrants of body)
Fatigue
Sleep disturbances
Cognitive difficulties ("fibro fog")
Headaches
Irritable bowel syndrome (common comorbidity)
Depression and anxiety
Numbness and tingling
Morning stiffness
Painful menstrual periods

Foods to EAT:

Anti-inflammatory diet (Mediterranean-style)
Fatty fish: salmon, sardines, mackerel (omega-3)
Extra virgin olive oil
Colorful fruits and vegetables (antioxidants)
Magnesium-rich foods: dark chocolate, spinach, almonds, avocado (muscle relaxation)
Vitamin D-rich foods (low vitamin D common in fibromyalgia)
Lean proteins
Whole grains
Turmeric and ginger
Tart cherry juice (anti-inflammatory, improves sleep)
Fermented foods: yogurt, kefir, kimchi (gut health - IBS common comorbidity)
Anti-inflammatory herbs: rosemary, basil, oregano
Adequate water
Small, frequent meals

Foods to AVOID:

Sugar and refined carbohydrates (worsen pain and inflammation)
Processed foods
MSG (may trigger symptoms)
Aspartame (may trigger symptoms)
Caffeine (disrupts sleep)
Alcohol
Gluten (in some individuals)
Nightshade vegetables (in some individuals): tomatoes, peppers, eggplant, potatoes
Fried foods
Red meat in excess
Dairy (in some individuals)
Excessive salt

Supplements:

Supplement	Dose	Purpose
Vitamin D	2,000-4,000 IU/day	Pain, immune function
Magnesium	300-500 mg/day	Muscle relaxation, sleep
Omega-3	1,000-2,000 mg/day	Anti-inflammatory
CoQ10	200-300 mg/day	Energy
B-complex	Daily	Energy, nerve function
SAMe	400-800 mg/day	Mood, pain
5-HTP	100-300 mg/day	Sleep, mood
Melatonin	1-5 mg at bedtime	Sleep
Probiotics	Multi-strain	Gut health
Turmeric/Curcumin	500-1,000 mg/day	Anti-inflammatory

Instructions:

Regular low-impact exercise: swimming, walking, yoga, tai chi
Warm water therapy (aquatic exercise)
Sleep hygiene
Stress management: meditation, deep breathing
Cognitive behavioral therapy
Medications: pregabalin (Lyrica), duloxetine (Cymbalta), milnacipran (Savella)
Pain management
Massage therapy
Acupuncture (some evidence)
Occupational therapy
Support groups
Pacing activities
Heat therapy for pain relief


4. SLEEP DISORDERS

A. INSOMNIA

What It Is:
Difficulty falling asleep, staying asleep, or waking too early and not being able to return to sleep.


Foods to EAT:

Tryptophan-rich foods: turkey, chicken, eggs, cheese, nuts, seeds (precursor to serotonin and melatonin)
Melatonin-containing foods: tart cherries, grapes, tomatoes, walnuts, pistachios
Magnesium-rich foods: dark chocolate, almonds, spinach, avocado, pumpkin seeds (muscle relaxation, GABA production)
Complex carbohydrates: whole grains, sweet potatoes (help tryptophan cross blood-brain barrier)
Kiwi fruit (2 kiwis 1 hour before bed shown to improve sleep)
Warm milk (tryptophan, calcium - psychological comfort)
Chamomile tea (apigenin binds to GABA receptors)
Valerian root tea
Passionflower tea
Lavender tea
Banana (tryptophan, magnesium, potassium)
Fatty fish (omega-3 + vitamin D - shown to improve sleep quality)
Tart cherry juice (natural melatonin source)
Honey (small amount - allows tryptophan to enter brain more easily)
Almonds and walnuts (melatonin, magnesium)

Foods to AVOID:

Caffeine: coffee, tea, chocolate, energy drinks (avoid after 2 PM; half-life 5-6 hours)
Alcohol (disrupts sleep architecture, reduces REM sleep)
Spicy foods (can cause heartburn, disrupt sleep)
High-fat foods (harder to digest, disrupt sleep)
Large meals close to bedtime (finish eating 2-3 hours before bed)
Sugar and refined carbohydrates (blood sugar fluctuations)
Excessive fluids close to bedtime (frequent urination)
Aged cheese and cured meats (tyramine - stimulating)
Tomatoes and tomato sauce (acid reflux)
Dark chocolate (caffeine)
Nicotine (stimulant)
Watermelon (diuretic)

Supplements:

Supplement	Dose	Purpose
Melatonin	0.5-5 mg, 30 min before bed	Sleep onset
Magnesium glycinate	300-400 mg at bedtime	Muscle relaxation, GABA
L-Theanine	200-400 mg at bedtime	Relaxation, alpha brain waves
Glycine	3 g before bed	Sleep quality
Valerian root	300-600 mg, 30 min before bed	Sleep onset
GABA	100-300 mg at bedtime	Calming
5-HTP	100-300 mg at bedtime	Serotonin/melatonin production
Passionflower	500 mg at bedtime	Sleep quality
Tart cherry extract	500 mg 2x/day	Natural melatonin
Ashwagandha	300-600 mg at bedtime	Stress, sleep

Instructions:

Consistent sleep schedule (same bedtime and wake time, even weekends)
Create a sleep-conducive environment: dark, cool (65-68°F), quiet
No screens 1 hour before bed (blue light suppresses melatonin)
Relaxation techniques: deep breathing, progressive muscle relaxation, meditation
Regular exercise (but not within 3 hours of bedtime)
Limit naps to 20-30 minutes before 3 PM
Cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) - first-line treatment
Sleep diary
Medications only as last resort (sleeping pills have dependency risk)
Address underlying causes: anxiety, depression, pain, sleep apnea


B. SLEEP APNEA

What It Is:
A potentially serious sleep disorder where breathing repeatedly stops and starts during sleep.


Types:

Obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) - most common; throat muscles relax
Central sleep apnea - brain doesn't send proper signals to breathing muscles
Complex sleep apnea - combination of both

Foods to EAT:

Anti-inflammatory diet (reduces airway inflammation)
Weight-loss promoting foods if overweight (major risk factor)
High-fiber foods
Lean proteins
Omega-3 fatty acids
Vitamin D-rich foods
Tart cherry juice (melatonin, anti-inflammatory)
Honey (anti-inflammatory, may lubricate throat)
Turmeric (anti-inflammatory)
Ginger (anti-inflammatory)
Adequate water (dehydration thickens mucus)
Whole grains
Fruits and vegetables

Foods to AVOID:

Excessive alcohol (relaxes throat muscles, worsens apnea)
Sedatives and sleeping pills (relax airway muscles)
Heavy meals close to bedtime
High-fat, high-calorie foods (contribute to weight gain)
Dairy before bed (may increase mucus production in some)
Refined carbohydrates
Excessive sugar
Caffeine late in the day
Processed foods
Red meat in excess

Instructions:

CPAP (Continuous Positive Airway Pressure) machine is primary treatment
Weight loss if overweight (can significantly improve or resolve OSA)
Sleep on your side (not back)
Elevate head of bed
Avoid alcohol before bed
Treat nasal congestion
Oral appliances from dentist
Surgery in severe cases
Regular follow-up with sleep specialist
Monitor blood pressure (sleep apnea increases hypertension risk)
Daytime sleepiness assessment


C. RESTLESS LEG SYNDROME (RLS)

What It Is:
An irresistible urge to move the legs, usually accompanied by uncomfortable sensations, typically worse at night.


Foods to EAT:

Iron-rich foods (low iron/ferritin is a common cause)
Folate-rich foods (folate deficiency linked to RLS)
Magnesium-rich foods (muscle relaxation)
Vitamin B12-rich foods
Vitamin D-rich foods
Vitamin C-rich foods (enhance iron absorption)
Potassium-rich foods
Lean proteins

Foods to AVOID:

Caffeine (major trigger)
Alcohol (major trigger)
Sugar and refined carbohydrates
Nicotine
Excessive dairy before bed (in some)
Chocolate (caffeine)

Instructions:

Check iron and ferritin levels (ferritin should be >50-75 ng/mL)
Iron supplementation if deficient
Regular moderate exercise
Leg stretching before bed
Warm baths before bed
Massage legs
Medications if severe: dopamine agonists, gabapentin
Review medications (some worsen RLS: antidepressants, antihistamines)


5. PAIN CONDITIONS

A. CHRONIC PAIN

What It Is:
Pain that persists for more than 3-6 months, beyond normal tissue healing time.


Foods to EAT:

Anti-inflammatory diet (Mediterranean-style)
Omega-3 fatty acids: salmon, sardines, mackerel, walnuts, chia seeds
Extra virgin olive oil (oleocanthal - natural ibuprofen)
Turmeric with black pepper (curcumin + piperine for absorption)
Ginger
Tart cherry juice
Berries: blueberries, strawberries, raspberries
Dark leafy greens
Nuts and seeds
Green tea
Bone broth (collagen, amino acids)
Probiotic-rich foods
Anti-inflammatory spices: rosemary, oregano, cinnamon, cloves

Foods to AVOID:

Sugar and refined carbohydrates (promote inflammation)
Processed and fried foods
Trans fats
Excessive omega-6 fatty acids: corn oil, soybean oil, sunflower oil
Red meat in excess
Alcohol
Nightshade vegetables (in some individuals)
Gluten (in some individuals)
Artificial additives
MSG

Supplements:

Supplement	Dose	Purpose
Omega-3	2,000-3,000 mg/day	Anti-inflammatory
Turmeric/Curcumin	500-1,000 mg 2x/day	Anti-inflammatory
Vitamin D	2,000-4,000 IU/day	Pain modulation
Magnesium	300-500 mg/day	Muscle relaxation, nerve function
Boswellia	300-500 mg 3x/day	Anti-inflammatory
Ginger	250 mg 4x/day	Anti-inflammatory
SAMe	400-1,200 mg/day	Pain, mood
Alpha-lipoic acid	300-600 mg/day	Neuropathic pain
CBD oil	As directed	Pain, inflammation

Instructions:

Multimodal approach: medication, physical therapy, psychological support
Regular gentle exercise
Cognitive behavioral therapy for pain
Mindfulness meditation
Acupuncture
Massage therapy
Heat/cold therapy
Adequate sleep
Stress management
Avoid opioid dependency
Pain management specialist referral


B. NEUROPATHIC PAIN

What It Is:
Pain caused by damage or dysfunction of the nervous system. Described as burning, shooting, stabbing, or electric shock-like.


Causes:

Diabetes (diabetic neuropathy)
Shingles (postherpetic neuralgia)
Chemotherapy
Nerve compression
Alcohol-related
Vitamin B12 deficiency

Foods to EAT:

B-vitamin rich foods: whole grains, eggs, meat, fish, dairy (nerve health)
Vitamin B12-rich foods: meat, fish, dairy, fortified foods
Alpha-lipoic acid-rich foods: spinach, broccoli, red meat (shown to help diabetic neuropathy)
Omega-3 fatty acids
Anti-inflammatory foods
Vitamin D-rich foods
Magnesium-rich foods
Acetyl-L-carnitine-rich foods
Turmeric
Ginger

Foods to AVOID:

Excessive alcohol (causes neuropathy)
Excessive sugar (diabetic neuropathy)
Refined carbohydrates
Trans fats
Processed foods

Supplements:

Supplement	Dose	Purpose
Alpha-lipoic acid	600 mg/day	Diabetic neuropathy
Vitamin B12	1,000 mcg/day	Nerve repair
Vitamin B6	50-100 mg/day	Nerve function
Acetyl-L-Carnitine	1,500-3,000 mg/day	Nerve regeneration
Vitamin D	2,000-4,000 IU/day	Nerve function
Magnesium	300-400 mg/day	Nerve function
Omega-3	2,000 mg/day	Anti-inflammatory

Instructions:

Control underlying cause (diabetes, alcohol)
Medications: gabapentin, pregabalin, duloxetine, amitriptyline
Topical treatments: capsaicin cream, lidocaine patches
Physical therapy
TENS (Transcutaneous Electrical Nerve Stimulation)
Regular blood work
Foot care (diabetic neuropathy)
Avoid alcohol


C. ARTHRITIS PAIN (See previous guide Sections 10B, 10C)

D. HEADACHE & MIGRAINE (See previous guide Section 11D)


6. ADDICTION & SUBSTANCE ABUSE RECOVERY

A. ALCOHOL ADDICTION RECOVERY

What It Is:
A chronic disease characterized by uncontrolled drinking and preoccupation with alcohol.


Nutritional Deficiencies in Alcoholism:

Thiamine (B1) - MOST CRITICAL (Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome)
Folate
Vitamin B6
Vitamin B12
Vitamin A
Vitamin D
Magnesium
Zinc
Potassium
Protein
Essential fatty acids

Foods to EAT:

Thiamine-rich foods: whole grains, pork, legumes, nuts, seeds
Folate-rich foods: leafy greens, beans, fortified cereals
Vitamin B12-rich foods: meat, fish, dairy, eggs
High-protein foods: lean meats, fish, eggs, legumes (liver repair)
Complex carbohydrates: whole grains, sweet potatoes (steady blood sugar, reduce cravings)
Omega-3 fatty acids (brain repair, mood)
Magnesium-rich foods: dark chocolate, spinach, almonds
Zinc-rich foods: oysters, beef, pumpkin seeds
Probiotic-rich foods: yogurt, kefir, sauerkraut (gut repair)
Liver-supporting foods: beets, artichokes, leafy greens, garlic, lemon
Adequate hydration
Small, frequent meals (stabilize blood sugar)
Bitter foods (support liver function)
Cruciferous vegetables (support detoxification)

Foods to AVOID:

ALL ALCOHOL (complete abstinence)
Sugar and refined carbohydrates (trigger cravings, blood sugar crashes)
Caffeine in excess (anxiety, sleep disruption)
Processed foods
Excessive salt
Foods that trigger cravings

Supplements:

Supplement	Dose	Purpose
Thiamine (B1)	100 mg/day (or IV in acute)	Prevent Wernicke-Korsakoff
Folate	400-1,000 mcg/day	Cell repair
Vitamin B12	1,000 mcg/day	Nerve function
Magnesium	400 mg/day	Muscle, nerve, sleep
Zinc	15-30 mg/day	Immune, liver
Vitamin D	2,000-4,000 IU/day	Bone, mood
Omega-3	2,000 mg/day	Brain repair, mood
NAC	600 mg 2x/day	Liver protection, antioxidant
Milk thistle	150-300 mg 2x/day	Liver protection
Probiotics	Multi-strain	Gut repair
Multivitamin	Daily	Overall nutrition
Vitamin C	500-1,000 mg 2x/day	Antioxidant

Instructions:

COMPLETE ABSTINENCE from alcohol
Medical detoxification if needed (alcohol withdrawal can be life-threatening)
Thiamine supplementation BEFORE glucose administration in acute withdrawal
Support groups: AA, SMART Recovery, Refuge Recovery
Individual therapy: CBT, motivational interviewing
Nutritional rehabilitation
Regular liver function monitoring
Address co-occurring mental health conditions
Avoid triggers
Build sober support network
Exercise regularly (reduces cravings, improves mood)
Adequate sleep
Naltrexone or acamprosate may help reduce cravings


B. OPIOID ADDICTION RECOVERY

Foods to EAT:

High-fiber foods (opioids cause severe constipation)
Probiotic-rich foods (gut repair)
Adequate hydration (combat constipation)
Lean proteins (muscle repair)
Complex carbohydrates (steady energy, mood)
Omega-3 fatty acids (brain repair)
B-vitamin rich foods
Magnesium-rich foods
Vitamin D-rich foods
Small, frequent meals
Warm foods (soothing)

Foods to AVOID:

Processed foods
Excessive sugar (cravings)
Alcohol
Caffeine in excess

Instructions:

Medication-assisted treatment: methadone, buprenorphine, naltrexone
Behavioral therapy
Address constipation aggressively (fiber, water, stool softeners)
Nutritional rehabilitation
Support groups
Mental health treatment
Regular medical follow-up


C. NICOTINE/SMOKING CESSATION

Foods to EAT:

Fruits and vegetables (make cigarettes taste less appealing)
Vitamin C-rich foods (smoking depletes vitamin C)
Carrots, celery sticks (oral fixation)
Sugar-free gum or mints
Water (flushes toxins, reduces cravings)
Lean proteins
Complex carbohydrates
Omega-3 fatty acids
Green tea
Milk and dairy (make cigarettes taste bad)

Foods to AVOID:

Alcohol (major trigger for smoking)
Caffeine (associated with smoking urges)
Sugar (blood sugar crashes trigger cravings)
Meat (may enhance desire to smoke in some)
Spicy foods (may trigger cravings in some)

Supplements:

Supplement	Dose	Purpose
Vitamin C	1,000-2,000 mg/day	Replenish depleted stores
Vitamin E	400 IU/day	Antioxidant
NAC	600 mg 2x/day	Antioxidant, may reduce cravings
Multivitamin	Daily	Overall nutrition

Instructions:

Nicotine replacement therapy: patches, gum, lozenges
Medications: varenicline (Chantix), bupropion (Wellbutrin)
Set a quit date
Remove all smoking materials
Identify and avoid triggers
Exercise (reduces cravings)
Deep breathing exercises
Support groups
Most people try 7-10 times before successful quit
Weight gain common after quitting (focus on healthy eating)


7. TRANSPLANT NUTRITION

A. KIDNEY TRANSPLANT

Foods to EAT (Post-Transplant):

Balanced diet with adequate protein (1.0-1.4 g/kg/day)
Fresh fruits and vegetables (well-washed)
Whole grains
Lean protein: chicken, fish, eggs, legumes
Healthy fats
Calcium and vitamin D-rich foods (bone protection - steroids)
Adequate hydration
Low-sodium diet
Heart-healthy diet (cardiovascular risk increases post-transplant)

Foods to AVOID:

Grapefruit and grapefruit juice (interacts with immunosuppressants: cyclosporine, tacrolimus)
Pomegranate (interacts with some immunosuppressants)
Starfruit (toxic to kidneys)
Raw or undercooked foods (immunosuppression increases infection risk)
Unpasteurized products
Raw sprouts
Excessive potassium and phosphorus (monitor levels)
Excessive sodium
Excessive sugar (steroids increase diabetes risk)
Alcohol
Excessive protein (strain on kidney)

Instructions:

Take immunosuppressant medications EXACTLY as prescribed (never miss doses)
Monitor blood levels of immunosuppressants
Regular follow-up with transplant team
Monitor kidney function
Monitor blood pressure, blood sugar, cholesterol
Infection prevention: food safety, hand hygiene, avoid sick contacts
Regular cancer screenings (immunosuppression increases cancer risk)
Bone density monitoring (steroids)
Dental care
Vaccinations (no live vaccines)
Exercise regularly
Maintain healthy weight


B. LIVER TRANSPLANT

Foods to EAT:

Balanced diet with adequate protein
Fresh, well-washed produce
Whole grains
Lean proteins
Healthy fats
Calcium and vitamin D-rich foods
Low-sodium diet (prevent fluid retention)
Adequate hydration

Foods to AVOID:

ALCOHOL (absolutely forbidden - damages new liver)
Grapefruit (interacts with immunosuppressants)
Raw or undercooked foods
Unpasteurized products
Excessive sodium
Excessive sugar
Processed foods
High-fat foods

Instructions:

Complete abstinence from alcohol
Immunosuppressant medications
Regular liver function monitoring
Infection prevention
Cancer screenings
Weight management
Exercise
Mental health support


C. HEART TRANSPLANT

Foods to EAT:

Heart-healthy diet (Mediterranean-style)
Low sodium (<1,500-2,000 mg/day)
Lean protein
Omega-3 fatty acids
Fiber-rich foods
Fresh fruits and vegetables
Whole grains

Foods to AVOID:

Grapefruit
Excessive sodium
Saturated and trans fats
Alcohol
Raw/undercooked foods
Excessive sugar (steroid-induced diabetes)

Instructions:

Immunosuppressant medications
Regular cardiac monitoring
Rejection surveillance
Infection prevention
Exercise as tolerated
Maintain healthy weight
Blood pressure and cholesterol management


D. BONE MARROW / STEM CELL TRANSPLANT

Foods to EAT:

Neutropenic diet during low immunity: well-cooked foods only, no raw produce
High-protein diet (immune cell production)
High-calorie diet (healing)
Pasteurized products only
Well-cooked meats
Canned or cooked fruits and vegetables
Adequate hydration

Foods to AVOID (Neutropenic Precautions):

ALL raw foods during neutropenia (ANC <500)
Raw fruits and vegetables
Raw or rare meat, fish, eggs
Unpasteurized dairy
Raw nuts
Deli meats
Soft cheeses
Yogurt with live cultures (during severe neutropenia)
Well water
Sushi
Buffet foods

Instructions:

Strict food safety
Neutropenic diet until immune recovery
Calorie counting and weight monitoring
TPN (Total Parenteral Nutrition) if unable to eat
Manage mucositis: soft, bland, cool foods
Manage nausea and vomiting
Regular blood counts
Infection prevention
Gradual reintroduction of normal diet as immunity recovers


8. SPORTS & EXERCISE NUTRITION

A. GENERAL ATHLETIC NUTRITION

Macronutrient Needs:

Nutrient	Endurance Athletes	Strength Athletes	General Fitness
Carbohydrates	5-10 g/kg/day	4-7 g/kg/day	3-5 g/kg/day
Protein	1.2-1.6 g/kg/day	1.6-2.2 g/kg/day	1.0-1.4 g/kg/day
Fat	20-35% of calories	20-35% of calories	20-35% of calories

Foods to EAT:


Pre-Workout (1-3 hours before):

Complex carbohydrates: oatmeal, whole grain toast, banana, sweet potato
Moderate protein: eggs, yogurt, chicken
Low fat (slow to digest)
Adequate hydration
Examples: banana with peanut butter, oatmeal with berries, turkey sandwich

During Exercise (>60 minutes):

Sports drink with electrolytes (for exercise >60 min)
Energy gels or chews
Banana
Dates
Water (for exercise <60 min)

Post-Workout (within 30-60 minutes):

Protein: 20-40g (whey protein, chicken, eggs, Greek yogurt)
Carbohydrates: 1.0-1.5 g/kg (replenish glycogen)
Ratio: 3:1 or 4:1 carbs to protein
Examples: chocolate milk, protein shake with banana, chicken with rice

Daily:

Complex carbohydrates: whole grains, oats, quinoa, sweet potatoes
Lean proteins: chicken, fish, eggs, Greek yogurt, legumes
Healthy fats: avocado, nuts, olive oil
Colorful fruits and vegetables
Adequate hydration: minimum 0.5-1 oz per pound of body weight

Foods to AVOID:

Excessive processed foods
Sugary drinks (except during prolonged exercise)
Alcohol (impairs recovery, dehydrating)
Excessive caffeine before evening workouts (sleep disruption)
High-fat meals immediately before exercise (slow digestion)
Large meals close to exercise
Excessive fiber immediately before exercise (GI distress)
Artificial sweeteners (in some athletes - GI distress)

Hydration Guidelines:

Timing	Amount
2-4 hours before exercise	16-20 oz water
10-20 min before exercise	8-12 oz water
During exercise	7-10 oz every 10-20 min
After exercise	16-24 oz per pound lost

Supplements for Athletes:

Supplement	Dose	Purpose
Creatine monohydrate	3-5 g/day	Strength, power, muscle
Whey protein	20-40 g post-workout	Muscle recovery
Beta-alanine	3-6 g/day	Endurance, buffers lactic acid
Caffeine	3-6 mg/kg, 30-60 min pre	Performance, endurance
BCAAs	5-10 g/day	Muscle recovery
Electrolytes	During prolonged exercise	Prevent cramping
Vitamin D	2,000-4,000 IU/day	Bone, muscle, immune
Iron	If deficient	Oxygen transport
Omega-3	1,000-2,000 mg/day	Anti-inflammatory, recovery
Tart cherry juice	8-12 oz 2x/day	Recovery, anti-inflammatory


B. MARATHON / ENDURANCE SPORTS

Foods to EAT:

Carb-loading 2-3 days before event: 8-12 g/kg/day
During race: 30-60g carbs/hour (gels, sports drink, dates)
Post-race: protein + carbs within 30 minutes
Iron-rich foods (runners lose iron through sweat and foot-strike hemolysis)
Sodium replacement during long events
Adequate calories (endurance athletes often undereat)

Instructions:

Practice nutrition strategy during training (nothing new on race day)
Test gels/drinks in training
Avoid high-fiber foods before race
Hydrate well in days before race
Post-race: rehydrate, refuel, rest


C. BODYBUILDING / MUSCLE BUILDING

Foods to EAT:

HIGH protein: 1.6-2.2 g/kg/day
Protein at every meal (20-40g)
Leucine-rich foods: whey protein, eggs, chicken, fish, soy
Complex carbohydrates: rice, oats, sweet potatoes
Healthy fats
Pre-workout: carbs + moderate protein
Post-workout: 20-40g protein + carbs
Casein protein before bed (slow-release)
Adequate calories (caloric surplus for muscle gain)

Instructions:

Meal timing: every 3-4 hours
Progressive overload in training
Adequate sleep (7-9 hours - growth hormone release)
Track macros
Creatine supplementation
Don't neglect rest days


D. WEIGHT CUTTING (Combat Sports, Wrestling)

Foods to EAT (During Weight Cut):

Lean protein (preserve muscle)
Vegetables (low calorie, filling)
Moderate carbohydrates (enough for training)
Adequate hydration (then controlled water manipulation)
Small, frequent meals

Foods to AVOID:

Excessive sodium (water retention)
Processed foods
Sugary foods
Alcohol
Excessive carbohydrates

Instructions:

Work with sports dietitian
Gradual weight loss (1-2 lbs/week)
Avoid crash diets and dehydration methods
Rehydrate and refuel immediately after weigh-in
Monitor health markers


9. OCCUPATIONAL HEALTH CONDITIONS

A. SHIFT WORK DISORDERS

What It Is:
Health problems caused by working non-traditional hours (night shifts, rotating shifts).


Health Risks:

Sleep disorders
Obesity
Diabetes
Cardiovascular disease
Gastrointestinal problems
Depression
Increased accident risk

Foods to EAT:

Light, balanced meals during night shifts
Complex carbohydrates (steady energy)
Lean protein (satiety)
Fruits and vegetables
Small, frequent meals
Melatonin-containing foods before daytime sleep: tart cherries, walnuts
Magnesium-rich foods (sleep quality)
Main meal before shift, light meal during shift
Healthy snacks: nuts, yogurt, fruit

Foods to AVOID:

Heavy meals during night shifts (GI distress)
Excessive caffeine (stop 4-6 hours before planned sleep)
Sugar and junk food (energy crashes)
Alcohol (disrupts daytime sleep)
Spicy foods at night (heartburn)
Large meals close to sleep time

Instructions:

Consistent sleep schedule even on days off
Dark, quiet sleep environment (blackout curtains)
Light exposure during shift (bright lights)
Melatonin supplementation (0.5-5 mg before daytime sleep)
Regular exercise
Regular health screenings
Rotate shifts forward (morning → afternoon → night) if possible
Limit consecutive night shifts


B. SEDENTARY WORK (OFFICE WORK)

Health Risks:

Obesity
Cardiovascular disease
Type 2 diabetes
Back pain
Neck pain
Eye strain
Deep vein thrombosis

Foods to EAT:

Balanced meals with controlled portions
High-fiber foods (satiety)
Lean protein
Healthy snacks: nuts, fruit, yogurt, vegetables
Adequate water (keep at desk)
Anti-inflammatory foods (combat sitting-related inflammation)

Foods to AVOID:

Desk snacking on junk food
Excessive coffee and sugary drinks
Vending machine foods
Large lunches (afternoon lethargy)
Excessive calories

Instructions:

Stand every 30 minutes
Take walking breaks
Desk exercises
Ergonomic workstation
150+ minutes of exercise per week
Lunchtime walks
Standing desk option
Regular eye breaks (20-20-20 rule: every 20 min, look 20 feet away, for 20 seconds)


C. HEAVY PHYSICAL LABOR

Foods to EAT:

HIGH calorie diet
HIGH protein: 1.2-1.6 g/kg/day
Complex carbohydrates (sustained energy)
Adequate hydration: more than typical (10+ glasses/day)
Electrolyte replacement (sodium, potassium, magnesium)
Iron-rich foods
Calcium and vitamin D-rich foods (bone protection)
Frequent meals and snacks
Healthy fats (calorie-dense)

Instructions:

Hydrate before, during, and after work
Eat breakfast before work
Pack nutritious lunch and snacks
Wear protective equipment
Ergonomic practices
Regular health check-ups
Adequate rest


D. EXPOSURE TO OCCUPATIONAL HAZARDS

Lead Exposure:

Foods to EAT: calcium-rich foods, iron-rich foods, vitamin C (reduce lead absorption)
Foods to AVOID: empty stomach (increases lead absorption)
Instructions: blood lead level monitoring, PPE, handwashing

Mercury Exposure:

Foods to EAT: selenium-rich foods (selenium binds mercury), fiber
Foods to AVOID: high-mercury fish
Instructions: blood/urine mercury testing, PPE, ventilation

Asbestos Exposure:

Foods to EAT: antioxidant-rich foods, vitamin C, vitamin E, selenium
Instructions: regular chest imaging, smoking cessation (asbestos + smoking = dramatically higher lung cancer risk)


10. ENVIRONMENTAL & TOXIN-RELATED CONDITIONS

A. HEAT-RELATED ILLNESS

Types:

Heat cramps
Heat exhaustion
Heat stroke (medical emergency)

Foods to EAT:

Water: at least 8-12 glasses/day (more in heat)
Electrolyte-rich beverages: coconut water, sports drinks, ORS
Water-rich fruits: watermelon, cucumber, oranges, strawberries, grapes
Water-rich vegetables: celery, lettuce, zucchini, tomatoes
Salty snacks (replace sodium lost through sweat)
Light, cool meals
Small, frequent meals

Foods to AVOID:

Alcohol (dehydrating)
Excessive caffeine (dehydrating)
Heavy, hot meals
Excessive protein (increases metabolic heat)
High-sugar drinks (may worsen dehydration)

Instructions:

Drink before feeling thirsty
Wear light, loose clothing
Stay in shade/air conditioning during peak heat
Never leave children or pets in cars
Know signs of heat exhaustion: heavy sweating, weakness, cold/pale/clammy skin, fast/weak pulse, nausea, fainting
Heat stroke: high body temp (>103°F), hot/red/dry skin, rapid pulse, confusion, unconsciousness - CALL 911


B. COLD-RELATED ILLNESS

Types:

Hypothermia
Frostbite

Foods to EAT:

WARM foods and beverages: soups, stews, hot chocolate, herbal tea
HIGH calorie foods (body burns more calories in cold)
Complex carbohydrates (thermogenesis)
Healthy fats (insulation, calorie-dense)
Iron-rich foods (cold intolerance associated with anemia)
Vitamin C-rich foods (cold resistance)
Ginger and cinnamon (warming spices)
Hot meals

Foods to AVOID:

Alcohol (causes heat loss, impairs judgment)
Caffeine (diuretic, may cause dehydration)

Instructions:

Dress in layers
Cover extremities
Stay dry
Recognize hypothermia signs: shivering, confusion, slurred speech, drowsiness
Seek medical attention for frostbite


C. RADIATION EXPOSURE

Foods to EAT:

Antioxidant-rich foods: berries, colorful vegetables, green tea
Seaweed (iodine - protects thyroid from radioactive iodine)
Cruciferous vegetables (support detoxification)
Sulfur-rich foods: garlic, onions, cruciferous vegetables
Pectin-rich foods: apples, citrus (binds radioactive elements)
Adequate hydration
High-protein diet
Vitamin C, E, A, selenium

Instructions:

Potassium iodide (KI) if directed by authorities (nuclear event)
Follow evacuation/shelter guidelines
Decontamination procedures
Medical monitoring


D. HEAVY METAL DETOXIFICATION SUPPORT

Foods to EAT:

Cruciferous vegetables (support liver detoxification)
Garlic and onions (sulfur compounds bind metals)
Cilantro (may help mobilize metals)
Chlorella (may bind heavy metals)
Fiber-rich foods (bind metals in gut)
Vitamin C-rich foods
Selenium-rich foods (binds mercury)
Sulfur-rich foods
Adequate hydration
Probiotic-rich foods

Foods to AVOID:

High-mercury fish: shark, swordfish, king mackerel, tilefish
Contaminated water
Excessive alcohol (impairs detoxification)
Processed foods

Instructions:

Identify and eliminate source of exposure
Chelation therapy if medically indicated (DMSA, DMPS, EDTA)
Blood and urine testing for heavy metals
Professional medical detoxification
Support liver and kidney function


11. GENETIC & RARE DISEASES

A. PHENYLKETONURIA (PKU)

What It Is:
An inherited disorder causing buildup of phenylalanine (an amino acid) in the body due to deficiency of the enzyme phenylalanine hydroxylase.


Foods to EAT:

LOW phenylalanine medical formula (primary protein source)
Fruits and vegetables (low in phenylalanine)
Special low-protein breads, pastas, and cereals
Limited amounts of regular foods as prescribed
Medical foods and supplements

Foods to AVOID:

HIGH phenylalanine foods: meat, fish, eggs, dairy, nuts, seeds, soy, beans
Aspartame (contains phenylalanine - found in diet sodas, sugar-free products)
Regular bread and pasta (in excess)
All foods high in protein (in excess)

Instructions:

LIFELONG diet therapy
Regular blood phenylalanine level monitoring
Medical formula as primary nutrition source
Genetic counseling
Newborn screening is critical
Pregnancy: strict phenylalanine control before and during pregnancy


B. GALACTOSEMIA

What It Is:
An inherited disorder affecting the ability to metabolize galactose (a sugar found in milk).


Foods to AVOID:

ALL dairy: milk, cheese, butter, yogurt, ice cream
Lactose-containing foods
Galactose-containing foods
Many processed foods containing dairy ingredients
Organ meats (contain galactose)
Some legumes

Foods to EAT:

Soy-based formulas (infants)
Calcium and vitamin D-fortified non-dairy alternatives
All fruits, vegetables, grains, meat, fish
Non-dairy milk: soy milk, almond milk, oat milk

Instructions:

Lifelong dairy-free diet
Calcium and vitamin D supplementation
Regular monitoring
Speech and developmental therapy (if diagnosed late)


C. CYSTIC FIBROSIS (See previous guide Section 8C)

D. SICKLE CELL DISEASE (See previous guide Section 15D)

E. DOWN SYNDROME NUTRITION

Foods to EAT:

Balanced, nutrient-dense diet
Adequate fiber (constipation is common)
Calcium and vitamin D (lower bone density)
Adequate protein
Iron-rich foods (if anemic)
Antioxidant-rich foods
Appropriate portions (lower caloric needs due to lower metabolic rate)

Foods to AVOID:

Excessive calories (obesity risk)
Processed foods
Excessive sugar

Instructions:

Monitor thyroid function (hypothyroidism common)
Monitor weight carefully
Celiac screening (higher risk)
Adequate hydration
Regular health check-ups
Dental care (higher risk of dental issues)
Address feeding difficulties if present


F. CELIAC DISEASE (See previous guide Section 6D)

G. LACTOSE INTOLERANCE (See previous guide Section 17B)

H. GLUTEN SENSITIVITY (See previous guide Section 17C)


12. DERMATOLOGICAL CONDITIONS (EXPANDED)

A. ECZEMA / ATOPIC DERMATITIS (EXPANDED)

Foods to EAT:

Anti-inflammatory foods
Omega-3 fatty acids: salmon, sardines, walnuts, flaxseeds
Probiotic-rich foods: yogurt, kefir, sauerkraut (gut-skin axis)
Vitamin D-rich foods
Vitamin E-rich foods: almonds, sunflower seeds, avocado
Vitamin A-rich foods: sweet potatoes, carrots, liver
Zinc-rich foods: pumpkin seeds, oysters, beef
Quercetin-rich foods: apples, onions, berries (natural antihistamine)
Turmeric (anti-inflammatory)
Bone broth (collagen, gut healing)
Prebiotic-rich foods: garlic, onions, asparagus, bananas
Flaxseeds (anti-inflammatory)
Coconut oil (topical and internal - antimicrobial)
Water (adequate hydration)

Foods to AVOID (Common Eczema Triggers):

Dairy products (cow's milk is a common trigger, especially in children)
Eggs (common trigger)
Peanuts and tree nuts
Wheat and gluten
Soy
Fish and shellfish (in some)
Tomatoes
Citrus fruits
Processed foods
Sugar and refined carbohydrates
Artificial additives: colors, flavors, preservatives
Alcohol
Spicy foods (in some)
Nightshades (in some): tomatoes, peppers, eggplant

Instructions:

Moisturize immediately after bathing (ceramide-containing creams)
Use fragrance-free products
Wear soft, cotton clothing
Avoid hot showers (lukewarm)
Manage stress (triggers flares)
Identify individual triggers through elimination diet
Topical corticosteroids as prescribed
Wet wrap therapy for severe flares
Antihistamines for itching
Dupilumab (Dupixent) for moderate-severe eczema
Regular dermatology follow-up
Keep nails short (prevent scratching damage)


B. PSORIASIS (EXPANDED)

Foods to EAT:

Anti-inflammatory diet (Mediterranean-style)
Fatty fish: salmon, mackerel, sardines (omega-3)
Extra virgin olive oil
Colorful fruits and vegetables
Whole grains
Lean protein
Turmeric (curcumin)
Vitamin D-rich foods
Probiotic-rich foods
Brazil nuts (selenium)
Green tea
Aloe vera juice (internal)

Foods to AVOID:

Alcohol (MAJOR trigger - increases inflammation and interferes with treatment)
Red meat (arachidonic acid promotes inflammation)
Processed foods
Refined sugar
Trans fats
Nightshade vegetables (in some): tomatoes, peppers, eggplant, white potatoes
Gluten (in some - especially if comorbid celiac)
Dairy (in some)
Fried foods
Processed vegetable oils

Supplements:

Supplement	Dose	Purpose
Vitamin D	2,000-4,000 IU/day	Immune modulation
Omega-3	2,000-3,000 mg/day	Anti-inflammatory
Turmeric/Curcumin	500-1,000 mg 2x/day	Anti-inflammatory
Selenium	200 mcg/day	Antioxidant
Vitamin B12	1,000 mcg/day	Skin health
Probiotics	Multi-strain	Gut-skin axis
Milk thistle	150-300 mg 2x/day	Liver support (if on methotrexate)
Folic acid	1-5 mg/day (if on methotrexate)	Prevent side effects

Instructions:

Topical treatments: corticosteroids, vitamin D analogs, retinoids
Phototherapy: UVB, PUVA
Systemic medications: methotrexate, cyclosporine
Biologics: TNF inhibitors, IL-17 inhibitors, IL-23 inhibitors
Maintain healthy weight (obesity worsens psoriasis)
Manage stress
Limit alcohol significantly
Regular dermatology follow-up
Monitor for psoriatic arthritis


C. ROSACEA

Foods to EAT:

Anti-inflammatory foods
Omega-3 fatty acids
Non-triggering vegetables: leafy greens, cucumbers, carrots
Low-histamine foods
Alkaline foods
Chamomile tea (anti-inflammatory)
Turmeric
Zinc-rich foods
Probiotic-rich foods

Foods to AVOID (Common Triggers):

Alcohol: especially red wine (most common trigger)
Spicy foods
Hot beverages: coffee, tea
Caffeine
Chocolate
Tomatoes
Citrus fruits
Vinegar
Fermented foods
Histamine-rich foods
Cinnamon
Vanilla
Avocado (in some)
Eggplant (in some)
Spinach (in some)

Instructions:

Identify individual triggers through food diary
Topical treatments: metronidazole, azelaic acid, ivermectin
Oral antibiotics for inflammation
Laser therapy for visible blood vessels
Gentle skincare routine
Sun protection (major trigger)
Avoid extreme temperatures
Manage stress


D. VITILIGO

What It Is:
A condition causing loss of skin color in patches due to destruction of melanocytes.


Foods to EAT:

Antioxidant-rich foods: berries, colorful vegetables, green tea
Vitamin B12-rich foods: meat, fish, dairy
Folate-rich foods: leafy greens, beans
Vitamin D-rich foods
Vitamin C-rich foods (protects melanocytes)
Vitamin E-rich foods
Zinc-rich foods: pumpkin seeds, oysters, beef
Copper-rich foods: liver, oysters, dark chocolate, nuts
Omega-3 fatty acids
Turmeric
Ginkgo biloba (some evidence)

Foods to AVOID:

Excessive vitamin C (in some theories - may affect melanin)
Alcohol
Processed foods
Excessive sugar
Foods that may trigger autoimmune response (in some)

Instructions:

Sun protection (SPF 30+ on affected areas)
Topical corticosteroids
Phototherapy (NB-UVB)
Topical calcineurin inhibitors
Psychological support
Camouflage makeup
Ginkgo biloba supplements (some evidence)
Regular dermatology follow-up


E. ACNE (EXPANDED)

Foods to EAT:

Low-glycemic diet: whole grains, vegetables, legumes
Omega-3 fatty acids
Zinc-rich foods: pumpkin seeds, oysters, lentils, chickpeas
Vitamin A-rich foods: sweet potatoes, carrots, leafy greens
Vitamin E-rich foods: almonds, sunflower seeds
Probiotic-rich foods
Green tea
Turmeric
Fiber-rich foods
Chromium-rich foods: broccoli, grape juice

Foods to AVOID:

High-glycemic foods: white bread, sugary cereals, pastries, white rice
Dairy: especially skim milk (IGF-1, hormones)
Whey protein supplements (strong association with acne)
Chocolate (in some individuals)
Fried and greasy foods
Excessive sugar
Processed foods
Iodine-rich foods in excess (seaweed, iodized salt excess)

Instructions:

Gentle cleansing twice daily
Non-comedogenic products
Don't pick or squeeze
Benzoyl peroxide, salicylic acid, retinoids
Oral antibiotics for moderate acne
Isotretinoin for severe/cystic acne
Hormonal treatments (spironolactone, oral contraceptives)
Regular dermatology follow-up


F. HAIR LOSS (ALOPECIA)

Foods to EAT:

Iron-rich foods (iron deficiency is a common cause)
Protein-rich foods (hair is made of protein - keratin)
Biotin-rich foods: eggs, nuts, sweet potatoes, onions
Zinc-rich foods: oysters, pumpkin seeds, beef
Omega-3 fatty acids
Vitamin D-rich foods
Vitamin E-rich foods
Vitamin A-rich foods (sebum production)
Vitamin C-rich foods (collagen, iron absorption)
B-vitamin rich foods
Selenium-rich foods
Collagen-rich foods: bone broth, fish
Silica-rich foods: bananas, oats, green beans
MSM-rich foods: cruciferous vegetables, onions

Foods to AVOID:

Excessive vitamin A supplements (can cause hair loss)
Crash dieting and extreme calorie restriction
Excessive sugar
Processed foods
Alcohol
Mercury-rich fish
Raw egg whites (avidin binds biotin)

Supplements:

Supplement	Dose	Purpose
Biotin	2,500-5,000 mcg/day	Hair growth
Iron	If deficient	Oxygen to hair follicles
Zinc	15-30 mg/day	Hair tissue growth
Vitamin D	2,000-4,000 IU/day	Hair follicle cycling
Omega-3	1,000-2,000 mg/day	Scalp health
Collagen	10-15 g/day	Hair structure
Vitamin C	500-1,000 mg/day	Collagen, iron absorption
Vitamin E	400 IU/day	Scalp circulation
Saw palmetto	320 mg/day	DHT-related hair loss
MSM	1,000-3,000 mg/day	Hair growth

Instructions:

Identify underlying cause: thyroid, iron deficiency, hormonal, stress, autoimmune
Blood tests: ferritin, thyroid, vitamin D, zinc, B12
Treat underlying condition
Gentle hair care
Avoid tight hairstyles
Minoxidil (topical) for androgenetic alopecia
Finasteride (oral) for male pattern baldness
PRP (Platelet-Rich Plasma) therapy
Hair transplant for severe cases
Manage stress
Adequate sleep


G. DANDRUFF / SEBORRHEIC DERMATITIS

Foods to EAT:

Omega-3 fatty acids
Zinc-rich foods
B-vitamin rich foods
Probiotic-rich foods
Anti-inflammatory foods
Vitamin D-rich foods

Foods to AVOID:

Sugar and refined carbohydrates (feeds Malassezia yeast)
Excessive dairy
Alcohol
Processed foods
Excessive saturated fat
Trans fats
Fried foods

Instructions:

Anti-dandruff shampoo: zinc pyrithione, ketoconazole, selenium sulfide, coal tar
Regular shampooing
Manage stress
Sun exposure (moderate)


H. URTICARIA (HIVES)

Foods to EAT (Low-Histamine Diet):

Fresh meat and fish (cook immediately)
Fresh fruits (except strawberries, citrus, tomatoes)
Fresh vegetables
Rice, oats
Fresh dairy (not aged)
Olive oil, coconut oil
Herbal teas
Fresh eggs

Foods to AVOID (High-Histamine):

Aged cheeses
Fermented foods: sauerkraut, kimchi, yogurt, kefir
Cured meats: salami, ham, bacon
Alcohol: especially wine, beer
Vinegar
Canned fish
Dried fruits
Spinach, tomatoes, eggplant, avocado
Chocolate
Leftover foods
Histamine liberators: strawberries, citrus, shellfish, pineapple

Instructions:

Antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine, fexofenadine)
Identify and avoid triggers
Keep a food diary
Cool compresses
Avoid tight clothing
Omalizumab (Xolair) for chronic urticaria


13. EAR, NOSE & THROAT CONDITIONS

A. SINUSITIS

Foods to EAT:

Warm fluids: herbal teas, warm water with honey and lemon
Chicken soup (anti-inflammatory, clears congestion)
Spicy foods: horseradish, chili peppers, wasabi (clear sinuses)
Pineapple (bromelain - anti-inflammatory)
Vitamin C-rich foods
Garlic and onions (antimicrobial)
Ginger (anti-inflammatory)
Turmeric
Probiotic-rich foods
Adequate hydration (thin mucus)
Horseradish (natural decongestant)
Apple cider vinegar (diluted - may thin mucus)

Foods to AVOID:

Dairy (may thicken mucus in some)
Sugar (suppresses immune function)
Refined carbohydrates
Alcohol (dehydrating, worsens congestion)
Fried foods
Processed foods

Instructions:

Nasal saline irrigation (neti pot)
Steam inhalation
Warm compresses on face
Antibiotics if bacterial (not viral)
Decongestants (short-term only)
Nasal corticosteroid sprays
Allergy management
Humidifier
See doctor if symptoms >10 days, severe headache, high fever


B. TONSILLITIS

Foods to EAT:

Soft, cool, soothing foods: smoothies, yogurt, ice cream, popsicles
Warm broths and soups
Honey (antimicrobial, soothing)
Warm water with honey and lemon
Mashed potatoes
Scrambled eggs
Oatmeal
Bananas
Adequate hydration

Foods to AVOID:

Crunchy, sharp foods: chips, crackers, toast (irritate throat)
Spicy foods
Acidic foods: citrus, tomatoes
Very hot foods
Rough-textured foods

Instructions:

Antibiotics if bacterial (strep throat - complete full course)
Rest voice
Warm salt water gargle
Pain relievers: acetaminophen, ibuprofen
Adequate fluids
Tonsillectomy if recurrent (7+ episodes/year)


C. TINNITUS

Foods to EAT:

Zinc-rich foods: oysters, pumpkin seeds, beef (zinc deficiency linked to tinnitus)
Magnesium-rich foods (protects hearing)
Vitamin B12-rich foods
Omega-3 fatty acids
Folate-rich foods
Pineapple (anti-inflammatory)
Anti-inflammatory foods
Adequate hydration

Foods to AVOID:

Excessive salt (worsens tinnitus in some)
Excessive sugar
Caffeine (may worsen)
Alcohol (worsens)
Excessive saturated fat
MSG
Quinine (tonic water)

Instructions:

Protect ears from loud noise
Sound therapy / white noise machines
Cognitive behavioral therapy
Hearing aids if hearing loss present
Review medications (some cause tinnitus: aspirin, NSAIDs, some antibiotics)


D. HEARING LOSS

Foods to EAT:

Folate-rich foods (linked to hearing preservation)
Potassium-rich foods (inner ear fluid regulation)
Magnesium-rich foods (protects against noise-induced hearing loss)
Zinc-rich foods
Omega-3 fatty acids
Vitamin D-rich foods
Vitamin C-rich foods
Antioxidant-rich foods

Foods to AVOID:

Excessive noise exposure
Smoking (impairs blood flow to ears)
Excessive alcohol

Instructions:

Hearing protection in noisy environments
Regular hearing tests
Hearing aids if needed
Cochlear implants for severe hearing loss


14. UROLOGICAL CONDITIONS

A. URINARY TRACT INFECTIONS (UTIs) — EXPANDED

Foods to EAT:

Water: 8-10+ glasses/day (MOST IMPORTANT)
Cranberry juice (unsweetened) or cranberry supplements (proanthocyanidins prevent bacterial adhesion)
Blueberries (similar compounds)
Probiotic-rich foods: yogurt, kefir (Lactobacillus restores vaginal/urinary flora)
Vitamin C-rich foods (acidifies urine)
Garlic (antibacterial)
Pineapple (bromelain)
Parsley tea (natural diuretic)
Celery, cucumber (high water content)
D-mannose supplements (prevents E. coli adhesion)

Foods to AVOID:

Sugar (feeds bacteria)
Alcohol (irritates bladder)
Caffeine (irritates bladder)
Carbonated beverages
Artificial sweeteners
Spicy foods
Acidic foods if bladder irritation

Supplements:

Supplement	Dose	Purpose
D-Mannose	500 mg 2-3x/day	Prevents E. coli adhesion
Cranberry extract	36 mg PACs/day	Prevents bacterial adhesion
Probiotics (Lactobacillus)	Multi-strain	Restores flora
Vitamin C	500 mg 2x/day	Acidifies urine

Instructions:

Urinate frequently; don't hold
Wipe front to back
Urinate after intercourse
Avoid irritating products
Cotton underwear
Complete antibiotics if prescribed
For recurrent UTIs (3+/year): prophylactic antibiotics, vaginal estrogen (postmenopausal), D-mannose


B. BENIGN PROSTATIC HYPERPLASIA (BPH)

Foods to EAT:

Tomatoes (lycopene - cooked is better)
Cruciferous vegetables
Green tea
Pumpkin seeds (zinc, phytosterols)
Soy foods (isoflavones)
Fatty fish (omega-3)
Berries
Watermelon (lycopene)
Zinc-rich foods
Saw palmetto (supplement)

Foods to AVOID:

Excessive alcohol
Caffeine (worsens urinary symptoms)
Spicy foods
Red meat in excess
Excessive dairy
Sodium
Fluids before bedtime

Instructions:

Limit fluids before bed
Double-void (urinate, wait, try again)
Alpha-blockers or 5-alpha reductase inhibitors if prescribed
Regular PSA screening
Saw palmetto supplements (some evidence)
Regular urology follow-up


C. KIDNEY STONES (See previous guide Section 4D)

D. INTERSTITIAL CYSTITIS (See previous guide Section 13)

E. ERECTILE DYSFUNCTION

Foods to EAT:

Nitric oxide-boosting foods: beets, leafy greens, watermelon (citrulline), dark chocolate
Omega-3 fatty acids (blood flow)
Zinc-rich foods: oysters, pumpkin seeds, beef
Vitamin D-rich foods
Flavonoid-rich foods: berries, citrus, tea, dark chocolate
Pistachios (shown to improve ED)
Olive oil
Garlic (improves blood flow)
Pomegranate juice
Adequate hydration

Foods to AVOID:

Excessive alcohol (impairs function)
Processed foods
Trans fats (impair blood flow)
Excessive saturated fat
Excessive sugar
Soy in excess (in some theories)
Mint in excess (some evidence of reducing testosterone)

Instructions:

Exercise regularly (improves blood flow)
Maintain healthy weight
Quit smoking
Manage cardiovascular risk factors
Manage stress and anxiety
PDE5 inhibitors if prescribed (sildenafil, tadalafil)
Pelvic floor exercises
Counseling if psychological component


15. HEMATOLOGICAL CONDITIONS (EXPANDED)

A. IRON-DEFICIENCY ANEMIA (EXPANDED)

Foods to EAT:

Heme iron (15-35% absorption): red meat, liver, shellfish, dark poultry
Non-heme iron (5-12% absorption): lentils, spinach, fortified cereals, tofu, dark chocolate
Vitamin C WITH every iron-rich meal (enhances absorption 2-3x)
Blackstrap molasses (1 tbsp = 3.6 mg iron)
Cook in cast iron cookware (leaches iron)
Dried apricots, raisins, prunes
Quinoa
Beetroot
Dark leafy greens

Foods to AVOID (At Iron-Rich Meals):

Coffee and tea (tannins reduce absorption by 50-60%)
Calcium supplements and dairy
Whole grains and bran (phytates)
Soy protein
Separate by 1-2 hours

Instructions:

Iron supplements: ferrous sulfate 325 mg (65 mg elemental iron) 1-3x/day
Take on empty stomach with vitamin C
Expect: constipation, dark stools, nausea
IV iron if oral not tolerated
Ferritin target: >50 ng/mL
Treat underlying cause of blood loss


B. THALASSEMIA

What It Is:
An inherited blood disorder causing reduced hemoglobin production.


Foods to EAT (Non-Transfusion Dependent):

Folate-rich foods (supports red blood cell production)
Antioxidant-rich foods (reduce oxidative stress)
Vitamin E-rich foods
Vitamin C-rich foods
Balanced nutrition

Foods to AVOID (Transfusion Dependent - Iron Overload Risk):

Excessive iron-rich foods
Iron-fortified foods
Vitamin C supplements WITH meals (increases iron absorption)
Excessive red meat
Alcohol (liver stress)

Instructions:

Regular blood transfusions if needed
Iron chelation therapy (deferasirox, deferoxamine) to remove excess iron
Monitor ferritin levels
Folic acid supplementation
Regular monitoring: liver, heart, endocrine organs
Bone density monitoring
Hepatitis B and C screening (transfusion risk)
Genetic counseling
Splenectomy if enlarged spleen


C. HEMOPHILIA

Foods to EAT:

Balanced, nutritious diet
Maintain healthy weight (reduces joint stress)
Vitamin K-rich foods (supports clotting): leafy greens
Iron-rich foods (if bleeding causes anemia)
Calcium and vitamin D-rich foods
Anti-inflammatory foods (joint health)
Omega-3 fatty acids

Foods to AVOID:

Excessive alcohol (impairs liver, increases bleeding risk)
NSAIDs: aspirin, ibuprofen (increase bleeding)
Blood-thinning supplements: fish oil in high doses, vitamin E in high doses, ginkgo, garlic supplements
Excessive weight (joint stress)

Instructions:

Factor replacement therapy as prescribed
Avoid contact sports
Protect joints
Regular hematology follow-up
Vaccinations: hepatitis A and B
Gene therapy (emerging treatment)
Physical therapy for joint health
Dental care (prevent bleeding)


D. LEUKEMIA

Foods to EAT (During Treatment):

Well-cooked foods (infection prevention during immunosuppression)
High-protein diet (immune recovery, tissue repair)
Adequate calories
Soft, bland foods if mouth sores
Small, frequent meals
Pasteurized products only
Iron-rich foods if anemic
Probiotics (when not severely immunosuppressed)
Adequate hydration

Foods to AVOID (During Neutropenia):

Raw foods
Unpasteurized products
Raw sprouts
Deli meats (unless heated)
Soft cheeses
Buffet foods
Well water
Grapefruit (interacts with some chemotherapy)

Instructions:

Treatment: chemotherapy, radiation, stem cell transplant, targeted therapy
Neutropenic precautions
Regular blood counts
Infection prevention
Nutritional support
Mental health support
Survivorship care plan


E. LYMPHOMA

Foods to EAT:

Anti-inflammatory diet
High-protein foods during treatment
Antioxidant-rich foods
Adequate calories
Well-cooked foods during immunosuppression

Foods to AVOID:

Raw foods during treatment
Alcohol
Processed foods
Grapefruit (interacts with some medications)

Instructions:

Treatment: chemotherapy, radiation, immunotherapy, stem cell transplant
Regular monitoring
Infection prevention
Nutritional support


16. PEDIATRIC CONDITIONS (EXPANDED)

A. FAILURE TO THRIVE (EXPANDED)

Foods to EAT:

Calorie-dense foods appropriate for age
Fortified foods
Nutrient-rich foods
Small, frequent meals
Age-appropriate textures
Breast milk or formula (infants)
Healthy fats: avocado, nut butters, olive oil
Full-fat dairy

Instructions:

Identify underlying cause: medical, nutritional, psychosocial
Regular growth monitoring
Feeding therapy if needed
Address oral motor issues
Caloric supplementation
Multivitamin supplementation
Regular pediatric follow-up


B. CHILDHOOD OBESITY (EXPANDED)

Foods to EAT:

Fruits and vegetables (half the plate)
Whole grains
Lean proteins
Low-fat dairy (after age 2)
Water as primary beverage
Family meals together
Age-appropriate portions

Foods to AVOID:

Sugar-sweetened beverages
Fast food
Processed snacks
Excessive screen time eating
Large portions
Juice in excess

Instructions:

Family-based lifestyle changes (most effective)
Limit screen time to <2 hours/day
60 minutes of physical activity daily
Don't use food as reward or punishment
Don't put child on restrictive diet without medical supervision
Model healthy eating behaviors
Regular pediatric check-ups
BMI monitoring on growth charts
Address emotional eating
School-based interventions


C. PEDIATRIC TYPE 1 DIABETES

Foods to EAT:

Balanced diet with carbohydrate counting
Consistent carbohydrate amounts at meals
Whole grains, fruits, vegetables
Lean protein
Healthy fats
Regular meal and snack times

Foods to AVOID:

Sugar-sweetened beverages
Excessive sweets
Large carbohydrate loads at once

Instructions:

Insulin therapy (basal-bolus or pump)
Carbohydrate counting education
Blood glucose monitoring: 4-10x/day
School diabetes management plan
Glucagon kit available
Medical ID bracelet
HbA1c target: age-appropriate
Regular screening for complications
Family involvement
Psychological support


D. PEDIATRIC ASTHMA

Foods to EAT:

Fruits and vegetables (antioxidants)
Fatty fish (omega-3)
Vitamin D-rich foods
Magnesium-rich foods
Apples (quercetin)

Foods to AVOID:

Known food allergens
Sulfites
Excessive processed foods
Excessive omega-6 fatty acids

Instructions:

Asthma action plan
Controller medications daily
Rescue inhaler available
Avoid triggers
Annual flu vaccine
Regular follow-up


E. ATTENTION DEFICIT HYPERACTIVITY DISORDER (ADHD) (EXPANDED)

Foods to EAT:

Omega-3 fatty acids: fatty fish, walnuts, flaxseeds
Protein-rich foods (stabilize blood sugar, neurotransmitter production)
Complex carbohydrates
Iron-rich foods (iron deficiency linked to ADHD)
Zinc-rich foods
Magnesium-rich foods
Vitamin B6-rich foods
Colorful fruits and vegetables
Adequate breakfast

Foods to AVOID:

Artificial colors: Red 40, Yellow 5, Yellow 6
Artificial flavors
Artificial preservatives: BHA, BHT, sodium benzoate
Excessive sugar
Caffeine (in children)
Processed foods
Potential allergens (in sensitive children)
MSG

Supplements:

Supplement	Dose	Purpose
Omega-3 (EPA/DHA)	1,000-2,000 mg/day	Brain function
Iron	If deficient	Dopamine synthesis
Zinc	15-30 mg/day	Neurotransmitter function
Magnesium	200-400 mg/day	Calming, focus
Vitamin B6	25-50 mg/day	Neurotransmitter production
Vitamin D	1,000-2,000 IU/day	Brain health

Instructions:

Behavioral therapy (first-line for young children)
Medication if prescribed (stimulants, non-stimulants)
Structured routines
Adequate sleep
Regular exercise
Consider elimination diet under supervision
School accommodations (504 plan, IEP)
Parent training
Social skills training
Regular follow-up


F. AUTISM SPECTRUM DISORDER (ASD)

Foods to EAT:

Nutrient-dense whole foods
Omega-3 fatty acids (brain development)
Probiotic-rich foods (gut-brain axis)
Magnesium-rich foods
Zinc-rich foods
Vitamin D-rich foods
Vitamin B6-rich foods
Foods matching sensory preferences (texture, temperature)
Adequate hydration

Foods to AVOID (If Sensitive):

Gluten (in some children - trial of gluten-free diet)
Casein (dairy protein - in some children)
Artificial colors and flavors
Excessive sugar
Processed foods
Soy (in some)

Supplements:

Supplement	Dose	Purpose
Omega-3	1,000-2,000 mg/day	Brain function
Vitamin D	1,000-2,000 IU/day	Immune, brain
Probiotics	Multi-strain	Gut health
Magnesium	100-200 mg/day	Calming
Vitamin B6 + Magnesium	As directed	Some evidence for behavior
Melatonin	1-3 mg at bedtime	Sleep (common issue)
Iron	If deficient	Common deficiency

Instructions:

Applied Behavior Analysis (ABA) therapy
Speech therapy
Occupational therapy
Sensory integration therapy
Social skills training
Gluten-free/casein-free diet trial (under supervision)
Address feeding issues and food selectivity
Consistent routines
Parent training and support
Regular developmental monitoring


G. CHILDHOOD CONSTIPATION

Foods to EAT:

High-fiber foods: fruits (pears, prunes, berries), vegetables, whole grains
Adequate water
Prune juice
Pear juice
Kiwi fruit
Flaxseeds (ground)
Physical activity
Establish regular toilet routine

Foods to AVOID:

Excessive dairy (in some children)
White bread, white rice
Processed foods
Excessive bananas (unripe)
Excessive apple juice (sorbitol in small amounts helps, excess worsens)

Instructions:

Increase fiber gradually (age + 5 = grams of fiber needed)
Adequate fluids
Regular toilet routine (after meals)
Positive reinforcement
Glycerin suppositories if needed
PEG 3350 (Miralax) if prescribed
See doctor if persistent


H. PEDIATRIC FOOD ALLERGIES (EXPANDED)

Common Allergens in Children:

1.Cow's milk
2.Eggs
3.Peanuts
4.Tree nuts
5.Wheat
6.Soy
7.Fish
8.Shellfish
9.Sesame

Early Introduction (Prevention):

Current evidence supports early introduction of allergenic foods (4-6 months) to REDUCE allergy risk
Peanut: introduce at 4-6 months for high-risk infants (after skin prick test)
Egg: introduce cooked egg at 4-6 months
Multiple allergens introduced early

Instructions:

Strict avoidance of confirmed allergens
Read ALL food labels
EpiPen always available
Inform all caregivers, schools, restaurants
Medical alert bracelet
Allergy action plan
Regular allergy testing
Some children outgrow allergies: milk (80%), egg (70%), wheat (80%), soy (70%)
Peanut and tree nut allergies are less likely to be outgrown
Oral immunotherapy (OIT) may be an option
Regular follow-up with allergist


I. PEDIATRIC CELIAC DISEASE

Foods to EAT:

Naturally gluten-free foods
All fruits and vegetables
All fresh meats, fish, poultry
Rice, corn, quinoa, potatoes
Gluten-free labeled products
Calcium and vitamin D-fortified alternatives

Foods to AVOID:

Wheat, barley, rye
All gluten-containing products
Cross-contaminated foods

Instructions:

Strict lifelong gluten-free diet
Family education
School accommodations
Growth monitoring
Nutritional deficiency screening
Regular follow-up


J. PEDIATRIC ECZEMA (See Section 12A)

K. CROUP

Foods to EAT:

Warm fluids: clear broth, warm water
Honey (if over 1 year old)
Cool mist humidifier
Rest

Foods to AVOID:

Cold air exposure
Irritating foods

Instructions:

Cool mist humidifier
Steam from hot shower
Honey for cough (over 1 year)
Oral corticosteroids
Epinephrine for severe cases
Seek emergency care for stridor at rest


17. GERIATRIC CONDITIONS (EXPANDED)

A. SARCOPENIA (EXPANDED)

What It Is:
Age-related loss of muscle mass, strength, and function. Begins around age 30 and accelerates after 60.


Foods to EAT:

HIGH protein: 1.0-1.2 g/kg/day (or higher)
Leucine-rich foods (2.5-3g leucine per meal triggers muscle protein synthesis):
Whey protein (highest leucine content)
Eggs
Chicken, fish, beef
Soybeans
Protein at EVERY meal and snack (25-40g per meal)
Vitamin D-rich foods (muscle function)
Omega-3 fatty acids (muscle protein synthesis)
Creatine-rich foods: meat, fish (or supplement 3-5g/day)
HMB-rich foods (beta-hydroxy beta-methylbutyrate)
Adequate calories
Calcium-rich foods
Antioxidant-rich foods

Supplements:

Supplement	Dose	Purpose
Whey protein	20-40 g/day	Leucine, muscle protein synthesis
Vitamin D	2,000-4,000 IU/day	Muscle function
Creatine	3-5 g/day	Muscle strength, function
HMB	3 g/day	Muscle preservation
Omega-3	2,000 mg/day	Muscle protein synthesis
Leucine	2-3 g with meals	Muscle protein synthesis

Instructions:

Resistance training 2-3x/week (MOST IMPORTANT intervention)
Protein at every meal
Vitamin D supplementation
Prevent falls: balance exercises, home safety
Regular strength assessment
Adequate sleep
Avoid prolonged bed rest


B. MALNUTRITION IN ELDERLY (EXPANDED)

Risk Factors:

Decreased appetite
Dental problems
Difficulty swallowing
Medications affecting appetite
Social isolation
Depression
Financial constraints
Limited mobility
Chronic diseases
Cognitive decline

Foods to EAT:

Nutrient-dense foods
Easy-to-prepare meals
Soft foods if chewing difficulty
Fortified foods
Adequate fluids
Small, frequent meals
Calorie-dense foods: avocado, nut butters, olive oil, cheese
Protein at every meal

Instructions:

Screen for malnutrition regularly (MNA - Mini Nutritional Assessment)
Address barriers: dental care, meal delivery, social dining
Home-delivered meals (Meals on Wheels)
Oral nutritional supplements (Ensure, Boost)
Multivitamin supplementation
Regular weight monitoring
Involve family and caregivers


C. DYSPHAGIA (SWALLOWING DIFFICULTY) (EXPANDED)

Foods to EAT:

Pureed foods: smooth consistency
Thickened liquids (as prescribed by speech therapist)
Moist, soft foods
Smooth textures
Honey-thick, nectar-thick, or pudding-thick liquids (as prescribed)
Foods that hold together (avoid crumbly)
High-calorie, high-protein purees

Foods to AVOID:

Thin liquids (if on thickened liquids)
Dry, crumbly foods
Mixed textures (liquid with chunks)
Tough, chewy foods
Sticky foods: peanut butter, caramel
Bread (unless moistened)
Nuts, seeds
Raw vegetables

Instructions:

Speech-language pathologist assessment
Modified diet textures as prescribed
Chin tuck technique
Small bites and sips
Upright positioning during and 30 min after meals
Aspiration precautions
Oral care before meals (improves swallow reflex)
Adequate time for meals
Thickener products for liquids


D. DEMENTIA NUTRITION (EXPANDED)

Foods to EAT:

MIND diet foods (see previous guide Section 11A)
Easy-to-eat foods: finger foods, soft textures
Nutrient-dense foods
Adequate hydration (often forgotten)
Familiar foods
Colorful foods (visual cues)

Foods to AVOID:

Difficult-to-eat foods
Excessive alcohol
Excessive sugar

Instructions:

Supervised mealtimes
Simple meal presentations
Consistent meal routine
Finger foods for independence
Adaptive utensils
Adequate hydration reminders
Monitor weight
Address swallowing difficulties
Manage behavioral eating issues


E. OSTEOPOROSIS (See previous guide Section 10A and 41)

F. URINARY INCONTINENCE IN ELDERLY

Foods to EAT:

Adequate fluids (don't restrict - worsens incontinence)
Fiber-rich foods (prevent constipation - worsens incontinence)
Magnesium-rich foods
Vitamin D-rich foods

Foods to AVOID (Bladder Irritants):

Caffeine
Alcohol
Carbonated beverages
Artificial sweeteners
Citrus fruits
Tomatoes
Spicy foods
Chocolate

Instructions:

Pelvic floor exercises (Kegels)
Bladder training
Timed voiding
Medications if prescribed
Incontinence products
Pelvic floor physical therapy


G. CONSTIPATION IN ELDERLY (EXPANDED)

Foods to EAT:

High-fiber foods: 25-30 g/day
Prunes and prune juice (natural laxative)
Kiwi fruit (2 daily shown to help)
Warm water (especially in morning)
Flaxseeds (ground)
Adequate fluids: 6-8 glasses/day
Probiotic-rich foods
Olive oil (1 tbsp on empty stomach)

Foods to AVOID:

Processed foods
White bread, white rice
Excessive dairy
Excessive bananas (unripe)
Dehydrating beverages

Instructions:

Increase fiber gradually
Adequate fluids
Regular physical activity
Establish toilet routine
Review medications (many cause constipation)
Stool softeners if needed
Osmotic laxatives: PEG 3350
Avoid stimulant laxatives long-term


H. DEHYDRATION IN ELDERLY

Foods to EAT:

Water-rich fruits: watermelon, grapes, oranges, strawberries
Water-rich vegetables: cucumber, celery, lettuce, zucchini
Soups and broths
Popsicles
Jell-O
Adequate water throughout day

Instructions:

Monitor fluid intake
Offer fluids frequently
Track urine color (pale yellow = adequate)
Address barriers: mobility, incontinence fear, medications
IV fluids if severe


18. WEIGHT MANAGEMENT & BARIATRIC NUTRITION

A. MEDICAL WEIGHT MANAGEMENT

Foods to EAT:

Non-starchy vegetables (unlimited)
Lean protein (quarter of plate)
Whole grains (quarter of plate)
Healthy fats (in moderation)
High-fiber foods
Water-rich foods
Low-calorie-dense foods
Small, frequent meals

Foods to AVOID:

Highly processed foods
Sugar-sweetened beverages
Fried foods
Large portions
Liquid calories
Excessive snacking

Instructions:

Create calorie deficit of 500-750 cal/day
Track food intake
Regular exercise: 150-300 min/week
Behavioral therapy
Medications if prescribed: semaglutide (Wegovy), tirzepatide (Zepbound), phentermine-topiramate
Regular monitoring
Address emotional eating


B. PRE-BARIATRIC SURGERY NUTRITION

Foods to EAT:

Liver-shrinking diet (2-4 weeks before surgery):
High protein: 60-120g/day
Low carbohydrate
Low fat
800-1,200 calories/day
Protein shakes
Lean protein
Non-starchy vegetables
Sugar-free, non-carbonated beverages

Foods to AVOID:

Sugar
Fried foods
High-fat foods
Carbonated beverages
Alcohol
Bread, pasta, rice

Instructions:

Strict adherence to pre-op diet (reduces liver size for safer surgery)
Vitamin and mineral supplementation
Psychological evaluation
Smoking cessation
Regular pre-op appointments


C. POST-BARIATRIC SURGERY NUTRITION

Phase 1 (Days 1-14): Clear Liquids

Water, broth, sugar-free gelatin, sugar-free popsicles
Protein shakes (clear)
1-2 oz at a time
Sip, don't gulp

Phase 2 (Days 14-28): Full Liquids

Protein shakes
Skim milk
Strained soups
Sugar-free yogurt (thin)
2-4 oz at a time

Phase 3 (Weeks 4-8): Pureed Foods

Pureed lean protein: eggs, chicken, fish, cottage cheese
Pureed vegetables
Mashed fruits
2-4 oz at a time
Protein goal: 60-80g/day

Phase 4 (Weeks 8-12): Soft Foods

Soft, moist proteins
Well-cooked vegetables
Soft fruits
Low-fat cheese
4-6 oz at a time

Phase 5 (3+ Months): Regular Diet

Lean protein first (eat protein first at every meal)
Then vegetables
Then complex carbohydrates
1 cup total per meal
Protein goal: 60-80g/day

Lifelong Guidelines:

Protein first at every meal
Small portions: 1/2 to 1 cup per meal
Chew thoroughly (20-30 chews per bite)
No drinking 30 minutes before or after meals
No drinking with meals
No carbonated beverages
No sugar
No fried foods
Take bariatric multivitamins for life
Regular follow-up with bariatric team
Monitor for nutritional deficiencies

Lifelong Supplements:

Supplement	Dose	Purpose
Bariatric multivitamin	2x/day	Overall nutrition
Calcium citrate	1,200-1,500 mg/day (divided)	Bone health
Vitamin B12	1,000 mcg/day (sublingual) or monthly injection	Prevent deficiency
Iron	18-36 mg/day (women), 18 mg/day (men)	Prevent anemia
Vitamin D	3,000-5,000 IU/day	Bone health
Vitamin A	5,000-10,000 IU/day	Vision, immune
B-complex	Daily	Energy, nerve function
Omega-3	1,000-2,000 mg/day	Heart health
Probiotics	Multi-strain	Gut health


19. WOUND HEALING & SURGICAL RECOVERY

A. GENERAL WOUND HEALING NUTRITION

Foods to EAT:

PROTEIN: 1.2-1.5 g/kg/day (or higher for large wounds)
Eggs, chicken, fish, lean meat, dairy, legumes
Vitamin C: 250-500 mg/day (collagen synthesis)
Citrus, bell peppers, kiwi, strawberries, broccoli
Vitamin A: 10,000 IU/day (cell growth, immune function)
Sweet potatoes, carrots, liver, leafy greens
Zinc: 15-40 mg/day (cell division, immune function)
Oysters, beef, pumpkin seeds, chickpeas
Iron: adequate (oxygen transport to wound)
Red meat, beans, spinach
Vitamin E: 200-400 IU/day (antioxidant, skin health)
Almonds, sunflower seeds, avocado
Omega-3 fatty acids (anti-inflammatory)
Adequate calories (healing requires energy)
Adequate hydration (tissue perfusion)
Arginine-rich foods: turkey, chicken, pumpkin seeds, soybeans (collagen synthesis, immune function)
Glutamine-rich foods: bone broth, beef, chicken, fish (gut healing, immune function)

Foods to AVOID:

Excessive sugar (impairs immune function, promotes inflammation)
Alcohol (impairs healing)
Processed foods
Trans fats
Excessive caffeine (dehydrating)
Smoking (MAJORLY impairs healing - reduces blood flow)

Supplements:

Supplement	Dose	Purpose
Vitamin C	500-1,000 mg 2x/day	Collagen synthesis
Zinc	15-40 mg/day	Cell division
Vitamin A	10,000 IU/day (short-term)	Cell growth
Vitamin E	200-400 IU/day	Antioxidant
Glutamine	5-10 g/day	Gut healing, immune
Arginine	5-10 g/day	Collagen synthesis
Protein supplement	As needed	Amino acids
Probiotics	Multi-strain	Gut healing


B. POST-SURGICAL RECOVERY

Foods to EAT:

Clear liquids immediately after surgery (as tolerated)
Progress to soft foods, then regular diet
High protein: 1.5-2.0 g/kg/day
High calorie
Small, frequent meals
Adequate fluids
Anti-inflammatory foods
Vitamin C-rich foods
Zinc-rich foods
Iron-rich foods (if blood loss occurred)

Foods to AVOID:

Gas-producing foods initially: beans, cabbage, carbonated beverages
Spicy foods (GI irritation)
High-fiber foods initially (after abdominal surgery)
Alcohol
Processed foods
Excessive sugar

Instructions:

Follow surgeon's dietary instructions
Gradual diet progression
Adequate protein for tissue repair
Pain management
Wound care
Activity restrictions
Follow-up appointments
Watch for signs of infection


C. BURN RECOVERY

Foods to EAT:

VERY HIGH calorie: 1.5-2x normal needs (burns dramatically increase metabolism)
VERY HIGH protein: 1.5-2.0 g/kg/day
Vitamin C: 500-2,000 mg/day
Zinc: 15-40 mg/day
Vitamin A: 10,000-25,000 IU/day
Vitamin E: 400 IU/day
Arginine: 10-20 g/day
Glutamine: 10-20 g/day
Omega-3 fatty acids
Small, frequent meals
Nutritional supplements (Ensure, Boost)
TPN if unable to eat

Instructions:

Nutritional support is CRITICAL for burn recovery
May need feeding tube
Regular nutritional assessment
Monitor for infection
Physical therapy
Psychological support


20. PREVENTIVE HEALTH & WELLNESS

A. IMMUNE SYSTEM OPTIMIZATION

Foods to EAT:

Vitamin C-rich foods: citrus, bell peppers, kiwi, strawberries, broccoli
Vitamin D-rich foods: fatty fish, eggs, fortified milk, sunlight
Zinc-rich foods: oysters, pumpkin seeds, beef, chickpeas
Vitamin E-rich foods: almonds, sunflower seeds, avocado
Vitamin A-rich foods: sweet potatoes, carrots, liver, leafy greens
Selenium-rich foods: Brazil nuts (1-2/day), fish, eggs
Probiotic-rich foods: yogurt, kefir, kimchi, sauerkraut, miso
Prebiotic-rich foods: garlic, onions, asparagus, bananas, oats
Garlic and onions (allicin, quercetin)
Ginger and turmeric
Green tea (catechins)
Mushrooms: shiitake, maitake, reishi (beta-glucans)
Elderberry
Adequate sleep (7-9 hours)
Regular exercise
Stress management

Foods to AVOID:

Excessive sugar (suppresses immune function for hours)
Excessive alcohol
Processed foods
Trans fats
Excessive saturated fat
Smoking


B. LIVER HEALTH OPTIMIZATION

Foods to EAT:

Cruciferous vegetables: broccoli, cauliflower, cabbage (support detoxification)
Beets and beet juice (betalains support liver function)
Garlic (allicin, selenium)
Grapefruit (naringenin, naringin)
Green tea
Turmeric
Olive oil
Avocado
Walnuts
Fatty fish (omega-3)
Coffee (2-3 cups/day - protective)
Leafy greens
Citrus fruits
Apples (pectin)
Artichokes (cynarin stimulates bile production)

Foods to AVOID:

Alcohol (or limit significantly)
Excessive sugar (fructose especially - causes fatty liver)
Fried foods
Processed foods
Excessive acetaminophen (Tylenol)
Trans fats
Excessive red meat
High-fructose corn syrup
Contaminated foods (moldy grains, aflatoxins)


C. GUT HEALTH OPTIMIZATION

Foods to EAT:

Probiotic-rich foods: yogurt, kefir, kimchi, sauerkraut, miso, kombucha, tempeh
Prebiotic-rich foods: garlic, onions, leeks, asparagus, bananas, oats, apples, flaxseeds
Fiber-rich foods: 25-35g/day
Polyphenol-rich foods: berries, dark chocolate, green tea, red wine (moderate)
Fermented foods
Bone broth (glutamine for gut lining)
Colorful vegetables
Whole grains
Diverse plant foods (aim for 30+ different plants per week)

Foods to AVOID:

Excessive sugar (feeds harmful bacteria)
Artificial sweeteners (may disrupt microbiome)
Excessive alcohol
Processed foods
Excessive antibiotics (when not necessary)
Excessive red meat
Trans fats
Emulsifiers in processed foods (may damage gut lining)


D. BRAIN HEALTH OPTIMIZATION

Foods to EAT:

MIND diet foods (see previous guide)
Fatty fish (DHA - brain structure)
Blueberries (anthocyanins)
Turmeric (curcumin)
Dark chocolate (flavonoids)
Nuts: walnuts (omega-3), almonds (vitamin E)
Leafy greens (folate, vitamin K)
Coffee (moderate - caffeine and polyphenols)
Green tea (L-theanine, EGCG)
Eggs (choline)
Avocado (healthy fats)
Extra virgin olive oil
Berries
Whole grains

Foods to AVOID:

Trans fats (associated with brain shrinkage)
Excessive sugar (impairs memory)
Highly processed foods
Excessive alcohol (brain damage)
Excessive saturated fat
Refined carbohydrates
Artificial sweeteners (in excess)


E. HEART HEALTH OPTIMIZATION

Foods to EAT:

Mediterranean diet foods
Fatty fish: 2-3 servings/week
Olive oil (extra virgin)
Nuts: walnuts, almonds
Legumes
Whole grains
Colorful fruits and vegetables
Berries
Dark chocolate (70%+ cocoa, in moderation)
Green tea
Garlic
Avocado
Fiber-rich foods: 25-35g/day

Foods to AVOID:

Trans fats (eliminate completely)
Excessive saturated fat (<6% of calories)
Excessive sodium (<2,300 mg/day, ideally <1,500 mg)
Sugar-sweetened beverages
Processed meats
Excessive red meat
Refined carbohydrates
Excessive alcohol
Fried foods


F. CANCER PREVENTION (EXPANDED)

Foods to EAT:

Cruciferous vegetables: broccoli, cauliflower, Brussels sprouts, cabbage (sulforaphane, indoles)
Berries: blueberries, raspberries, strawberries (anthocyanins, ellagic acid)
Tomatoes (lycopene - especially cooked)
Allium vegetables: garlic, onions, leeks (organosulfur compounds)
Green tea (EGCG)
Turmeric (curcumin)
Fiber-rich foods (colorectal cancer prevention)
Fatty fish (omega-3)
Whole grains
Legumes
Mushrooms: shiitake, maitake (beta-glucans)
Ginger
Brazil nuts (selenium)
Pomegranate
Leafy greens
Carrots and sweet potatoes (beta-carotene)

Foods to AVOID:

Processed meats (Group 1 carcinogen - WHO classification)
Red meat: limit to <18 oz/week
Alcohol (increases risk of multiple cancers)
Highly processed foods
Sugar-sweetened beverages
Refined carbohydrates
Charred or heavily grilled meats (HCAs, PAHs)
Excessively salty and preserved foods (stomach cancer)
Very hot beverages (esophageal cancer)
Aflatoxin-contaminated foods (moldy peanuts, corn)

Lifestyle Factors:

Maintain healthy weight
Exercise: 150+ minutes/week
Don't smoke
Limit alcohol
Sun protection
Vaccinations: HPV, Hepatitis B
