Regular cancer screenings
Breastfeeding (reduces breast cancer risk)


G. BONE HEALTH OPTIMIZATION

Foods to EAT:

Calcium: 1,000-1,200 mg/day
Vitamin D: 800-2,000 IU/day
Vitamin K: leafy greens
Magnesium: nuts, seeds, dark chocolate
Phosphorus: meat, dairy, fish
Protein: adequate
Vitamin C: citrus, bell peppers
Prunes: 5-6 daily (bone density)
Soy foods (isoflavones)

Foods to AVOID:

Excessive sodium
Excessive caffeine
Excessive alcohol
Carbonated cola drinks
Excessive vitamin A supplements


H. SKIN HEALTH OPTIMIZATION

Foods to EAT:

Vitamin C-rich foods (collagen synthesis)
Vitamin E-rich foods (skin protection)
Omega-3 fatty acids (skin moisture)
Vitamin A-rich foods (skin cell turnover)
Zinc-rich foods (wound healing)
Selenium-rich foods (UV protection)
Lycopene-rich foods (sun protection)
Beta-carotene-rich foods (sun protection)
Water: adequate hydration
Bone broth (collagen)
Avocado (healthy fats, vitamin E)
Dark chocolate (flavonoids - improve skin hydration)
Green tea (EGCG - anti-aging)

Foods to AVOID:

Excessive sugar (glycation - damages collagen)
Processed foods
Excessive alcohol (dehydrating)
Trans fats
Excessive dairy (in some - acne)
Fried foods
Excessive caffeine (dehydrating)


21. QUICK REFERENCE TABLES

Autism	Gut-brain supportive	Omega-3, probiotics, magnesium	Gluten/casein (in some), artificial additives	Omega-3, D, Probiotics
Heavy Metal Toxicity	Detox-supportive	Cruciferous vegetables, garlic, cilantro	Contaminated foods, high-mercury fish	Selenium, Fiber

Iron	Fatigue, pallor, shortness of breath, brittle nails	Red meat, lentils, spinach	325 mg ferrous sulfate

Isoniazid	Vitamin B6	Supplement pyridoxine

Oral contraceptives	B6, Folate, B12, Mg	Consider supplementation

Cholestyramine	Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K)	Supplement separately

BREAKFAST DISHES


1. IDLI

Nutritional Note: Low-calorie, low-fat, rich in carbohydrates and protein from the rice-lentil combination. Fermentation increases B-vitamins and improves digestibility. Excellent breakfast option for weight management.


Ingredients:

2 cups parboiled rice (idli rice)
1 cup urad dal (split black gram)
1/2 teaspoon fenugreek seeds
Salt to taste
Water as needed
Oil for greasing

Detailed Recipe:
Wash the parboiled rice thoroughly in running water 3-4 times until the water runs clear, then soak it in plenty of water for at least 6-8 hours or overnight. Separately, wash the urad dal and fenugreek seeds together and soak them in water for the same duration. After soaking, drain the urad dal and grind it in a wet grinder or high-speed blender, adding water little by little, until you get a very smooth, fluffy, and airy batter that should increase in volume significantly. Transfer this to a large vessel. Next, drain the rice and grind it to a slightly coarse but smooth paste with minimal water, keeping the batter thick. Combine both batters together in a large vessel, add salt, and mix thoroughly using your hands as the warmth of your hands aids fermentation. Cover the vessel with a lid and leave it in a warm place for 8-12 hours to ferment; the batter should rise to almost double in volume and have a slightly sour aroma. Once fermented, gently stir the batter without deflating it too much. Grease idli moulds with a little oil, pour a ladleful of batter into each mould, and steam in an idli steamer or pressure cooker (without the weight) for 10-12 minutes on medium-high heat. Remove from the steamer, let cool for 2 minutes, then gently unmould with a wet spoon. Serve hot with coconut chutney and sambar. The fermentation process produces beneficial probiotics, increases bioavailability of iron and other minerals, and makes the batter easy to digest even for those with sensitive stomachs.



2. DOSA

Nutritional Note: Crispy fermented crepe rich in carbohydrates and protein. Fermentation enhances B-vitamins and mineral absorption. Lower in calories than most breads. Can be made healthier by using less oil.


Ingredients:

3 cups parboiled rice
1 cup urad dal
1/2 cup poha (flattened rice)
1 teaspoon fenugreek seeds
Salt to taste
Oil or ghee for cooking

Detailed Recipe:
Soak the parboiled rice and poha together in water for 6-8 hours, and separately soak the urad dal with fenugreek seeds for the same duration. Grind the urad dal first in a wet grinder or blender until it becomes a smooth, fluffy paste, adding water gradually, and transfer to a large vessel. Then grind the soaked rice and poha together to a smooth but slightly grainy batter, using minimal water to keep the batter thick enough to pour. Combine both batters, add salt, and mix well with your hands to incorporate air and aid fermentation. Cover and let ferment in a warm place for 8-12 hours until the batter doubles in volume and develops a pleasant sour smell. After fermentation, gently stir the batter; if it is too thick, add a small amount of water to achieve a pourable but not watery consistency. Heat a cast iron tawa or non-stick pan on high heat until very hot, then reduce to medium, sprinkle a few drops of water to check readiness (it should sizzle and evaporate immediately). Wipe the tawa with a cut onion dipped in oil. Pour a ladleful of batter in the center and quickly spread it in a circular motion from the center outward to form a thin crepe. Drizzle a teaspoon of oil or ghee around the edges and on the surface. Cook on medium-high heat for 2-3 minutes until the edges turn golden brown and start lifting from the tawa. Using a flat spatula, gently fold the dosa in half or roll it. Serve immediately with coconut chutney, tomato chutney, and sambar. For extra crispiness, use a well-seasoned cast iron tawa and ensure the batter is properly fermented.



3. UTTAPAM

Nutritional Note: Thicker version of dosa, loaded with vegetables. Rich in carbohydrates, protein, and vitamins from the vegetable toppings. Good source of fiber when topped with onions, tomatoes, and peppers.


Ingredients:

2 cups dosa batter (fermented)
1 large onion, finely chopped
2 tomatoes, finely chopped
2 green chillies, finely chopped
1/4 cup grated carrot
1/4 cup chopped coriander leaves
1/4 cup capsicum, finely chopped
Salt to taste
Oil for cooking

Detailed Recipe:
Prepare or use leftover fermented dosa batter; the batter for uttapam can be slightly thicker than regular dosa batter, so if it has thinned out after fermentation, do not add extra water. Heat a cast iron tawa or non-stick pan on medium heat and drizzle a little oil, spreading it evenly. Pour a thick ladleful of batter onto the tawa and gently spread it into a thick circle about 6-7 inches in diameter, keeping it thicker than a dosa. Immediately sprinkle the chopped onion, tomato, green chillies, grated carrot, capsicum, and coriander leaves evenly over the top surface. Press the toppings gently into the batter with a spatula so they adhere well. Drizzle about a teaspoon of oil around the edges and over the toppings. Cover with a lid and cook on medium-low heat for 3-4 minutes until the bottom turns golden brown and the edges look cooked. Flip the uttapam carefully using a wide spatula and cook the other side for another 2-3 minutes until the vegetable side gets lightly browned and the toppings are cooked through. The uttapam should be soft and spongy inside with a crispy exterior. Serve hot with coconut chutney, tomato chutney, or sambar. You can customize toppings with grated cheese, paneer crumbles, sweet corn, or any vegetables of your choice. This is an excellent way to sneak vegetables into a child's breakfast.



4. POHA (FLATTENED RICE)

Nutritional Note: Light, easily digestible breakfast. Low in gluten, rich in iron (especially if using thicker poha), carbohydrates for quick energy. Adding peanuts and vegetables increases protein and fiber content. Suitable for all ages including elderly and sick.


Ingredients:

2 cups thick poha (flattened rice / beaten rice)
2 tablespoons peanuts
1 medium onion, finely chopped
1 small potato, finely diced
2 green chillies, slit
1/2 teaspoon mustard seeds
1/2 teaspoon cumin seeds
1/4 teaspoon turmeric powder
8-10 curry leaves
2 tablespoons oil
Salt to taste
1 tablespoon lemon juice
2 tablespoons fresh coriander, chopped
1 tablespoon sugar (optional)
Sev for garnish (optional)

Detailed Recipe:
Place the thick poha in a large strainer or colander and rinse it under running water for about 30 seconds, gently tossing it with your hands to ensure even washing; the poha should become soft but not mushy. Let it drain completely for 5-10 minutes, then sprinkle a little salt and turmeric powder over it and gently toss to combine, keeping the poha aside. Heat oil in a large pan or kadhai on medium heat, add the peanuts and fry them until they turn golden brown and crunchy, then remove and set aside. In the same oil, add mustard seeds and let them splutter, then add cumin seeds, curry leaves, and slit green chillies, sautéing for 30 seconds until fragrant. Add the finely chopped onion and sauté for 2-3 minutes until translucent and soft. Add the diced potato, mix well, cover and cook for 4-5 minutes on low heat until the potato pieces are tender and cooked through. Add the prepared poha to the pan along with the fried peanuts, sugar if using, and additional salt if needed. Gently mix everything together using a spatula, being careful not to break the poha flakes. Cook on low heat for 2-3 minutes, tossing gently, allowing the flavors to meld together. Remove from heat, squeeze fresh lemon juice over the top, garnish with chopped coriander leaves and sev if desired. Serve immediately while warm. Poha is a staple breakfast in Maharashtra and Madhya Pradesh, and each region has its own variation; some add grated coconut, pomegranate seeds, or fresh peas for extra nutrition and color.



5. UPMA

Nutritional Note: Made from semolina (rava), rich in carbohydrates and quick to prepare. Adding vegetables increases fiber and vitamin content. A filling breakfast that provides sustained energy. Good source of B-vitamins when made with whole wheat rava.


Ingredients:

1 cup rava (semolina / sooji)
2.5 cups water
1 medium onion, finely chopped
1 small carrot, finely diced
1/4 cup green peas
2 green chillies, slit
1 inch ginger, finely chopped
1/2 teaspoon mustard seeds
1/2 teaspoon urad dal
1/2 teaspoon chana dal
8-10 curry leaves
2 tablespoons oil or ghee
2 tablespoons cashew nuts
Salt to taste
1 tablespoon lemon juice
2 tablespoons fresh coriander, chopped

Detailed Recipe:
Dry roast the rava in a pan on medium-low heat for 4-5 minutes, stirring continuously until it turns light golden and aromatic; this step is crucial to prevent the upma from becoming sticky. Remove the roasted rava and set it aside. In the same pan, heat oil or ghee on medium heat, add cashew nuts and fry until golden, then remove and set aside. Add mustard seeds to the hot oil and let them splutter, then add urad dal and chana dal, frying until they turn light golden brown. Add curry leaves, slit green chillies, and chopped ginger, sautéing for 30 seconds. Add the chopped onion and sauté for 2-3 minutes until translucent. Add the diced carrot and green peas, stir well, and cook for 2-3 minutes. Now add 2.5 cups of water and salt, bring to a rolling boil on high heat. Once boiling, reduce heat to low and add the roasted rava gradually in a steady stream with one hand while stirring vigorously with the other hand using a whisk or spatula to prevent lumps from forming. Continue stirring for 1-2 minutes until the rava absorbs all the water and the upma pulls away from the sides of the pan. Cover with a lid and cook on the lowest heat for 3-4 minutes. Remove from heat, add lemon juice, fried cashews, and chopped coriander, fluff gently with a fork. Serve hot with coconut chutney or as is. For a healthier version, use whole wheat rava or millet rava instead of refined semolina.



6. PARATHA (ALOO PARATHA)

Nutritional Note: Stuffed flatbread providing carbohydrates from wheat and potassium/vitamin C from potatoes. Higher in calories due to ghee/oil. Can be made healthier by using less oil and adding vegetables to the stuffing.


Ingredients:For Dough:

2 cups whole wheat flour (atta)
1 tablespoon oil
Salt to taste
Water as needed

For Stuffing:

4 medium potatoes, boiled and mashed
1 small onion, finely chopped
2 green chillies, finely chopped
1/2 teaspoon red chilli powder
1/2 teaspoon garam masala
1/2 teaspoon amchur (dry mango powder)
1/2 teaspoon cumin powder
2 tablespoons fresh coriander, chopped
Salt to taste

Detailed Recipe:
Prepare the dough by combining whole wheat flour, salt, and oil in a large bowl, mixing with your fingers until the oil is incorporated and the flour resembles breadcrumbs. Gradually add warm water and knead for 8-10 minutes to form a soft, pliable, smooth dough that does not stick to your hands. Cover with a damp cloth and let it rest for 20-30 minutes. Meanwhile, prepare the stuffing: boil the potatoes until fork-tender, peel and mash them thoroughly ensuring there are no lumps. Add finely chopped onion, green chillies, red chilli powder, garam masala, amchur, cumin powder, chopped coriander, and salt. Mix everything together thoroughly and divide into 6-8 equal portions. To assemble, take a portion of dough, roll it into a small circle about 3 inches in diameter, place a portion of the potato stuffing in the center, gather the edges of the dough around the filling and pinch to seal completely. Flatten gently with your palms, dust with dry flour, and carefully roll out into a circle about 6-7 inches in diameter, applying even pressure so the stuffing does not break through. Heat a tawa on medium-high heat, place the rolled paratha on it, cook for about 1 minute until small bubbles appear on the surface, flip, apply ghee or oil generously on the cooked side, flip again, apply ghee on the other side, and press gently with a spatula until both sides are golden brown with crispy spots. Serve hot with yogurt, pickle, and butter. Aloo paratha is the quintessential Punjabi breakfast enjoyed across India.



7. THEPLA

Nutritional Note: Gujarati flatbread made with wheat flour and fenugreek leaves. Rich in iron, calcium, fiber, and vitamins A and C from methi leaves. Good travel food as it stays fresh for days. Excellent for increasing greens intake.


Ingredients:

2 cups whole wheat flour
1 cup fresh methi (fenugreek) leaves, washed and chopped
1/2 teaspoon turmeric powder
1 teaspoon red chilli powder
1/2 teaspoon cumin powder
1/2 teaspoon coriander powder
1 tablespoon sesame seeds
1 tablespoon yogurt
1 tablespoon oil
1 teaspoon sugar
Salt to taste
Water as needed
Oil for cooking

Detailed Recipe:
Wash the methi leaves thoroughly, chop them finely, and sprinkle a little salt over them, mixing gently and setting aside for 10 minutes; this draws out excess moisture and reduces bitterness. Squeeze out the excess water from the methi leaves by pressing them between your palms. In a large mixing bowl, combine the whole wheat flour, squeezed methi leaves, turmeric powder, red chilli powder, cumin powder, coriander powder, sesame seeds, yogurt, oil, sugar, and salt. Mix all the dry ingredients together with your fingers, then gradually add water as needed and knead into a smooth, firm dough that is slightly tighter than regular chapati dough; the methi leaves will release some moisture so be careful not to add too much water. Cover with a damp cloth and rest for 15-20 minutes. Divide the dough into 8-10 equal portions and roll each into a ball. Take one ball, dust with dry flour, and roll out into a thin circle about 7-8 inches in diameter; thepla should be rolled thinner than paratha. Heat a tawa on medium-high heat, place the rolled thepla on it, cook for about 30 seconds until small bubbles appear, flip and drizzle a little oil on the cooked side, flip again and apply oil on the other side, pressing gently with a spatula. Cook until both sides have golden brown spots and the thepla is cooked through. Serve hot with yogurt, pickle, or sweet mango chutney. Thepla can be stored for 2-3 days at room temperature wrapped in a cloth, making it ideal for travel, tiffin boxes, and picnics.



8. APPAM

Nutritional Note: South Indian (Kerala) fermented rice pancake with a soft, spongy center and crispy edges. Fermentation increases B-vitamins and probiotic content. Low in fat when cooked without excess oil. Rich in carbohydrates.


Ingredients:

2 cups raw rice (pachari or any short-grain rice)
1/2 cup cooked rice
1 cup grated coconut
1/2 teaspoon active dry yeast
1 tablespoon sugar
1/2 teaspoon salt
Water as needed
Coconut oil for cooking

Detailed Recipe:
Soak the raw rice in water for 4-6 hours. Grind the soaked rice together with the cooked rice and grated coconut in a wet grinder or blender, adding water gradually to form a smooth, thick batter similar to dosa batter consistency. In a small bowl, dissolve the active dry yeast and sugar in 2 tablespoons of warm water and let it sit for 5-10 minutes until frothy and activated. Add this yeast mixture to the rice batter along with salt, mix thoroughly, cover with a lid, and allow to ferment in a warm place for 6-8 hours or overnight; the batter should rise and become bubbly with a slightly sweet-sour aroma. After fermentation, the batter should be of pouring consistency; if too thick, add a little water. Heat a special appam chatti (a small deep round pan with a lid) or a small non-stick pan on medium heat. Grease lightly with coconut oil, pour a ladleful of batter into the center, and immediately lift the pan and swirl it in a circular motion so the batter coats the sides while remaining thick in the center. Cover with a lid and cook for 2-3 minutes on medium-low heat until the edges become thin, crispy, and golden brown while the center remains soft, spongy, and slightly fluffy. Do not flip the appam; it cooks only on one side. Gently remove from the pan using a spatula. Serve hot with vegetable stew, chicken curry, egg curry, or sweetened coconut milk. Appam is a beloved Kerala breakfast that pairs beautifully with both sweet and savory accompaniments.



9. PUTTU

Nutritional Note: Steamed Kerala breakfast made with rice flour and coconut. Gluten-free, low in fat, and easily digestible. Coconut provides healthy medium-chain fatty acids. High in fiber when made with brown rice flour.


Ingredients:

2 cups rice flour (puttu podi)
1 cup grated fresh coconut
3/4 cup water (approximately)
1/2 teaspoon salt
A puttu maker or steamer

Detailed Recipe:
Take the rice flour in a large mixing bowl and add salt. Gradually sprinkle warm water over the flour, mixing with your fingers after each addition, until the flour reaches a crumbly, moist texture that holds together when pressed in your fist but crumbles apart easily; this is the perfect consistency and it is crucial not to make it too wet. Some people also lightly steam or dry roast the rice flour before using to improve texture. Let the moistened flour rest for 10-15 minutes so the water is evenly absorbed. Prepare the puttu maker by adding water to the bottom vessel and bringing it to a boil. If using a traditional puttu kutti (cylindrical mould), first add a tablespoon of grated coconut at the bottom, then add 2-3 tablespoons of the rice flour mixture, then another layer of grated coconut, continuing to alternate layers until the cylinder is filled, finishing with a layer of coconut on top. If using a puttu maker with a plate, place the perforated plate inside, add alternating layers of coconut and rice flour. Place the mould on the boiling water vessel, ensuring steam can pass through, and steam for 5-7 minutes on medium-high heat until steam rises steadily from the top and the puttu is cooked through. Carefully push the puttu out of the mould using the wooden stick provided. Serve hot with kadala curry (black chickpea curry), banana, sugar, or papadam. Puttu is a wholesome, light breakfast that is very popular in Kerala and is also given to infants and elderly as it is easy to digest.



10. DHOKLA

Nutritional Note: Gujarati steamed savory cake, very low in calories and fat. Made from gram flour (besan) which is rich in protein and fiber. Steaming makes it one of the healthiest Indian snacks. Good for weight management and diabetic diets.


Ingredients:

1 cup besan (gram flour)
1 tablespoon semolina (rava)
1 tablespoon lemon juice
1 teaspoon sugar
1/2 teaspoon turmeric powder
1 teaspoon ginger-green chilli paste
Salt to taste
3/4 cup water
1 teaspoon fruit salt (Eno) or 1/2 teaspoon baking soda
1 tablespoon oil

For Tempering:

2 tablespoons oil
1 teaspoon mustard seeds
1 teaspoon sesame seeds
2 green chillies, slit
8-10 curry leaves
1/2 cup water
1 tablespoon sugar
1 tablespoon lemon juice
2 tablespoons fresh coriander, chopped
2 tablespoons grated coconut

Detailed Recipe:
In a large mixing bowl, sift the besan to remove lumps, then add semolina, lemon juice, sugar, turmeric powder, ginger-green chilli paste, salt, and oil. Gradually add water and whisk vigorously to form a smooth, lump-free batter with a consistency slightly thicker than pancake batter. Let the batter rest for 15 minutes. Meanwhile, prepare the steamer by filling the bottom with water and bringing it to a rolling boil. Grease a thali or flat steel plate with oil. Just before steaming, add the fruit salt (Eno) to the batter and immediately add 1 tablespoon of lemon juice over it; the batter will froth and rise. Gently fold the batter 2-3 times to incorporate the fruit salt evenly but do not overmix or the batter will deflate. Pour the frothy batter immediately into the greased plate, filling it to about 1 inch thickness. Place in the steamer, cover, and steam on high heat for 15-18 minutes without opening the lid. To check if done, insert a toothpick in the center; it should come out clean. Remove from steamer and let cool for 5 minutes. Cut into diamond or square pieces. Prepare the tempering: heat oil in a small pan, add mustard seeds and let splutter, add sesame seeds, slit green chillies, and curry leaves, sauté for 30 seconds. Add water, sugar, and lemon juice, bring to a boil, then pour this tempering evenly over the cut dhokla pieces. Garnish with chopped coriander and grated coconut. Serve with green chutney. Dhokla is a guilt-free snack that is light, fluffy, and full of flavor.



11. MOONG DAL CHILLA

Nutritional Note: Protein-rich savory pancake made from yellow moong dal. Excellent vegetarian protein source, low in fat, high in fiber. Ideal for diabetic diets, weight management, and muscle building. Quick to prepare and easily digestible.


Ingredients:

1 cup yellow moong dal (split)
2 green chillies
1 inch ginger
1/2 teaspoon cumin seeds
1/4 teaspoon turmeric powder
1/2 teaspoon red chilli powder
2 tablespoons finely chopped onion
2 tablespoons finely chopped tomato
2 tablespoons fresh coriander, chopped
Salt to taste
Water as needed
Oil for cooking

Detailed Recipe:
Wash the moong dal thoroughly 3-4 times and soak it in water for 3-4 hours; this soaking step is essential as it softens the dal and makes it easier to grind while also improving digestibility. After soaking, drain the water completely. Grind the soaked moong dal together with green chillies, ginger, and cumin seeds in a blender, adding minimal water (just 2-3 tablespoons) to form a smooth, thick batter that is slightly coarser than dosa batter. Transfer the batter to a bowl, add turmeric powder, red chilli powder, salt, chopped onion, chopped tomato, and fresh coriander. Mix everything well. The batter should be of pouring consistency; add a little water if too thick. Heat a non-stick tawa or cast iron pan on medium heat, drizzle a few drops of oil, and wipe with a tissue. Pour a ladleful of batter in the center and spread it in a circular motion to form a thin circle about 6-7 inches in diameter. Drizzle a little oil around the edges and on the surface. Cook on medium heat for 2-3 minutes until the bottom turns golden brown and the edges start to lift. Flip carefully and cook the other side for 1-2 minutes until golden spots appear. The chilla should be crispy on the outside and soft inside. Serve hot with green chutney, tomato ketchup, or yogurt. You can also stuff the chilla with grated paneer, crumbled tofu, or sautéed vegetables for a more filling meal. This is one of the healthiest Indian breakfasts and is commonly recommended by nutritionists.



12. BESAN CHILLA

Nutritional Note: Gram flour pancake rich in protein and fiber. Gluten-free, suitable for celiac disease. Low in calories, high in satiety. Good source of iron and B-vitamins from besan.


Ingredients:

1 cup besan (gram flour)
1/4 cup finely chopped onion
1/4 cup finely chopped tomato
2 tablespoons finely chopped capsicum
2 green chillies, finely chopped
2 tablespoons fresh coriander, chopped
1/2 teaspoon red chilli powder
1/2 teaspoon cumin powder
1/4 teaspoon turmeric powder
1/2 teaspoon ajwain (carom seeds)
Salt to taste
3/4 cup water (approximately)
Oil for cooking

Detailed Recipe:
Sieve the besan into a mixing bowl to remove any lumps. Add red chilli powder, cumin powder, turmeric powder, ajwain, and salt, and mix the dry ingredients together. Gradually add water while whisking continuously to form a smooth, lump-free batter with a consistency slightly thinner than pancake batter; it should flow easily when poured. Add the chopped onion, tomato, capsicum, green chillies, and fresh coriander to the batter and mix well. Let the batter rest for 10-15 minutes to allow the besan to absorb the water and the flavors to meld. Heat a non-stick tawa or cast iron pan on medium heat, lightly grease with oil. Pour a ladleful of batter in the center and spread it in a circular motion to form a thin, even circle. Drizzle a little oil around the edges. Cook on medium heat for 2-3 minutes until the bottom is golden brown and crisp. Flip and cook the other side for 1-2 minutes. Serve hot with green chutney or yogurt. Besan chilla is a quick, nutritious breakfast that can be prepared in under 15 minutes and is perfect for busy mornings. It is also an excellent option for Navratri fasting.



13. MEDU VADA

Nutritional Note: Crispy South Indian fritter made from urad dal. Good source of protein and iron from urad dal. Deep-fried so higher in calories; can be air-fried for a healthier version. Best paired with sambar for a complete protein meal.


Ingredients:

1 cup urad dal (split black gram, without skin)
1 inch ginger, finely chopped
2 green chillies, finely chopped
1 teaspoon black peppercorns, coarsely crushed
10-12 curry leaves, finely chopped
1 small onion, finely chopped (optional)
1/2 teaspoon cumin seeds
Salt to taste
Oil for deep frying

Detailed Recipe:
Wash the urad dal thoroughly and soak in water for 3-4 hours; do not over-soak as this can make the batter too watery. Drain completely and grind in a wet grinder or high-speed blender, adding very minimal water (just 2-3 tablespoons at a time); the key to crispy vadas is a thick, fluffy batter that is well-aerated. Grind for 15-20 minutes in a wet grinder or 5-7 minutes in a blender until the batter is smooth, fluffy, and light; you can test by dropping a small ball of batter in water - it should float, indicating proper aeration. Transfer the batter to a bowl and beat it vigorously with your hand in a circular motion for 2-3 minutes to incorporate air. Add finely chopped ginger, green chillies, crushed peppercorns, curry leaves, cumin seeds, chopped onion if using, and salt. Mix gently. Heat oil in a deep kadhai for deep frying. Wet your hands with water, take a lemon-sized portion of batter, place it on your palm, flatten slightly, make a hole in the center with your wet thumb (this ensures even cooking), and carefully slide it into the hot oil. Fry 3-4 vadas at a time on medium heat, turning occasionally, until they are deep golden brown and crispy on all sides, about 4-5 minutes. Drain on paper towels to remove excess oil. Serve hot with coconut chutney and sambar. For a healthier version, shape the vadas and air-fry at 180°C for 12-15 minutes, flipping halfway through.



14. POORI WITH ALOO SABZI

Nutritional Note: Deep-fried puffed bread with spiced potato curry. Higher in calories due to frying but provides energy for active mornings. Potatoes provide potassium, vitamin C, and carbohydrates. A traditional North Indian breakfast enjoyed on special occasions.


Ingredients:For Poori:

2 cups whole wheat flour
1 tablespoon oil
1/2 teaspoon salt
Water as needed
Oil for deep frying

For Aloo Sabzi:

4 medium potatoes, boiled and cubed
2 tablespoons oil
1 teaspoon cumin seeds
1/4 teaspoon asafoetida (hing)
1/2 teaspoon turmeric powder
1 teaspoon red chilli powder
1 teaspoon coriander powder
2 green chillies, slit
1 inch ginger, grated
2 cups water
1 tablespoon chopped coriander
Salt to taste

Detailed Recipe:For Poori: Combine wheat flour, salt, and oil in a bowl. Gradually add water and knead into a stiff dough that is harder than chapati dough; a stiff dough ensures puffed pooris. Knead well for 5-7 minutes, cover with a damp cloth, and rest for 15-20 minutes. Divide into small lemon-sized balls. Roll each ball into a small circle about 4-5 inches in diameter, applying even pressure; do not use dry flour as it burns in oil. Heat oil in a deep kadhai to 180-190°C (test by dropping a small piece of dough - it should rise immediately). Gently slide the rolled poori into the hot oil, press lightly with a slotted spoon, and it will puff up beautifully. Flip and cook the other side for a few seconds until golden. Drain on paper towels.


For Aloo Sabzi: Heat oil in a pan, add cumin seeds and asafoetida, let splutter. Add grated ginger and green chillies, sauté for 30 seconds. Add turmeric, red chilli powder, and coriander powder, stir for 10 seconds. Add boiled potato cubes, mix well to coat with spices. Add 2 cups of water, salt, bring to a boil, then simmer for 5-7 minutes, lightly mashing a few potato pieces to thicken the gravy. Garnish with coriander. Serve hot pooris with the aloo sabzi, pickle, and yogurt.



15. RAVA IDLI

Nutritional Note: Quick alternative to regular idli made with semolina. No fermentation required, making it a faster option. Adding vegetables increases nutritional value. Light and easy to digest.


Ingredients:

1 cup rava (semolina / sooji)
1 cup yogurt (curd)
1/2 cup water
1/2 teaspoon eno fruit salt or 1/4 teaspoon baking soda
1/2 teaspoon mustard seeds
1 teaspoon urad dal
1 teaspoon chana dal
1 tablespoon cashew nuts, broken
8-10 curry leaves
2 green chillies, finely chopped
1 tablespoon grated carrot
1 tablespoon chopped coriander
2 tablespoons oil
1/2 teaspoon salt

Detailed Recipe:
Dry roast the rava in a pan on medium-low heat for 3-4 minutes until it turns slightly golden and aromatic; do not let it brown. Transfer to a bowl and let cool. In a small pan, heat oil, add mustard seeds and let splutter, then add urad dal, chana dal, cashew nuts, and curry leaves, frying until the dals turn golden. Pour this tempering over the roasted rava. Add yogurt, water, salt, chopped green chillies, grated carrot, and coriander. Mix well to form a smooth batter with a thick but pourable consistency. Let the batter rest for 15-20 minutes; the rava will absorb moisture and swell. Just before steaming, add eno fruit salt and sprinkle a little water over it; the batter will froth. Gently fold 2-3 times. Grease idli moulds with oil, pour batter into each mould, and steam for 12-15 minutes. Let cool for 2 minutes before unmoulding. Serve with coconut chutney and sambar.



RICE DISHES


16. PLAIN BASMATI RICE

Nutritional Note: Staple carbohydrate source providing quick energy. Basmati rice has a lower glycemic index than other white rices. Naturally gluten-free. Provides B-vitamins and manganese.


Ingredients:

1 cup basmati rice
2 cups water
1/2 teaspoon salt
1 teaspoon ghee or oil (optional)

Detailed Recipe:
Wash the basmati rice gently in 3-4 changes of cold water until the water runs relatively clear; this removes excess surface starch that makes rice sticky. Soak the washed rice in water for 20-30 minutes, then drain completely; soaking allows the grains to absorb water evenly and cook to fluffy perfection. Bring 2 cups of water to a rolling boil in a heavy-bottomed pot with salt and ghee. Add the drained rice to the boiling water, stir once gently to separate the grains, and bring back to a boil. Reduce heat to the lowest setting, cover tightly with a lid, and cook undisturbed for 15-18 minutes; resist the urge to open the lid as steam is essential. After 15 minutes, turn off the heat and let the rice rest, still covered, for 5 minutes; this allows the residual steam to finish cooking the grains. Remove the lid, fluff the rice gently with a fork, separating the grains without breaking them. The rice should be perfectly cooked with each grain separate, long, and fluffy. Serve as a base for any curry, dal, or stir-fry. For extra flavor, you can add a bay leaf, a few cardamom pods, or a cinnamon stick to the boiling water before adding rice.



17. JEERA RICE

Nutritional Note: Cumin-flavored rice that aids digestion. Cumin is rich in iron and has carminative properties. A simple, aromatic rice dish that pairs well with any Indian curry or dal.


Ingredients:

1 cup basmati rice
2 cups water
2 tablespoons ghee or oil
2 teaspoons cumin seeds (jeera)
1 bay leaf
2-3 green cardamom pods
1 small cinnamon stick
2-3 cloves
1/2 teaspoon salt
1 tablespoon chopped coriander for garnish

Detailed Recipe:
Wash and soak the basmati rice for 20-30 minutes, then drain. Heat ghee in a heavy-bottomed pot on medium heat. Add cumin seeds and let them sizzle and turn a shade darker, about 30 seconds; this releases their essential oils and deepens the flavor. Add bay leaf, cardamom, cinnamon, and cloves, sauté for another 20 seconds until fragrant. Add the drained rice and gently stir for 1-2 minutes, coating each grain with the ghee and cumin-infused oil; this toasting step helps keep the grains separate. Add 2 cups of water and salt, bring to a rolling boil. Stir once, reduce heat to the lowest setting, cover tightly with a lid, and cook undisturbed for 15-18 minutes. Turn off heat and let rest for 5 minutes. Remove lid, discard the whole spices if desired, fluff gently with a fork, and garnish with chopped coriander. Serve with dal, paneer butter masala, or any curry of your choice.



18. LEMON RICE

Nutritional Note: South Indian tangy rice dish. Lemon juice provides vitamin C and aids iron absorption. Peanuts add protein. Turmeric provides anti-inflammatory benefits. Light, refreshing, and easy to digest.


Ingredients:

2 cups cooked rice (day-old rice works best)
3 tablespoons lemon juice
2 tablespoons peanuts
2 tablespoons oil
1 teaspoon mustard seeds
1 teaspoon urad dal
1 teaspoon chana dal
2 dried red chillies, broken
2 green chillies, slit
1/4 teaspoon asafoetida
1/2 teaspoon turmeric powder
10-12 curry leaves
1 tablespoon cashew nuts
Salt to taste
2 tablespoons fresh coriander, chopped

Detailed Recipe:
Spread the cooked rice on a plate and allow it to cool completely; using day-old refrigerated rice works best as it has less moisture and the grains stay separate. Heat oil in a large pan on medium heat, add peanuts and fry until golden, then add cashew nuts and fry until light brown; remove both and set aside. In the same oil, add mustard seeds and let them splutter, then add urad dal and chana dal, frying until golden brown. Add dried red chillies, green chillies, curry leaves, and asafoetida, sautéing for 30 seconds. Add turmeric powder and stir for 10 seconds. Turn off the heat and add the cooled rice, salt, and lemon juice. Mix everything gently but thoroughly, ensuring the rice is evenly coated with the turmeric-yellow tempering and lemon juice. Add the fried peanuts and cashews. Taste and adjust salt and lemon juice as needed; the rice should be distinctly tangy. Garnish with chopped coriander. Serve at room temperature or slightly warm. Lemon rice is a popular South Indian travel food and tiffin item because it stays fresh for hours without refrigeration and tastes even better after resting.



19. COCONUT RICE

Nutritional Note: South Indian rice made with fresh coconut. Coconut provides healthy medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs), manganese, and copper. Light and aromatic. Pairs well with spicy curries.


Ingredients:

2 cups cooked rice
1 cup fresh grated coconut
2 tablespoons oil
1 teaspoon mustard seeds
1 teaspoon urad dal
1 teaspoon chana dal
2 dried red chillies
2 tablespoons cashew nuts
10-12 curry leaves
1/4 teaspoon asafoetida
Salt to taste

Detailed Recipe:
Spread the cooked rice on a plate and let it cool completely; the rice should be at room temperature with separate, non-sticky grains. If using freshly grated coconut, lightly dry roast it in a pan for 2-3 minutes on low heat until it turns slightly golden and aromatic; this enhances the flavor and extends shelf life. Heat oil in a pan on medium heat, add mustard seeds and let splutter, then add urad dal and chana dal and fry until golden. Add broken dried red chillies, cashew nuts, curry leaves, and asafoetida, sautéing until the cashews turn light golden. Turn off the heat, add the cooled rice, roasted coconut, and salt. Mix gently but thoroughly using a spatula or your hands, ensuring the coconut is evenly distributed throughout the rice without mashing the grains. Adjust salt to taste. Serve at room temperature with any South Indian curry, sambar, or rasam. Coconut rice is a festive dish in South India, especially prepared for temple offerings and special occasions.



20. BIRYANI (HYDERABADI CHICKEN)

Nutritional Note: A complete one-pot meal with protein from chicken, carbohydrates from rice, and various spices with medicinal properties. Saffron provides antioxidants. High in calories, best enjoyed in moderation. A celebration dish.


Ingredients:For Chicken Marination:

750g chicken, cut into pieces
1 cup yogurt
2 tablespoons biryani masala
1 teaspoon red chilli powder
1/2 teaspoon turmeric powder
1 tablespoon ginger-garlic paste
1 tablespoon lemon juice
Salt to taste
Fresh mint and coriander leaves

For Rice:

2 cups basmati rice, soaked 30 minutes
6 cups water
2 bay leaves
4 green cardamom pods
4 cloves
1 cinnamon stick
1 star anise
1 teaspoon shahi jeera
Salt to taste

For Layering:

3 tablespoons ghee
2 large onions, thinly sliced and fried golden
1/2 cup warm milk with saffron strands
Fresh mint and coriander leaves
1 tablespoon ghee for top

Detailed Recipe:
Marinate the chicken pieces with yogurt, biryani masala, red chilli powder, turmeric, ginger-garlic paste, lemon juice, salt, mint, and coriander leaves. Mix well, cover, and refrigerate for at least 2 hours, preferably overnight. Meanwhile, prepare the fried onions (birista) by thinly slicing 2 large onions and deep-frying them in oil on medium heat until deep golden brown and crispy; drain on paper towels. Soak saffron strands in warm milk for 20 minutes. Boil 6 cups of water with bay leaves, cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, star anise, shahi jeera, and salt. Add the soaked and drained rice and cook until 70% done (the grain should be long but have a slight bite when pressed); this usually takes 5-6 minutes. Drain immediately and spread on a plate to stop cooking. In a heavy-bottomed pot, spread the marinated chicken at the bottom in an even layer. Sprinkle half the fried onions, half the mint and coriander leaves over the chicken. Layer the 70% cooked rice evenly over the chicken. Drizzle the saffron milk, remaining fried onions, remaining herbs, and ghee over the top. Seal the pot tightly with aluminum foil, then place a heavy lid on top to trap all steam. Cook on high heat for 3-4 minutes until you see steam, then reduce to the lowest possible heat and cook for 25-30 minutes (dum cooking). Turn off heat and let rest for 10 minutes without opening. Gently mix layers when serving. Serve with raita and salan.



21. VEGETABLE PULAO

Nutritional Note: Aromatic one-pot rice with mixed vegetables. Provides carbohydrates, fiber, vitamins, and minerals. Lighter than biryani. Good way to include multiple vegetables in one meal.


Ingredients:

1.5 cups basmati rice, soaked 20 minutes
2.5 cups water
1 cup mixed vegetables (carrots, peas, beans, cauliflower, potato)
2 tablespoons ghee or oil
1 bay leaf
2 green cardamom pods
3-4 cloves
1 cinnamon stick
1 teaspoon cumin seeds
1 medium onion, thinly sliced
1 teaspoon ginger-garlic paste
1/2 teaspoon turmeric powder
1/2 teaspoon garam masala
Salt to taste
Fresh coriander and mint leaves

Detailed Recipe:
Wash and soak basmati rice for 20 minutes, then drain. Heat ghee in a heavy-bottomed pot on medium heat, add bay leaf, cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and cumin seeds, sautéing for 30 seconds until fragrant. Add thinly sliced onion and cook for 3-4 minutes until golden. Add ginger-garlic paste and sauté for 1 minute until the raw smell disappears. Add the mixed vegetables and stir-fry for 2-3 minutes. Add turmeric, garam masala, and salt, mixing well. Add the drained rice and gently stir for 1-2 minutes, coating the grains with the ghee and spices. Add 2.5 cups of water, bring to a rolling boil, add fresh mint and coriander leaves, stir once gently. Reduce heat to the lowest setting, cover tightly, and cook undisturbed for 15-18 minutes. Turn off heat and rest for 5 minutes. Fluff gently with a fork. Serve with raita, pickle, or any curry. For extra richness, top with fried cashews and raisins.



22. TOMATO RICE

Nutritional Note: Tangy South Indian rice dish. Tomatoes provide lycopene, vitamin C, and potassium. Simple, quick, and nutritious. Good option for lunch boxes.


Ingredients:

2 cups cooked rice
3 medium tomatoes, finely chopped
2 tablespoons oil
1 teaspoon mustard seeds
1 teaspoon urad dal
1 teaspoon chana dal
2 dried red chillies
1/4 teaspoon asafoetida
1/2 teaspoon turmeric powder
1/2 teaspoon red chilli powder
10-12 curry leaves
1 tablespoon peanuts
Salt to taste
Fresh coriander

Detailed Recipe:
Heat oil in a pan on medium heat, add peanuts and fry until golden, then set aside. In the same oil, add mustard seeds and let splutter, add urad dal and chana dal and fry until golden. Add dried red chillies, curry leaves, and asafoetida. Add finely chopped tomatoes, turmeric, red chilli powder, and salt. Cook on medium heat for 8-10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the tomatoes break down completely and the oil separates from the masala; this is the key step - the tomato masala must be well-cooked. Add the cooked rice and fried peanuts, mix gently but thoroughly. Garnish with coriander. Serve with papadam or yogurt.



23. CURD RICE

Nutritional Note: Cooling South Indian comfort food. Yogurt provides probiotics, calcium, and protein. Excellent for digestion and gut health. Soothing during hot weather. Often the last course in a South Indian meal.


Ingredients:

1 cup cooked rice, mashed slightly
1 cup fresh yogurt (curd)
1/4 cup milk
2 tablespoons oil
1 teaspoon mustard seeds
1 teaspoon urad dal
2 dried red chillies
1/4 teaspoon asafoetida
8-10 curry leaves
1 tablespoon fresh coriander, chopped
1 tablespoon pomegranate seeds (optional)
Salt to taste

Detailed Recipe:
Cook the rice until very soft, slightly overcooking it compared to regular rice. While still warm, mash it lightly with a ladle. Add the yogurt and milk to the warm rice and mix thoroughly; the warmth of the rice helps the yogurt blend smoothly. Add salt and mix. In a small pan, heat oil, add mustard seeds and let splutter, add urad dal and fry until golden, add dried red chillies, curry leaves, and asafoetida. Pour this tempering over the curd rice and mix. Garnish with chopped coriander and pomegranate seeds. Serve at room temperature or slightly chilled. Curd rice tastes best when eaten fresh; if refrigerated, the rice absorbs the yogurt, so you may need to add a little more yogurt or milk before serving. It is a perfect summer meal that cools the body and aids digestion.



24. TAMARIND RICE (PULIHORA)

Nutritional Note: Tangy Andhra/Tamil rice dish. Tamarind is rich in antioxidants, B-vitamins, and minerals. Peanuts add protein. Traditional temple prasadam. Stays fresh without refrigeration for hours.


Ingredients:

2 cups cooked rice
2 tablespoons tamarind paste
2 tablespoons oil
1 teaspoon mustard seeds
1 teaspoon urad dal
1 teaspoon chana dal
2 dried red chillies
2 green chillies, slit
1/4 teaspoon asafoetida
1/2 teaspoon turmeric powder
2 tablespoons peanuts
1 tablespoon sesame seeds, dry roasted and powdered
1 tablespoon jaggery
10-12 curry leaves
Salt to taste

Detailed Recipe:
Mix tamarind paste with 1/4 cup water, jaggery, and a little salt. Heat oil in a pan, add peanuts and fry until golden, remove and set aside. In the same oil, add mustard seeds, let splutter, add urad dal, chana dal, dried red chillies, green chillies, curry leaves, and asafoetida. Add turmeric and the tamarind-jaggery mixture. Cook on medium heat for 8-10 minutes until the raw tamarind smell disappears and the mixture thickens to a paste-like consistency. Add the sesame powder and mix. Add the cooked rice and fried peanuts, mix gently but thoroughly, ensuring the rice is evenly coated with the tangy tamarind masala. Adjust salt and tanginess. Serve at room temperature.



25. PEAS PULAO

Nutritional Note: Simple, mildly spiced rice with green peas. Peas provide protein, fiber, vitamin K, and folate. Light and aromatic. Kid-friendly.


Ingredients:

1.5 cups basmati rice, soaked 20 minutes
1 cup green peas (fresh or frozen)
2.5 cups water
2 tablespoons ghee
1 bay leaf
2 cardamom pods
3-4 cloves
1 cinnamon stick
1 teaspoon cumin seeds
1 medium onion, thinly sliced
Salt to taste
Fresh mint and coriander

Detailed Recipe:
Heat ghee in a heavy-bottomed pot, add whole spices and cumin, sauté 30 seconds. Add sliced onion, cook until golden. Add green peas and stir for 1 minute. Add drained rice, stir gently for 1-2 minutes. Add water, salt, mint, bring to boil. Cover, reduce heat, cook 15-18 minutes. Rest 5 minutes, fluff, serve.



DAL & LEGUME DISHES


26. DAL TADKA

Nutritional Note: The most common Indian dal preparation. Yellow lentils are rich in protein, fiber, folate, and iron. The tempering (tadka) adds flavor and beneficial compounds from garlic, cumin, and mustard seeds. A complete protein when paired with rice.


Ingredients:

1 cup toor dal (split pigeon pea) or yellow moong dal
3 cups water
1/2 teaspoon turmeric powder
Salt to taste

For Tadka (Tempering):

2 tablespoons ghee
1 teaspoon cumin seeds
1/4 teaspoon asafoetida
4-5 garlic cloves, sliced
2 dried red chillies, broken
2 green chillies, slit
1 medium onion, finely chopped
1 medium tomato, finely chopped
1/2 teaspoon red chilli powder
Fresh coriander leaves

Detailed Recipe:
Wash the toor dal thoroughly in 3-4 changes of water until clear. Pressure cook the dal with 3 cups of water, turmeric powder, and a pinch of salt for 3-4 whistles on medium heat. Once the pressure releases naturally, open the cooker, mash the dal with the back of a ladle until smooth and creamy; add more water if needed to achieve your desired consistency - some prefer it thick, others like it soupy. Bring the mashed dal to a gentle simmer on low heat. Meanwhile, prepare the tadka: heat ghee in a small pan on medium-high heat, add cumin seeds and let them crackle and turn a shade darker. Add sliced garlic and fry until it turns light golden and aromatic. Add dried red chillies, green chillies, and asafoetida, sautéing for a few seconds. Add finely chopped onion and cook for 2-3 minutes until translucent. Add chopped tomato and red chilli powder, cook for 2-3 minutes until the tomato softens and the oil separates. Pour this sizzling tadka directly into the simmering dal - it should hiss and sizzle dramatically. Stir well, simmer for 2-3 more minutes to let the flavors meld. Garnish with fresh coriander and a final drizzle of ghee. Serve hot with steamed rice, jeera rice, or roti. Dal tadka is comfort food at its finest and is the backbone of everyday Indian cooking.



27. DAL MAKHANI

Nutritional Note: Rich, creamy Punjabi dal made from black lentils and kidney beans. High in protein and fiber. The slow cooking process develops deep flavors. Higher in calories due to butter and cream; can be made lighter by reducing dairy.


Ingredients:

1 cup whole black urad dal (sabut urad)
1/4 cup rajma (kidney beans)
6 cups water for soaking
4 cups water for cooking
2 tablespoons butter
1 tablespoon oil
1 teaspoon cumin seeds
2 bay leaves
1 large onion, finely pureed
2 large tomatoes, pureed
1 tablespoon ginger-garlic paste
1/2 teaspoon turmeric powder
1 teaspoon red chilli powder
1 teaspoon coriander powder
1/2 teaspoon garam masala
1/2 cup fresh cream
2 tablespoons butter for finishing
Salt to taste
Fresh coriander for garnish

Detailed Recipe:
Soak the black urad dal and rajma together in plenty of water overnight or for at least 8 hours; this long soaking is essential for these tough legumes to cook properly and become creamy. Drain the soaked dal and rajma, add to a pressure cooker with 4 cups of water, 1/2 teaspoon salt, and pressure cook for 8-10 whistles on medium heat until completely soft and mushy; the dal should break easily between your fingers. In a separate heavy-bottomed pot, heat butter and oil on medium heat, add cumin seeds and bay leaves, sauté for 30 seconds. Add the pureed onion and cook for 5-7 minutes, stirring frequently, until the raw smell disappears and the onion turns golden. Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1-2 minutes. Add turmeric, red chilli powder, and coriander powder, stir for 30 seconds. Add the tomato puree and cook for 8-10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the oil separates from the masala and the tomato mixture darkens slightly. Add the cooked dal and rajma along with their cooking liquid. Stir well, bring to a boil, then reduce heat to the lowest setting. Simmer uncovered for 30-45 minutes, stirring occasionally; the slow cooking is what makes dal makhani special - the longer it simmers, the creamier it becomes. Add water if it becomes too thick. Add garam masala, fresh cream, and butter, stir well, and simmer for another 5 minutes. Garnish with a swirl of cream and fresh coriander. Serve with naan, jeera rice, or lachha paratha. Traditionally, dal makhani is cooked overnight on a slow flame, which gives it its signature velvety texture.



28. SAMBAR

Nutritional Note: South Indian lentil and vegetable stew. Toor dal provides protein; mixed vegetables provide fiber, vitamins, and minerals. Tamarind adds vitamin C and aids iron absorption. Sambar powder contains beneficial spices. A complete, balanced dish.


Ingredients:

1 cup toor dal
3 cups water for dal
1 small drumstick, cut into 2-inch pieces
1 small brinjal (eggplant), cubed
1 small carrot, cubed
1 small onion, quartered
1 small tomato, quartered
10-12 small pearl onions (sambar onions)
Small piece of pumpkin, cubed
1 lemon-sized ball of tamarind, soaked in 1/2 cup warm water
2 tablespoons sambar powder
1/2 teaspoon turmeric powder
Salt to taste

For Tempering:

2 tablespoons oil or ghee
1 teaspoon mustard seeds
1 teaspoon cumin seeds
2 dried red chillies
1/4 teaspoon fenugreek seeds
1/4 teaspoon asafoetida
10-12 curry leaves
2 tablespoons fresh coriander

Detailed Recipe:
Pressure cook the toor dal with 3 cups of water and turmeric for 3-4 whistles until soft. Mash thoroughly and set aside. In a separate pot, cook the vegetables (drumstick, brinjal, carrot, onion, tomato, pearl onions, pumpkin) with just enough water to cover them, adding a pinch of salt and turmeric, until the vegetables are tender but not mushy; drumstick takes longest, so add it first. Extract tamarind juice by squeezing the soaked tamarind in the water and straining. Add the tamarind juice to the cooked vegetables along with sambar powder and salt. Boil for 5-7 minutes until the raw tamarind smell disappears. Add the mashed dal, mix well, and add water to achieve desired consistency. Simmer for 10 minutes. Prepare the tempering: heat oil in a small pan, add mustard seeds and let splutter, add cumin seeds, fenugreek seeds, dried red chillies, curry leaves, and asafoetida. Pour the tempering over the simmering sambar, stir, garnish with coriander, and serve with rice, idli, dosa, or vada.



29. RASAM

Nutritional Note: Spicy, tangy South Indian soup. Pepper and cumin aid digestion and have anti-inflammatory properties. Tamarind provides vitamin C. Garlic has antimicrobial properties. Excellent for colds, flu, and digestion. Very low in calories.


Ingredients:

1/2 cup toor dal, cooked and mashed
1 lemon-sized ball tamarind, soaked
2 medium tomatoes, chopped
1 teaspoon black peppercorns
1 teaspoon cumin seeds
4-5 garlic cloves
2 dried red chillies
1/2 teaspoon turmeric powder
1/4 teaspoon asafoetida
2 tablespoons oil
1 teaspoon mustard seeds
10-12 curry leaves
Fresh coriander
Salt to taste
3 cups water

Detailed Recipe:
Coarsely crush the peppercorns, cumin seeds, and garlic together using a mortar and pestle or pulse briefly in a blender; this coarse crush releases more flavor than fine powder. Soak tamarind in warm water, extract juice, and strain. In a pot, add the tamarind juice, chopped tomatoes, crushed pepper-cumin-garlic mixture, turmeric, salt, and 3 cups of water. Bring to a boil and simmer for 10-12 minutes until the tomatoes are completely soft and the raw tamarind smell is gone. Add the cooked, mashed dal and stir well. Simmer for another 5 minutes. The rasam should be thin and watery, not thick like sambar. Prepare tempering: heat oil, add mustard seeds, dried red chillies, curry leaves, and asafoetida. Pour the tempering into the rasam. Garnish with coriander. Serve hot as a soup or with rice.



30. CHANA MASALA

Nutritional Note: Chickpea curry rich in plant protein, fiber, iron, and folate. One of the best vegetarian protein sources. The tomato-based gravy provides lycopene and vitamin C. A complete, satisfying meal.


Ingredients:

1.5 cups chickpeas (kabuli chana), soaked overnight
2 large onions, finely chopped
2 large tomatoes, pureed
1 tablespoon ginger-garlic paste
2 green chillies, slit
2 tablespoons oil
1 teaspoon cumin seeds
1 bay leaf
1 teaspoon coriander powder
1 teaspoon cumin powder
1/2 teaspoon turmeric powder
1 teaspoon red chilli powder
1 teaspoon chana masala powder (or garam masala)
1/2 teaspoon amchur (dry mango powder)
1 teaspoon kasuri methi (dried fenugreek leaves)
Salt to taste
Fresh coriander and lemon wedges

Detailed Recipe:
Soak the chickpeas overnight in plenty of water. Drain and pressure cook with fresh water and 1/2 teaspoon salt for 6-8 whistles until tender but not mushy; they should hold their shape. Reserve 1 cup of the cooking water. Heat oil in a heavy-bottomed pan on medium heat, add cumin seeds and bay leaf, sauté for 30 seconds. Add finely chopped onions and cook for 6-8 minutes, stirring frequently, until deep golden brown. Add ginger-garlic paste and green chillies, cook for 2 minutes until fragrant. Add coriander powder, cumin powder, turmeric, and red chilli powder, stir for 30 seconds. Add the tomato puree and cook for 8-10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the oil separates and the masala darkens. Add the cooked chickpeas along with the reserved cooking water. Add chana masala, amchur, and salt. Bring to a boil, then simmer on low heat for 15-20 minutes, lightly pressing some chickpeas against the side of the pan to thicken the gravy. Crush kasuri methi between your palms and add. Garnish with coriander and serve with bhatura, naan, or rice with lemon wedges on the side.



31. RAJMA

Nutritional Note: Kidney bean curry, a Punjabi favorite. Kidney beans are rich in protein, fiber, iron, potassium, and folate. Excellent for heart health and blood sugar management. A complete protein when paired with rice.


Ingredients:

1.5 cups rajma (kidney beans), soaked overnight
2 large onions, finely chopped
2 large tomatoes, pureed
1 tablespoon ginger-garlic paste
2 tablespoons oil
1 teaspoon cumin seeds
1 bay leaf
1 teaspoon coriander powder
1/2 teaspoon turmeric powder
1 teaspoon red chilli powder
1/2 teaspoon garam masala
Salt to taste
Fresh coriander and cream (optional)

Detailed Recipe:
Soak rajma overnight in plenty of water. Drain and pressure cook with fresh water and a pinch of salt for 8-10 whistles until very tender; undercooked rajma is hard and indigestible. Heat oil, add cumin and bay leaf. Add onions, cook until deep golden (8-10 minutes). Add ginger-garlic paste, cook 2 minutes. Add dry spices, stir 30 seconds. Add tomato puree, cook 10 minutes until oil separates. Add cooked rajma with some cooking water. Simmer 20-25 minutes, mashing some beans to thicken. Add garam masala. Garnish with coriander and cream. Serve with steamed rice.



32. MOONG DAL FRY

Nutritional Note: Quick-cooking yellow lentil dish. Easily digestible, rich in protein. Lighter than toor dal. Good for convalescents, elderly, and those with digestive issues.


Ingredients:

1 cup yellow moong dal
3 cups water
1/2 teaspoon turmeric
Salt to taste
2 tablespoons ghee
1 teaspoon cumin seeds
2 garlic cloves, sliced
2 green chillies, slit
1 small onion, chopped
1 small tomato, chopped
1/2 teaspoon red chilli powder
Fresh coriander

Detailed Recipe:
Wash moong dal, pressure cook with water, turmeric, and salt for 2-3 whistles. Mash lightly. Heat ghee, add cumin, garlic, green chillies. Add onion, cook 2 minutes. Add tomato, cook 2 minutes. Add chilli powder, pour tempering into dal. Simmer 5 minutes. Garnish with coriander. Serve with rice or roti.



33. DAL FRY (RESTAURANT STYLE)

Nutritional Note: Rich, restaurant-style dal combining toor and moong dal. Creamy texture with aromatic tempering. Higher in ghee than home-style. Balanced protein from two lentil sources.


Ingredients:

1/2 cup toor dal
1/2 cup yellow moong dal
3 cups water
1/2 teaspoon turmeric
Salt to taste
3 tablespoons ghee
1 teaspoon cumin seeds
2 dried red chillies
4 garlic cloves, sliced
1 inch ginger, julienned
1 large onion, finely chopped
2 tomatoes, finely chopped
1/2 teaspoon red chilli powder
1/2 teaspoon garam masala
1 tablespoon kasuri methi
1 tablespoon butter
Fresh coriander, cream for garnish

Detailed Recipe:
Wash both dals, pressure cook together with water, turmeric, and salt for 3-4 whistles. Mash until smooth. Heat ghee, add cumin and dried red chillies. Add sliced garlic and ginger, fry until garlic turns golden. Add onion, cook until golden. Add tomatoes, cook until soft. Add chilli powder, pour into dal. Simmer 10 minutes. Add garam masala, kasuri methi, butter. Garnish with coriander and cream swirl.



34. AMTI (MAHARASHTRIAN DAL)

Nutritional Note: Sweet-sour-spicy Maharashtrian dal. Goda masala provides unique flavor and digestive benefits. Jaggery adds iron. Kokum provides antioxidants. A balanced everyday dal.


Ingredients:

1 cup toor dal
3 cups water
1/2 teaspoon turmeric
2 tablespoons goda masala (or garam masala)
1 tablespoon jaggery
2 pieces kokum (or 1 tablespoon tamarind paste)
Salt to taste
2 tablespoons oil
1 teaspoon mustard seeds
1/2 teaspoon cumin seeds
1/4 teaspoon asafoetida
2 dried red chillies
10-12 curry leaves
Fresh coriander

Detailed Recipe:
Pressure cook dal with water, turmeric, and salt until soft. Mash well. Add goda masala, jaggery, kokum, and water to desired consistency. Simmer 10 minutes. Prepare tempering with oil, mustard seeds, cumin, asafoetida, red chillies, curry leaves. Pour over dal. Garnish with coriander. Serve with rice and bhakri.



35. PANCHMEL DAL

Nutritional Note: Rajasthani five-lentil dal providing diverse amino acids, fiber, and minerals. Each lentil contributes unique nutrients. A complete protein source.


Ingredients:

1/4 cup each: toor dal, chana dal, moong dal, masoor dal, urad dal
4 cups water
1/2 teaspoon turmeric
Salt to taste
3 tablespoons ghee
1 teaspoon cumin seeds
2 dried red chillies
1/4 teaspoon asafoetida
2 onions, chopped
2 tomatoes, chopped
1 teaspoon red chilli powder
1 teaspoon coriander powder
1/2 teaspoon garam masala
Fresh coriander

Detailed Recipe:
Wash all five dals together, soak 30 minutes. Pressure cook with water, turmeric, salt for 4-5 whistles. Mash. Heat ghee, add cumin, red chillies, asafoetida. Add onions, cook until golden. Add tomatoes, dry spices, cook until oil separates. Pour into dal, simmer 15 minutes. Garnish with coriander. Serve with rice or bajra roti.



VEGETABLE DISHES


36. ALOO GOBI

Nutritional Note: Classic potato and cauliflower dry curry. Cauliflower is rich in vitamin C, vitamin K, and fiber. Potatoes provide potassium and vitamin C. A simple, nutritious everyday vegetable dish.


Ingredients:

1 medium cauliflower, cut into florets
2 medium potatoes, cubed
2 tablespoons oil
1 teaspoon cumin seeds
1/4 teaspoon asafoetida
1 medium onion, finely chopped
1 tablespoon ginger-garlic paste
2 green chillies, slit
1/2 teaspoon turmeric powder
1 teaspoon red chilli powder
1 teaspoon coriander powder
1/2 teaspoon garam masala
1/2 teaspoon amchur (dry mango powder)
Salt to taste
Fresh coriander

Detailed Recipe:
Wash cauliflower florets and soak in warm salted water for 10 minutes to remove any insects. Heat oil in a heavy-bottomed pan or kadhai on medium heat. Add cumin seeds and asafoetida, let splutter. Add chopped onion and cook for 3-4 minutes until golden. Add ginger-garlic paste and green chillies, sauté for 1 minute. Add turmeric, red chilli powder, and coriander powder, stir for 30 seconds. Add the potato cubes first, stir well to coat with spices, cover and cook for 5 minutes on medium-low heat as potatoes take longer than cauliflower. Then add the cauliflower florets, mix gently, add salt, cover and cook on low heat for 15-20 minutes, stirring every 5 minutes, until both vegetables are tender but not mushy; do not add water as the vegetables release their own moisture. If the vegetables start sticking, sprinkle a tablespoon of water. Once cooked, add garam masala and amchur, toss gently. Garnish with fresh coriander. Serve with roti, paratha, or puri. For a variation, you can add green peas, tomatoes, or a squeeze of lemon juice.



37. PALAK PANEER

Nutritional Note: Spinach and cottage cheese curry. Spinach is rich in iron, calcium, vitamins A, C, K, and folate. Paneer provides protein and calcium. One of the most nutritious Indian vegetarian dishes.


Ingredients:

250g paneer (cottage cheese), cubed
4 cups fresh spinach (palak), washed
1 large onion, roughly chopped
2 tomatoes, roughly chopped
1 tablespoon ginger-garlic paste
2 green chillies
2 tablespoons oil or butter
1 teaspoon cumin seeds
1/2 teaspoon turmeric powder
1/2 teaspoon red chilli powder
1/2 teaspoon garam masala
1/4 cup fresh cream
1 teaspoon kasuri methi
Salt to taste

Detailed Recipe:
Blanch the spinach in boiling water for 2-3 minutes until wilted, then immediately transfer to ice-cold water to preserve the bright green color. Drain and blend to a smooth puree. In the same pan, heat oil, sauté onion until golden, add ginger-garlic paste and green chillies, cook 2 minutes. Add tomatoes, cook until soft. Blend this onion-tomato mixture to a smooth paste. In a pan, heat butter, add cumin seeds, pour the onion-tomato paste, add turmeric and red chilli powder, cook 5 minutes. Add spinach puree, simmer 5 minutes. Add paneer cubes, garam masala, kasuri methi, cream. Simmer 3-4 minutes. Serve with naan or rice.



38. BAINGAN BHARTA

Nutritional Note: Smoky roasted eggplant mash. Eggplant is rich in fiber, antioxidants (nasunin), and B-vitamins. Low in calories. The roasting process adds depth of flavor without added fat.


Ingredients:

1 large eggplant (baingan)
2 tablespoons oil
1 large onion, finely chopped
2 tomatoes, finely chopped
1 tablespoon ginger-garlic paste
2 green chillies, finely chopped
1/2 teaspoon turmeric powder
1 teaspoon red chilli powder
1 teaspoon coriander powder
1/2 teaspoon garam masala
Salt to taste
Fresh coriander

Detailed Recipe:
Roast the whole eggplant directly over an open gas flame, turning with tongs every 2-3 minutes, for 10-15 minutes until the skin is completely charred and blackened and the flesh inside is soft and collapsed; alternatively, roast in a 200°C oven for 30-40 minutes. Place the roasted eggplant in a bowl, cover with a plate, and let it steam for 5 minutes. Peel off the charred skin, mash the flesh coarsely with a fork; keep some texture, do not puree. Heat oil in a pan, add chopped onion and cook until golden. Add ginger-garlic paste and green chillies, sauté 1 minute. Add tomatoes, cook until soft. Add turmeric, red chilli powder, coriander powder, cook 2 minutes. Add the mashed eggplant and salt, mix well. Cook on medium heat for 8-10 minutes, stirring occasionally. Add garam masala. Garnish with coriander. Serve with roti or paratha.



39. BHINDI MASALA

Nutritional Note: Spiced okra curry. Okra is rich in fiber, vitamin C, vitamin K, folate, and magnesium. Low in calories. The mucilage in okra aids digestion and helps manage blood sugar.


Ingredients:

250g okra (bhindi), washed, dried, and cut into 1-inch pieces
2 tablespoons oil
1 teaspoon cumin seeds
1/4 teaspoon asafoetida
1 large onion, thinly sliced
1 tablespoon ginger-garlic paste
2 green chillies, slit
1/2 teaspoon turmeric powder
1 teaspoon red chilli powder
1 teaspoon coriander powder
1 teaspoon amchur
1/2 teaspoon garam masala
Salt to taste

Detailed Recipe:
Wash okra and pat COMPLETELY dry with a kitchen towel; moisture is the enemy of crispy okra. Cut off the tops and tails, slice into 1-inch pieces. Heat oil in a wide pan on high heat (high heat reduces sliminess). Add okra pieces and stir-fry on high heat for 5-7 minutes without covering until the sliminess disappears and the okra turns slightly crispy. Remove okra and set aside. In the same pan, add cumin seeds and asafoetida. Add sliced onion, cook until golden. Add ginger-garlic paste and green chillies. Add turmeric, red chilli powder, coriander powder. Return the okra to the pan, add amchur, garam masala, and salt. Toss gently and cook for 2-3 minutes. Serve with roti and dal.



40. ALOO MATAR

Nutritional Note: Potato and green pea curry. Peas provide protein, fiber, and vitamin K. Potatoes provide potassium and vitamin C. A simple, satisfying everyday dish.


Ingredients:

3 medium potatoes, cubed
1 cup green peas
2 tablespoons oil
1 teaspoon cumin seeds
1/4 teaspoon asafoetida
1 large onion, pureed
2 tomatoes, pureed
1 tablespoon ginger-garlic paste
1/2 teaspoon turmeric
1 teaspoon red chilli powder
1 teaspoon coriander powder
1/2 teaspoon garam masala
Salt to taste
Fresh coriander

Detailed Recipe:
Heat oil, add cumin and asafoetida. Add onion puree, cook 5 minutes. Add ginger-garlic paste, cook 1 minute. Add tomato puree and dry spices, cook 8 minutes until oil separates. Add potatoes and peas, stir well. Add 1 cup water, salt, cover, cook 15-20 minutes until potatoes are tender. Add garam masala. Garnish with coriander. Serve with roti or puri.



41. MIXED VEGETABLE CURRY

Nutritional Note: Nutrient-dense dish with multiple vegetables providing a wide spectrum of vitamins, minerals, and fiber. The coconut-based gravy adds healthy fats. A great way to consume 3-4 servings of vegetables in one dish.


Ingredients:

1 cup cauliflower florets
1 carrot, cubed
10-12 green beans, cut
1 potato, cubed
1/2 cup green peas
1/2 cup French beans
2 tablespoons oil
1 teaspoon cumin seeds
1 bay leaf
1 large onion, finely chopped
1 tablespoon ginger-garlic paste
2 tomatoes, pureed
1/2 teaspoon turmeric
1 teaspoon red chilli powder
1 teaspoon coriander powder
1/2 teaspoon garam masala
1/4 cup coconut paste (optional)
Salt to taste
Fresh coriander

Detailed Recipe:
Blanch the harder vegetables (cauliflower, carrot, beans, potato) in boiling salted water for 3-4 minutes, drain. Heat oil, add cumin and bay leaf. Add onion, cook until golden. Add ginger-garlic paste. Add tomato puree and dry spices, cook 8 minutes. Add blanch vegetables and peas. Add 1 cup water, salt. Simmer 15 minutes. Add coconut paste if using. Add garam masala. Garnish with coriander. Serve with rice or roti.



42. PANEER BUTTER MASALA

Nutritional Note: Rich, creamy North Indian paneer curry. Paneer provides protein and calcium. The tomato-cashew gravy is rich in lycopene. Higher in calories due to butter and cream; a celebration dish best enjoyed in moderation.


Ingredients:

250g paneer, cubed
2 tablespoons butter
1 tablespoon oil
1 bay leaf
2 green cardamom pods
1 large onion, pureed
2 large tomatoes, pureed
1 tablespoon ginger-garlic paste
10-12 cashew nuts, soaked and ground to paste
1/2 teaspoon turmeric
1 teaspoon red chilli powder (Kashmiri for color)
1 teaspoon coriander powder
1/2 teaspoon garam masala
1/2 teaspoon sugar
1/2 cup fresh cream
1 teaspoon kasuri methi
Salt to taste
Fresh coriander

Detailed Recipe:
Lightly fry paneer cubes in a tablespoon of butter until golden on all sides; set aside. In the same pan, heat remaining butter and oil, add bay leaf and cardamom. Add onion puree, cook 5-7 minutes until golden. Add ginger-garlic paste, cook 1 minute. Add tomato puree and dry spices, cook 10 minutes until oil separates. Add cashew paste, cook 3 minutes. Add 1 cup water, simmer 5 minutes. Strain the gravy through a sieve for silky smoothness (optional but restaurant-style). Add paneer, cream, kasuri methi, sugar, salt. Simmer 5 minutes. Garnish with cream swirl and coriander. Serve with naan or butter naan.



43. MALAI KOFTA

Nutritional Note: Fried paneer-potato balls in creamy gravy. Higher in calories due to frying and cream. Rich in protein and calcium from paneer. A festive dish.


Ingredients:For Kofta:

200g paneer, grated
2 potatoes, boiled and mashed
2 tablespoons cornflour
2 tablespoons raisins and cashews
1/2 teaspoon garam masala
Salt to taste
Oil for frying

For Gravy:

Same as Paneer Butter Masala gravy (recipe above)

Detailed Recipe:
Mix grated paneer, mashed potato, cornflour, garam masala, salt. Take a small portion, press a raisin and cashew in the center, shape into smooth balls. Deep fry on medium heat until golden. Prepare the gravy as in Paneer Butter Masala. Add the fried kofta balls to the gravy just before serving (so they don't become soggy). Garnish with cream and coriander.



44. NAVRATAN KORMA

Nutritional Note: Nine-gem vegetable curry with nuts and fruits. Extremely nutrient-dense with nine different vegetables, dry fruits, and nuts providing a wide range of vitamins, minerals, and antioxidants. Rich and festive.


Ingredients:

1/2 cup each: carrots, beans, peas, cauliflower, potato (cubed and blanched)
1/4 cup paneer cubes (fried)
2 tablespoons cashews
2 tablespoons raisins
2 tablespoons oil + 1 tablespoon butter
1 large onion, pureed
1 tablespoon ginger-garlic paste
1/2 cup yogurt
1/2 cup cashew paste
1/2 teaspoon turmeric
1 teaspoon red chilli powder
1/2 teaspoon garam masala
1/4 cup cream
1 teaspoon sugar
Salt to taste
Fresh coriander

Detailed Recipe:
Heat oil and butter, add onion puree, cook until golden. Add ginger-garlic paste. Add yogurt (whisked), cook 5 minutes. Add cashew paste, dry spices, cook 5 minutes. Add blanched vegetables, paneer, cashews, raisins. Add 1/2 cup water, simmer 10 minutes. Add cream, sugar, garam masala. Garnish with cream and coriander. Serve with naan or pulao.



45. GOBI MANCHURIAN

Nutritional Note: Indo-Chinese cauliflower fritters in tangy sauce. Cauliflower provides vitamin C and fiber. The batter adds carbohydrates. Deep-fried version is higher in calories; can be baked or air-fried for a healthier option.


Ingredients:For Cauliflower Fritters:

1 medium cauliflower, cut into florets
1/2 cup all-purpose flour (maida)
1/4 cup cornflour
1 tablespoon soy sauce
1/2 teaspoon red chilli powder
1/2 teaspoon black pepper
Salt to taste
Water for batter
Oil for frying

For Sauce:

2 tablespoons oil
1 tablespoon garlic, finely chopped
1 tablespoon ginger, finely chopped
2 green chillies, slit
1 large onion, diced
1/4 cup capsicum, diced
2 tablespoons soy sauce
1 tablespoon chilli sauce
1 tablespoon tomato ketchup
1 tablespoon vinegar
1 teaspoon sugar
1 tablespoon cornflour mixed in 1/4 cup water
Spring onion greens

Detailed Recipe:
Blanch cauliflower florets in boiling water for 2 minutes, drain. Mix maida, cornflour, soy sauce, chilli powder, pepper, salt with enough water to make a thick batter. Dip florets in batter, deep fry until golden and crispy. For sauce: heat oil, sauté garlic, ginger, green chillies. Add onion and capsicum, stir-fry 2 minutes. Add soy sauce, chilli sauce, ketchup, vinegar, sugar. Add cornflour slurry, cook until thick. Add fried cauliflower, toss to coat. Garnish with spring onions.



46. STUFFED CAPSICUM

Nutritional Note: Bell peppers are rich in vitamin C (more than oranges), vitamin A, and antioxidants. The stuffing can be customized with potatoes, paneer, or rice. Colorful and nutritious.


Ingredients:

4 large bell peppers (mixed colors)
2 potatoes, boiled and mashed
1/2 cup paneer, crumbled
1 small onion, finely chopped
1/2 teaspoon each: turmeric, red chilli powder, garam masala, cumin powder
2 tablespoons oil
Salt to taste
Fresh coriander
2 tablespoons tomato puree

Detailed Recipe:
Cut the tops off the bell peppers and remove seeds. Mix mashed potato, crumbled paneer, chopped onion, all spices, salt, and coriander. Stuff each pepper with this mixture. Heat oil in a pan, place stuffed peppers upright, add tomato puree and 2 tablespoons water around the peppers. Cover and cook on low heat for 15-20 minutes, turning occasionally, until peppers are tender and filling is heated through. Serve with roti.



47. LAUKI KOFTA

Nutritional Note: Bottle gourd dumplings in gravy. Bottle gourd is 96% water, very low in calories, rich in fiber, and cooling. An excellent way to make lauki appealing.


Ingredients:For Kofta:

2 cups grated bottle gourd (lauki), squeezed dry
1/2 cup besan
2 green chillies, chopped
1/2 teaspoon red chilli powder
Salt to taste
Oil for frying

For Gravy:

2 tablespoons oil
1 teaspoon cumin seeds
1 onion, pureed
2 tomatoes, pureed
1 tablespoon ginger-garlic paste
1/2 teaspoon turmeric, 1 tsp red chilli powder, 1 tsp coriander powder
1/2 teaspoon garam masala
Salt to taste
Fresh coriander

Detailed Recipe:
Mix squeezed lauki, besan, chillies, spices, salt. Shape into small balls, deep fry until golden. Prepare onion-tomato gravy with spices. Add koftas to gravy before serving. Garnish with coriander.



48. KADDU KI SABZI

Nutritional Note: Sweet and tangy pumpkin curry. Pumpkin is rich in beta-carotene (vitamin A), vitamin C, and potassium. Low in calories. Traditional North Indian dish.


Ingredients:

500g pumpkin, peeled and cubed
2 tablespoons oil
1 teaspoon cumin seeds
1/4 teaspoon fenugreek seeds
1/4 teaspoon asafoetida
1/2 teaspoon turmeric
1 teaspoon red chilli powder
1 teaspoon coriander powder
1 tablespoon jaggery
1 tablespoon tamarind paste
Salt to taste
Fresh coriander

Detailed Recipe:
Heat oil, add cumin, fenugreek seeds, asafoetida. Add pumpkin cubes, turmeric, chilli powder, coriander powder, salt. Stir well, cover, cook on low heat 15-20 minutes until pumpkin is soft and mushy. Add jaggery and tamarind paste, mix, cook 5 more minutes. Garnish with coriander. Serve with puri or paratha.



49. SARSON KA SAAG

Nutritional Note: Punjabi winter delicacy made from mustard greens. Extremely rich in vitamins A, C, K, iron, calcium, and fiber. The addition of spinach and bathua increases nutrient diversity. Traditionally served with makki ki roti.


Ingredients:

2 bunches mustard greens (sarson)
1 bunch spinach (palak)
1/2 bunch bathua (chenopodium) if available
2 cups water
2 inches ginger, chopped
4 green chillies
2 tablespoons makki ka atta (cornmeal)
3 tablespoons butter or ghee
2 tablespoons oil
1 large onion, finely chopped
2 tomatoes, chopped
1 tablespoon garlic, chopped
Salt to taste

Detailed Recipe:
Wash all greens thoroughly. Pressure cook mustard greens, spinach, and bathua with water, ginger, and green chillies for 5-6 whistles. Cool, drain excess water (reserve), and blend to a coarse puree. Return to pan, add cornmeal (dissolved in reserved water), simmer 20-30 minutes on low heat, stirring frequently, until thick. In a separate pan, heat butter and oil, add chopped garlic and onion, cook until golden. Add tomatoes, cook until soft. Pour this tadka into the saag, mix well. Add salt. Serve with a generous dollop of butter and makki ki roti.



50. CHANA SAAG

Nutritional Note: Chickpeas cooked with spinach. Double protein from chickpeas and paneer, plus iron and vitamins from spinach. A powerhouse of nutrition.


Ingredients:

1 cup chickpeas, soaked and boiled
2 cups spinach, blanched and pureed
2 tablespoons oil
1 teaspoon cumin seeds
1 onion, finely chopped
1 tablespoon ginger-garlic paste
2 tomatoes, pureed
1/2 teaspoon turmeric
1 teaspoon red chilli powder
1 teaspoon coriander powder
1/2 teaspoon garam masala
Salt to taste
Fresh coriander

Detailed Recipe:
Heat oil, add cumin. Add onion, cook until golden. Add ginger-garlic paste. Add tomato puree and dry spices, cook 8 minutes. Add spinach puree, cook 5 minutes. Add boiled chickpeas, salt, simmer 15 minutes. Add garam masala. Garnish with coriander. Serve with naan or rice.



51. BEANS PORIYAL

Nutritional Note: South Indian stir-fried green beans with coconut. Green beans provide fiber, vitamin C, vitamin K, and folate. Coconut adds healthy fats. Light and quick.


Ingredients:

250g French beans, finely chopped
2 tablespoons oil
1 teaspoon mustard seeds
1 teaspoon urad dal
2 dried red chillies
10-12 curry leaves
1/4 teaspoon asafoetida
1/4 teaspoon turmeric
3 tablespoons grated coconut
Salt to taste

Detailed Recipe:
Heat oil, add mustard seeds, let splutter. Add urad dal, dried red chillies, curry leaves, asafoetida. Add chopped beans, turmeric, salt. Stir well, add 2 tablespoons water, cover, cook on low heat 8-10 minutes until beans are tender but still have a bite. Add grated coconut, toss well. Serve with rice and sambar.



52. AVIAL

Nutritional Note: Kerala mixed vegetable dish with coconut and yogurt. Contains 7-8 different vegetables providing diverse nutrients. Coconut provides healthy fats. A traditional Onam Sadya dish.


Ingredients:

2 cups mixed vegetables (drumstick, carrot, beans, raw banana, yam, pumpkin, ash gourd), cut into 2-inch pieces
1/2 cup yogurt
10-12 curry leaves
2 tablespoons coconut oil
Salt to taste

Coconut Paste:

1 cup grated coconut
4 green chillies
1 teaspoon cumin seeds
1/2 teaspoon turmeric

Detailed Recipe:
Grind coconut paste ingredients to a coarse paste. Cook vegetables with minimal water and salt until just tender. Add coconut paste, simmer 5 minutes. Add yogurt, stir gently (don't boil). Add curry leaves and coconut oil. Serve with rice.



53. BEETROOT THORAN

Nutritional Note: South Indian stir-fried beetroot with coconut. Beetroot is rich in folate, manganese, potassium, iron, and nitrates that improve blood flow. Vibrant color indicates high antioxidant content.


Ingredients:

2 medium beetroots, finely grated
2 tablespoons oil
1 teaspoon mustard seeds
1 teaspoon urad dal
2 dried red chillies
10 curry leaves
1/4 teaspoon turmeric
3 tablespoons grated coconut
Salt to taste

Detailed Recipe:
Heat oil, add mustard seeds, urad dal, red chillies, curry leaves. Add grated beetroot, turmeric, salt. Stir-fry 5-6 minutes with minimal water until beetroot is cooked but still has texture. Add coconut, toss. Serve with rice.



54. DRUMSTICK CURRY

Nutritional Note: Drumstick (moringa pods) is a superfood rich in vitamin C, potassium, calcium, iron, and protein. The leaves are even more nutrient-dense. Coconut-based gravy adds healthy fats.
