11.Cook the egg noodles in the boiling salted water according to package directions until al dente, then drain and toss with the remaining tablespoon of butter to prevent sticking.
12.Remove the skillet from the heat and let it cool for 2 minutes before stirring in the sour cream — adding it off the heat prevents curdling and ensures a smooth, creamy sauce.
13.Return the seared beef strips and any accumulated juices to the sauce, stirring gently to coat the beef without breaking the tender strips, and let everything warm through for 2-3 minutes on very low heat.
14.Taste the stroganoff and adjust the seasoning with salt, pepper, and an additional teaspoon of Worcestershire sauce if you want more umami depth.
15.Serve the stroganoff over the buttered egg noodles, garnished with a generous sprinkle of fresh chopped parsley and an extra dollop of sour cream on top.


Recipe 9: Margherita Pizza

Ingredients:

Dough: 300g bread flour, 1 tsp instant yeast, 1 tsp sugar, 1 tsp salt, 2 tbsp olive oil, 180ml warm water. Topping: 200g San Marzano tomato sauce (or crushed canned tomatoes), 250g fresh mozzarella (sliced), fresh basil leaves, 2 tbsp extra virgin olive oil, pinch of salt, pinch of dried oregano.

Steps:

1.In a large mixing bowl, combine the bread flour, instant yeast, sugar, and salt, whisking the dry ingredients together briefly to distribute the yeast evenly throughout the flour.
2.Add the warm water (around 110°F / 43°C) and olive oil to the flour mixture, then stir with a wooden spoon until a shaggy dough forms and no dry flour remains visible in the bowl.
3.Turn the dough out onto a lightly floured surface and knead it vigorously for 8-10 minutes, using the heel of your palm to push the dough forward, then folding it back on itself — the dough should become smooth, elastic, and slightly tacky.
4.To test if the dough is properly kneaded, perform the windowpane test: stretch a small piece of dough between your fingers — it should stretch thin enough to see light through without tearing.
5.Shape the dough into a smooth ball, place it in a lightly oiled bowl, cover tightly with plastic wrap or a damp towel, and let it rise in a warm spot for 1-1.5 hours until doubled in size.
6.While the dough rises, prepare the tomato sauce by crushing the San Marzano tomatoes by hand or with a fork, then season with salt, a drizzle of olive oil, and a pinch of dried oregano — do not cook the sauce, as it will cook on the pizza.
7.Slice the fresh mozzarella into ¼-inch thick rounds and place them on a paper towel-lined plate to drain excess moisture for at least 20 minutes, which prevents a soggy pizza.
8.Preheat your oven to its maximum temperature (typically 500-550°F / 260-290°C) for at least 45 minutes with a pizza stone or inverted baking sheet on the middle rack to build up intense heat.
9.Punch down the risen dough to release the air bubbles, turn it out onto a lightly floured surface, and let it rest for 10 minutes covered with a towel — this relaxes the gluten and makes stretching easier.
10.Using your fingertips (never a rolling pin, which destroys the air bubbles), press the dough from the center outward, leaving a ½-inch border untouched around the edge to create the puffy crust.
11.Lift the dough and drape it over your knuckles, gently rotating and stretching it with gravity until it is approximately 12 inches in diameter and thin in the center with a thicker rim.
12.Place the stretched dough on a piece of parchment paper or a well-floured pizza peel, then spread the tomato sauce in a thin, even layer over the surface, leaving the border clear.
13.Arrange the drained mozzarella slices evenly over the sauce, leaving small gaps between each slice to allow the cheese to melt and bubble without pooling into a uniform sheet.
14.Slide the pizza (on the parchment) onto the preheated stone or baking sheet and bake for 8-12 minutes until the crust is golden brown, puffed at the edges, and the cheese is melted with light brown spots.
15.Remove the pizza from the oven using the peel or a large spatula, immediately scatter fresh basil leaves over the hot cheese (the residual heat will slightly wilt them and release their aroma).
16.Drizzle the extra virgin olive oil over the finished pizza in a zigzag pattern, let it rest for 2 minutes (this allows the cheese to set slightly), then slice and serve immediately.


Recipe 10: Tom Yum Soup

Ingredients:

500g shrimp (peeled, deveined, shells reserved), 4 cups water or chicken stock, 3 stalks lemongrass (cut into 2-inch pieces, smashed), 5 slices galangal (or ginger), 5 kaffir lime leaves (torn), 5 Thai bird's eye chilies (smashed), 200g mushrooms (oyster or straw, halved), 3 tbsp fish sauce, 3 tbsp lime juice, 2 tbsp Thai chili paste (nam prik pao), 1 tbsp sugar, 1 cup cherry tomatoes (halved), 2 tbsp cilantro (chopped), 1 tbsp evaporated milk (for creamy version, optional).

Steps:

1.Begin by making a quick shrimp stock: place the reserved shrimp shells in a pot with 4 cups of water, bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer for 15 minutes, pressing the shells with a spoon to extract maximum flavor.
2.Strain the shrimp stock through a fine-mesh sieve into a clean pot, discarding the shells, and bring the strained stock back to a simmer over medium-high heat.
3.Add the smashed lemongrass stalks, galangal slices, and torn kaffir lime leaves to the simmering stock, pressing each herb with the flat side of a knife before adding to release their essential oils.
4.Add the smashed Thai bird's eye chilies (use fewer for milder heat, more for fiery intensity) and let the aromatics simmer in the stock for 5-7 minutes until the broth is intensely fragrant and infused with herbal flavor.
5.Add the halved mushrooms to the broth and cook for 3-4 minutes until they soften slightly but still retain some bite and texture.
6.Stir in the Thai chili paste (nam prik pao), fish sauce, and sugar, mixing well until the chili paste dissolves completely and the broth takes on a reddish-orange color with visible chili oil on the surface.
7.Add the cherry tomatoes and cook for 2 minutes — they should soften slightly but hold their shape, adding a burst of sweetness and acidity to the soup.
8.Add the peeled and deveined shrimp to the simmering broth and cook for exactly 2-3 minutes until they turn pink and curl into a C-shape — overcooking will make them rubbery and tough.
9.Remove the pot from the heat immediately and stir in the fresh lime juice — adding it off the heat preserves the bright, fresh acidity that defines tom yum soup.
10.If making the creamy version (tom yum nam khon), stir in the evaporated milk at this stage, which gives the broth a rich, opaque appearance and a slightly mellowed heat.
11.Taste the soup and adjust the balance of flavors: add more fish sauce for saltiness, more lime juice for sourness, more sugar for sweetness, or more chilies for heat — the hallmark of great tom yum is the perfect equilibrium.
12.Ladle the soup into deep bowls, making sure each serving has a generous amount of shrimp, mushrooms, and tomatoes distributed evenly.
13.Garnish each bowl with fresh cilantro leaves, a few extra slices of fresh chili for color, and a wedge of lime on the rim for squeezing at the table.
14.Serve immediately while the soup is steaming hot, accompanied by steamed jasmine rice on the side, as the broth is intensely flavored and pairs beautifully with plain rice.


Recipe 11: Shakshuka

Ingredients:

6 large eggs, 1 large onion (diced), 1 red bell pepper (diced), 4 cloves garlic (minced), 800g canned crushed tomatoes, 2 tbsp tomato paste, 2 tsp cumin, 2 tsp paprika, 1 tsp chili flakes, 1 tsp sugar, 3 tbsp olive oil, salt and pepper to taste, fresh parsley or cilantro for garnish, crumbled feta cheese (optional), crusty bread for serving.

Steps:

1.Heat the olive oil in a large, deep skillet (preferably cast iron) over medium heat until the oil shimmers and flows easily across the surface of the pan.
2.Add the diced onion and cook for 6-7 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the onion is soft, translucent, and beginning to turn golden at the edges, which builds the sweet aromatic base.
3.Add the diced red bell pepper and continue cooking for 4-5 minutes until the pepper softens and begins to caramelize slightly, adding natural sweetness and color to the sauce.
4.Add the minced garlic and cook for 1 minute, stirring constantly to prevent burning, until the garlic is fragrant and has released its pungent oils into the vegetable mixture.
5.Stir in the cumin, paprika, and chili flakes, toasting the spices in the hot oil for 30-60 seconds while stirring constantly — this blooms the spices and intensifies their flavor dramatically.
6.Add the tomato paste and cook it with the spices for 1-2 minutes, stirring continuously, until the paste darkens slightly and begins to caramelize on the bottom of the pan.
7.Pour in the crushed tomatoes and sugar, stir everything together well, and bring the mixture to a gentle simmer, scraping up any browned bits from the bottom of the skillet.
8.Season the sauce with salt and pepper, then reduce the heat to medium-low and let it simmer uncovered for 15-20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce thickens considerably and the flavors concentrate.
9.While the sauce simmers, taste and adjust the seasoning — the sauce should be well-seasoned now since the eggs will dilute the flavor slightly, so it should taste slightly bolder than desired.
10.Using the back of a large spoon, create 6 evenly spaced wells (indentations) in the thickened tomato sauce, pressing down firmly to form deep hollows that will cradle the eggs.
11.Crack one egg into each well, being careful not to break the yolks, and spacing them evenly so each serving will have one egg nestled in the sauce.
12.Season the eggs with a light sprinkle of salt and pepper, then cover the skillet with a tight-fitting lid and cook on medium-low heat for 6-8 minutes until the egg whites are fully set and opaque but the yolks are still runny.
13.For firmer yolks, cook for 2-3 additional minutes with the lid on, or place the skillet under the broiler for 1-2 minutes to set the tops of the eggs without overcooking the bottoms.
14.Remove the lid, scatter crumbled feta cheese over the top (if using), and let it sit for 1 minute to warm slightly and soften.
15.Garnish generously with fresh chopped parsley or cilantro, drizzle with extra virgin olive oil, and sprinkle with an additional pinch of chili flakes for color and heat.
16.Serve the shakshuka directly in the skillet at the table with plenty of warm crusty bread, pita, or challah for dipping and scooping up the rich, spiced tomato sauce and runny egg yolks.


Recipe 12: Lamb Rogan Josh

Ingredients:

700g lamb shoulder (cut into 2-inch pieces), 2 large onions (thinly sliced), 1 cup yogurt, 4 tbsp mustard oil or ghee, 2 tbsp ginger-garlic paste, 4 whole dried Kashmiri red chilies, 4 green cardamom pods, 4 cloves, 1 cinnamon stick, 2 bay leaves, 1 tsp fennel powder, 1 tsp ginger powder (saunth), 2 tsp red chili powder (Kashmiri for color), 1 tsp turmeric, 1 tsp garam masala, ½ tsp asafoetida (hing), salt to taste, fresh cilantro for garnish, 2 cups warm water.

Steps:

1.Heat the mustard oil in a heavy-bottomed Dutch oven or pressure cooker over high heat until it begins to smoke lightly, then reduce the heat to medium — this removes the pungent raw mustard flavor and mellows the oil.
2.Add the whole dried Kashmiri red chilies, cardamom pods, cloves, cinnamon stick, and bay leaves to the hot oil, letting them crackle and sizzle for 30-45 seconds until they become fragrant and the oil is infused with their aroma.
3.Add the thinly sliced onions and cook on medium-high heat for 12-15 minutes, stirring frequently, until they turn deep golden brown — this long caramelization is essential for the rich, dark color characteristic of rogan josh.
4.Add the asafoetida and ginger-garlic paste, stirring well and cooking for 2-3 minutes until the raw smell disappears completely and the paste turns golden and aromatic.
5.Add the lamb pieces to the pot, increase the heat to high, and sear the meat on all sides for 5-6 minutes, turning occasionally, until each piece develops a deep brown crust — do not rush this step as browning creates flavor.
6.Reduce the heat to medium, add the turmeric, red chili powder, fennel powder, and ginger powder, and stir well to coat all the lamb pieces evenly with the spice mixture, cooking for 1-2 minutes.
7.Whisk the yogurt until completely smooth (to prevent curdling) and add it to the pot one tablespoon at a time, stirring vigorously after each addition and letting each tablespoon incorporate before adding the next.
8.Cook the yogurt-meat mixture for 8-10 minutes on medium heat, stirring occasionally, until the yogurt thickens, darkens, and the oil begins to separate and pool around the edges of the masala.
9.Add 2 cups of warm water, stir well, and bring the mixture to a boil, then reduce the heat to the lowest setting possible, cover the pot tightly with a heavy lid, and let it simmer for 1.5-2 hours.
10.Check the lamb every 30 minutes, stirring gently to prevent sticking on the bottom, and add a splash more water if the gravy becomes too thick before the meat is tender.
11.After 1.5 hours, test the lamb by piercing a piece with a fork — it should be completely tender and almost falling apart; if not, continue cooking for another 30 minutes.
12.Once the lamb is tender, remove the lid and increase the heat to medium, letting the gravy reduce for 10-15 minutes until it is thick, glossy, and clings to the meat pieces.
13.Add the garam masala, stir gently, and cook for 2 more minutes to let the finishing spice blend meld with the gravy.
14.Taste and adjust the salt, then remove and discard the whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaves) if desired, or leave them for presentation.
15.Transfer the rogan josh to a serving dish, garnish with fresh cilantro leaves, and serve hot with steamed basmati rice, naan bread, or roti for a traditional Kashmiri meal experience.


Recipe 13: Chicken Parmesan

Ingredients:

4 chicken breasts (pounded to ½-inch thickness), 1 cup all-purpose flour, 3 eggs (beaten), 2 cups Italian breadcrumbs, ½ cup grated Parmesan cheese, 1 tsp garlic powder, 1 tsp dried oregano, 1 tsp dried basil, salt and pepper to taste, 2 cups marinara sauce, 2 cups shredded mozzarella cheese, ½ cup fresh basil leaves, vegetable oil for frying, cooked spaghetti for serving.

Steps:

1.Preheat your oven to 425°F (220°C) and position a rack in the upper third of the oven, which will help the cheese melt and brown quickly under the broiler-like heat.
2.Place each chicken breast between two sheets of plastic wrap and pound them to an even ½-inch thickness using a meat mallet or rolling pin — even thickness ensures uniform cooking and prevents dry edges.
3.Set up a three-stage breading station: place flour seasoned with salt, pepper, and garlic powder in the first shallow dish; beaten eggs in the second dish; and a mixture of breadcrumbs, grated Parmesan, oregano, and basil in the third dish.
4.Dredge each chicken breast first in the seasoned flour, shaking off excess, then dip it into the beaten egg (letting excess drip off), and finally press it firmly into the breadcrumb mixture on both sides.
5.Place the breaded chicken on a wire rack set over a baking sheet and let it rest for 10 minutes — this allows the coating to adhere better and creates a crispier crust when fried.
6.Heat about ½ inch of vegetable oil in a large, heavy skillet over medium-high heat until it reaches 350°F (175°C) — test by dropping a breadcrumb into the oil; it should sizzle immediately and float.
7.Fry the breaded chicken breasts, two at a time to avoid crowding, for 3-4 minutes per side until the coating is deep golden brown and crispy, then transfer to a wire rack to drain.
8.Spread a thin layer of marinara sauce (about ½ cup) across the bottom of a large baking dish to prevent the chicken from sticking and to create a base layer of flavor.
9.Place the fried chicken breasts on top of the sauce in the baking dish, then spoon 2-3 tablespoons of marinara sauce over each breast, spreading it to cover most of the surface but leaving the edges exposed to stay crispy.
10.Top each chicken breast with a generous handful of shredded mozzarella cheese, distributing it evenly so it melts into a complete, golden layer.
11.Bake in the preheated oven for 15-20 minutes until the cheese is completely melted, bubbly, and beginning to develop golden-brown spots on the surface.
12.For extra browning, switch the oven to broil for the final 1-2 minutes, watching carefully to avoid burning — the cheese should be beautifully spotted with brown.
13.While the chicken bakes, cook the spaghetti according to package directions in well-salted water, drain, and toss with a ladle of the remaining warmed marinara sauce.
14.Remove the chicken from the oven, let it rest for 3-5 minutes to allow the cheese to set slightly and the juices in the chicken to redistribute.
15.Serve each chicken parmesan over a bed of sauced spaghetti, garnished with torn fresh basil leaves and an extra sprinkle of grated Parmesan cheese.


Recipe 14: Miso Ramen

Ingredients:

400g fresh ramen noodles, 4 cups chicken broth, 2 cups dashi stock (or water with 1 tsp dashi powder), 3 tbsp white miso paste, 1 tbsp soy sauce, 1 tbsp mirin, 1 tbsp sesame oil, 2 cloves garlic (minced), 1 tbsp fresh ginger (grated), 2 soft-boiled eggs (halved), 200g chashu pork (or sliced pork belly, seared), 1 cup corn kernels, 2 green onions (sliced), nori sheets, bean sprouts, sesame seeds, chili oil (optional).

Steps:

1.Begin by preparing the soft-boiled eggs: bring a pot of water to a rolling boil, gently lower the eggs into the water using a spoon, and cook for exactly 6 minutes and 30 seconds for a jammy, slightly runny yolk.
2.Transfer the eggs immediately to an ice bath (a bowl of ice water) and let them cool for at least 5 minutes before peeling — the ice bath stops the cooking and makes peeling much easier.
3.If using pork belly, slice it into ¼-inch thick pieces and sear them in a hot skillet with a drizzle of oil for 1-2 minutes per side until caramelized with crispy edges, then set aside.
4.In a large pot, heat the sesame oil over medium heat and sauté the minced garlic and grated ginger for 1-2 minutes until fragrant but not browned, stirring constantly.
5.Pour in the chicken broth and dashi stock, increase the heat to high, and bring the mixture to a boil, then reduce to a simmer and cook for 5 minutes to let the garlic and ginger infuse the broth.
6.Place the miso paste in a fine-mesh strainer or small ladle and submerge it partially in the hot broth, stirring and dissolving the miso through the strainer — this prevents lumps and ensures a smooth broth.
7.Add the soy sauce and mirin to the broth, stir well, and taste — the broth should be savory, slightly sweet, and deeply umami; adjust the miso or soy sauce as needed.
8.Keep the broth at a gentle simmer (never boil after adding miso, as high heat destroys the beneficial probiotics and dulls the flavor).
9.Cook the fresh ramen noodles in a separate pot of boiling water according to the package directions (usually 2-3 minutes), then drain immediately and divide among serving bowls.
10.Bring a small pot of water to a boil and blanch the bean sprouts for 30 seconds, then drain — this softens them slightly while maintaining their crunch.
11.Blanch the corn kernels in the same boiling water for 1 minute, then drain and set aside for topping the ramen.
12.Ladle the hot miso broth generously over the noodles in each bowl, filling it about three-quarters full.
13.Arrange the toppings artfully on top of the noodles: place the halved soft-boiled egg on one side (yolk facing up), lay the seared pork slices next to it, and add a mound of corn and bean sprouts.
14.Stand a sheet of nori vertically in the broth (it will soften slightly), scatter sliced green onions and sesame seeds over the top, and drizzle with chili oil if desired for heat.
15.Serve immediately while the broth is piping hot, instructing diners to mix the toppings into the broth as they eat and slurp the noodles loudly — slurping aerates the noodles and enhances the flavor.


Recipe 15: Moussaka

Ingredients:

3 large eggplants (sliced into ½-inch rounds), 500g ground lamb (or beef), 1 large onion (diced), 4 cloves garlic (minced), 400g canned crushed tomatoes, 2 tbsp tomato paste, 1 tsp cinnamon, 1 tsp allspice, 1 tsp oregano, ½ cup red wine, 2 tbsp olive oil, salt and pepper to taste. Béchamel: 4 tbsp butter, ¼ cup all-purpose flour, 3 cups whole milk, 2 egg yolks, ½ cup grated Parmesan, pinch of nutmeg.

Steps:

1.Preheat your oven to 400°F (200°C) and line two large baking sheets with parchment paper, as you will need space to roast the eggplant slices in a single layer.
2.Arrange the eggplant rounds on the baking sheets, brush both sides generously with olive oil, and season with salt — the oil helps them caramelize and the salt draws out bitter moisture.
3.Roast the eggplant slices for 20-25 minutes, flipping halfway through, until they are golden brown, tender, and slightly caramelized on the edges — roasting (not frying) is healthier and gives better flavor.
4.While the eggplant roasts, heat 2 tablespoons of olive oil in a large skillet over medium-high heat and cook the ground lamb, breaking it into small crumbles, for 8-10 minutes until browned and the fat has rendered.
5.Add the diced onion and cook for 5-6 minutes until soft and translucent, then add the minced garlic and cook for 1 more minute until fragrant.
6.Pour in the red wine and let it bubble and reduce by half, scraping up any browned bits from the bottom of the pan — this deglazing step adds depth and complexity to the meat sauce.
7.Add the crushed tomatoes, tomato paste, cinnamon, allspice, and oregano, stirring well to combine, then reduce the heat to low and simmer uncovered for 20-25 minutes until the sauce is thick and rich.
8.Season the meat sauce with salt and pepper to taste, then set aside while you prepare the béchamel — the sauce should be thick enough to hold its shape on a spoon.
9.For the béchamel, melt the butter in a medium saucepan over medium heat, add the flour, and whisk continuously for 2 minutes to cook out the raw flour taste and create a smooth roux.
10.Gradually pour in the milk in a slow, steady stream, whisking constantly to prevent lumps, and cook the sauce for 5-7 minutes until it thickens enough to coat the back of a spoon.
11.Remove the béchamel from the heat, let it cool for 3 minutes, then whisk in the egg yolks one at a time (tempering them prevents scrambling), followed by half the Parmesan and the nutmeg.
12.To assemble the moussaka, grease a 9x13-inch baking dish and create layers: start with a single layer of roasted eggplant slices on the bottom, overlapping slightly to cover the entire surface.
13.Spread all of the meat sauce evenly over the eggplant layer, then add another layer of roasted eggplant slices on top of the meat.
14.Pour the béchamel sauce over the top layer of eggplant, spreading it gently with a spatula to create a smooth, even surface that reaches the edges of the dish.
15.Sprinkle the remaining Parmesan cheese over the béchamel and bake at 375°F (190°C) for 40-45 minutes until the top is golden brown, puffed slightly, and the edges are bubbling.
16.Remove the moussaka from the oven and let it rest for at least 20 minutes before cutting — this resting period is crucial as it allows the layers to set and makes slicing much easier and neater.
17.Cut into squares and serve warm, garnished with a sprinkle of fresh oregano or parsley, alongside a simple green salad dressed with lemon and olive oil.


Recipe 16: Chicken Fajitas

Ingredients:

500g chicken breast (sliced into strips), 2 bell peppers (red and green, sliced), 1 large onion (sliced), 3 tbsp lime juice, 2 tbsp olive oil, 2 tsp chili powder, 1 tsp cumin, 1 tsp smoked paprika, 1 tsp garlic powder, ½ tsp oregano, salt and pepper to taste, 8 flour tortillas, sour cream, guacamole, shredded cheese, salsa, fresh cilantro, lime wedges.

Steps:

1.In a large bowl, combine the lime juice, 1 tablespoon olive oil, chili powder, cumin, smoked paprika, garlic powder, oregano, salt, and pepper, whisking together to create a vibrant, aromatic marinade.
2.Add the sliced chicken strips to the marinade, toss thoroughly to coat every piece, cover the bowl, and refrigerate for at least 30 minutes (up to 4 hours) to let the flavors penetrate the meat.
3.Heat a large cast iron skillet or heavy-bottomed pan over high heat until it begins to smoke slightly — this extremely high heat is essential for achieving the charred edges that define restaurant-quality fajitas.
4.Remove the chicken from the marinade (shaking off excess liquid) and add it to the hot skillet in a single layer, cooking without stirring for 2-3 minutes to develop a deep char on the first side.
5.Flip the chicken strips and cook for another 2-3 minutes until charred on the second side and cooked through, then transfer to a plate and tent loosely with foil to keep warm.
6.Add the remaining tablespoon of oil to the same hot skillet, then add the sliced bell peppers and onion, spreading them in a single layer and cooking without stirring for 2-3 minutes to develop char.
7.Toss the vegetables and continue cooking for another 3-4 minutes until they are tender-crisp with blistered, charred edges but still retain some firmness and vibrant color.
8.Return the chicken to the skillet with the vegetables, toss everything together, and squeeze fresh lime juice over the top — listen for the satisfying sizzle as the juice hits the hot pan.
9.Season the fajita mixture with additional salt and pepper to taste, and keep it warm over low heat while you prepare the tortillas and toppings.
10.Warm the flour tortillas by wrapping them in a damp paper towel and microwaving for 30 seconds, or by charring each one directly over a gas flame for 10-15 seconds per side.
11.Arrange the warm tortillas on a plate, place the sizzling chicken and vegetable mixture in a separate hot skillet or cast iron pan (the sizzle is part of the presentation).
12.Set up a topping station with bowls of sour cream, guacamole, shredded cheese, salsa, fresh cilantro, and lime wedges so everyone can build their own fajitas.
13.To assemble, lay a warm tortilla flat, spoon the chicken and pepper mixture down the center, add your preferred toppings, squeeze lime over everything, and fold or roll the tortilla.
14.Serve immediately with extra lime wedges and your favorite Mexican sides like refried beans, Mexican rice, or elote (Mexican street corn).


Recipe 17: Risotto alla Milanese (Saffron Risotto)

Ingredients:

300g Arborio rice, 1 liter warm chicken or vegetable stock, 1 large shallot (finely diced), ½ cup dry white wine, ½ tsp saffron threads (soaked in 3 tbsp warm stock), 3 tbsp butter, 2 tbsp olive oil, ½ cup grated Parmigiano-Reggiano, salt and white pepper to taste, extra butter for finishing.

Steps:

1.Pour the stock into a separate saucepan and keep it at a gentle simmer over low heat throughout the entire cooking process — adding cold stock to risotto shocks the rice and creates uneven cooking.
2.Soak the saffron threads in 3 tablespoons of the warm stock for at least 10 minutes, allowing the saffron to release its golden color and distinctive floral, honey-like aroma into the liquid.
3.In a large, heavy-bottomed saucepan or Dutch oven, heat the olive oil and 1 tablespoon of butter over medium heat until the butter melts and begins to foam but does not brown.
4.Add the finely diced shallot and cook for 3-4 minutes, stirring frequently, until it is completely soft and translucent but has no color — the shallot should melt into the risotto, not add crunch.
5.Add the Arborio rice to the pan and stir it constantly for 2 minutes, toasting the grains until the edges become slightly translucent while the center remains opaque — this toasting step helps the rice absorb liquid gradually.
6.Pour in the white wine all at once, stirring vigorously as it sizzles and bubbles, and continue stirring until the wine is completely absorbed by the rice and the pan is nearly dry.
7.Begin adding the warm stock one ladle (about ½ cup) at a time, stirring frequently after each addition, and wait until the liquid is almost completely absorbed before adding the next ladle.
8.Continue this process of adding stock and stirring for 18-20 minutes total, maintaining a gentle simmer — the constant stirring agitates the rice and releases its starches, creating the characteristic creamy texture.
9.After about 15 minutes of cooking, add the saffron-infused stock (threads and all) to the risotto, stirring well to distribute the saffron evenly — the risotto should turn a beautiful, vivid golden yellow.
10.Continue adding stock and stirring for another 3-5 minutes until the rice is al dente — each grain should be tender on the outside with a slight firmness at the very center when bitten.
11.Remove the pan from the heat and add the remaining 2 tablespoons of cold butter and the grated Parmigiano-Reggiano, stirring vigorously for 1 minute to create a final emulsion called the "mantecatura."
12.Season with salt and white pepper to taste, then cover the pan and let the risotto rest for 2 minutes — this final rest allows the starches to settle and the texture to become perfectly fluid.
13.The finished risotto should flow like lava when you tilt the plate — called "all'onda" (like a wave) — not sit in a stiff mound; add a splash more warm stock if needed to achieve this consistency.
14.Spoon the risotto onto warm plates (cold plates will cause it to seize), spreading it gently with the back of the spoon, and serve immediately with an extra grating of Parmesan on top.


Recipe 18: Beef Pho

Ingredients:

Broth: 2 kg beef bones (marrow and knuckle), 1 large onion (halved), 4-inch piece of ginger (halved), 5 star anise, 4 whole cloves, 2 cinnamon sticks, 1 tbsp coriander seeds, 3 tbsp fish sauce, 1 tbsp rock sugar (or brown sugar), 8 cups water, salt to taste. Assembly: 400g rice noodles (banh pho), 300g beef sirloin (sliced paper thin), bean sprouts, Thai basil, cilantro, lime wedges, jalapeño slices, hoisin sauce, sriracha, green onions (sliced).

Steps:

1.Preheat your oven to broil and place the halved onion and ginger cut-side up on a baking sheet, then broil for 8-10 minutes until deeply charred and blistered — this charring develops a smoky sweetness that is essential for authentic pho broth.
2.Place the beef bones in a large stockpot, cover with cold water, and bring to a rolling boil for 10 minutes, then drain and rinse the bones thoroughly under cold running water to remove all impurities and scum.
3.In a dry skillet over medium heat, toast the star anise, cloves, cinnamon sticks, and coriander seeds for 2-3 minutes, shaking the pan frequently, until they become fragrant and slightly darken — toasting intensifies their flavor.
4.Place the toasted spices in a spice bag or tie them in cheesecloth for easy removal later, then return the cleaned bones to the stockpot with 8 cups of fresh water.
5.Add the charred onion, charred ginger, and the spice bag to the pot, bring to a boil over high heat, then immediately reduce to a very gentle simmer — a rolling boil will make the broth cloudy.
6.Simmer the broth uncovered for 3-4 hours, skimming off any foam or fat that rises to the surface every 30 minutes — patience and frequent skimming are the keys to a crystal-clear broth.
7.After 3 hours, strain the broth through a fine-mesh sieve lined with cheesecloth into a clean pot, discarding all the solids, and return the clear broth to a gentle simmer.
8.Season the strained broth with fish sauce, rock sugar, and salt, tasting as you go — the broth should be deeply savory with a subtle sweetness and a clean, aromatic quality.
9.Cook the rice noodles according to the package directions (usually soaking in hot water for 5-8 minutes), then drain and divide among serving bowls.
10.Arrange the paper-thin raw beef slices on top of the noodles in each bowl — slice the beef against the grain as thinly as possible, and partially freezing the beef for 30 minutes beforehand makes slicing easier.
11.Bring the broth to a rolling boil and ladle it very hot over the raw beef and noodles — the boiling broth will cook the thin beef slices instantly, turning them pink and tender.
12.Garnish each bowl with bean sprouts, Thai basil leaves, cilantro, sliced jalapeño, green onions, and a squeeze of lime — these fresh herbs and aromatics are essential, not optional.
13.Serve with small side dishes of hoisin sauce and sriracha for diners to add to their bowls according to their personal preference, and eat immediately while the broth is steaming hot.


Recipe 19: Tiramisu

Ingredients:

6 egg yolks, ¾ cup granulated sugar, 500g mascarpone cheese, 2 cups heavy cream, 2 cups strong espresso (cooled), 3 tbsp coffee liqueur (Kahlúa or Marsala wine), 300g savoiardi (ladyfinger biscuits), 3 tbsp unsweetened cocoa powder, dark chocolate shavings for garnish.

Steps:

1.Brew 2 cups of strong espresso using your preferred method and let it cool completely to room temperature, then stir in the coffee liqueur or Marsala wine — the alcohol adds depth but can be omitted for a family-friendly version.
2.In a large heatproof bowl, whisk together the egg yolks and sugar until well combined, then place the bowl over a pot of gently simmering water (double boiler method), making sure the bowl does not touch the water.
3.Whisk the egg yolk mixture constantly over the simmering water for 8-10 minutes until it thickens significantly, becomes pale yellow, and falls from the whisk in thick ribbons that hold their shape briefly — this is called a sabayon.
4.Remove the bowl from the heat and let the sabayon cool for 10 minutes, stirring occasionally to prevent a skin from forming on the surface.
5.In a separate large bowl, beat the mascarpone cheese with a spatula or wooden spoon until it is smooth and creamy — do not use an electric mixer, as mascarpone can become grainy if overworked.
6.Fold the cooled sabayon into the mascarpone in three additions, using a large rubber spatula and gentle folding motions (cut down the center, sweep along the bottom, fold over the top) to maintain as much air as possible.
7.In another clean bowl, whip the heavy cream with an electric mixer until it forms stiff peaks — the cream should hold its shape firmly when the whisk is lifted and not droop at all.
8.Gently fold the whipped cream into the mascarpone-sabayon mixture in two additions, using the same folding technique, until the filling is uniformly light, airy, and cloud-like with no streaks remaining.
9.Pour the cooled coffee-liqueur mixture into a shallow dish. Working quickly, dip each ladyfinger into the coffee for exactly 2-3 seconds per side — do not soak them, as they will become soggy and fall apart.
10.Arrange the dipped ladyfingers in a single layer on the bottom of a 9x13-inch glass or ceramic dish, breaking some to fill any gaps and create a complete, even base layer.
11.Spread half of the mascarpone cream mixture evenly over the ladyfinger layer, using an offset spatula or the back of a spoon to smooth it into every corner and create a level surface.
12.Repeat the process: dip another layer of ladyfingers in coffee and arrange them over the cream, then top with the remaining mascarpone cream, smoothing the surface beautifully.
13.Cover the dish tightly with plastic wrap (without letting it touch the cream surface) and refrigerate for a minimum of 6 hours, but preferably overnight — this resting time allows the ladyfingers to soften and meld with the cream.
14.Just before serving, dust the top generously with unsweetened cocoa powder using a fine-mesh sieve for an even, professional-looking coating.
15.Garnish with dark chocolate shavings (use a vegetable peeler on a chocolate bar) and serve chilled, cutting clean slices with a sharp knife dipped in hot water for the neatest presentation.


Recipe 20: Dal Makhani

Ingredients:

1 cup whole black lentils (sabut urad dal), ¼ cup kidney beans (rajma), 3 tbsp butter, 1 tbsp oil, 1 large onion (finely chopped), 2 tomatoes (pureed), 1 tbsp ginger-garlic paste, 2 green chilies (slit), 1 tsp cumin seeds, 1 tsp turmeric, 1 tsp red chili powder, 1 tsp garam masala, ½ cup heavy cream, 1 tbsp kasuri methi (dried fenugreek leaves), salt to taste, fresh cilantro for garnish.

Steps:

1.Wash the black lentils and kidney beans thoroughly under cold running water, then soak them together in plenty of water for at least 8 hours or overnight — this long soak is essential for achieving the creamy texture that defines dal makhani.
2.Drain the soaked lentils and beans, transfer them to a pressure cooker with 4 cups of fresh water and ½ teaspoon of salt, and pressure cook on high heat until you hear 2 whistles, then reduce to low heat and cook for 30 more minutes.
3.Let the pressure release naturally (do not quick-release, as this can cause the dal to spew), then open the cooker and check the lentils — they should be completely soft and mashable between your fingers.
4.Using the back of a large spoon or a potato masher, mash about one-third of the lentils and beans directly in the pot — this creates a creamy, thick base while leaving the remaining two-thirds whole for texture.
5.In a separate large, heavy-bottomed pan or kadai, heat the butter and oil together over medium heat until the butter melts and begins to foam, then add the cumin seeds and let them crackle for 30 seconds.
6.Add the finely chopped onion and cook for 8-10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the onion turns deep golden brown — this caramelization adds sweetness and depth to the dal.
7.Add the ginger-garlic paste and slit green chilies, cooking for 2 minutes while stirring constantly until the raw smell disappears and the paste turns fragrant and lightly golden.
8.Pour in the pureed tomatoes, add the turmeric and red chili powder, and cook the masala for 8-10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the tomatoes darken, thicken, and the oil begins to separate at the edges.
9.Add the mashed and whole lentils along with their cooking liquid to the tomato masala, stirring well to combine everything into a thick, cohesive mixture.
10.Simmer the dal on low heat for 45 minutes to 1 hour, stirring every 10 minutes and adding splashes of hot water if it becomes too thick — the long, slow cooking is what develops the rich, complex flavor.
11.Crush the kasuri methi between your palms and add it to the dal along with the garam masala, stirring well and simmering for 5 more minutes.
12.Pour in the heavy cream, stir gently to incorporate, and simmer for a final 5 minutes until the dal is luxuriously thick, creamy, and glossy with visible butter pooling on the surface.
13.Taste and adjust the salt, then transfer to a serving bowl, drizzle with a swirl of cream, a pat of butter, and fresh cilantro leaves.
14.Serve hot with steamed basmati rice, naan, or roti — dal makhani tastes even better the next day after the flavors have continued to meld overnight.


Recipe 21: Salmon en Croûte

Ingredients:

2 salmon fillets (skinless, about 200g each), 1 sheet puff pastry (thawed if frozen), 200g fresh spinach, 100g cream cheese, 2 tbsp Dijon mustard, 1 egg (beaten, for egg wash), 1 tbsp lemon juice, 2 cloves garlic (minced), salt and pepper to taste, 1 tbsp butter, fresh dill for garnish.

Steps:

1.Preheat your oven to 400°F (200°C) and line a baking sheet with parchment paper, as the pastry needs a non-stick surface and even heat distribution for proper puffing.
2.Season both salmon fillets generously with salt, pepper, and lemon juice on all sides, then set aside at room temperature while you prepare the filling.
3.Heat butter in a skillet over medium-high heat, add the minced garlic, and cook for 30 seconds until fragrant, then add all the fresh spinach and toss continuously with tongs until completely wilted (about 2-3 minutes).
4.Transfer the wilted spinach to a clean kitchen towel or cheesecloth, let it cool for 5 minutes, then squeeze out as much liquid as possible — excess moisture will make the pastry soggy and prevent proper puffing.
5.In a small bowl, mix the cream cheese with the squeezed spinach, a pinch of salt, pepper, and a teaspoon of lemon juice until well combined into a smooth, spreadable mixture.
6.Roll out the puff pastry sheet on a lightly floured surface to approximately 12x14 inches, ensuring it is large enough to fully enclose both fillets with overlap for sealing.
7.Spread a thin layer of Dijon mustard over the center of the pastry where the salmon will sit — the mustard acts as both a flavor component and a moisture barrier between the fish and pastry.
8.Spread half of the spinach-cream cheese mixture in a rectangle on the pastry, place one salmon fillet on top, then spread the remaining spinach mixture over the salmon.
9.Place the second salmon fillet on top of the spinach layer, pressing down gently to create a compact stack that will cook evenly inside the pastry.
10.Fold the puff pastry over the salmon stack, starting with the long sides first, then folding the short ends underneath, pressing and crimping the edges firmly with a fork to seal completely.
11.Carefully transfer the wrapped salmon to the prepared baking sheet, seam-side down, and brush the entire surface generously with the beaten egg wash for a deep golden, glossy finish.
12.Using a sharp knife, score the top of the pastry with a decorative pattern (diagonal lines, crosshatch, or leaf design) being careful to cut only through the top layer and not into the salmon.
13.Bake for 25-30 minutes until the pastry is puffed, deeply golden brown, and the internal temperature of the salmon reaches 145°F (63°C) when checked with a meat thermometer.
14.Remove from the oven and let the en croûte rest for 5 minutes before slicing — this rest allows the juices to redistribute and makes cutting cleaner slices possible.
15.Slice into thick portions with a sharp serrated knife, revealing the beautiful layers of golden pastry, green spinach, pink salmon, and creamy filling, and garnish with fresh dill sprigs.


Recipe 22: Chicken Satay with Peanut Sauce

Ingredients:

500g chicken thighs (cut into thin strips), 1 cup coconut milk, 2 tbsp soy sauce, 2 tbsp brown sugar, 1 tbsp curry powder, 1 tbsp turmeric, 2 tsp ground coriander, 1 tbsp fish sauce, 2 cloves garlic (minced), 1 stalk lemongrass (minced), wooden skewers (soaked in water for 30 minutes). Peanut Sauce: ½ cup peanut butter, 3 tbsp sweet chili sauce, 2 tbsp soy sauce, 1 tbsp lime juice, 1 tbsp brown sugar, 2 tbsp warm water, 1 tsp sesame oil.

Steps:

1.In a large bowl, combine the coconut milk, soy sauce, brown sugar, curry powder, turmeric, ground coriander, fish sauce, minced garlic, and minced lemongrass, whisking until the sugar dissolves completely and the marinade is smooth and aromatic.
2.Add the chicken strips to the marinade, ensuring every piece is fully submerged and coated, then cover and refrigerate for a minimum of 2 hours, or ideally overnight, turning the bag or stirring once during marination.
3.Soak the wooden skewers in water for at least 30 minutes to prevent them from burning during grilling — this step is essential and often overlooked.
4.Remove the chicken from the marinade, shaking off excess liquid, and thread each strip onto a soaked wooden skewer in a weaving, serpentine pattern, pushing the meat down to create a compact, even layer.
5.Preheat your grill, grill pan, or broiler to high heat and lightly oil the grates or surface to prevent the chicken from sticking and tearing when you flip the skewers.
6.Grill the satay skewers for 3-4 minutes per side, turning only once, until the chicken is cooked through with beautiful char marks and the edges are slightly caramelized from the sugar in the marinade.
7.While the chicken grills, prepare the peanut sauce by combining the peanut butter, sweet chili sauce, soy sauce, lime juice, brown sugar, warm water, and sesame oil in a small bowl.
8.Whisk the peanut sauce vigorously until completely smooth and pourable — if it's too thick, add more warm water one tablespoon at a time until you reach a dipping consistency.
9.Taste the peanut sauce and adjust the balance: add more lime juice for tanginess, more sugar for sweetness, more chili sauce for heat, or more soy sauce for saltiness.
10.Transfer the grilled satay skewers to a serving platter, arrange the peanut sauce in a small bowl alongside, and garnish with chopped peanuts, sliced cucumber, and fresh cilantro.
11.Serve immediately with the warm peanut dipping sauce, alongside steamed jasmine rice or a fresh cucumber salad for a complete Southeast Asian meal experience.


Recipe 23: Pasta Carbonara

Ingredients:

400g spaghetti (or rigatoni), 200g guanciale (or pancetta, cut into small strips), 4 large egg yolks, 2 whole eggs, 1 cup freshly grated Pecorino Romano, ½ cup freshly grated Parmigiano-Reggiano, freshly cracked black pepper (generous amount), salt for pasta water.

Steps:

1.Bring a very large pot of water to a rolling boil and salt it generously — the water should taste like the sea, as this is the only opportunity to season the pasta itself.
2.In a large bowl, whisk together the 4 egg yolks and 2 whole eggs until well combined, then add both grated cheeses and a very generous amount of freshly cracked black pepper, mixing until you have a thick, creamy paste.
3.Cook the spaghetti in the boiling salted water for 1 minute less than the package directions indicate, as the pasta will continue cooking in the hot pan with the other ingredients.
4.While the pasta cooks, place the guanciale strips in a large, cold skillet (starting cold renders the fat slowly and evenly), then turn the heat to medium and cook for 8-10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the guanciale is crispy and golden and has released its flavorful fat.
5.Remove the skillet from the heat and let it cool for 2-3 minutes — this is critically important because adding the egg mixture to a screaming hot pan will scramble the eggs instead of creating a creamy sauce.
6.Reserve 1 cup of the starchy pasta cooking water before draining the pasta, then add the drained pasta directly to the skillet with the guanciale and its rendered fat.
7.Toss the pasta vigorously in the fat for 30 seconds to coat every strand, then pour the egg and cheese mixture over the hot pasta, tossing continuously and rapidly with tongs.
8.Add splashes of the reserved pasta water (2-3 tablespoons at a time) while tossing, which helps create a silky, emulsified sauce that clings to the pasta — the starch in the water is the secret to the creamy consistency.
9.Continue tossing and adding pasta water until the sauce is glossy, creamy, and coats each strand of spaghetti in a smooth, velvety layer — the residual heat from the pasta and pan gently cooks the eggs to the perfect consistency.
10.The entire process from adding the egg mixture to serving should take no more than 1-2 minutes — speed and constant motion are essential to prevent the eggs from scrambling.
11.Serve immediately on warmed plates (carbonara waits for no one), topped with an additional grating of Pecorino Romano and a very generous amount of freshly cracked black pepper — the pepper is not a garnish, it is a key flavor component.


Recipe 24: Greek Moussaka (Vegetarian)

Ingredients:

3 large eggplants (sliced into ½-inch rounds), 2 large zucchini (sliced), 400g canned lentils (drained), 1 large onion (diced), 3 cloves garlic (minced), 400g canned crushed tomatoes, 2 tbsp tomato paste, 1 tsp cinnamon, 1 tsp oregano, ½ tsp allspice, 3 tbsp olive oil, salt and pepper to taste. Béchamel: 3 tbsp butter, 3 tbsp flour, 2.5 cups milk, 1 egg yolk, ½ cup grated Parmesan, pinch of nutmeg.

Steps:

1.Preheat the oven to 400°F (200°C) and arrange the eggplant and zucchini slices on oiled baking sheets in single layers, brushing both sides with olive oil and seasoning with salt.
2.Roast the vegetables for 20-25 minutes, flipping once, until golden and tender, then set aside — roasting develops better flavor than salting and frying, and is much less greasy.
3.Heat olive oil in a large skillet, sauté the onion for 6-7 minutes until soft, add garlic for 1 minute, then add the drained lentils, crushed tomatoes, tomato paste, cinnamon, oregano, and allspice.
4.Simmer the lentil sauce for 15-20 minutes until thick and rich, season with salt and pepper, then set aside — the sauce should be thick enough to hold its shape when spooned.
5.For the béchamel, melt butter in a saucepan, whisk in flour for 2 minutes, gradually add milk while whisking constantly, and cook until thick enough to coat a spoon (5-7 minutes).
6.Remove from heat, cool slightly, then whisk in the egg yolk, half the Parmesan, and nutmeg until smooth and creamy.
7.In a 9x13 baking dish, layer: eggplant, lentil sauce, zucchini, remaining lentil sauce, remaining eggplant, then pour the béchamel over the top, spreading evenly.
8.Sprinkle the remaining Parmesan on top and bake at 375°F for 40-45 minutes until golden and bubbling, then rest for 20 minutes before cutting to allow the layers to set.


Recipe 25: Thai Green Curry

Ingredients:

500g chicken thighs (sliced), 3 tbsp green curry paste, 400ml coconut milk, 1 cup coconut cream, 2 tbsp fish sauce, 1 tbsp palm sugar, 1 cup Thai eggplant (halved), 1 red bell pepper (sliced), 1 cup bamboo shoots, 5 kaffir lime leaves, 1 cup Thai basil leaves, 2 tbsp vegetable oil, 2 Thai chilies (sliced), jasmine rice for serving.

Steps:

1.Heat the vegetable oil in a wok or large skillet over medium-high heat, add the green curry paste, and fry it in the oil for 2-3 minutes, stirring constantly, until the paste is fragrant and the oil begins to separate and turn green.
2.Add 1 cup of coconut cream (the thick top layer from the can) to the curry paste and cook for 3-4 minutes, stirring frequently, until the mixture thickens and the green oil separates and pools on the surface — this technique is called "cracking" the coconut cream.
3.Add the sliced chicken thighs to the wok, stir to coat with the curry paste mixture, and cook for 4-5 minutes until the chicken is lightly browned on the outside but not fully cooked through.
4.Pour in the remaining coconut milk, fish sauce, and palm sugar, stirring well to combine, then add the torn kaffir lime leaves and bring the curry to a gentle simmer.
5.Add the Thai eggplant halves and cook for 5 minutes, then add the sliced bell pepper and bamboo shoots, continuing to simmer for another 5-7 minutes until all the vegetables are tender but still have some bite.
6.Taste the curry and adjust the seasoning — add more fish sauce for saltiness, more palm sugar for sweetness, or more curry paste for intensity and heat.
7.Remove from heat, stir in the fresh Thai basil leaves (they will wilt beautifully from the residual heat), and add sliced Thai chilies for extra fire if desired.
8.Serve immediately over steamed jasmine rice, garnished with additional Thai basil and a kaffir lime leaf, with lime wedges on the side for squeezing.


Recipe 26: Classic Beef Burger

Ingredients:

600g ground beef (80/20 chuck), 4 brioche burger buns, 4 slices cheddar cheese, 1 large tomato (sliced), lettuce leaves, 1 red onion (sliced into rings), dill pickle slices, salt and pepper, 2 tbsp butter. Special sauce: 3 tbsp mayo, 1 tbsp ketchup, 1 tbsp yellow mustard, 1 tbsp pickle relish, ½ tsp garlic powder, ½ tsp smoked paprika.

Steps:

1.Divide the ground beef into 4 equal portions and gently form each into a ball — handle the meat as little as possible to avoid compacting it, which would result in dense, tough burgers rather than juicy, tender ones.
2.Press each ball into a flat patty about ¾-inch thick and slightly wider than your buns (they will shrink during cooking), then press a shallow dimple in the center of each patty with your thumb to prevent it from puffing up into a dome.
3.Season both sides of each patty generously with salt and pepper immediately before cooking — salting too early draws out moisture and makes the meat tough.
4.Heat a cast iron skillet or grill pan over high heat until it is smoking hot, then place the patties in the dry pan (no oil needed, as the beef fat will render and provide lubrication).
5.Cook the patties undisturbed for 3-4 minutes on the first side until a deep, dark crust forms and the edges begin to look cooked, then flip once using a spatula.
6.Place a slice of cheddar cheese on each patty immediately after flipping, cover the pan with a lid or large metal bowl for 1 minute to trap steam and melt the cheese completely.
7.Cook for another 3 minutes for medium doneness (pink center), then remove the patties and let them rest on a plate for 2 minutes — resting allows the juices to redistribute.
8.While the patties rest, split the brioche buns and toast them cut-side down in the same skillet with butter for 1-2 minutes until golden brown and slightly crispy.
9.Mix all the special sauce ingredients together in a small bowl until smooth and well combined.
10.Spread the special sauce generously on both cut sides of each toasted bun, then layer lettuce on the bottom bun, place the cheese-topped patty on the lettuce, and add tomato slices, onion rings, and pickle slices.
11.Close the burger with the top bun, press down gently, and serve immediately with crispy french fries and a cold drink.


Recipe 27: Chicken Korma

Ingredients:

700g chicken thighs (boneless, cubed), 1 cup yogurt, 1 cup heavy cream, 1 large onion (thinly sliced), ¼ cup cashews (soaked and blended into paste), 2 tbsp ginger-garlic paste, 4 green cardamom, 4 cloves, 1 cinnamon stick, 1 bay leaf, 1 tsp turmeric, 1 tsp garam masala, 1 tsp coriander powder, ½ tsp white pepper, 3 tbsp ghee, 2 tbsp oil, saffron pinch soaked in warm milk, salt to taste, cilantro and toasted almonds for garnish.

Steps:

1.Heat the ghee and oil in a heavy-bottomed pot over medium heat, add the cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and bay leaf, and let them sizzle for 30 seconds until fragrant and the oil is infused with their aroma.
2.Add the thinly sliced onions and cook on medium-low heat for 15-20 minutes, stirring frequently, until they turn deep golden brown — this slow caramelization creates the sweet, rich base that distinguishes korma from other curries.
3.Add the ginger-garlic paste and cook for 2 minutes until the raw smell disappears, then add the turmeric, coriander powder, and white pepper, stirring for 1 minute to bloom the spices.
4.Add the chicken pieces and sear on medium-high heat for 5-6 minutes, turning occasionally, until lightly browned on all sides and the exterior has sealed.
5.Whisk the yogurt until smooth and add it to the pot one tablespoon at a time, stirring vigorously after each addition to prevent curdling, and cook for 5-6 minutes until the yogurt thickens and oil separates.
6.Stir in the cashew paste, mixing thoroughly to incorporate it into the sauce, which adds a subtle nutty sweetness and luxurious thickening to the curry.
7.Pour in the heavy cream and saffron-infused milk, stir gently, reduce the heat to low, cover the pot, and simmer for 20-25 minutes until the chicken is completely tender and the sauce is rich and creamy.
8.Add the garam masala, season with salt, stir gently, and simmer uncovered for 5 more minutes until the sauce reaches your desired consistency.
9.Garnish with fresh cilantro and toasted slivered almonds, serve with steamed basmati rice, naan, or paratha for a mild, luxurious, and deeply comforting curry experience.


Recipe 28: Caprese Salad

Ingredients:

4 large ripe heirloom tomatoes (various colors), 400g fresh buffalo mozzarella (or burrata), fresh basil leaves (large), extra virgin olive oil (high quality), balsamic glaze, flaky sea salt (Maldon), freshly cracked black pepper, optional: prosciutto, arugula.

Steps:

1.Remove the mozzarella from the refrigerator 30 minutes before serving to bring it to room temperature — cold mozzarella has less flavor and a rubbery texture compared to room-temperature cheese.
2.Slice the heirloom tomatoes into ¼-inch thick rounds, discarding the very top and bottom slices, and arrange them on paper towels for 5 minutes to absorb excess moisture that would water down the dressing.
3.Slice the mozzarella into rounds of similar thickness to the tomatoes, creating a visual harmony and ensuring each bite has an equal ratio of tomato to cheese.
4.Arrange the tomato and mozzarella slices in an alternating, overlapping pattern on a large serving platter, creating a beautiful circular or linear presentation with the colors interplaying.
5.Tuck large, whole fresh basil leaves between the tomato and mozzarella slices, pressing them gently so they don't curl — tearing the basil slightly releases more of its aromatic oils.
6.Drizzle the highest quality extra virgin olive oil you have generously over the entire salad in a slow, steady stream, ensuring every slice is lightly coated — this is not the time for cheap oil.
7.Drizzle balsamic glaze (not regular balsamic vinegar, which is too thin and acidic) in a zigzag pattern over the top for concentrated sweetness and a beautiful visual contrast.
8.Sprinkle flaky sea salt (such as Maldon) over everything, finishing with freshly cracked black pepper — the salt crystals should sit visibly on the tomato slices for both flavor and texture.
9.Let the salad sit for 5 minutes at room temperature before serving, allowing the salt to draw out the tomato juices, which combine with the olive oil to create a natural dressing.
10.Serve immediately at room temperature with crusty bread for soaking up the pooled juices and olive oil — never chill this salad, as cold mutes the flavors.


Recipe 29: Chicken Shawarma

Ingredients:

700g chicken thighs (boneless), ¼ cup plain yogurt, 3 tbsp olive oil, 3 tbsp lemon juice, 3 cloves garlic (minced), 2 tsp cumin, 2 tsp paprika, 1 tsp turmeric, 1 tsp cinnamon, ½ tsp cardamom, ½ tsp cayenne, salt and pepper. Garlic sauce: ½ cup mayo, 3 cloves garlic (minced), 1 tbsp lemon juice, 1 tbsp olive oil. Serve with: pita bread, pickled turnips, sliced tomatoes, shredded lettuce, tahini sauce, pickles.

Steps:

1.In a large bowl, combine the yogurt, olive oil, lemon juice, minced garlic, cumin, paprika, turmeric, cinnamon, cardamom, cayenne, salt, and pepper, whisking until smooth to create a thick, vibrant, and intensely aromatic marinade.
2.Add the chicken thighs to the marinade, ensuring every piece is thoroughly coated, then cover and refrigerate for at least 4 hours, ideally overnight — the yogurt tenderizes the meat while the spices penetrate deeply.
3.Preheat your oven to 475°F (245°C) and line a baking sheet with foil, placing a wire rack on top to allow air circulation and prevent the chicken from sitting in its own juices.
4.Remove the chicken from the marinade, shaking off excess, and arrange the thighs on the wire rack in a single layer without overlapping for even cooking and maximum browning.
5.Roast for 20-25 minutes until the chicken develops deeply charred, caramelized edges and the internal temperature reaches 165°F (74°C) — the high heat creates the signature shawarma char.
6.While the chicken roasts, prepare the garlic sauce (toum) by combining mayo, minced garlic, lemon juice, and olive oil in a bowl, whisking until smooth and pungent.
7.Remove the chicken from the oven and let it rest for 5 minutes, then slice it into thin strips against the grain — the pieces should be juicy, tender, and coated in the caramelized spice crust.
8.Warm the pita bread by wrapping in foil and heating in the oven for 5 minutes, or charring directly over a gas flame for 15-20 seconds per side.
9.To assemble, spread garlic sauce on the warm pita, layer with shredded lettuce, sliced tomatoes, pickled turnips, and a generous pile of the sliced shawarma chicken.
10.Drizzle with tahini sauce, add pickles, wrap tightly in parchment paper or foil for easy eating, and serve immediately with extra garlic sauce and lemon wedges on the side.


Recipe 30: Lobster Mac and Cheese

Ingredients:

400g elbow macaroni, 2 lobster tails (about 200g each, cooked and chopped), 3 tbsp butter, 3 tbsp all-purpose flour, 3 cups whole milk, 2 cups sharp white cheddar (grated), 1 cup Gruyère (grated), ½ cup Parmesan (grated), 1 tsp Dijon mustard, ½ tsp paprika, pinch of cayenne, salt and white pepper. Topping: 1 cup panko breadcrumbs, 2 tbsp melted butter, 2 tbsp fresh chives (chopped).

Steps:

1.Preheat your oven to 375°F (190°C) and butter a 9x13-inch baking dish thoroughly, ensuring you coat the corners and sides to prevent the macaroni from sticking during baking.
2.Cook the elbow macaroni in a large pot of well-salted boiling water for 2 minutes less than the package directions (it will continue cooking in the oven), then drain and toss with a small drizzle of olive oil to prevent sticking.
3.Steam or poach the lobster tails for 8-10 minutes until the shells turn bright red and the meat is opaque, then let them cool, remove the meat from the shells, and chop into ½-inch chunks.
4.In a large saucepan, melt 3 tablespoons of butter over medium heat, then add the flour and whisk continuously for 2 minutes to create a smooth, bubbling roux with no raw flour taste.
5.Gradually pour in the milk in a slow, steady stream, whisking constantly to prevent lumps, and cook the béchamel for 5-7 minutes until it thickens enough to coat the back of a spoon.
6.Remove the sauce from heat and stir in the white cheddar, Gruyère, and half the Parmesan, adding one handful at a time and stirring until each addition is completely melted and the sauce is smooth and velvety.
7.Stir in the Dijon mustard, paprika, cayenne, salt, and white pepper, then fold in the cooked macaroni and chopped lobster meat, gently distributing the lobster chunks evenly throughout.
8.Pour the macaroni and cheese mixture into the prepared baking dish, spreading it into an even layer.
9.In a small bowl, toss the panko breadcrumbs with the melted butter until evenly coated, then scatter them over the macaroni along with the remaining Parmesan cheese.
10.Bake for 25-30 minutes until the top is golden brown, the edges are bubbling vigorously, and the panko is crispy and toasted.
11.Let the mac and cheese rest for 5 minutes out of the oven (it will be molten), then garnish with fresh chopped chives and serve immediately while the cheese is still stretchy and gooey.


Recipe 31: Chicken Adobo (Filipino)

Ingredients:

1 kg chicken (bone-in, skin-on pieces), ½ cup soy sauce, ½ cup white vinegar, 1 cup water, 8 cloves garlic (crushed), 2 bay leaves, 1 tbsp whole black peppercorns, 2 tbsp vegetable oil, 1 tbsp brown sugar, 2 green onions (sliced), steamed rice for serving.

Steps:

1.Combine the chicken pieces, soy sauce, vinegar, crushed garlic, bay leaves, and whole black peppercorns in a large bowl or zip-lock bag, toss to coat, and marinate in the refrigerator for at least 1 hour (up to overnight) for the deepest flavor.
2.Remove the chicken from the marinade and pat the pieces dry with paper towels, reserving the marinade (including the garlic, bay leaves, and peppercorns) — drying the chicken is essential for achieving a crispy, golden skin.
3.Heat the vegetable oil in a large Dutch oven or deep skillet over medium-high heat until it shimmers, then place the chicken pieces skin-side down and sear without moving for 4-5 minutes until the skin is deeply golden and crispy.
4.Flip the chicken and sear the other side for 3-4 minutes until browned, then transfer the chicken to a plate, leaving the rendered fat in the pot.
5.Pour the reserved marinade (with all the aromatics) into the pot, add the water and brown sugar, and bring to a boil, scraping up any browned bits from the bottom of the pan.
6.Return the seared chicken to the pot, nestling the pieces into the liquid, and bring everything back to a boil.
7.Reduce the heat to low, cover the pot, and simmer for 35-40 minutes, turning the chicken pieces once halfway through, until the meat is completely tender and nearly falling off the bone.
8.Remove the lid, increase the heat to medium-high, and let the sauce reduce for 10-15 minutes until it is thick, glossy, and coats the chicken like a glaze — the sauce should be intensely savory, tangy, and slightly sweet.
9.Taste and adjust the balance by adding more vinegar for tang, more soy for saltiness, or more sugar for sweetness — the hallmark of great adobo is the harmonious interplay of these three flavors.
10.Transfer to a serving platter, spoon the reduced sauce over the chicken, and garnish with sliced green onions. Serve with steamed white rice to soak up the incredible sauce.


Recipe 32: Lamb Kofta Kebabs

Ingredients:

500g ground lamb, 1 small onion (grated and squeezed dry), 3 cloves garlic (minced), 2 tbsp fresh parsley (finely chopped), 2 tbsp fresh mint (finely chopped), 1 tsp cumin, 1 tsp coriander, 1 tsp paprika, ½ tsp cinnamon, ½ tsp allspice, ¼ tsp cayenne, salt and pepper, 1 tbsp olive oil, wooden or metal skewers (soaked if wooden). Serve with: pita, tzatziki, pickled onions, sumac, fresh herbs.

Steps:

1.Grate the onion on the fine side of a box grater, then place the grated onion in a clean kitchen towel and squeeze out as much liquid as possible — excess moisture will cause the kofta to fall apart on the grill.
2.In a large bowl, combine the ground lamb, squeezed grated onion, minced garlic, chopped parsley, chopped mint, cumin, coriander, paprika, cinnamon, allspice, cayenne, salt, and pepper.
3.Mix the lamb mixture vigorously with your hands for 3-4 minutes, kneading and slapping it against the bowl — this develops the myosin proteins in the meat, which acts as a natural binder and gives the kofta a firm, cohesive texture.
4.Grab a small handful of the mixture and slap it back and forth between your hands 5-6 times to remove air pockets, then test a small piece by cooking it in a skillet — taste and adjust the seasoning of the raw mixture if needed.
5.Divide the mixture into 8 equal portions, then mold each portion around a skewer in a long, thin sausage shape, about 1 inch in diameter, pressing firmly and smoothing the surface with wet hands to prevent sticking.
6.Place the assembled kofta skewers on a lightly oiled plate or tray, cover with plastic wrap, and refrigerate for at least 30 minutes — this firming step helps them hold their shape during grilling.
7.Preheat your grill or grill pan to high heat and brush the grates with oil to prevent sticking, as the lamb fat can cause flare-ups.
8.Grill the kofta kebabs for 3-4 minutes per side (turning only once) until they are charred on the outside and cooked through but still juicy inside — avoid pressing them with a spatula, which squeezes out the juices.
9.Remove the kofta from the grill and let them rest for 3 minutes, then serve on warm pita bread with a generous drizzle of tzatziki sauce, pickled onions, a sprinkle of sumac, and fresh herb leaves.


Recipe 33: Mushroom Risotto

Ingredients:

300g Arborio rice, 400g mixed mushrooms (cremini, shiitake, oyster — sliced), 1 liter warm vegetable stock, ½ cup dry white wine, 1 large shallot (finely diced), 3 cloves garlic (minced), 3 tbsp butter, 2 tbsp olive oil, ½ cup grated Parmesan, 2 tbsp fresh thyme leaves, salt and pepper to taste, truffle oil (optional, for finishing).

Steps:

1.Keep the vegetable stock simmering in a separate saucepan throughout the cooking process, as adding cold stock to risotto interrupts the cooking and creates uneven texture.
2.Heat 1 tablespoon each of butter and olive oil in a large, heavy skillet over high heat until the butter foams, add the sliced mushrooms in a single layer, and cook without stirring for 3-4 minutes until golden brown on one side.
3.Toss the mushrooms and cook for another 2-3 minutes until browned on all sides and their moisture has completely evaporated, then season with salt, pepper, and half the thyme, transfer to a plate, and set aside.
4.In a large saucepan, heat the remaining butter and olive oil over medium heat, add the diced shallot, and cook for 3-4 minutes until soft and translucent without any browning.
5.Add the Arborio rice and toast it for 2 minutes, stirring constantly, until the edges of the grains become slightly translucent — this toasting step helps the rice absorb liquid evenly.
6.Pour in the white wine, stir until completely absorbed, then begin adding the warm stock one ladle at a time, stirring frequently and waiting until each addition is nearly absorbed before adding the next.
7.After about 15 minutes of adding stock, fold in the sautéed mushrooms and continue adding stock and stirring for another 5-7 minutes until the rice is al dente — creamy on the outside with a slight bite at the center.
8.Remove from heat, add the Parmesan, remaining butter, and remaining thyme, stir vigorously for 1 minute (the mantecatura), then cover and rest for 2 minutes.
9.Serve immediately on warm plates, drizzled with truffle oil if desired, and topped with extra Parmesan and thyme — the risotto should flow like lava when the plate is tilted.


Recipe 34: Jerk Chicken (Jamaican)

Ingredients:

1 whole chicken (cut into 8 pieces), 4 green onions (chopped), 4 Scotch bonnet peppers (seeded), 4 cloves garlic, 2-inch piece ginger, 3 tbsp soy sauce, 2 tbsp lime juice, 2 tbsp brown sugar, 1 tbsp allspice, 1 tbsp thyme (fresh), 1 tsp cinnamon, 1 tsp nutmeg, 2 tbsp vegetable oil, salt and pepper, lime wedges for serving.

Steps:

1.Combine the green onions, Scotch bonnet peppers (use gloves when handling), garlic, ginger, soy sauce, lime juice, brown sugar, allspice, thyme, cinnamon, nutmeg, vegetable oil, salt, and pepper in a blender or food processor.
2.Blend the mixture until it forms a smooth, thick, vibrant green paste, scraping down the sides as needed — this is the jerk marinade, and it should be intensely aromatic and fiery.
3.Score the chicken pieces by making 2-3 deep slashes through the skin and into the meat on each piece — these cuts allow the marinade to penetrate deeply into the flesh.
4.Rub the jerk marinade generously over every surface of the chicken, pushing it into the slashes and under the skin wherever possible, then cover and refrigerate for at least 4 hours, preferably overnight.
5.Preheat your grill to medium heat (about 350°F / 175°C) and set it up for indirect grilling — pile the coals on one side or turn off one burner to create a cooler zone for slower cooking.
6.Place the chicken pieces skin-side up on the cooler side of the grill, close the lid, and cook for 30-35 minutes, turning once, until the internal temperature reaches 165°F (74°C).
7.Move the chicken to the hot side of the grill for the final 5 minutes to crisp and slightly char the skin, watching carefully as the sugar in the marinade can burn quickly.
8.Remove the chicken from the grill and let it rest for 10 minutes, then serve with lime wedges, rice and peas, and fried plantains for an authentic Jamaican meal.


Recipe 35: Crème Brûlée

Ingredients:

6 large egg yolks, ½ cup granulated sugar (plus extra for topping), 2 cups heavy cream, 1 vanilla bean (split and scraped, or 2 tsp vanilla extract), pinch of salt.

Steps:

1.Preheat your oven to 325°F (160°C) and place four 6-ounce ramekins in a deep baking dish, ensuring they are evenly spaced and stable.
2.In a medium saucepan, heat the heavy cream with the split vanilla bean (seeds and pod) over medium heat until it just begins to simmer around the edges — do not let it boil, as boiling can cause a skin to form and alter the texture.
3.Remove the cream from heat, cover the pot, and let the vanilla steep for 15 minutes to infuse the cream with maximum vanilla flavor, then remove the pod (rinse and dry it for another use).
4.In a separate bowl, whisk the egg yolks and sugar together vigorously for 2-3 minutes until the mixture is pale yellow, thick, and falls from the whisk in a steady ribbon.
5.Very slowly pour the warm vanilla cream into the egg yolk mixture in a thin, steady stream, whisking constantly to temper the yolks gradually — this prevents the eggs from scrambling from the heat of the cream.
6.Strain the custard through a fine-mesh sieve into a clean bowl or measuring cup with a pour spout, pressing any solids with a spoon — straining removes any chalazae or cooked egg bits for a perfectly silky texture.
7.Divide the custard evenly among the ramekins, filling each one nearly to the top, then use a spoon to skim off any tiny bubbles on the surface for a smooth, flawless finish.
8.Pour enough hot water into the baking dish to reach halfway up the sides of the ramekins, creating a water bath (bain-marie) that ensures gentle, even cooking and prevents the custard from cracking.
9.Carefully transfer the baking dish to the oven and bake for 40-45 minutes until the custards are set around the edges but still have a gentle wobble (like gelatin) in the center when you lightly shake the pan.
10.Remove the ramekins from the water bath and let them cool to room temperature on a wire rack, then cover with plastic wrap and refrigerate for at least 4 hours (up to 2 days) until completely chilled and firm.
11.Just before serving, sprinkle 1-2 teaspoons of granulated sugar evenly over the surface of each custard, tilting and tapping the ramekin to create a thin, uniform layer of sugar.
12.Using a kitchen torch, hold the flame 2-3 inches from the surface and move it in slow, even circles until the sugar melts, bubbles, and caramelizes into a deep amber, glass-like shell — listen for the satisfying crackle.
13.Let the caramelized sugar cool and harden for 1-2 minutes, then serve immediately — the contrast between the warm, crackly sugar crust and the cold, silky custard beneath is the entire magic of crème brûlée.


Recipe 36: Paneer Butter Masala

Ingredients:

400g paneer (cubed), 3 tbsp butter, 1 tbsp oil, 1 large onion (pureed), 4 tomatoes (pureed), 1 tbsp ginger-garlic paste, 2 green chilies, 1 tsp cumin, 1 tsp coriander powder, 1 tsp garam masala, ½ tsp turmeric, 1 tsp red chili powder, ½ cup heavy cream, 1 tbsp sugar, 1 tbsp kasuri methi, salt to taste, cilantro for garnish.

Steps:

1.Heat 1 tablespoon of butter with the oil in a large pan over medium heat, add the paneer cubes in a single layer, and pan-fry them for 2-3 minutes per side until golden brown on at least two sides, then transfer to a plate.
2.In the same pan, add the remaining 2 tablespoons of butter, then add the pureed onion and cook for 8-10 minutes, stirring frequently, until the raw smell disappears and the onion paste turns golden.
3.Add the ginger-garlic paste and slit green chilies, cooking for 2 minutes until fragrant, then add the cumin, coriander, turmeric, and red chili powder, stirring for 1 minute to bloom the spices.
4.Pour in the pureed tomatoes, add the sugar, and cook for 12-15 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the tomato paste thickens, darkens in color, and the oil begins to separate at the edges of the masala.
5.Add the garam masala and crushed kasuri methi, stir well, and cook for 1 more minute, then let the sauce cool slightly and blend it in a blender or with an immersion blender until perfectly smooth.
6.Return the smooth sauce to the pan over low heat, stir in the heavy cream, and mix until the sauce turns a beautiful, creamy orange color.
7.Add the fried paneer cubes to the sauce, stir gently to coat, and simmer on low heat for 5-7 minutes until the paneer absorbs some of the sauce and becomes tender.
8.Adjust seasoning with salt, transfer to a serving bowl, garnish with a swirl of cream and fresh cilantro, and serve hot with naan, roti, or jeera rice.


Recipe 37: Fish and Chips

Ingredients:

4 cod or haddock fillets (about 150g each), 4 large russet potatoes, oil for deep frying. Batter: 1 cup all-purpose flour, ½ cup cornstarch, 1 tsp baking powder, 1 tsp salt, ½ tsp turmeric, 1 cup cold sparkling water (or beer), 1 egg. Serve with: malt vinegar, tartar sauce, lemon wedges, mushy peas.

Steps:

1.Peel the potatoes and cut them into thick chips (about ½-inch wide and 3-4 inches long), then place them in a large bowl of cold water for 30 minutes to remove excess starch, which results in crispier chips.
2.Drain the chips, pat them completely dry with clean kitchen towels (moisture causes dangerous splatter in hot oil), and set aside.
3.Heat the oil in a deep fryer or large, heavy pot to 325°F (160°C) — use a thermometer to monitor the temperature, as accurate oil temperature is critical for proper frying.
4.Blanch the chips by frying them in batches for 5-6 minutes until they are cooked through and soft but have no color — remove them to a wire rack and let them cool completely.
5.While the chips cool, prepare the batter: whisk together the flour, cornstarch, baking powder, salt, and turmeric in a large bowl, then make a well in the center.
6.Add the egg to the well, then gradually pour in the cold sparkling water while whisking from the center outward until you have a smooth, thick batter with the consistency of heavy cream — lumps are fine and actually preferable.
7.Increase the oil temperature to 375°F (190°C) for the final frying and for cooking the fish.
8.Pat the fish fillets completely dry with paper towels, season with salt and pepper, then dust them lightly in plain flour (this helps the batter adhere) and shake off the excess.
9.Dip each floured fillet into the batter, letting the excess drip off for a few seconds, then carefully lower it into the hot oil, holding it just below the surface for 3 seconds before releasing to set the batter.
10.Fry the battered fish for 6-8 minutes, turning once, until the batter is deep golden brown, puffed, and crispy, and the fish inside is cooked through and flakes easily.
11.While the fish fries, return the blanched chips to the 375°F oil and fry for 3-4 minutes until they are golden brown, crispy on the outside, and fluffy on the inside.
12.Remove the chips to a wire rack, season immediately with salt and malt vinegar while hot (the seasoning adheres best to hot, freshly fried surfaces).
13.Serve the fish and chips together on newspaper (for tradition) or a plate, with tartar sauce, lemon wedges, and mushy peas on the side.


Recipe 38: Chicken Pot Pie

Ingredients:

500g cooked chicken (shredded), 1 sheet puff pastry (thawed), 3 tbsp butter, 1 onion (diced), 2 carrots (diced), 2 celery stalks (diced), 3 tbsp flour, 2 cups chicken broth, ½ cup heavy cream, 1 cup frozen peas, 2 tbsp fresh thyme, 1 egg (beaten for egg wash), salt and pepper to taste.

Steps:

1.Preheat your oven to 400°F (200°C) and position a rack in the lower third, which will help the bottom of the pie cook through while the top browns.
2.Melt the butter in a large, deep skillet or Dutch oven over medium heat, add the diced onion, carrots, and celery (the mirepoix), and cook for 8-10 minutes until the vegetables are softened and the onion is translucent.
3.Sprinkle the flour over the cooked vegetables and stir continuously for 2 minutes to cook out the raw flour taste and create a roux that will thicken the filling.
4.Gradually pour in the chicken broth while stirring constantly to prevent lumps, then add the heavy cream, and cook for 5-7 minutes, stirring frequently, until the sauce thickens enough to coat the back of a spoon.
5.Add the shredded cooked chicken, frozen peas, and fresh thyme to the sauce, stir to combine, and season generously with salt and pepper — the filling should be well-seasoned and creamy.
